Result: LayerCAM ranked InceptionV3 as most stable (IoU=0.777); GradCAM++ favored DenseNet201, reducing InceptionV3's stability score by 17.3%. Stability rankings reversed across attribution methods.

Conclusion: Explanation stability is an emergent property of the model-method pair, not intrinsic to the model. Claims should be validated across multiple attribution methods, and regulatory submissions must specify attribution operators used.

Abstract: This position paper argues that claims about explanation stability are scientifically invalid without cross method validation. Just as statistical significance requires the test statistic to be specified, stability should either be evaluated across multiple attribution paradigms or explicitly scoped to the computational objective of a single method. In controlled chest X ray experiments, DenseNet201, ResNet50V2, and InceptionV3 achieved AUC values above 99%, yet their stability rankings reversed across attribution methods. LayerCAM ranked InceptionV3 as the most stable model, with an IoU of 0.777, whereas GradCAM++ favored DenseNet201 and reduced InceptionV3 stability score by 17.3%. These findings demonstrate that explanation stability is an emergent property of the model method pair rather than an intrinsic characteristic of the model alone. We therefore argue that explanation based claims should be validated across multiple attribution methods and that regulatory submissions should explicitly specify the attribution operators used to avoid creating illusory safety assurances.

</details>


### [28] [OFD-Net: Teacher-Free Reliable Semi-supervised Medical Image Segmentation with Orthogonal Feature Disentanglement Net of Foreground-Background](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16705)
*Shao-feng Jiang,Zhe-yang Jing,Qin Lu,Huan-huan Shi,Zhen Chen,Cong-xuan zhang,Chen Yi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: OFD-Net is a teacher-free, single-network semi-supervised learning framework for medical image segmentation that uses orthogonal feature disentanglement to separate foreground and background representations, improving pseudo-label quality and reducing confirmation bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SSL methods for medical image segmentation rely on pseudo-labels from teacher-student or cross-network consistency but lack explicit structural references to judge pseudo-label quality, leading to unreliable training, error accumulation, and confirmation bias when processing unlabeled data with appearance variations.

Method: OFD-Net proposes: (1) Orthogonal Feature Disentanglement Module (OFDM) to separate unlabeled data into background/foreground representations; (2) Disentanglement Guidance Module (DGM) to inject structural priors into decoder via deformable convolution; (3) Reliability-aware pseudo-label learning that evaluates structural consistency between main prediction and disentangled responses, down-weighting unreliable regions.

Result: Extensive experiments on ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC benchmarks validate OFD-Net's effectiveness, showing orthogonal foreground-background disentanglement enables efficient and reliable SSL within a teacher-free single-network framework.

Conclusion: Orthogonal foreground-background disentanglement can establish an efficient and reliable training paradigm for semi-supervised medical image segmentation without requiring teacher networks, effectively reducing error accumulation and confirmation bias.

Abstract: Semi-supervised learning (SSL) is an effective solution for medical image segmentation with limited annotations. Existing SSL methods mainly rely on pseudo-labels generated by teacher-student supervision or cross-network consistency. However, these methods lack an explicit structural reference for judging pseudo-label quality. Low-quality pseudo-labels may lead to unreliable training, error accumulation and confirmation bias when processing unlabeled data with substantial appearance variations. To address this issue, we proposed OFD-Net, a teacher-free single-network framework for reliable semi-supervised medical image segmentation. OFD-Net employs an Orthogonal Feature Disentanglement Module (OFDM) to capture OFD features for reliable SSL by disentangling unlabeled data into background and foreground representations with a reliable structural distribution, thereby effectively reducing error accumulation and alleviating confirmation bias among unlabeled data. Specifically, OFD-Net explicitly employs a Disentanglement Guidance Module (DGM) to inject the resulting structural priors of foreground-background into the decoder by deformable convolution processing, and outputs predictions with clearer foreground representations. Based on DGM and the OFDM, we further develop a reliability-aware pseudo-label learning mechanism that evaluates unlabeled supervision according to the structural consistency between the main prediction and the disentangled foreground-background responses, and then down-weights unreliable regions during training. Extensive experiments on four public medical image segmentation benchmarks, namely ISIC-2016, Kvasir-SEG, Synapse, and ACDC, validate the effectiveness of OFD-Net. These results confirm that orthogonal foreground-background disentanglement enables OFD-Net to establish an efficient and reliable training paradigm within a teacher-free single-network framework.

</details>


### [29] [Spatiotemporal Facial Action Unit Detection using Twin Cycle Autoencoders for Driver Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16760)
*Sai Sidharth D*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Twin Cycle Autoencoder (TCA), a spatiotemporal architecture that jointly learns AU-relevant appearance and temporal dynamics via cycle-consistent autoencoder branches, improving AU detection in challenging driving conditions and achieving real-time performance on embedded platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Driver monitoring systems need robust, real-time detection of subtle and transient facial Action Units under challenging in-cabin conditions (variable lighting, occlusion, head motion), but existing AU detectors treat spatial appearance and temporal dynamics separately, limiting their ability to leverage unlabeled driving video data effectively.

Method: TCA has two coupled cycle-consistent autoencoder branches: (1) a Spatial Cycle Autoencoder that disentangles AU-relevant appearance from identity via image-level cycle consistency, and (2) a Temporal Cycle Autoencoder that enforces forward-backward consistency over latent AU trajectories to model onset-apex-offset dynamics. The branches are coupled through cross-branch latent alignment loss and fused via an attention module before multi-label AU classification.

Result: TCA achieves consistent improvements over CNN-RNN, 3D-CNN, and graph-based AU baselines on DISFA, BP4D, and a naturalistic driving dataset. It performs particularly well on low-intensity and rapidly transitioning AUs (AU45, AU43 for fatigue; AU26 for yawning). The model maintains real-time throughput on Jetson Xavier NX.

Conclusion: The proposed TCA effectively integrates spatial and temporal self-supervision for AU detection in driving contexts, outperforms existing approaches on key fatigue-related AUs, and is deployable on embedded hardware for production ADAS.

Abstract: Driver monitoring systems (DMS) increasingly rely on facial cues to infer drowsiness, distraction, and cognitive load in real time. Facial Action Units (AUs), grounded in the Facial Action Coding System (FACS), provide an objective and interpretable representation of such states, but their automatic detection in the driving context is complicated by low and variable illumination, partial occlusion, head-pose variation, and the subtlety and short duration of relevant AU activations. Existing AU detectors largely treat spatial appearance and temporal dynamics separately, limiting their ability to exploit self-supervisory signal from abundant unlabeled driving video. We propose the Twin Cycle Autoencoder (TCA), a spatiotemporal architecture composed of two coupled cycle-consistent autoencoder branches: a Spatial Cycle Autoencoder that disentangles AU-relevant appearance from identity through image-level cycle consistency, and a Temporal Cycle Autoencoder that enforces forward-backward consistency over latent AU trajectories to capture onset-apex-offset dynamics. The two branches are coupled through a cross-branch latent alignment loss and fused via an attention module before multi-label AU classification. We evaluate TCA on the DISFA and BP4D benchmarks and on an in-cabin naturalistic driving dataset, and observe consistent improvements over CNN-RNN, 3D-CNN, and graph-based AU baselines, particularly for low-intensity and rapidly transitioning AUs relevant to fatigue (AU45, AU43) and yawning (AU26). We further show the model sustains real-time throughput on an embedded Jetson Xavier NX platform, supporting its use in production-grade advanced driver assistance systems (ADAS).

</details>


### [30] [Pediatric Bone Age Prediction Using Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16936)
*Al Zadid Sultan Bin Habib,Md. Ekramul Islam,Md Asif Bin Syed,Md Younus Ahamed,Tanpia Tasnim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper uses EfficientNet with Additive Attention on over 12,000 X-ray images to automate pediatric bone age prediction, finding that EfficientNetB4 with Additive Attention outperforms EfficientNetB0 and baseline B4.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional bone age prediction is labor-intensive and requires specialized radiological expertise, motivating the need for an automated DL-based solution.

Method: The approach preprocesses X-ray images into three-channel inputs, then trains EfficientNetB0, EfficientNetB4, and EfficientNetB4 with Additive Attention (EN-AA) on the RSNA bone age dataset (12,000+ images) for regression-based age prediction.

Result: EfficientNetB4 and EN-AA predicted bone ages more accurately than EfficientNetB0. Learning curves show effective training without overfitting or underfitting.

Conclusion: The DL-based approach, especially EN-AA, provides an effective and accurate solution for pediatric bone age prediction, aiding endocrine disease diagnosis.

Abstract: Pediatric bone age prediction is a crucial task in clinical practice that can help diagnose endocrine disorders and provide insight into a child's growth and development. However, conventional bone age prediction methods are often labor-intensive and require specialized radiological expertise. This paper presents a Deep Learning (DL)-based approach to pediatric bone age prediction using EfficientNet with Additive Attention, a state-of-the-art neural network architecture for image classification and regression tasks. The method utilizes over 12,000 X-ray images from the RSNA bone age dataset. It involves image preprocessing, transforming them into three-channel images, and training a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to automatically learn the features of hand bone images. This approach provides a more effective and accurate solution for predicting bone age, which is critical in diagnosing pediatric endocrine diseases. This work uses two variations of the EfficientNet model (B0 and B4), where EfficientNetB4 is also finetuned with the Additive Attention mechanism. These three models predict the age for the original age, and their comparison is shown in curves. The predicted ages depict that in most cases, EfficientNetB4 and EfficientNetB4 with Additive Attention (EN-AA) successfully predicted the bone ages more accurately regarding the original age, and their performance was better than the EfficientNetB0. Specific performance metrics are provided to underscore this improvement. Learning curves for training and validation loss confirm effective learning without overfitting or underfitting, further validating our approach's efficacy in pediatric endocrine disease diagnosis.

</details>


### [31] [SLT: Robust Quantum Neural Networks for Noisy-Label Medical Image Classification via Supermartingale-based Label Transition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16293)
*Jun Zhuang,Mohammad Al Hasan,Yiyu Shi,Chaowen Guan*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Supermartingale-based Label Transition (SLT), an anchor-free loss correction framework that uses supermartingale theory to model entropy reduction in quantum neural networks, enabling robust noisy-label learning for small-scale medical image classification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Noisy-label learning in small-scale medical image classification is challenging; QNNs show potential in limited-data regimes but their use for noisy-label learning remains under-explored. QNNs' "natural smoothness" can obscure high-confidence samples needed for noise-transition estimation.

Method: SLT models entropy reduction in predictive distributions as a supermartingale, using its monotonic behavior to identify stable transition-matrix refinement steps. This enables dynamic transition updates while reducing noise-driven oscillations during QNN training, with convergence analysis provided.

Result: Experiments on multiple public small-scale medical image datasets show SLT consistently improves QNN-based classification and stably outperforms classic noisy-label learning baselines under both synthetic and real-world label noise.

Conclusion: The SLT framework effectively addresses noisy-label learning in QNNs for medical image classification by leveraging supermartingale-based dynamics for robust transition estimation.

Abstract: Noisy-label learning in small-scale medical image classification is challenging and hinders the superiority of deep neural networks. Recent studies suggest that quantum neural networks (QNNs) have shown potential in limited-data regimes, yet their use for noisy-label learning remains under-explored. A key obstacle is QNNs' intrinsic "natural smoothness", which may regularize training but also obscure high-confidence samples needed for noise-transition estimation. We propose Supermartingale-based Label Transition (SLT), an anchor-free loss correction framework for robust QNN-based medical image classification under noisy labels. SLT models entropy reduction in predictive distributions as a supermartingale and uses its monotonic behavior to identify stable transition-matrix refinement steps. This enables dynamic transition updates while reducing noise-driven oscillations during QNN training. We further provide a convergence analysis showing that the proposed transition-refinement process reaches a steady state. Experiments on multiple public small-scale medical image datasets demonstrate that SLT consistently improves QNN-based classification and stably outperforms classic noise-label learning baselines under synthetic and real-world label noise.

</details>


### [32] [Automated Cardiac Adipose Tissue Segmentation in Computed Tomography: A Literature Review](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16992)
*Andreas W. Aspe,Jonas Jalili Pedersen,Andreas Ohrt Johansen,Klaus Fuglsang Kofoed,Kristine Aavild Sørensen,Rasmus Reinhold Paulsen,Josefine Vilsbøll Sundgaard*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This review covers automated CT segmentation methods for Epicardial and Pericardial Adipose Tissue, comparing AI and non-AI approaches, showing automated methods achieve human-level accuracy while addressing challenges like limited datasets and threshold optimization.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Cardiac fat quantification (EAT/PAT) is clinically important for cardiovascular disease research, but manual segmentation is time-consuming and suffers from inter-observer variability. Automated methods are needed for efficient, consistent analysis.

Method: Comprehensive review of automated segmentation methods on CT, covering both AI-based and non-AI approaches for EAT and PAT segmentation.

Result: Automated methods achieve segmentation results comparable to human annotation quality, demonstrating their clinical potential.

Conclusion: Automated methods show promise as clinical tools for discovering new biomarkers and improving patient outcomes, though challenges remain in obtaining larger annotated public datasets and optimizing attenuation thresholds for contrast-enhanced CT.

Abstract: This review provides an overview of recent advancements in automated segmentation methods on Computed Tomography (CT) for two types of cardiac fat: Epicardial adipose Tissue (EAT) and Pericardial Adipose Tissue (PAT). These fat deposits, separated by the pericardium, have been linked to various cardiovascular diseases, with EAT receiving the most research attention. Their complex anatomical context makes manual quantification highly time-consuming and prone to considerable inter-observer variability. Automated methods effectively address these complications, offering a more efficient and consistent solution. This study encompasses a broad range of methods, spanning AI as well as non-AI approaches. Additionally, it presents the remaining challenges, including the need for larger annotated public datasets and optimized attenuation thresholds for contrast-enhanced CT. It is demonstrated that automated methods are able to achieve segmentation results comparable to the quality of human annotation, proving their potential as a clinical tool for discovering new biomarkers and enhancing patient outcomes.

</details>


### [33] [SGMCE: Segment-Grounded Morphological Concept Explanation for Malaria Parasite Species Identification in Thick Blood Smears](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16324)
*Ahmed Tahiru Issah,Charles B. Delahunt,Carine Mukamakuza*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SGMCE is a post-hoc framework that generates natural-language explanations for malaria parasite detections in thick blood smears by combining mask-guided crops, handcrafted morphological features, and GPT-4o queries conditioned on WHO knowledge bases—achieving high consistency and faithfulness scores without needing additional training or annotations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current deep learning detectors for malaria diagnosis classify Plasmodium species but lack morphological evidence for their predictions, preventing microscopists from auditing those predictions at the case level.

Method: SGMCE extracts mask-guided crop thumbnails, computes 14 handcrafted morphological features (shape, color, chromatin, hemozoin pigment) using adaptive within-mask thresholds, and queries GPT-4o with visual evidence and measurements conditioned on a thick-smear knowledge base from WHO bench aids, producing structured explanations with species discrimination reasoning.

Result: On 737 detections from 139 thick-smear images across four Plasmodium species and WBCs: mean KBC=0.91, mean DS=0.99, mean CCF=0.97. Per-rule CCF breakdown confirms CV-grounded claims are consistent with cited measurements.

Conclusion: SGMCE effectively generates faithful, discriminative explanations grounded in morphological features without requiring additional training data or annotations, enabling auditability of deep learning malaria diagnoses.

Abstract: Malaria diagnosis in endemic regions depends on species-level identification of Plasmodium parasites in thick blood smears, but deep learning detectors classify detections without providing morphological evidence for their predictions, limiting the ability of microscopists to audit those predictions at the case level. We present SGMCE (Segment-Grounded Morphological Concept Explanation), a post-hoc explanation framework that requires no additional training, no morphological annotations, and no labelled explanation data, yet produces per-detection natural-language explanations anchored in thick-smear morphology. For each detection, SGMCE extracts mask-guided crop thumbnails, computes fourteen handcrafted computer-vision morphological features (shape, colour, chromatin, haemozoin pigment) using adaptive within-mask thresholds, and queries GPT-4o with both visual evidence and computed measurements, conditioned on a thick-smear-specific knowledge base compiled from the World Health Organization bench aids. The primary output is a structured explanation identifying which morphological features support the detected species and why the competing species are excluded. Explanations are validated by four automatic metrics: Knowledge-Base Consistency (KBC), CV-Claim Faithfulness (CCF), Discriminativeness Score (DS), and LLM-as-Judge (LLMj). A sentence-level semantic scoring rule with species-aware negation filtering resolves the vocabulary mismatch between clinical prose and knowledge-base terms. Across 737 detections from 139 thick-smear images spanning four Plasmodium species and white blood cells, parasite-class mean KBC is 0.91, mean DS is 0.99, and mean CCF is 0.97, while a per-rule CCF breakdown confirms that the CV-grounded claims made by the vision-language model are consistent with the measurements they cite.

</details>


### [34] [RegionFM: Interpretable Region-Based Brain MRI Classification Using Foundation Model Embeddings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16325)
*Wei Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: RegionFM integrates anatomical segmentation with brain MRI foundation-model embeddings to provide region-level, interpretable explanations for cognitive-impairment classification, maintaining competitive performance while aligning with anatomy-based clinical reasoning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Foundation models for brain MRI analysis lack anatomically interpretable predictions; conventional explanation methods produce voxel- or patch-level importance maps that do not explicitly quantify contributions of individual brain regions, creating a mismatch with clinical assessment practices organized around anatomically defined structures.

Method: RegionFM divides each MRI scan into anatomical regions, constructs separate MRI volumes per region, encodes each region using a frozen foundation model, and applies a region-additive logistic model where each anatomical region contributes an explicit scalar term to the final prediction.

Result: RegionFM maintains performance comparable to less interpretable fine-tuning approaches while providing anatomically grounded explanations; randomized embedding ablations yield near-chance performance, confirming reliance on meaningful structure rather than simple feature statistics.

Conclusion: RegionFM better aligns model explanations with anatomy-based clinical reasoning while maintaining competitive predictive performance, supporting both subject-level and cohort-level analyses of regional contributions.

Abstract: Foundation models provide powerful representations for brain MRI analysis, but their predictions remain difficult to interpret in anatomically meaningful terms. Clinical assessment of brain MRI is commonly organized around anatomically defined structures and regional abnormalities, whereas conventional explanation methods typically produce voxel- or patch-level importance maps that do not explicitly quantify the contributions of individual brain regions. To address this mismatch, we propose RegionFM, an interpretable framework that integrates anatomical segmentation with brain MRI foundation-model embeddings. RegionFM first divides each MRI scan into anatomical regions and constructs a separate MRI volume for each region. A frozen foundation model then encodes each region into an embedding, and a region-additive logistic model combines these embeddings such that every anatomical region contributes an explicit scalar term to the final prediction. This formulation supports both subject-level and cohort-level analyses of regional contributions. We evaluate RegionFM on cognitive-impairment classification using embeddings from multiple pretrained brain MRI foundation models. The results show that RegionFM maintains performance comparable to less interpretable fine-tuning approaches while providing anatomically grounded explanations. Randomized embedding ablations yield near-chance performance, indicating that the predictions rely on meaningful structure captured by the foundation-model embeddings rather than simple feature statistics. Overall, RegionFM better aligns model explanations with anatomy-based clinical reasoning while maintaining competitive predictive performance.

</details>


### [35] [Disentangling Model and Human Data Uncertainty in Apparent Facial Age Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16378)
*Andrei Foitos,Ivo Pascal de Jong,Matias Valdenegro-Toro*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper explores uncertainty estimation in apparent age estimation from facial images, distinguishing between epistemic (model knowledge) and aleatoric (inherent noise) uncertainty. Using Bayesian Neural Networks (MC-DropConnect, Flipout, Deep Ensembles) on the APPA-REAL dataset, authors show that aleatoric uncertainty stays stable regardless of dataset size, while epistemic uncertainty increases with smaller datasets, confirming the ability to quantify different uncertainty sources.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Apparent age estimation is challenging due to subjective perception and data variability; the authors aim to understand and quantify different sources of uncertainty (epistemic vs. aleatoric) in this task, particularly how they behave with varying dataset sizes.

Method: Used Bayesian Neural Networks with three approximations (MC-DropConnect, Flipout, Deep Ensembles) trained on the APPA-REAL dataset, which provides human aleatoric uncertainty labels. Models output both predicted apparent age and separate aleatoric/epistemic uncertainty estimates, trained on datasets of varying sizes.

Result: Aleatoric uncertainty remains stable across different dataset sizes, while epistemic uncertainty increases as training data decreases, confirming the hypothesis about the distinct behavior of these uncertainty types.

Conclusion: Different sources of uncertainty (epistemic and aleatoric) can be effectively quantified in face age estimation tasks, with the finding that aleatoric uncertainty is inherent and data-independent, while epistemic uncertainty reflects model knowledge gaps.

Abstract: Estimating the apparent age of individuals from facial images is challenging due to the subjective nature of perception and the inherent variability of the data. We investigate the role of uncertainty estimation, attributing uncertainty jointly to a lack of knowledge (epistemic) or inherent noise/chance (aleatoric). Leveraging the APPA-REAL dataset, we train Bayesian Neural Networks on datasets of varying sizes using three BNN approximations: MC-DropConnect, Flipout, and Deep Ensembles using supervision on human aleatoric uncertainty available in the APPA-REAL dataset. Each model outputs both the predicted apparent age and the amount of aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty. Our results confirm the hypothesis that the inherent aleatoric uncertainty remains stable across dataset sizes, while epistemic uncertainty increases as training data decreases. These findings demonstrate that different sources of uncertainty can be quantified in face age estimation.

</details>


### [36] [EvoGUI: An Evolution-Aware Benchmark for GUI State-Transition Understanding](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17050)
*Yaohan Yang,Minglei Shi,Borui Zhang,Jie Zhou,Jiwen Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EvoGUI is a diagnostic framework and benchmark (EvoGUI-Bench) that evaluates GUI agents' state-transition understanding through three visual QA probes, finding that even the strongest model achieves only 60.4% accuracy and that scale/specialization don't reliably predict performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: End-to-end success rates in GUI agents conflate state-transition reasoning with perception, grounding, planning, and recovery. The authors aim to isolate and diagnose the specific ability of understanding how actions transform interface states.

Method: EvoGUI converts normalized GUI trajectories into three VQA probes: (1) temporal ordering, (2) inverse action/value prediction, (3) contrastive one-step successor discrimination. Labels come from trajectory order and logged actions without extra annotation. Built from Mind2Web and WebLINX (3,000 instances, 120 domains), evaluated with 28 VLMs zero-shot.

Result: Best model achieves 60.4 EvoGain; model scale and GUI specialization do not reliably predict performance, showing large headroom for improvement.

Conclusion: EvoGUI-Bench serves as a scalable, diagnostic complement to end-to-end GUI agent evaluation, revealing substantial gaps in state-transition understanding that are obscured by overall task success metrics.

Abstract: GUI agents must reason about how actions transform interface states, but end-to-end success rates entangle this ability with perception, grounding, planning, and recovery. We introduce EvoGUI, a diagnostic framework that converts normalized GUI trajectories into three complementary visual question answering probes: temporal ordering, inverse action/value prediction, and contrastive one-step successor discrimination. Their labels are derived from trajectory order and logged actions, requiring no additional task-label annotation after trajectory normalization. We instantiate EvoGUI-Bench from Mind2Web and WebLINX, yielding 3,000 instances across 120 domains, and evaluate 28 vision-language model configurations zero-shot. The strongest model reaches only 60.4 EvoGain, while model scale and GUI specialization do not reliably predict performance. These results establish EvoGUI-Bench as a scalable diagnostic complement to end-to-end GUI-agent evaluation while exposing substantial headroom in state-transition understanding. The source code is publicly available at https://github.com/Yyhhh6/EvoGUI.

</details>


### [37] [ALLUDE: A Unified Evaluation System for Configurable Attacks in Differentiable Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17077)
*Mansi Phute,Alexander Greenhalgh,Matthew Hull,Haoran Wang,Alec Helbling,ShengYun Peng,Elliott Faa,Willian Lunardi,Martin Andreoni,Wenke Lee,Duen Horng Chau*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ALLUDE is a unified, cross-platform framework for robust, end-to-end evaluation of adversarial attacks against vision models, enabling customizable configurations across scenes, weather, optimizers, trajectories, and detectors.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing adversarial attack evaluations are limited in scope, lacking a unified system with rich, customizable configurations for diverse real-world conditions.

Method: ALLUDE bridges simulation and differentiable rendering, with evaluation via Latin Hypercube Sampling across 5,400 configurations (10 scene-object pairs, 9 weather conditions, 4 optimizers, 5 camera trajectories, 3 detectors) and stress-testing existing attacks (CAMOU, RAUCA, FCA).

Result: Revealed degradation of attack success across all tested attacks (CAMOU, RAUCA, FCA) under diverse weather conditions and continuous camera trajectories, exposing evaluation gaps in prior work.

Conclusion: ALLUDE provides first-of-its-kind evaluation capabilities for adversarial attacks across varied real-world deployment conditions, with open-source cross-platform code.

Abstract: Adversarial attacks against vision models like object detectors are often evaluated under limited conditions, leaving their performance under-characterized. Bridging simulation and differentiable rendering enables more robust, end-to-end evaluation of these adversarial attacks, yet there is no easy-to-use, unified system that offers a rich set of customizable configurations for adversarial attacks across multiple scenes, objects, environmental and lighting conditions, and camera trajectories. We present ALLUDE, which addresses these gaps, offering first-of-its-kind evaluation capabilities across Linux and Windows. We comprehensively demonstrate ALLUDE's evaluation breadth through a two-pronged strategy: (1) using Latin Hypercube Sampling, we draw a representative subset from 5,400 configurations spanning 10 scene-object pairs, 9 weather conditions, 4 optimizers, 5 camera trajectories, and 3 detection models; (2) we stress-test existing attacks (CAMOU, RAUCA, FCA) under diverse weather conditions and continuous camera trajectories, revealing degradation of attack success across every attack, exposing evaluation gaps in prior work. Through ALLUDE's end-to-end differentiable rendering, adversarial attacks can be optimized against shifting real-world deployment conditions. Our cross-platform code is open source.

</details>


### [38] [DepthART: Scaling Foundation Monocular Depth to Tiny Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17099)
*Feng Xue,Wu Chen,Mingshuai Zhao,Guofeng Zhong,Anlong Ming,Haozhe Wang,Dianqiao Lei,Zhaowen Lin,Haiyang Zhang,Nicu Sebe*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DepthART is a compact monocular depth estimation model that bridges the performance gap between large geometric foundation models and tiny on-device models by addressing overfitting to dataset bias and unstable metric adaptation through bias-resistant sampling and camera-conditioned fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Recent geometric foundation models (Metric3D, Depth Anything, UniDepth) excel at monocular depth estimation in cross-scene generalization and metric-scale prediction, but these advances have not been successfully transferred to tiny models suitable for on-device deployment.

Method: DepthART combines two strategies: (1) a bias-resistant data sampling scheme to reduce dataset-specific distribution bias under the same training budget, and (2) a camera-conditioned fine-tuning protocol that freezes the distilled encoder and adjusts metric scale conditioned on camera intrinsics while preserving cross-dataset generalization.

Result: DepthART consistently surpasses previous tiny baselines in zero-shot generalization and metric accuracy (e.g., zero-shot δ1=0.964 for DepthART-S on NYUD v2). DepthART-S achieves 347/245 FPS on RTX A6000 at 224²/448², 102 FPS on Orin NX 8GB, and over 15 FPS on Jetson Nano 4GB.

Conclusion: DepthART successfully bridges the gap between large foundation models and tiny models for on-device monocular depth estimation, providing a scalable model family that approaches the performance of heavy models while maintaining high inference speeds on resource-constrained hardware.

Abstract: Recent geometric foundation models (e.g., Metric3D, Depth Anything and UniDepth) have substantially improved monocular depth estimation (MDE) in both cross-scene generalization and metric-scale prediction, yet these gains have not translated to tiny models. We bridge this gap with DepthART (Depth Anything Rethought for Tiny Models), which is a compact MDE model for on-device deployment across diverse scenes. We first identify two capacity-driven bottlenecks in tiny models: (i) overfitting to dataset-specific distribution bias and (ii) unstable metric adaptation under camera shift, where full fine-tuning easily damages transferable geometry. Accordingly, DepthART combines two simple but effective strategies: a bias-resistant data sampling scheme to reduce distribution bias under the same training budget, and a camera-conditioned fine-tuning protocol that freezes the distilled encoder and adjusts metric scale conditioned on intrinsics while better preserving cross-dataset generalization. Across datasets, DepthART consistently surpasses previous tiny baselines in both zero-shot generalization and metric accuracy (e.g., zero-shot $δ_1$=0.964 for DepthART-S on NYUD v2), and in some cases approaches heavy models. We further provide a scalable model family, with DepthART-S reaching 347/245 FPS (strict FP32) on an RTX A6000 at $224^2/448^2$, 102 FPS (TF32) on a Orin NX 8GB, and over 15 FPS (FP32) on a Jetson Nano 4GB.

</details>


### [39] [Spectral-Morphological Attention U-Net: An Efficient Network for Active Wildfire Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16472)
*Yugong Zeng,Jonathan Wu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes SMA-UNet, a deep learning model combining spectral attention, residual attention UNet, channel-spatial modulator, and differentiable morphological gates for early wildfire detection from satellite imagery. It achieves state-of-the-art performance (75.16% IoU on TS-SatFire, 22.50% on Sen2Fire) and demonstrates robustness through ablation studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Global wildfires are increasing steadily. Early detection and precise localization can minimize hazards. Machine learning on satellite images offers automatic monitoring of remote areas, motivating the need for a more accurate and robust wildfire detection model.

Method: Proposes SMA-UNet with four key components: (1) spectral attention module, (2) residual attention UNet backbone, (3) channel-spatial modulator, and (4) a pair of differentiable morphological gates. These modules (except backbone) are applied to active fire detection for the first time. The model is trained and evaluated on two datasets with ablation studies.

Result: Achieved highest IoU scores: 75.16% on TS-SatFire and 22.50% on Sen2Fire. Ablation studies confirmed each module's independent contribution and their combined integration yields a highly robust framework for segmentation consistency across diverse environmental conditions.

Conclusion: The integrated modules form a robust framework improving segmentation consistency. Future work will validate the architecture on large-scale, multi-regional, multi-sensor datasets to establish broader generalizability for global wildfire detection.

Abstract: Over the past decades, the frequency of global wildfires has been increasing steadily. Therefore, if the fire can be detected and precisely located at an early stage, the potential hazards caused by it can be minimized to the greatest extent. The machine learning methods based on satellite images, due to their ability to automatically monitor extremely remote and vast areas, have shown great potential for application in the field of wildfire detection. To address this challenge, we proposed a new model named spectral-morphological attention U-Net(SMA-UNet), which includes a spectral attention module, a residual attention UNet backbone, a channel-spatial modulator, and a pair of differentiable morphological gates. We trained and evaluated this model with two datasets. These modules, excluding the backbone, are used to detect active fire events for the first time, especially the pair of differentiable morphological gates, which is innovatively developed. The proposed model achieved the highest scores in both datasets (e.g., intersection over union 75.16% in TS-SatFire, 22.50% in Sen2Fire). By conducting ablation studies of each module, we compared their independent contributions and tested their combinations. Ultimately, the integration of these modules yields a highly robust framework that significantly improves segmentation consistency across diverse and complex environmental conditions. Future work will focus on validating the proposed architecture across large-scale, multi-regional datasets from different satellite sensors to establish its broader generalizability for global wildfire detection.

</details>


### [40] [Noise-Robust Box-Supervised Infrared Small Target Detection via Physics-Inspired Soft Label Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17148)
*Xizhe Zhang,Fan Shi,Mianzhao Wang,Jiangpeng Zheng,Xu Cheng,Shengyong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes HALO, a method for box-supervised infrared small target detection that converts noisy box annotations into stable pixel-level soft labels using radiometric anchors and Gaussian modeling, improving robustness under imperfect box supervision.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Pixel-level mask supervision for IRSTD is costly and inherently uncertain due to blurred boundaries and weak textures of infrared targets, motivating the need for cheaper box supervision that can handle noisy annotations.

Method: HALO localizes a radiometric anchor inside each box using local background-statistics constraints, then synthesizes a Physically Anchored Gaussian (PAG) soft label around the anchor, turning noisy box supervision into continuous pixel-level soft labels offline before training.

Result: HALO is competitive with representative box-supervised methods under standard tight boxes, and substantially more robust under looser or shifted box annotations that approximate real scenarios, while remaining consistent across backbones.

Conclusion: Box-supervised IRSTD can be effectively addressed with offline soft label generation (HALO), which is robust to imperfect annotations and provides a contamination-aware operating-regime analysis linking signal-to-clutter ratio to performance.

Abstract: Infrared small target detection (IRSTD) commonly relies on pixel-level mask supervision. Such annotations, however, are costly and inherently uncertain because infrared targets have blurred boundaries and weak textures. We formulate box-supervised IRSTD as a problem distinct from generic box-to-mask segmentation and point-supervised IRSTD. Its central challenge is to construct stable pixel-level soft supervision from highly contaminated boxes. To this end, we propose Hotspot-Anchored Label Optimization (HALO). HALO localizes a radiometric anchor inside each box under local background-statistics constraints, then synthesizes a Physically Anchored Gaussian (PAG) soft label around the anchor. This turns noisy box supervision into continuous, pixel-level soft labels. The entire process is performed offline before training, remains decoupled from the detector backbone, and requires no online label updates. Experiments on public datasets show that HALO is competitive with representative box-supervised methods under standard tight boxes. Under looser or shifted box annotations that better approximate real scenarios, HALO is substantially more robust while remaining consistent across backbones. We further introduce a contamination-aware operating-regime analysis to characterize the effective boundary of this class of methods and reveal how intrinsic signal-to-clutter ratio relates to performance.

</details>


### [41] [VLA-ReID: Video-Level Association for Re-Identification in Multi-Object Tracking with Highly Similar Objects](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17157)
*Yanrong Qin,Xiaoyan Cao,Yao Yao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: VLA-ReID reformulates re-ID as video-level association modeling and introduces Frame-Common Appearance Estimation (FCAE) with Common-Appearance Suppression (CAS) to reduce identity switches in multi-object tracking, achieving significant improvements on the BEE24 benchmark (e.g., 28% fewer identity switches).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-object tracking suffers from a training-inference mismatch: re-ID models are trained via single-instance assignment but must perform global (video-level) assignment at inference, causing identity switches among visually similar objects like bees.

Method: VLA-ReID performs video-level association using aggregated historical trajectory features as queries and all current-frame detections as candidates. FCAE estimates common appearance direction from detections, and CAS removes that component to amplify discriminative differences without extra annotations.

Result: On BEE24, VLA-ReID improves HOTA by 1.1, MOTA by 0.3, AssR by 2.6, AssA by 0.7, and IDF1 by 0.8, while reducing identity switches by 28% over state-of-the-art trackers.

Conclusion: Video-level re-ID modeling effectively addresses the mismatch between training and inference in MOT, and appearance suppression techniques enhance discrimination among similar objects without additional annotation cost.

Abstract: Multi-object tracking (MOT) aims to localize multiple objects in videos while preserving their identities over time. Long-term identity preservation remains difficult when objects are small, densely distributed, and highly similar in appearance, as in bee swarm scenes. Existing trackers rely on re-identification (re-ID) models trained through single-instance assignment (instance-level querying). At inference, however, MOT requires global assignment between multiple trajectories and detections, corresponding to video-level querying. This training-inference mismatch can cause identity switches among visually similar objects. Existing approaches also often require substantial additional annotations to enhance appearance discrimination. We propose Video-Level Association re-ID (VLA-ReID), which reformulates re-ID as video-level association modeling. It uses aggregated historical trajectory features as queries and all current-frame detections as candidates, enabling direct optimization of their global association at each frame. In addition, Frame-Common Appearance Estimation (FCAE) estimates a common appearance direction from current-frame detections, while Common-Appearance Suppression (CAS) removes the corresponding component along this direction from trajectory and detection features. This amplifies discriminative differences among highly similar objects without additional annotations. Experiments on BEE24 show that VLA-ReID improves HOTA by 1.1, MOTA by 0.3, AssR by 2.6, AssA by 0.7, and IDF1 by 0.8 over state-of-the-art trackers, while reducing identity switches by 28%. These results demonstrate the effectiveness of video-level re-ID modeling for appearance-based association in MOT.

</details>


### [42] [Transferable Low-Rank Convolutional Bases for Onboarding Unseen Medical Imaging Modalities](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16888)
*Ranat Das Prangon,Istiaque Ahmed,Shajid Hasan Naim,Waseem Mustak Zisan,Hossain Md Shakhawat*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a convolutional low-rank adaptation (conv-LoRA) method for onboarding unseen medical imaging modalities into frozen pre-trained backbones, achieving strong target accuracy with only 0.78% of parameters and zero degradation to source modality performance, while demonstrating that convolutional rather than decision-layer adaptation is necessary for transfer.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying medical imaging models that must later accommodate a modality never seen during training is a practical problem, as retraining shared representations is expensive and degrades performance on existing modalities.

Method: The authors adopt a strict leave-one-domain-out protocol where a convolutional backbone is pre-trained on source modalities (Kidney CT, Brain MRI), frozen, and then adapted to an unseen modality (Chest X-ray). They compare decision-layer fine-tuning (linear probe, fully-connected LoRA) with convolutional LoRA (conv-LoRA), which freezes a low-rank convolutional basis learned from source modalities and trains only the up-projections.

Result: Convolutional LoRA outperforms decision-layer methods (linear probe and FC-LoRA) by a large margin, achieving accuracy 6.11 pp above a random basis of identical size. It uses only 0.78% of full fine-tuning's parameters. Adapter-based onboarding leaves source accuracy unchanged (Δ=0.00 pp), while full fine-tuning causes catastrophic forgetting. A Mahalanobis score on frozen features effectively detects the unseen modality.

Conclusion: Convolutional adaptation is essential for transferring to unseen modalities; the low-rank convolutional basis learned on source modalities transfers effectively, enabling parameter-efficient onboarding without degrading source performance. A Mahalanobis-based trigger can detect when onboarding is needed.

Abstract: Deploying a medical imaging model that must later accommodate a modality it has never seen is a recurring practical problem: retraining the shared representation is expensive and destroys performance on the modalities already in service. We study this \emph{onboarding} problem under a strict leave-one-domain-out protocol, in which a convolutional backbone is pre-trained on source modalities (Kidney CT and Brain MRI), frozen permanently, and then required to accommodate an unseen modality (Chest X-ray). Under this protocol we establish three findings. First, decision-layer parameter-efficient fine-tuning is insufficient when the backbone has never observed the target modality: a linear probe and fully-connected LoRA both fall well short, whereas convolutional LoRA recovers most of the achievable accuracy, showing that adaptation must reach the convolutional features. Second, and centrally, the low-rank convolutional \emph{basis} learned on the source modalities \emph{transfers}: freezing that basis and training only its up-projections onboards the unseen modality using just $0.78\%$ of full fine-tuning's parameters, at an accuracy $6.11$ percentage points above a random basis of identical size, while an equivalent decision-layer basis exhibits no reliable transfer. Third, adapter-based onboarding leaves source-modality accuracy exactly unchanged ($Δ= 0.00$ pp), whereas full fine-tuning reaches the highest target accuracy only by catastrophically degrading the source modalities. A Mahalanobis score on frozen backbone features detects the unseen modality with high sensitivity at a strict source-retention threshold, providing a practical trigger for when onboarding is required. All results are reported over three seeds with paired bootstrap confidence intervals.

</details>


### [43] [STAR: Skeletal Token Alignment and Rearrangement for Interaction Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17342)
*Yuhang Wen,Mengyuan Liu,Zixuan Tang,Junsong Yuan,Sirui Li,Beichen Ding*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: STAR is a method for human-robot and human-human interaction recognition that aligns skeleton and RGB video representations via contrastive learning. It uses a skeleton encoder with Entity Rearrangement and Interactive Spatiotemporal Tokens, plus a Visual Interaction Encoding with Focus on Interactions strategy. During inference, it only needs skeletons but retains benefits from visual data, achieving SOTA on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing skeleton-based interaction recognition methods struggle with (1) effectively learning interaction-specific cues from skeletal data, and (2) compensating for the lack of visual information absent in skeletons alone.

Method: STAR has three components: (1) Skeleton encoder with Entity Rearrangement (ER) and Interactive Spatiotemporal Tokens (ISTs) to capture fine-grained interdependencies; (2) Visual Interaction Encoding with Focus on Interactions (FoI) strategy to attend to interaction-relevant regions in RGB videos; (3) Contrastive learning to align skeleton and RGB representations, with a refinement head. During training, both skeleton and RGB data are used; at inference, only skeletons are needed.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods on Chico, HARPER, NTU Mutual 11 and 26 datasets.

Conclusion: The proposed STAR approach effectively aligns skeleton and visual representations, enabling skeleton-only inference with visual-informed benefits, achieving superior performance on multiple interaction recognition benchmarks.

Abstract: Understanding physical human-robot and human-human interactions is a challenging yet emerging topic in 3D vision. While most existing methods rely on skeleton sequences--effective in low-light and privacy-sensitive environment--they face two major challenges: 1) learning and effectively exploiting interaction cues from skeletal data, and 2) compensating for the lack of visual information absent in skeletons alone. To address these challenges, we propose skeletal token alignment and rearrangement (STAR) for human-robot and human-human interaction recognition. It learns interaction-specific skeleton features and enriches them using visual cues by aligning skeleton and RGB video representations in a shared latent space. Specifically, STAR consists of three key components. First, we design a skeleton encoder that captures fine-grained interdependencies using Entity Rearrangement (ER) and Interactive Spatiotemporal Tokens (ISTs). Second, we present Visual Interaction Encoding that introduces a Focus on Interactions (FoI) strategy to attend to spatiotemporal regions relevant to interactions in RGB videos. Finally, these representations are aligned via a contrastive learning objective, with a refinement head further refines predictions. During training, STAR leverages both skeleton and RGB video data to learn robust, discriminative interaction representations. At inference time, it operates on skeletons alone, retaining visual-informed benefits while preserving skeleton-only efficiency. Extensive experiments on Chico, HARPER, NTU Mutual 11 and 26 datasets consistently validate our approach by demonstrating superior performance over state-of-the-art methods. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Necolizer/STAR.

</details>


### [44] [DecoyFace: Beyond Obfuscation via Controllable and Imperceptible Identity Misdirection for Privacy-Preserving Face Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17504)
*Zhihan Ren,Lijun He,Xinyao Wang,Xinzhu Fu,Fan Li*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DecoyFace is a privacy-preserving face recognition framework that injects decoy identity cues into the reconstruction-sensitive subspace of intermediate features, so that unauthorized reconstruction yields a plausible but incorrect identity, while authorized recognition remains accurate via a canonicalization module.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing privacy-preserving face recognition methods produce visibly degraded reconstructions that reveal the existence of protection, motivating adaptive attacks. The authors aim to achieve imperceptible protection that steers unauthorized reconstruction toward a wrong but plausible identity.

Method: DecoyFace decomposes intermediate representations into a reconstruction-sensitive subspace (where decoy identity cues are injected) and a complementary subspace (retaining recognition-relevant true identity evidence). On the server, an authorized canonicalization module suppresses decoy-dominant components to recover a recognition-friendly representation.

Result: DecoyFace preserves competitive recognition accuracy while reducing identity leakage to 2.93% under U-Net attacks and 0.74% under Flow-Matching attacks, with over 99.78% face validity on LFW dataset.

Conclusion: DecoyFace effectively balances privacy and utility by producing imperceptible, decoy-oriented protection that misleads attackers with plausible but incorrect identities, without degrading recognition performance for authorized use.

Abstract: Split face recognition reduces client-side computation but exposes intermediate features to feature inversion attacks and unauthorized analysis by honest-but-curious (HBC) servers. Existing privacy-preserving face recognition methods mainly aim to resist unauthorized reconstruction, typically producing features whose inversion yields visibly degraded results, which may reveal the existence of protection and motivate adaptive attacks. To address this issue, we propose DecoyFace, an imperceptible decoy-oriented framework that steers unauthorized reconstruction toward a plausible but incorrect identity while preserving recognition utility. The key idea is to decompose the intermediate representation into a reconstruction-sensitive subspace and its complementary subspace. The client injects decoy identity cues into the reconstruction-sensitive subspace, while limited recognition-relevant evidence from the true sample is retained in the complementary subspace. On the server side, an authorized canonicalization module suppresses decoy-dominant components and recovers a recognition-friendly representation. This design addresses both attacker-side inversion from intercepted features and HBC server-side reconstruction from canonicalized representations. Experiments show that DecoyFace preserves competitive recognition accuracy while substantially reducing identity leakage to 2.93% under U-Net attacks and 0.74% under Flow-Matching attacks while yielding visually plausible and imperceptible reconstructions, with over 99.78% face validity on LFW dataset.

</details>


### [45] [DA-MergeLoRA: Hypernetwork-Based LoRA Merging for Few-Shot Test-Time Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17467)
*Siobhan Reid,Zhixiang Chi,Li Gu,Omid Reza Heidari,Ziqiang Wang,Yang Wang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces a FSTT-DA framework that combines LoRA fine-tuning with model merging, using a meta-learning hypernetwork to generate merging factors for adapting source-specific LoRA modules to target domains, achieving SOTA results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior FSTT-DA methods fail to effectively leverage source domain-specific knowledge, relying on shallow BN updates, black-box prompt methods, or ensembling that ignores cross-domain relationships.

Method: Separate LoRA modules are fine-tuned on CLIP's vision encoder per source domain. A hypernetwork trained via meta-learning generates per-column merging factors to combine LoRA modules based on a small batch of target images, producing a single adapted representation.

Result: State-of-the-art performance across various domain adaptation datasets.

Conclusion: LoRA fine-tuning preserves generalized knowledge while learning domain-specific features, and hypernetwork-based merging effectively adapts source knowledge to target domains in few-shot settings.

Abstract: Few-shot Test-Time Domain Adaptation (FSTT-DA) seeks to adapt models to novel domains using only a handful of unlabeled target samples. This setting is more realistic than typical domain adaptation setups, which assume access to target data during source training. However, prior FSTT-DA approaches fail to effectively leverage source domain-specific knowledge, relying on shallow batch normalization updates, prompt-based methods that treat the model as a black box, or ensembling strategies that do not capture cross-domain relationships. To address these limitations, we introduce a new FSTT-DA framework that integrates LoRA fine-tuning with model merging. In our approach, separate LoRA modules are fine-tuned on CLIP's vision encoder for each source domain. Since LoRA modifies only a small fraction of the model's parameters, it retains the base model's generalized knowledge while internally learning domain-specific features. To adapt the learned knowledge to a specific target domain, we propose a hypernetwork trained via meta-learning that generates per-column merging factors to combine LoRA modules. Given a small batch of target images, the hypernetwork produces merging weights that fuse source LoRA modules into a single adapted representation. Our results demonstrate state-of-the-art performance across various domain adaptation datasets. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/nahbois4321/DA-MergeLoRA.

</details>


### [46] [Thinking in Video: Can Video Generators Really Reason About the Real World?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17523)
*Yongheng Zhang,Guang Yang,Ruihan Hou,Qiguang Chen,Ziang Liu,Xiaolong Liu,Manman Zhang,Yanchao Hao,Zheng Wei,Hao Wu,Libo Qin,Peishan Dai,Yinghui Li,Di Yin,Xing Sun*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces CGDJ, a dual-judge framework to evaluate whether video generative models truly reason causally (Thinking in Video) or merely memorize appearances, revealing a Perception-Prediction Gap where models can generate plausible videos without causal understanding.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To verify whether recent video generative models (termed "Thinking in Video") possess genuine causal reasoning capabilities rather than just memorized appearance patterns, as existing evaluation metrics fail to separate perceptual fidelity from semantic logic.

Method: Proposes Causal-Generative Dual-Judge (CGDJ) with two perspectives: (1) Explicit Causal Perception via spatio-temporal flattened VQA, and (2) Implicit Generative Perception-Prediction Gap measuring consistency between causal reasoning and future video generation.

Result: Open-source models generate plausible dynamics but have near-zero explicit causal perception; advanced closed-source systems show stronger but still limited reasoning-generation alignment. Audio-visual misalignment revealed—models verbalize correct causal logic better than rendering it visually.

Conclusion: The claimed "world simulator" narrative is challenged by the identified Perception-Prediction Gap and audio-visual misalignment, suggesting current video generative models lack genuine causal understanding.

Abstract: Recent advances in world models and video generation have given rise to an emerging reasoning paradigm that leverages video generative models to simulate, predict, and reason about real-world dynamics. We redefine this paradigm as Thinking in Video, where video is not merely an output artifact but a medium for constructing, extending, and verifying causal thought. However, this promise remains unverified: convincing rollouts may reflect memorized appearances rather than causal understanding, while existing metrics separate perceptual fidelity from semantic logic. To evaluate whether video generators support such reasoning, we introduce the Causal-Generative Dual-Judge (CGDJ), auditing World Model Consistency from two perspectives. Explicit Causal Perception tests whether a generator reads a video scenario as a reasoning problem through spatio-temporal flattened visual question answering, while Implicit Generative Perception-Prediction Gap evaluates whether it renders the causal consequence as a consistent future video. Applying CGDJ to representative open- and closed-source generators reveals a clear Perception-Prediction Gap: open-source models produce plausible dynamics despite near-zero explicit causal perception, whereas advanced closed-source systems show stronger but still limited alignment between reasoning and generation. Further analysis exposes audio-visual misalignment, where models verbalize correct causal logic more reliably than they render it, challenging the "world simulator" narrative.

</details>


### [47] [Hierarchy-Aware and Anatomy-Guided Learning for Lung Ultrasound Video Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17551)
*Alya Almsouti,Lotfi Mecharbat,Noha Aboukhater,Yousef Alabrach,Siddiq Anwar,Andre Kumar,Ibrahim Almakky,Mohammad Yaqub*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deep learning framework for multi-class lung ultrasound video classification that combines hierarchy-aware training and anatomy-guided (pleural line) supervision, achieving 65.7% macro-F1 on an open dataset of 1,886 videos and demonstrating competitive performance on external COVID-BLUeS data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Lung ultrasound is useful for assessing pulmonary edema but automated analysis is challenged by speckle noise, imaging artifacts, and operator variability.

Method: A deep learning framework integrating hierarchy-aware training strategies and pleural line mask supervision to guide attention toward anatomically relevant regions, evaluated on four classes (healthy, B-lines, consolidations, mixed).

Result: Hierarchy-aware training improves pathological separation over flat classification; mask-guided attention achieves 65.7% mean macro-F1 with more localized attention patterns. Competitive transfer learning results on COVID-BLUeS dataset.

Conclusion: Combining clinically structured objectives with anatomy-guided supervision is a practical approach to robust and interpretable LUS video analysis.

Abstract: Lung ultrasound (LUS) is a bedside tool for assessing pulmonary edema in patients at risk due to heart failure or impaired kidney function. However, automated LUS analysis remains challenging because of speckle noise, imaging artifacts, and operator-dependent acquisition variability. In this work, we present a deep learning framework for multi-class LUS video classification that explores two components: hierarchy-aware training, and anatomy-guided learning. Starting from a strong baseline, we introduce hierarchical training strategies and then introduce pleural line mask supervision to guide model attention toward anatomically relevant regions. We study four clinically relevant classes--healthy, B-lines, consolidations, and mixed B-lines with consolidations--using an open-access dataset of 1,886 videos from 219 patients, evaluated with patient-level five-fold cross-validation. Results show that hierarchy-aware training improves pathological separation relative to flat classification, while mask-guided attention supervision achieves the highest mean macro-F1 of 65.7\% and produces more localized attention patterns. Transfer experiments on the external COVID-BLUeS dataset further show competitive and parameter-efficient adaptation while preserving pleural-focused attention behavior. These findings suggest that combining clinically structured objectives with anatomy-guided supervision is a practical approach to robust, interpretable LUS video analysis. Code and model implementations are available at https://github.com/Alya-Almsouti/LUS-video-classification.

</details>


### [48] [Semantic Color Naturalness Breaker: Preventing Illegitimate Colorization via Content-Aware Color Priors](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17610)
*Yuki Nii,Futa Waseda,Ching-Chun Chang,Isao Echizen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes Semantic Color Naturalness Breaker (SCNB), a method that adds imperceptible perturbations to grayscale images to prevent unauthorized AI colorization, along with a new metric (CaCDD) to measure color plausibility without ground truth.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automatic image colorization enables large-scale unauthorized reuse of grayscale media (e.g., manga panels, archival photos). There is a practical need for proactive, content-side protection at publication time to prevent unauthorized colorized derivatives.

Method: Builds on Uncolorable Examples (UE) by proposing a semantic-level framework (SCNB) that adds imperceptible perturbations to released grayscale images, driving colorization outputs toward content-inconsistent colors while preserving visual fidelity of the original grayscale media. Also introduces CaCDD, a ground-truth-free, content-aware metric for color plausibility.

Result: Experiments on ImageNet show the method remains effective under small perturbation budgets and common post-processing, supporting practical deployment in real-world content-sharing pipelines.

Conclusion: SCNB provides a proactive defense against unauthorized AI colorization with practical efficacy under realistic constraints, measured via the novel CaCDD metric.

Abstract: Automatic image colorization enables large-scale and low-cost reuse of grayscale media (e.g., manga panels and archival photographs), facilitating unauthorized reuse and redistribution. Once released online, grayscale content can be readily turned into unauthorized colorized derivatives using off-the-shelf models, creating a practical need for proactive, content-side protection at publication time. Building on Uncolorable Examples (UE), which add imperceptible perturbations to released grayscale images to degrade unauthorized colorization, we propose Semantic Color Naturalness Breaker (SCNB) -- a semantic-level UE framework that drives colorization outputs toward content-inconsistent colors while preserving the visual fidelity of the released grayscale media. We further introduce Content-aware Color Distributional Distance (CaCDD), a ground-truth-free, content-aware measure of color plausibility derived from semantic color priors, used both as the optimization objective of SCNB and as an evaluation metric. Experiments on ImageNet show that our method remains effective under small perturbation budgets and common post-processing, supporting practical deployment in real-world content-sharing pipelines.

</details>


### [49] [Coarse-to-fine Framework for Generative MEF via Implicit Neural Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17611)
*Sangmin Han,Jinho Kim,Jinwoo Kim,Dongyoung Kim,Seon Joo Kim*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LIIFusion proposes a coarse-to-fine diffusion-based framework for multi-exposure fusion that achieves up to 3.5× speed-up over existing generative methods while maintaining quality, using low-resolution generative fusion with adaptive exposure correction in the coarse stage and a local implicit image function adapted for MEF in the fine stage.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing diffusion-based generative MEF methods are computationally expensive and struggle to preserve fine structures in saturated regions, limiting their practical applicability.

Method: A coarse-to-fine framework: (1) coarse stage performs low-resolution generative fusion with adaptive exposure correction to recover lost structures; (2) fine stage adapts a local implicit image function into a multi-exposure fusion function, querying arbitrary target coordinates conditioned on HR sources and coarse output.

Result: Achieves up to 3.5× speed-up over existing generative MEF methods while maintaining or improving structural fidelity and perceptual quality.

Conclusion: LIIFusion provides an effective pathway toward making generative MEF more practical for real-world applications by balancing fusion quality and efficiency.

Abstract: Multi-exposure fusion (MEF) expands the luminance range beyond what a single exposure can capture. Combining images taken at different exposure levels requires handling geometric differences while naturally merging their complementary brightness information. It often demands generative completion where details are missing. Diffusion-based generative methods address these challenges, however, they are computationally expensive and struggle to preserve fine structures in saturated regions. We propose LIIFusion, a coarse-to-fine framework that balances fusion quality and efficiency in generative MEF. The coarse stage performs low resolution generative fusion, enhanced by an adaptive exposure correction that recovers structure lost in saturated over-exposed areas. The fine stage adapts a local implicit image function into a multi-exposure fusion function: conditioned on the HR OE/UE sources and the coarse output, it queries arbitrary target coordinates and fuses source evidence regardless of the HR input resolution. LIIFusion achieves up to 3.5$\times$ speed-up over existing generative methods while maintaining or improving structural fidelity and perceptual quality. We believe this framework provides an effective pathway toward making generative MEF more practical in real-world applications.

</details>


### [50] [Brain-Aligned Multi-Stream Video Transformers with Sparse Self-Selection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17625)
*Amir Hosein Fadaei,Mahyar Maleki,Mohammad-Reza A. Dehaqani*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A neuro-inspired video transformer with dual pathways (what/where) and sparse token selection achieves brain-aligned efficiency, outperforming baselines in both accuracy and neural correlation (0.18, ~78% noise ceiling).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern video transformers ignore primate vision principles and are rarely evaluated against neural data, limiting biological interpretability.

Method: 1) Sparse winner-takes-all token selection replacing dense self-attention; 2) Split-and-fuse architecture with a high-resolution low-frame-rate "what" stream and a low-resolution high-frame-rate "where" stream.

Result: Pareto-optimal on Kinetics-400 & Something-Something V2; peak brain-model correlation of 0.18 (~78% noise ceiling), outperforming strong video transformer baselines.

Conclusion: Pathway specialization and sparse competition are useful inductive biases for efficient, brain-aligned video understanding.

Abstract: Modern video transformers typically ignore principles from primate vision and are rarely evaluated against neural data, limiting their biological interpretability. We introduce a sparse winner-takes-all token selection module that replaces dense self-attention to improve efficiency and approximate competitive routing observed in biological visual circuits. We further propose a neuro-inspired split-and-fuse video transformer which uses two complementary pathways: a high-resolution, low-frame-rate "what" stream and a low-resolution, high-frame-rate "where" stream, fused before classification. On Kinetics-400 and Something-Something V2, our best variant operates on the Pareto frontier of accuracy versus inference time among models of comparable scale and pretraining, and showing improved robustness to spatial perturbations. Using representational similarity analysis between model embeddings and time-resolved EEG recordings for the same video stimuli, our model attains a peak brain-model correlation of 0.18 (about 78% of the noise ceiling) and consistently outperforms strong video transformer baselines, suggesting that pathway specialization and sparse competition are useful inductive biases for efficient, brain-aligned video understanding.

</details>


### [51] [LFM: Leveraging Foundation Models for Source-Free Universal Domain Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17653)
*Jing Li,Pan Liu,Meng Zhao,Wanli Xue,Yanhong Yang,Xu Cheng,Fan Shi,Jianhua Zhang,Qinghua Hu,Shengyong Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: LFM leverages vision-language and large language models (foundation models) to improve source-free universal domain adaptation (SF-UniDA) by generating text-based pseudo-labels and refining them with a target model, outperforming existing methods across various label shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SF-UniDA methods rely on inefficient techniques (threshold tuning, clustering) and Foundation Models (FMs) with strong generalization/zero-shot capabilities remain underexplored for this task.

Method: Uses a VLM to compute similarities between target samples and text labels (including unknown-class labels generated by LLM prompting). Determines label shift type via coefficient of variation analysis. Identifies unknown samples using a binary Gaussian Mixture Model fitted to a similarity-based metric. Refines VLM pseudo-labels via a consensus strategy with the target model (initialized from pre-trained source model), integrating source and FM knowledge.

Result: Extensive experiments across all possible label shifts and multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of the proposed LFM framework.

Conclusion: LFM effectively integrates foundation models into SF-UniDA, achieving state-of-the-art performance without needing source data or inefficient tuning/clustering steps.

Abstract: Source-free universal domain adaptation (SF-UniDA) adapts a pre-trained source model to an unlabeled target domain under both covariate and label shifts, without access to source data. However, existing SF-UniDA methods rely on inefficient techniques such as threshold tuning and clustering. Foundation models (FMs), known for their generalization and zero-shot capabilities, remain underexplored in SF-UniDA. In this paper, we propose a framework that leverages foundation models (LFM) for SF-UniDA. We use a vision-language model (VLM) to compute similarities between target samples and text labels, including those for unknown classes generated by prompting a large language model. The label shift type is determined by analyzing the coefficient of variation of a similarity-based sample-level score. Unknown samples are identified using a binary Gaussian mixture model fitted to another similarity-based metric. Under a consensus strategy, the pseudo-labels generated by the VLM are refined by the target model initialized with the pre-trained source model, integrating knowledge from both the source domain and foundation models. Finally, these refined pseudo-labels are used to train the target model. Extensive experiments across all possible label shifts and multiple benchmarks demonstrate the effectiveness and superiority of our proposed LFM framework. Our code is available at https://github.com/iamjingli/LFM.

</details>


### [52] [Early Yield Prediction for Sugar Beet Fields using Satellite Data -- Learnings from Specialized Vision Transformers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17661)
*Philipp Vaeth,Bhumika Laxman Sadbhave,Denise Dejon,Gunther Schorcht,Magda Gregorova*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper combines domain knowledge with machine learning (using Vision Transformers on Sentinel-2 imagery) to forecast sugar beet harvest yields early, achieving effective low-yield field detection despite unconventional design choices like small patch sizes and using all spectral bands.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenge of effectively integrating domain knowledge into machine learning methods for agricultural monitoring, specifically for early sugar beet yield forecasting from publicly available satellite imagery.

Method: Uses Vision Transformers with very small patch sizes and all available Sentinel-2 spectral bands (uncommon design choices). Employs a modified training setup and ranking-based detection to identify underperforming fields early in the growth cycle.

Result: Small patch sizes and all spectral bands improved model performance. The approach successfully identified a large fraction of low-yield fields early in the growth cycle across different years.

Conclusion: Tight integration of domain knowledge and machine learning yields synergistic gains; unconventional design choices can outperform domain norms for specific tasks.

Abstract: Remote sensing has become an increasingly valuable tool for agricultural monitoring, particularly through the use of publicly available satellite imagery. However, effectively integrating domain knowledge into machine learning methods remains challenging. This study presents a real-world example of early sugar beet harvest yield forecasting from purely optical Sentinel-2 imagery, demonstrating how a tight integration of domain knowledge and machine learning can lead to synergistic gains. We empirically find that using very small vision transformer patch sizes and all available Sentinel-2 spectral bands improves our model despite being uncommon design choices in the domain. As a practical contribution, we were able to identify a large fraction of low-yield fields in a different year early on in the growth cycle through a modified training setup and a ranking-based detection of underperforming fields.

</details>


### [53] [Measuring Monosemanticity in Sparse Autoencoders via Latent Activation Coherence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17770)
*Katarzyna Filus,Sebastian Pokuciński*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: The paper proposes Tversky Monosemanticity Score (TMS), a label-free metric for evaluating monosemanticity of SAE features that doesn't require external embedding models, showing it is robust to encoder anisotropy while aligning with established indicators.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
