Motivation: Existing metrics for assessing monosemanticity of SAE features either require external concept labels or depend on pretrained embedding models, making them sensitive to the encoder's geometric properties (anisotropy). The authors aim to develop a label-free, encoder-independent metric.

Method: The authors introduce TMS, which operationalizes monosemanticity as activation-set coherence of binarized SAE latents. They evaluate it on SAEs trained on DINOv3, CLIP, and BLIP2 features, using TopK and BatchTopK regimes with varying sparsity and expansion factors.

Result: TMS is less affected by encoder anisotropy than embedding-based alternatives, aligns with established monosemanticity indicators, reveals distinct SAE training dynamics across base models, and provides stronger indication of probe-based concept deletion effectiveness under anisotropy.

Conclusion: TMS is a robust, label-free metric for monosemanticity that overcomes limitations of existing embedding-dependent approaches, particularly in anisotropic encoder settings.

Abstract: Within Explainable Artificial Intelligence, mechanistic interpretability uses Sparse Autoencoders (SAEs) to extract more interpretable features from neural representations. However, assessing their monosemanticity, and thus explanation quality, remains challenging. Existing metrics require external concept labels or depend on pretrained embedding models, making them sensitive to encoder's geometry. We introduce the Tversky Monosemanticity Score (TMS), a label-free metric that operationalizes monosemanticity as activation-set coherence of binarized SAE latents, and does not require external embedding encoders. We evaluate TMS on SAEs trained on features from pretrained vision and vision-language models (DINOv3, CLIP, BLIP2), two common SAE regimes (TopK, BatchTopK), multiple sparsity levels, and expansion factors. Our results show that TMS is less affected by encoder anisotropy than its embedding-based alternative, while remaining aligned with established monosemanticity indicators. TMS also reveals distinct SAE training dynamics across base models. Moreover, under encoder anisotropy, TMS provides a stronger indication of probe-based concept deletion effectiveness, while being competitive otherwise.

</details>


### [54] [DA-Fusion: Deformable Attention-Based RGB-D Fusion Transformer for Unseen Object Instance Segmentation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17754)
*Yesol Park,Hye-Jung Yoon,Juno Kim,Byoung-Tak Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: DA-Fusion proposes a deformable attention-based RGB-D fusion Transformer for unseen object instance segmentation in cluttered logistics environments, outperforming state-of-the-art methods on a new benchmark dataset OCBD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RGB methods over-segment due to texture reliance; depth methods under-segment due to geometric focus. The need for precise unseen object segmentation in cluttered logistics automation (bin-picking, shelf-picking) drives this work.

Method: DA-Fusion: a deformable attention-based RGB-D fusion Transformer that combines RGB and depth features for robust instance segmentation in cluttered environments.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse environments in extensive evaluations.

Conclusion: DA-Fusion is particularly suited for real-world logistics tasks requiring robust segmentation of unseen objects in cluttered, multi-layered scenes.

Abstract: In logistics automation, precise segmentation of unseen objects is crucial for efficient robotic manipulation in cluttered environments. Tasks such as bin-picking and shelf-picking require robust perception to handle occlusions, varying object shapes, and complex spatial arrangements. Traditional RGB-based methods tend to over-segment objects due to their reliance on texture, while depth-based methods often under-segment by focusing primarily on geometric features. To address these limitations, we propose DA-Fusion, a deformable attention-based RGB-D fusion Transformer designed for unseen object instance segmentation. DA-Fusion effectively combines the strengths of both RGB and depth data, enhancing segmentation accuracy in cluttered and multi-layered object environments. We also introduce the Object Clutter Bin Dataset (OCBD), a benchmark dataset specifically tailored for evaluating bin-picking scenarios in top-down views. Extensive evaluations demonstrate that DA-Fusion outperforms state-of-the-art methods across diverse environments, making it particularly suited for real-world logistics tasks.

</details>


### [55] [SciForma: Structure-Faithful Generation of Scientific Diagrams](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18091)
*Yuxuan Luo,Peng Zhang,Xinjie Zhang,Xun Guo,Zhouhui Lian,Yan Lu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SciForma introduces a framework for generating structurally faithful scientific methodology diagrams, using Multi-Dimensional Conjunctive Preference Optimization (M-DPO) and a structural inventory to ensure correctness across Components, Arrows, and Text, achieving state-of-the-art results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current open-source models fail to produce structurally faithful scientific diagrams because supervised fine-tuning (SFT) learns plausible layouts but cannot guarantee correctness, and scalar reward-based post-training obscures which structural dimension has failed.

Method: SciForma decomposes diagram quality into three axes (Component, Arrow, Text) guided by a structural inventory, curates SciFormaData-700K and SciFormaBench-2K, and proposes Multi-Dimensional Conjunctive Preference Optimization (M-DPO) to enforce joint correctness and adaptively route gradients to the most deficient dimension, plus iterative editing for residual errors.

Result: SciForma-9B exceeds all open-source baselines and GPT-Image-1.5 on both SciFormaBench-2K and AIBench, achieving near-proprietary-level structural fidelity.

Conclusion: Structural fidelity in scientific diagrams is inherently conjunctive; the proposed multi-dimensional optimization and editing approach successfully bridges the gap between open-source and proprietary models for this task.

Abstract: Structural fidelity is essential to scientific methodology diagrams. To communicate research logic, these diagrams must faithfully render components, directional relations, and textual annotations. Since a single error, such as a reversed arrow or an unreadable equation, can invalidate the entire figure, structural fidelity is inherently conjunctive: correctness on one axis cannot compensate for failure on another. Current open-source models fail to satisfy this criterion. Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) learns plausible layouts but cannot reliably ensure structural correctness, while scalar reward-based post-training obscures which structural dimension has failed. To address this, we introduce SciForma, a framework for the structure faithful generation of scientific methodology diagrams. Specifically, SciForma decomposes diagram quality into three structural axes: Component, Arrow, and Text, guided by a structural inventory. Built on this foundation, we curate SciFormaData-700K for structured training and SciFormaBench-2K for logic-verified evaluation. To close the gap left by SFT, we develop Multi-Dimensional Conjunctive Preference Optimization (M-DPO), which enforces simultaneous correctness across all axes and adaptively routes gradients to the most deficient dimension in post-training. The same structural inventory also enables iterative editing at inference time to correct residual errors. This combination allows SciForma-9B to exceed all open-source baselines and GPT-Image-1.5 on both SciFormaBench-2K and AIBench, bringing open scientific diagram generation close to proprietary-level structural fidelity. Our code and data will be available at: https://github.com/microsoft/SciForma.

</details>


### [56] [Certified Training for Convolutional Perturbations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18195)
*Benedikt Brückner,Alessio Lomuscio*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A novel Certified Training approach using efficient encoding of convolutional perturbations to train provably robust models against motion blur, achieving over 80% robust accuracy on CIFAR10 while maintaining standard accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision models are susceptible to perturbations like motion blur from shaking cameras, which can cause failures in critical applications. Existing methods like data augmentation and Adversarial Training lack formal safety guarantees, making it hard to identify hidden vulnerabilities.

Method: The paper introduces a Certified Training approach that leverages an efficient encoding of convolutional perturbations to train models with provable robustness guarantees against motion blur.

Result: The method significantly outperforms Adversarial Training, achieving over 80% robust accuracy against motion blur of reasonable intensity on CIFAR10 while maintaining comparable standard accuracy.

Conclusion: The proposed Certified Training approach provides formal safety guarantees against motion blur perturbations, outperforming existing empirical methods like Adversarial Training.

Abstract: Vision models have been found to be susceptible to perturbations such as motion blur induced at runtime by a shaking camera. This impedes their deployment in critical applications since phenomena such as slightly blurred vision might lead to failures, for example an object detector missing objects. While methods such as data augmentation or Adversarial Training can improve empirical robustness, they lack formal safety guarantees, making it difficult to identify and mitigate hidden vulnerabilities. We introduce a novel Certified Training approach that leverages an efficient encoding of convolutional perturbations to train provably robust models. Our method significantly outperforms Adversarial Training, achieving, for example, over 80% robust accuracy against motion blur of reasonable intensity on CIFAR10 while maintaining comparable standard accuracy.

</details>


### [57] [The Many Senses of Visual Similarity: A Text-Prompted Image Perceptual Metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18237)
*Sheng-Yu Wang,Yotam Nitzan,Aaron Hertzmann,Jun-Yan Zhu,Eli Shechtman,Alexei A. Efros,Richard Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper introduces TPIPS, a text-prompted perceptual similarity metric that captures multiple aspects of human visual similarity (e.g., shape vs. color), outperforms existing VLMs, and enables text-guided retrieval and generative model evaluation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing perceptual similarity metrics collapse multiple nuanced aspects of visual similarity (e.g., shape, color) into a single scalar, lacking the ability to condition on specific semantic aspects.

Method: The authors collect a large-scale dataset of human similarity judgments over image triplets annotated across multiple free-form semantic aspects, then fine-tune a VLM to produce the Text-Prompted Image Perceptual Similarity (TPIPS) metric that captures similarity conditioned on text prompts.

Result: TPIPS aligns more closely with human perception than frontier VLMs, generalizes beyond training distribution, and enables new capabilities in text-guided retrieval, compositional search, and fine-grained generative model evaluation.

Conclusion: Text-prompted perceptual similarity (TPIPS) effectively bridges the gap between scalar similarity metrics and humans' context-dependent, multi-aspect similarity judgments, with strong generalization and practical utility.

Abstract: Human visual similarity judgments are context-dependent. For example, two images may be similar in shape but distinct in color. Existing perceptual similarity metrics, however, collapse these nuances into a single scalar value, offering no mechanism to condition on specific aspects. To bridge this gap, we introduce a large-scale dataset of human similarity judgments over image triplets, where each triplet is annotated across multiple, free-form semantic aspects of similarity. Benchmarking a broad range of frontier vision-language models (VLMs) reveals a considerable performance gap compared to human annotators' consensus. Leveraging our data, we fine-tune a VLM to produce our Text-Prompted Image Perceptual Similarity (TPIPS) metric, capturing multiple senses of visual similarity depending on the specified text prompt. We demonstrate that TPIPS aligns more closely with human perception and generalizes reliably beyond the training distribution. Finally, we show that TPIPS unlocks new capabilities in text-guided retrieval, compositional search, and the fine-grained evaluation of generative models. Our code, data, and trained models are at https://peterwang512.github.io/TPIPS

</details>


### [58] [CDIS: Cross-Dimensional Class-Agnostic 3D Instance Segmentation via 2D Mask Tracking and 3D-2D Projection Merging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17778)
*Juno Kim,Hye-Jung Yoon,Yesol Park,Byoung-Tak Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CDIS is a zero-shot framework for class-agnostic 3D instance segmentation that uses a feedback loop between 2D tracking and 3D superpoints to produce globally consistent 3D instance labels without 3D-specific training.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods project 2D masks into 3D and merge them, which breaks object identities over time and yields fragmented 3D instances.

Method: CDIS explicitly tracks 2D instance masks across frames, associates them with 3D superpoints, and creates a cross-dimensional feedback loop between 2D tracks and 3D regions.

Result: CDIS achieves higher accuracy and consistency than state-of-the-art zero-shot methods on benchmark datasets, and is efficient and scalable to diverse real-world environments.

Conclusion: Cross-dimensional reasoning (2D tracking + 3D superpoints) effectively produces globally consistent 3D instance segmentation without any 3D-specific training.

Abstract: Class-agnostic 3D instance segmentation is critical for robotic systems operating in unknown environments, enabling perception of previously unseen objects for reliable manipulation and navigation. Existing approaches typically project per-frame 2D instance masks into 3D and merge them, which often breaks object identities across time and yields fragmented 3D instances. We introduce Cross-Dimensional Class-Agnostic 3D Instance Segmentation (CDIS), a zero-shot framework that explicitly tracks 2D instance masks across frames and associates them with 3D superpoints, creating a feedback loop between 2D and 3D. This cross-dimensional reasoning links temporally stable 2D tracks with spatially coherent 3D regions, producing globally consistent 3D instance labels without any 3D-specific training. Experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that CDIS achieves higher accuracy and consistency than state-of-the-art zero-shot methods, while remaining efficient and scalable to diverse real-world environments.

</details>


### [59] [BrainNext: A General-Purpose Self-Supervised Foundation Model for Brain MRI Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17782)
*Moona Mazher,Abdul Qayyum,Steven A. Niederer,Daniel C. Alexander*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: BrainNext is a self-supervised foundation model for volumetric brain MRI analysis, using MAE pretraining on 60,551 scans and a 3D Bi-Directional xLSTM-UNet architecture, achieving second place overall and first in meningioma segmentation on the FOMO 2025 challenge.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing foundation models for neuroimaging are limited by task-specific training, slice-based learning, or small pretraining datasets, restricting generalizability across diverse brain MRI applications.

Method: Combines masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining with a native three-dimensional Bi-Directional xLSTM-UNet architecture, trained on 60,551 unlabeled brain MRI exams across multiple modalities, followed by lightweight task-specific fine-tuning.

Result: Achieved second place overall and first in meningioma segmentation on the FOMO 2025 challenge leaderboard, demonstrating strong transferability across classification, segmentation, and brain-age estimation tasks.

Conclusion: Large-scale self-supervised pretraining can learn robust and transferable volumetric representations, and BrainNext serves as a scalable foundation model for diverse brain MRI applications.

Abstract: Foundation models pretrained using self-supervised learning have transformed computer vision by learning transferable representations from large-scale unlabeled data. However, existing foundation models for neuroimaging remain limited by task-specific training, slice-based learning strategies, or relatively small pretraining datasets, restricting their generalizability across diverse brain MRI applications. In this work, we present BrainNext, a general-purpose self-supervised foundation model for volumetric brain MRI analysis. BrainNext combines masked autoencoder (MAE) pretraining with a native three-dimensional Bi-Directional xLSTM-UNet architecture to learn rich anatomical representations from 60,551 unlabeled brain MRI examinations spanning multiple MRI modalities. The pretrained model is subsequently adapted to downstream tasks through lightweight task-specific fine-tuning. We evaluate BrainNext on the Foundation Models for Medical Imaging (FOMO) 2025 Method Track, encompassing classification, segmentation, and brain-age estimation, where it achieved second place overall and ranked first in the meningioma segmentation task on the official FOMO 2025 challenge leaderboard, demonstrating strong transferability across heterogeneous neuroimaging tasks. These results highlight the potential of large-scale self-supervised pretraining to learn robust and transferable volumetric representations, establishing BrainNext as a scalable foundation model for diverse brain MRI applications.

</details>


### [60] [Medical Imaging Fusing Vision Transformer: Laryngeal Cancer Screening with Explanation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17789)
*Haiyang Wang,Luca Mainardi*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: This paper proposes an AI system using transformers and attention mechanisms to analyze NBI endoscopy images for laryngeal cancer screening, achieving 82.33% accuracy and 82.72% F1 score, with explainability provided by MedSAM segmentation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Early laryngeal cancer screening is critical but NBI endoscopy requires skilled clinicians, is time-consuming, and suffers from interobserver variability, motivating the need for AI-assisted diagnosis.

Method: Applied transformer and attention mechanism to analyze NBI images for benign vs. malignant lesion classification, integrated with MedSAM for explainable segmentation highlighting pathological regions.

Result: Achieved F1 score of 82.72% and accuracy of 82.33% in classifying laryngeal lesions, with explainable visualization for clinicians.

Conclusion: The fusion of classification and segmentation offers a translatable AI approach for laryngeal cancer screening, balancing performance with explainability.

Abstract: Early and timely screening of laryngeal cancer is crucial for improving clinical outcomes. In recent years, NBI endoscopy has become a standard diagnostic tool for the detection of laryngeal lesions. However, its effective use requires well-trained clinicians and the procedure is time-consuming and subject to interobserver variability. In this context, the application of artificial intelligence (AI) offers a promising solution to support clinical decision-making. In this work, we proposed applying transformer and attention mechanism for analyzing the narrow band imaging and distinguish benign and malignant lesions. Results show it has good classification performance with F1 (82.72%), accuracy(82.33%). In addition, the result of laryngeal cancer screening is explainable for clinicians. The explainability is utilizing the state of art segmentation method (MedSAM) to provide the useful pathological information area for clinicians. The proposed methodology fusing classification and segmentation provides a translating on laryngeal cancer screening.

</details>


### [61] [ReViV: Reconstructing the Viewer and the View in 4D from Monocular Egocentric Video](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17790)
*Xiaozhong Lyu,Gen Li,Zhiyin Qian,Xucong Zhang,Marc Pollefeys,Siyu Tang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: ReViV is the first unified framework for holistic egocentric 4D reconstruction from a single monocular RGB video, jointly estimating viewer dynamics (full-body, hand, gaze motion) and view dynamics (camera trajectory, depth) using a Masked Generative Egocentric Transformer, achieving SOTA accuracy and fast inference across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing egocentric 4D reconstruction approaches rely on auxiliary inputs (e.g., pre-computed camera trajectories), treat viewer and view modeling separately despite strong interdependencies, and suffer from slow inference. The authors aim to build a unified, efficient, feed-forward model that jointly reconstructs all aspects of egocentric 4D from a single RGB video.

Method: ReViV formulates the task as learning the joint probability distribution over multimodal signals (RGB video, camera trajectory, gaze, full-body motion, hand motion, depth). It uses a Masked Generative Egocentric Transformer with a single feed-forward architecture to simultaneously reconstruct temporally consistent 4D representations of both the viewer and the view.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art accuracy across holistic ego-body, hand, and gaze reconstruction, and camera tracking on HoloAssist, HOT3D, ARCTIC, Aria Digital Twin, and TACO benchmarks. Also maintains highly competitive depth estimation without heavy task-specific priors, with fast inference speed.

Conclusion: ReViV demonstrates that a unified, multimodal generative transformer can efficiently solve holistic egocentric 4D reconstruction without task-specific priors or auxiliary inputs. The open-sourced code and models enable further research in this direction.

Abstract: Egocentric devices, such as wearable front-facing cameras, provide a unique perspective for capturing the continuous interaction between a human viewer and the surrounding environment. A holistic and efficient multimodal model capable of reconstructing this 4D representation is therefore highly desirable. However, existing approaches often rely on auxiliary inputs such as pre-computed camera trajectories, treat scene perception and human ego-motion modeling as separate problems despite their strong interdependency, and suffer from slow inference time. To address these limitations, we present ReViV, the first unified framework for holistic egocentric 4D reconstruction that extracts both viewer and view dynamics from a single monocular RGB video. We formulate the task as learning the full joint probability distribution over multimodal signals, including RGB video, camera trajectory, gaze direction, full-body motion, hand motion, and depth. Powered by a Masked Generative Egocentric Transformer, ReViV operates within a single feed-forward architecture to simultaneously reconstruct the temporally consistent 4D reconstruction across the viewer and the view with fast inference speed. Extensive experiments on diverse benchmarks, including HoloAssist, HOT3D, ARCTIC, Aria Digital Twin, and TACO, demonstrate that ReViV achieves state-of-the-art accuracy and efficiency across holistic ego-body, hand, and gaze reconstruction, camera tracking, while maintaining highly competitive egocentric depth estimation without relying on heavy task-specific priors. Code and models are fully open-sourced: https://reviv4d.github.io/.

</details>


### [62] [Vis2Reg: Visibility-Aware Landmark-Free Geometric 3D--2D Registration for Liver Laparoscopy](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17810)
*Jiaming Feng,Xukun Zhang,Shahid Farid,Sharib Ali*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Vis2Reg is a visibility-aware self-supervised framework for 3D-2D liver registration in AR-guided surgery, achieving 92.6% Dice and 1.43mm Chamfer Distance at 111ms per frame.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Accurate 3D-2D liver registration is critical for AR-guided laparoscopic surgery but remains challenging due to severe occlusion, limited visibility, and lack of 3D ground-truth supervision.

Method: Vis2Reg uses a visibility-aware self-supervision that derives visible-domain 3D signals from intraoperative masks via differentiable point rasterization and mask-guided back-projection, combined with a geometric rigid initialization module and an implicit neural deformation field.

Result: 92.6% Dice score, 1.43 mm Chamfer Distance on real intraoperative datasets, 111 ms per-frame inference time.

Conclusion: Vis2Reg demonstrates both accuracy and practical efficiency for AR-guided laparoscopic surgery, with robust self-supervised learning under severe occlusion.

Abstract: Accurate 3D--2D liver registration, which aligns preoperative 3D models to partial, view-dependent intraoperative surface observations, is critical for AR-guided laparoscopic surgery but remains challenging due to severe occlusion, limited visibility, and the lack of 3D ground-truth supervision. Existing landmark-free approaches perform partial-to-complete geometric alignment, yet robust self-supervision under extreme partial visibility remains difficult. We propose Vis2Reg, a visibility-aware registration framework that explicitly constrains deformation using mask-consistent visible regions. We introduce a visibility-aware self-supervision that derives a visible-domain 3D supervision signal from intraoperative masks, enabled by differentiable point rasterization and mask-guided back-projection. This formulation improves robustness under severe occlusion while maintaining fully self-supervised learning. Vis2Reg combines a robust geometric rigid initialization module with an implicit neural deformation field for stable alignment. Vis2Reg achieves a Dice score of 92.6\% and a Chamfer Distance of 1.43 mm on real intraoperative datasets, with 111 ms per-frame inference time, demonstrating both accuracy and practical efficiency.

</details>


### [63] [Measuring and Improving Complex-Atomic Answer Consistency in Endoscopic VQA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17834)
*Yuhao Liu,Cheng Zhao,Guanghui Yue*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: EndoCA is a benchmark for evaluating complex-atomic answer consistency in endoscopic VQA, and ASR is a training-free mechanism to improve that consistency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Complex endoscopic VQA questions combine multiple answer components, yet models may score well on complex answers while failing on the underlying atomic questions. There is no existing benchmark to evaluate this inconsistency.

Method: (1) EndoCA benchmark with two suites: EndoCA-Core (common patterns) and EndoCA-Diagnostic (controlled complexity). (2) ASR mechanism: uses model-generated atomic answers as contextual premises for revision (ASR-Revise) and selective answering (ASR-Selective).

Result: VLMs show high complex-answer accuracy but substantially lower atomic-answer accuracy and complex-atomic consistency. ASR-Revise improves paired correctness with modest accuracy changes; ASR-Selective improves accuracy on answered cases via abstention.

Conclusion: EndoCA provides a consistency-aware benchmark, and ASR offers a training-free mechanism for answer reconciliation and selective answering in endoscopic VQA.

Abstract: Endoscopic visual question answering (VQA) increasingly asks complex questions that combine several endoscopic answer components rather than isolated factual queries. Such complex answers may be scored as correct even when the same model fails on associated atomic questions. We introduce EndoCA, a paired complex-atomic answer consistency benchmark for evaluating whether complex answers remain consistent with same-image atomic answers. EndoCA contains two suites: EndoCA-Core evaluates compact question-complexity patterns commonly seen in practical endoscopic VQA, and EndoCA-Diagnostic supports controlled analysis across increasing question complexity. We evaluate 11 VLMs spanning open, medical, endoscopy-adapted, and closed-source models on EndoCA. Some VLMs achieve high complex-answer accuracy, yet their atomic-answer accuracy and complex-atomic answer consistency remain substantially lower. To reduce this complex-atomic inconsistency, we introduce Atomic-Support Reconciliation (ASR), a training-free mechanism that uses model-generated atomic answers as contextual premises for answer revision and consistency-guided selective answering. On four selected publicly available models, ASR-Revise improves paired complex-atomic correctness with modest changes in complex-answer accuracy, while ASR-Selective improves accuracy on answered cases by allowing the model to abstain from less reliable cases. Together, EndoCA and ASR provide a consistency-aware benchmark and a training-free mechanism for answer reconciliation and selective answering in endoscopic VQA.

</details>


### [64] [CaT-GS: Efficient 3DGS Rendering for Large Scale Scenes via Inter-frame Caching and Tile Scheduling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17842)
*Tingjia Zhang,Bo Chen,Shengzhong Liu,Fan Wu,Guihai Chen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: CaT-GS accelerates large-scale 3D Gaussian Splatting rendering by up to 10x through speculative multi-frame preprocessing, inter-frame caching, and tile load balancing.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: 3DGS suffers significant performance degradation in large-scale scenes due to tile-based rasterization bottlenecks, and existing optimizations either require costly retraining or address only narrow pipeline aspects.

Method: CaT-GS proposes three key innovations: (1) speculative multi-frame preprocessing to eliminate redundant inter-frame computations, (2) inter-frame caching to remove viewpoint-based occlusion redundancy, and (3) refactored rasterization kernels to mitigate tile-level load imbalance.

Result: Achieves up to 10x speedup over original 3DGS and up to 70% improvement over prior state-of-the-art methods in large-scale scene rendering.

Conclusion: CaT-GS sets a new benchmark for high-fidelity, real-time large-scale scene rendering by systematically addressing three critical inefficiencies in the 3DGS pipeline.

Abstract: Recent breakthroughs in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) have advanced neural rendering with high fidelity and speed. However, its performance degrades significantly in large-scale scenes due to the computational burden of tile-based rasterization. Existing optimization efforts either require costly scene re-training or focus on narrow aspects of the pipeline, overlooking critical inefficiencies in real-world deployments. Through a comprehensive analysis, we identify three primary sources of redundancy and low GPU utilization: redundant inter-frame pre-processing, viewpoint-based occlusion redundancy, and severe tile-level load imbalance. To address these issues, we propose CaT-GS, a novel and efficient 3DGS rendering pipeline. CaT-GS introduces a speculative multi-frame preprocessing method to eliminate redundant computations across consecutive frames, and an inter-frame caching mechanism to eliminate viewpoint redundant rendering stages. Furthermore, it refactors rasterization tasks with a dedicated kernel to mitigate tile load imbalance, significantly boosting GPU utilization. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CaT-GS achieves a speedup of up to 10 times over the original 3DGS and up to 70% over previous state-of-the-art methods, establishing a new benchmark for high-fidelity, real-time rendering of large-scale scenes.

</details>


### [65] [HAS: Highlight-guided Attention Steering for Multimodal LLM Video Summarization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17994)
*Rui Chu,Yingjie Lao*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: HAS (Highlight-guided Attention Steering) improves video summarization by computing a continuous global highlight distribution across frames and using it as an attention steering vector for MLLMs, enabling better focus on important frames while retaining less important ones, outperforming existing methods on benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing video summarization methods rely on discrete selected frames, which reduces understanding coherence, loses important information, and wastes MLLMs' capacity. The authors argue for a global perspective on frame importance rather than treating frames independently.

Method: HAS has two parts: (1) computing a continuous frame-level highlight distribution for the entire video globally; (2) applying this distribution as an attention steering vector to guide MLLM inference, putting more attention on highlighted frames while reducing (not removing) attention on less highlighted frames.

Result: HAS demonstrated convincing performance across various benchmarks for video summarization.

Conclusion: Continuous highlight-guided attention steering is more effective than discrete frame selection for video summarization with MLLMs, preserving coherence and information completeness.

Abstract: Video understanding has become more and more important with the growth of Artificial Intelligence (AI) for video generation. Recently, Multimodal Large Language Model(M-LLM) has shown its capability in video understanding. Video summarization, a specific domain of video understanding, has proven its importance for efficient navigation and retrieval. Both video understanding and video summarization require a good selection of key frames in a video. Current video summarization methods heavily focus on the selected key frames and correlated segment captions. However, existing approaches overlook the perspective of treating the importance of the frames globally. We argue that using discrete selected frames for summarization will not only reduce the understanding coherence, but also lost important information in the video, as well as wasting the original capacity of the MLLMs. In this paper, we propose HAS, a Highlight-guided Attention Steering method for video summarization. We consider a challenging but practical setting where the video given to MLLMs for summarize should be continuous but with highlight guidance. HAS mainly consists of two parts: The first part is to find a continuous frame-level highlight distribution for the video globally. The second part is to apply the highlight distribution as an attention steering vector for the MLLM, targeting a better understanding of the video, and thus during the model inference time, putting more attention on the highlighted frames, while avoiding lost entire information on less highlighted frames through putting less attention instead of forgetting them. We evaluated HAS on a variety of benchmarks, and it has shown convincing performance in video summarization.

</details>


### [66] [Anticipate Before Acting: Future-State-Conditioned Vision-Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18042)
*Lingfeng Zhang,Zhanguang Zhang,Liheng Ma,Tongtong Cao,Yingxue Zhang*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: FSC-VLN improves vision-language navigation by aligning hidden states with future visual embeddings during training, without needing future images at inference, boosting performance especially on long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current behavior cloning for VLN only supervises the next action and does not explicitly train policy states to be predictive of future visual outcomes, limiting decision quality.

Method: Future-State-Conditioned VLN (FSC-VLN) adds a future-query token and aligns its hidden state to a frozen visual embedding Δ steps ahead via a training-only target branch that is removed after deployment.

Result: On R2R val-unseen, FSC-VLN improves SR/OSR/SPL over a StreamVLN-style baseline under two training-data regimes, with larger gains on long-horizon episodes.

Conclusion: Future visual information, even when compressed and used only as training supervision, provides rich actionable cues for VLN, and the dual-query design (separating future and action queries) is beneficial.

Abstract: End-to-end vision-language navigation (VLN) with causal vision-language models can map instructions and egocentric observations directly to actions, but standard behavior cloning supervises only the next action and does not explicitly train the policy state to be predictive of future visual outcomes. We first ask a diagnostic question: if the policy is given an expert-trajectory future image as privileged input at training and testing time, is that additional visual evidence useful for choosing the current action? (These expert-trajectory future images are unavailable at test time in real deployment, so we use this setting only as a privileged-input diagnostic.) The answer is yes; this sanity check shows that future observations can provide rich, actionable cues.
  We then ask a deployable question: without accessing future images at inference, can we still benefit from future information by using a compressed future visual latent only as training supervision? We propose Future-State-Conditioned VLN (FSC-VLN), which adds a future-query token and aligns its hidden state to a frozen visual embedding $Δ$ steps ahead via a training-only target branch that is removed after training. On R2R val-unseen, FSC-VLN improves SR/OSR/SPL over a StreamVLN-style baseline under two training-data regimes, with larger gains on long-horizon episodes; ablations further support the dual-query design (separating future and action queries).

</details>


### [67] [Sparse Evidence Can Suffice: Agentic Evidence Seeking for Multimodal Video Misinformation Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18080)
*Haochen Zhao,Yongxiu Xu,Xinkui Lin,Dong Xie,Jiarui Lu,Yuqi Qian,Yubin Wang,Hongbo Xu,Gaopeng Gou*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: SIEVE is a sparse evidence verification framework for multimodal video misinformation detection. It uses an evidence-seeking agent to extract only decisive clues, then a verifier judges veracity. Trained with reinforcement learning, it outperforms baselines with compact evidence and improves transparency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multimodal video misinformation detection treats the entire video holistically, but real misinformation has sparse, compositional evidence—only a few clues matter. This approach introduces redundancy, obscures decisive evidence, and lacks transparency.

Method: SIEVE decouples evidence acquisition from verification: an evidence-seeking agent actively explores multimodal content to construct a compact evidence package, which a verifier then uses. The agent is trained with supervised evidence-seeking trajectories and evidence-aware reinforcement learning to encourage informative evidence acquisition and discourage unnecessary interactions.

Result: SIEVE consistently outperforms baselines on multiple video misinformation benchmarks, supports reliable verification using compact evidence packages, and provides inspectable evidence trails.

Conclusion: Sparse, agent-driven evidence extraction improves both accuracy and transparency in multimodal video misinformation detection, offering a promising alternative to exhaustive holistic reasoning.

Abstract: Multimodal video misinformation detection is commonly formulated as a holistic video-understanding task, where the entire video and its associated content are processed and judged in a single pass. However, real-world misinformation often exhibits a sparse and compositional evidence structure: a reliable decision may depend on only a few coupled clues, while most video content contributes limited additional information. Exhaustive multimodal reasoning may therefore introduce substantial redundancy and obscure decisive evidence. This motivates decoupling evidence acquisition from verification: first identifying sparse, decision-relevant clues and then judging veracity based on the acquired evidence. Accordingly, we propose SIEVE, a framework for Sparse Interactive Evidence Verification via Extraction in multimodal video misinformation detection. An evidence-seeking agent actively explores the available multimodal evidence and constructs a compact evidence package, which is then used by a verifier to determine veracity. The agent is trained with supervised evidence-seeking trajectories and an evidence-aware reinforcement learning objective that promotes informative evidence acquisition while discouraging unnecessary or invalid interactions. Experiments on multiple video misinformation benchmarks show that SIEVE consistently outperforms the evaluated baselines and supports reliable verification using compact evidence packages. Moreover, the resulting acquisition process provides an explicit and inspectable evidence trail, improving the transparency and groundedness of multimodal misinformation detection.

</details>


### [68] [O-VAD: Industrial Video Anomaly Detection through Object-Centric Tracking and Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18142)
*Mei Yuan,Qi Long,Qifeng Wu,Zhenyang Li,Yizhou Zhao,Lei Wang,Yang Liu,Min Xu*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A training-free agentic framework for industrial video anomaly detection that tracks object state evolution over time to identify anomalies without domain-specific knowledge or retraining, outperforming existing methods on IVAD datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing VLM-based anomaly reasoning methods perform poorly in industrial settings due to complex object transformations, strict physics, and procedural constraints. Prior approaches require retraining on normal clips or injecting domain knowledge, limiting applicability.

Method: A training-free agentic framework that tracks spatial-temporal dynamics and underlying transformations of detected objects over time, then reasons over object-wise temporal state trajectories to identify abnormal objects in grounded frames — mimicking human inspector behavior.

Result: Outperforms frontier VLMs, agentic frameworks, and traditional VAD methods fine-tuned on respective datasets across three IVAD datasets, while providing interpretable reports.

Conclusion: The proposed agentic approach effectively addresses industrial anomaly detection challenges by focusing on object state evolution, eliminating the need for domain-specific knowledge or retraining while achieving superior performance and interpretability.

Abstract: Industrial Video Anomaly Detection (IVAD) aims to identify anomalous objects and events in an industrial process, which is crucial for modern manufacturing and quality control systems. Existing VLM-based anomaly reasoning methods are capable of detecting open-ended anomalies in general domains. However, their performance declines in industrial settings characterized by intricate object transformations, strict physics, and procedural constraints. To tackle the complexity of such interaction-intensive detection, we introduce a training-free agentic framework for anomaly detection free of domain-specific knowledge, emphasizing object state evolution like humans inspectors. It is designed to track spatial-temporal dynamics and underlying transformations of detected objects over time, and then reason over the object-wise temporal state trajectories to identify abnormal objects in grounded frames. Our method overcomes limitations of prior approaches that rely on retraining on normal clips or injecting domain knowledge as context for test-time inference. Extensive experiments on three IVAD datasets demonstrate that our method outperforms frontier VLMs, agentic frameworks, and traditional VAD methods fine-tuned on the respective datasets, while providing interpretable reports over anomaly processes and types.

</details>


### [69] [GigaPath-Flash and GigaTIME-Flash: Efficient Pathology Foundation Models for Whole-Slide and Tumor Microenvironment Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18218)
*Naoto Usuyama,Jeya Maria Jose Valanarasu,Sicong Yao,Hanwen Xu,Jaspreet Bagga,Guanghui Qin,Robert E. Kramer,Cliff Wong,Soohee Lee,Hao Qiu,Theodore Zhengde Zhao,Racheli Ben Shimol,Angela Crabtree,Kevin Matlock,Eduardo Alejandro Lozano Garcia,Naiteek Sangani,Alberto Santamaria-Pang,Jason Entenmann,Alexandra Q. Bartlett,Bill J. Wright,Bernard A. Fox,Brian Piening,Sheng Zhang,Sheng Wang,Tristan Naumann,Carlo Bifulco,Hoifung Poon*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: Introduces GigaPath-Flash and GigaTIME-Flash, efficient open-weight pathology foundation models distilled from billion-parameter GigaPath, achieving ~97% slide-level performance with 50x less compute, and superior spatial proteomics prediction with 6x speedup and 8x less memory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing pathology foundation models are computationally expensive, operate only at image-tile level, and use restrictive licenses, limiting large-scale slide-level clinical and research use.

Method: GigaPath-Flash uses a 22M-parameter ViT-S tile encoder distilled from billion-parameter GigaPath (ViT-g) teacher, combined with a 21M-parameter LongNet slide encoder. GigaTIME-Flash extends this backbone for tumor immune microenvironment prediction from H&E images.

Result: GigaPath-Flash retains 97% of GigaPath's slide-level performance with 50x less compute. GigaTIME-Flash surpasses original CNN-based GigaTIME in prediction quality, running 6x faster with 8x less GPU memory.

Conclusion: These open-weight, Apache-2.0-licensed models provide accessible building blocks for computational pathology, immuno-oncology, and precision health at scale.

Abstract: Foundation models have emerged as a driving force in computational pathology, with the potential to transform cancer diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment selection by learning transferable representations from large-scale histopathology data. A growing landscape of pathology foundation models now spans diverse data sources, architectures, and downstream applications. However, most pretrained models operate only at the image-tile level, use restrictive licenses, and remain computationally expensive, limiting large-scale slide-level clinical and research use.
  Here, we introduce GigaPath-Flash and GigaTIME-Flash, efficient models for whole-slide pathology AI and spatial proteomics prediction. GigaPath-Flash combines a 22M-parameter ViT-S tile encoder with a 21M-parameter LongNet slide encoder, both pretrained on large-scale real-world histopathology data. Its compact tile encoder is distilled from the billion-parameter GigaPath (ViT-g) teacher and shared by both models. GigaPath-Flash retains 97% of GigaPath's average slide-level performance with 50x less compute. GigaTIME-Flash extends this backbone to predict the tumor immune microenvironment directly from routine H&E images. It surpasses the original CNN-based GigaTIME in prediction quality while running 6x faster and using 8x less GPU memory.
  Together with GigaPath and GigaTIME, these models form an open-weight, Apache-2.0-licensed family pretrained on large-scale real-world clinical data. By releasing all models and weights, we provide accessible building blocks for computational pathology, immuno-oncology, and precision health.

</details>


### [70] [Simple Domain Generalization for Strong Pixel-Level Image Tampering Detection in Modern VLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18230)
*Yi Tang,Xinyi Shang,Jiacheng Cui,Sondos Mahmoud Bsharat,Jiacheng Liu,Xiaohan Zhao,Tran Dinh Tien,Ahmed Elhagry,Salwa K. Al Khatib,Tianjun Yao,Yonina C. Eldar,Jing-Hao Xue,Hao Li,Salman Khan,Zhiqiang Shen*

Main category: cs.CV

TL;DR: A domain-generalized training framework for pixel-level image tampering detection in modern VLMs, using balanced minibatch sampling and late-injection strategy, achieving 26%+ improvement over prior SOTA PIXAR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Modern VLMs produce highly realistic manipulated images, making pixel-level tampering detection critical yet challenging under cross-model and out-of-distribution shifts. Prior methods lack robustness across diverse VLM-generated manipulation distributions.

Method: (1) Balanced minibatch sampling: strategically sample tampered and real images per minibatch to prevent biased optimization. (2) Late-injection strategy: train detector on large-scale base data first, then fine-tune on small supporting data from new VLM distributions to improve OOD adaptability.

Result: Outperforms PIXAR by 26.1% (gIoU) and 26.8% (cIoU) relative improvement across OOD VLMs: GPT-Images-2.0, Gemini-3.1, FLUX.2, and Seedream 4.5.

Conclusion: A simple yet effective domain-generalized framework significantly improves pixel-level tampering localization and OOD robustness across modern VLMs, setting a new state-of-the-art.

Abstract: Modern vision-language models (VLMs) have significantly improved image generation and editing capabilities, making pixel-level image tampering detection increasingly important yet challenging under cross-model and out-of-distribution shifts. This work studies domain generalization for pixel-level image tampering detection in modern VLMs like ChatGPT, Gemini, Qwen-Image, etc., aiming to learn tampering localization models that remain robust across diverse VLM-generated manipulation distributions. We propose a simple yet effective domain-generalized training framework built on two practical strategies. First, we introduce a balanced minibatch sampling scheme that strategically samples tampered and real images in each minibatch, preventing biased optimization toward either manipulated artifacts or clean-image priors and avoiding training collapse, ensuring that each optimization step receives proper sampled gradient signals. Second, we adopt a simple late-injection strategy, where the detector is first trained on large-scale base data until stable convergence, and then exposed to a small amount of newly selected supporting data from emerging VLM distributions, improving adaptability without overfitting to limited new domains. Together, these components provide a simple yet strong recipe for improving pixel-level tampering localization and OOD robustness across modern VLMs. Despite the conceptual simplicity, our framework outperforms the prior state-of-the-art PIXAR by a large margin of 26.1% and 26.8% relative improvement in average gIoU and cIoU, respectively, across OOD VLMs of GPT-Images-2.0, Gemini-3.1, FLUX.2, and Seedream 4.5. Our code is available at https://github.com/VILA-Lab/PIXAR-DG

</details>


<div id='cs.AI'></div>

# cs.AI [[Back]](#toc)

### [71] [Rater State Bias in RLHF Preference Data: An Audit Framework](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16195)
*Elena Kopteva,Vitaliy Hlynianyi-Zhuk*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper identifies "rater state shift" as a structured confound in RLHF: annotators' emotional states (e.g., stress) during labeling can bias preference data, and this bias can propagate through reward modeling and policy optimization. The authors define the problem, propose falsifiable predictions, and present an audit framework to detect this bias.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RLHF is widely used to align language models, but existing work focuses on label noise or disagreement. The authors argue that rater emotional state (e.g., stress, distress) can systematically shift preferences over time, creating a structured confound that is distinct from random noise and can survive aggregation. This source of bias is plausible, testable, and currently underexplored.

Method: The paper proposes a theoretical framework defining rater state shift, rater state confound, and correlated rater state bias. It introduces "survival level emotional authenticity" as a measurable response pattern based on lexical, pragmatic, discourse, and safety features. It derives five falsifiable predictions with effect size thresholds and outlines an audit protocol and pilot study plan using publicly available instruction-tuned models.

Result: The authors do not present empirical results; this is a hypothesis paper. They provide a formal framework for detecting rater state bias, including specific predictions and an audit design that can be applied without inferring training histories of deployed models.

Conclusion: Rater state shift is a plausible and testable structured confound in RLHF that warrants auditing. The proposed framework enables systematic investigation of this bias source, with the goal of improving the robustness and fairness of RLHF-based alignment.

Abstract: We identify a structured confound in Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). Pairwise preference labels are intended to reflect the compared outputs, but they may also reflect the rater's state during annotation. Under sustained stressful or distressing conditions, raters' preferences may shift over time. As a result, preference data can encode rater state alongside judgments about response quality. These shifts differ from ordinary disagreement or random label noise. They are state dependent, can be shared across annotators working under similar conditions, and can propagate through reward modeling and policy optimization. We therefore propose rater state shift as a plausible and testable source of structured bias in RLHF preference data. This paper develops a hypothesis and an audit framework for studying this source of bias. We define rater state shift, rater state confound, and correlated rater state bias. We also define survival level emotional authenticity as a measurable response pattern using lexical, pragmatic, discourse, and safety related features. We analyze how correlated rater state bias can survive aggregation and enter learned reward signals. We derive five falsifiable predictions and effect size thresholds for an initial audit. Finally, we present an audit protocol and pilot study plan that can be applied to publicly available instruction tuned models. We do not infer the training history of any specific deployed model. Our goal is to isolate a plausible and testable source of structured bias in RLHF preference data.

</details>


### [72] [Design and Validation of a Lightweight 1D CNN for Affective Touch Classification in Soft Plush Companions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16196)
*Aleksandrs Vališevskis,Aleksandrs Okss,Inese Tīģere,Aleksejs Kataševs,Dina Bethere,Anete Hofmane,Airisa Šteinberga,Undīne Gavriļenko,Santa Meļķe,Lucie Matoušková*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper presents an open-source MATLAB framework and a public dataset of 1326 labeled touch gesture sequences for affective touch recognition. Through exploration of 468 CNN models, a compact 13.2k-parameter dilated 1D CNN achieves 75% test accuracy and 85% leave-one-subject-out accuracy, enabling real-time deployment on microcontrollers for soft therapeutic companions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Soft sensorized companions need to interpret human affect through touch, but their deformability and multichannel tactile sensing make robust affect recognition difficult. Existing heuristic-based systems fail to detect subtle social touches, motivating the need for compact deep learning models that can run on embedded devices while preserving privacy.

Method: The authors (1) collected a FAIR-compliant dataset of 1326 gesture sequences from 25 participants across age groups, (2) systematically explored 468 CNN architectures varying hyperparameters, (3) identified dilated 1D CNNs as optimal, (4) performed quantization analysis for microcontroller deployment (3.2 MMAC/window), and (5) proposed a hybrid pipeline: heuristic threshold-based filtering for high-force negative interactions followed by CNN-based nuanced gesture classification.

Result: The best model (13.2k parameters) achieves 75% test accuracy and 85% mean leave-one-subject-out cross-validation accuracy. Theoretical inference analysis shows 3.2 MMAC per window, supporting 20 Hz real-time operation on target microcontrollers. PC-based real-time simulation shows the CNN detects subtle social touches missed by heuristic systems, while threshold logic better captures high-force negative interactions.

Conclusion: Emotionally meaningful, privacy-preserving touch interpretation is computationally feasible for direct embedding within soft therapeutic companions. The proposed hybrid inference pipeline (heuristic filtering + CNN classification) serves as the optimal embedded deployment strategy, with hardware integration addressed in a forthcoming study.

Abstract: Soft, sensorized companions offer a physically safe and emotionally intuitive interface for socially assistive technologies, yet their deformability and multichannel tactile sensing complicate the robust interpretation of human affect. This study presents a complete open-source MATLAB-based framework for the development and validation of compact deep learning models for affective touch recognition in soft interactive companions. As a primary contribution, a diverse FAIR-compliant dataset of 1326 labelled gesture sequences collected from 25 participants spanning children, teenagers, and adults is made publicly available, providing a reusable resource for future research in affective touch recognition. Through systematic architecture and hyperparameter exploration across 468 CNN models, the study identifies compact dilated one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D CNNs) as the most effective solution, with a 13.2k-parameter model achieving 75% test accuracy and 85% mean leave-one-subject-out cross-validation accuracy. Theoretical inference-time analysis shows that quantized deployment requires 3.2 MMAC per window, compatible with 20 Hz real-time operation on the target microcontroller. PC-based real-time simulation with the physical toy streaming sensor data demonstrates that the CNN resolves subtle social touches that the previous heuristic system failed to detect, whereas high-force negative interactions are captured more reliably by trivial threshold-based logic. The resulting hybrid inference pipeline - instantaneous heuristic filtering followed by CNN-based nuanced gesture classification - is proposed as the embedded deployment strategy. The study demonstrates that emotionally meaningful, privacy-preserving touch interpretation is computationally feasible for direct embedding within soft therapeutic companions, with hardware integration addressed in a forthcoming study.

</details>


### [73] [Some Large Language Models Exhibit Consistent Risk Attitudes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16197)
*Bowen Sun,Rui Min,Yuxi Wang,Brian Odegaard,Qi Wang,Jing Du*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper tests six LLMs and 100 humans across three tasks (navigation, triage, finance) and finds LLMs exhibit stable, consistent risk attitudes—both within tasks and across domains—converging to a narrower risk-attitude range than humans.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: As AI is deployed in high-stakes, open-ended settings, we lack a way to measure how perceived risk translates into action. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically testing whether LLMs have stable risk attitudes.

Method: A cross-domain framework decouples contextual risk belief from categorical decision. Applied to six LLMs and 100 humans across spatial navigation, clinical triage, and financial allocation. Regression models extract belief-to-decision mappings, quantifying risk sensitivity and risk attitude bias.

Result: LLMs show (i) robust intra-task consistency, (ii) cross-domain rank-order stability (relative risk posture preserved across tasks), and (iii) convergence to a narrower risk-attitude distribution compared to the broader human baseline.

Conclusion: Risk attitude is a stable, previously uncharacterized dimension of LLM behavior, establishing a foundation for evaluating and aligning AI systems in open-ended decision-making.

Abstract: As artificial intelligence systems are deployed in open-ended, high-stakes settings, a critical dimension remains unmeasured: how perceived risk is translated into action. We test whether large language models (LLMs) exhibit systematic and consistent risk attitudes under uncertainty. We introduce a cross-domain framework that decouples contextual risk belief from categorical decision, and apply it to six representative LLMs and 100 human participants across spatial navigation, clinical triage, and financial allocation tasks. Using regression models, we extract each agents belief-to-decision mapping and quantify risk sensitivity and risk attitude bias. We find that most tested LLMs exhibit (i) robust intra-task consistency, indicating stable mappings from contextual belief to risk decision within a fixed task domain; (ii) cross-domain rank-order stability, preserving relative risk posture across tasks; and (iii) a convergence toward a restricted risk-attitude distribution relative to the broader human baseline. These results reveal risk attitude as a stable and previously uncharacterized dimension of LLM behavior, establishing a foundation for evaluating and aligning AI systems in open-ended decision-making and motivating further investigation into the origins of these intrinsic behavioral dispositions.

</details>


### [74] [A Survey on GNN-based Link Prediction: Techniques, Applications, and Challenges](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16198)
*Chengcheng Sun,Yajie Song,Cheng Zhai,Jiayun Tian,Jia Yang,Xiaobin Rui,Jian Zhang,Zhixiao Wang,Philip S. Yu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper provides a comprehensive review of GNN-based link prediction, proposing a new taxonomy categorizing methods by GNN architectures (GCN, GAE, GAT, GFormer) and applications (knowledge graphs, recommendation systems), while discussing challenges and future directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing reviews lack systematic exploration specifically targeting underlying GNN architectures and diverse graph structures for link prediction, creating a critical gap that this paper aims to fill.

Method: The authors propose an innovative taxonomy that categorizes recent GNN-based link prediction advancements from two perspectives: (1) technique perspective focusing on GNN encoder architectures (GCN-based, GAE-based, GAT-based, GFormer-based) and (2) application perspective highlighting use cases in knowledge graphs and recommendation systems.

Result: The paper systematically categorizes and discusses the strengths and limitations of GCN, GAE, GAT, and GFormer-based methods for link prediction, while demonstrating real-world impact through knowledge graph and recommendation system applications.

Conclusion: The paper identifies current challenges in GNN-based link prediction and discusses promising future research directions to advance the field.

Abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have emerged as the leading paradigm for link prediction, enabling the inference of missing connections and the anticipation of potential future links. However, existing reviews lack systematic exploration specifically targeting underlying GNN architectures and diverse graph structures. To address this critical gap, this paper provides a comprehensive review of GNN-based link prediction from a novel and dedicated GNN perspective. We propose an innovative taxonomy that categorizes recent advancements based on techniques and applications. From a technique perspective, we focus on key GNN encoder architectures, including GCN-based, GAE-based, GAT-based, and GFormer-based methods, discussing their strengths and limitations. From an application perspective, we highlight prominent use cases of link prediction in knowledge graphs and recommendation systems, demonstrating their real-world impact. In addition, we examine the current challenges and discuss promising future directions.

</details>


### [75] [PlanFlip: Attacking Multi-Agent LLM Systems via Planning-Phase Prompt Injection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16199)
*Yuhang Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PlanFlip introduces four planning-phase prompt injection attacks (PF-1 to PF-4) against multi-agent LLM systems, showing that stronger models are more vulnerable (GPT-5 ASR=0.68), homogeneous pipelines have correlated blind spots, and reasoning-augmented models resist attacks. Two defenses (GoalAnchorCheck, CrossAgentConsensus) achieve up to 1.00 detection rate.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Planner phase in multi-agent LLM systems is identified as a critical attack surface where a single injection can cascade-amplify to corrupt all downstream sub-tasks, yet this vulnerability has been underexplored.

Method: Four planning-phase prompt injection attacks (GoalSubstitution, PriorityInversion, ContextPollution, RoleConfusion) disguised as plausible tool outputs; evaluated on 9 LLMs across 3,479 episodes; two defenses proposed: GoalAnchorCheck and CrossAgentConsensus.

Result: (1) Capability amplifies vulnerability: GPT-5 highest ASR=0.68; (2) Homogeneous pipelines have correlated-agent blind spots; (3) Reasoning-augmented models (DeepSeek-R1) resist injections (StepShift=0.00). Defenses achieve detection rates up to 1.00, outperforming baselines in 15/16 cells.

Conclusion: Heterogeneous model diversity is a security prerequisite for multi-agent systems; homogeneous backbones provide no protection against planning-phase attacks.

Abstract: Multi-agent LLM systems increasingly rely on a Planner to decompose goals into sub-task sequences that downstream Executor and Critic agents execute and audit. We identify the planning phase as a critical attack surface: a single injection into the Planner's context achieves cascade amplification, corrupting all downstream sub-tasks simultaneously. We introduce PlanFlip, a framework comprising four planning-phase prompt injection attacks -- GoalSubstitution (PF-1), PriorityInversion (PF-2), ContextPollution (PF-3), and RoleConfusion (PF-4) -- each disguised as plausible tool outputs to evade keyword filters. Evaluating nine frontier LLMs across 3,479 episodes, we uncover three findings: (1) capability amplifies vulnerability -- GPT-5 achieves the highest attack success rate (ASR = 0.68), contradicting the assumption that stronger models are inherently more secure; (2) homogeneous pipelines exhibit a correlated-agent blind spot -- GPT-4o and Llama-3.3-70B show ASR near 0 yet Stealth = 1.00 and StepShift > 0, with attacks restructuring plans while the same-backbone Critic reports alignment (two independent judges confirm -0.20 to -0.32 semantic deviation, r = 0.943); (3) reasoning-augmented models resist injections -- DeepSeek-R1 achieves StepShift = 0.00 across all attacks. We propose GoalAnchorCheck (D1) and CrossAgentConsensus (D2), achieving detection rates up to 1.00 and outperforming same-backbone baselines in 15 of 16 cells. Our key insight: heterogeneous model diversity is a security prerequisite for multi-agent systems; redundancy within a homogeneous backbone provides no protection against planning-phase attacks.

</details>


### [76] [Deterministic Replay for AI Agent Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16200)
*Rasheed Mudasiru*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: agrepl is a CLI framework for deterministic replay of AI agent executions. It uses a MITM proxy to intercept and serialize all external interactions, then replays them in an isolated environment with zero network access. It achieves perfect replay fidelity (F=1.0) and 98.3% median latency reduction across five workloads.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agent systems combining LLMs with external tools/APIs are inherently non-deterministic due to LLM sampling variance, API state changes, CDN header variation, and execution-environment noise. Existing observability platforms capture logs but cannot reproduce runs in isolation, making debugging and reproducibility difficult.

Method: The system (1) intercepts all external interactions at the transport layer via a MITM proxy, (2) serialises them as structured execution traces, (3) replays them in a strictly isolated environment with zero outbound network access. It formalizes an agent execution model, defines a request-key matching function K(s), proves a determinism invariant, and introduces a noise-aware diff algorithm classifying HTTP header divergence into signal/noise tiers.

Result: Empirical evaluation across five workloads (n=250 replay instances) demonstrates replay fidelity F=1.0 and a median per-step latency reduction of 98.3%.

Conclusion: agrepl successfully enables deterministic replay of non-deterministic AI agent executions, achieving perfect fidelity and dramatic latency reduction. It is implemented in Go as a single static binary and released under MIT license.

Abstract: AI agent systems that couple large language models (LLMs) with external tools and APIs are inherently non-deterministic: LLM sampling variance, external API state, CDN infrastructure headers, and execution-environment noise collectively prevent any prior agent run from being faithfully re-executed. Existing observability platforms capture execution logs but cannot reproduce a run in isolation. We present agrepl, a developer-first CLI framework for deterministic replay of agent executions. agrepl intercepts all external interactions at the transport layer via a man-in-the-middle (MITM) proxy, serialises them as structured execution traces, and replays them in a strictly isolated environment with zero outbound network access. We formalise the agent execution model, define the request-key matching function K(s), and prove the determinism invariant. We introduce a noise-aware diff algorithm classifying HTTP header divergence into signal and noise tiers. Empirical evaluation across five workloads (n = 250 replay instances) demonstrates replay fidelity F = 1.0 and a median per-step latency reduction of 98.3%. agrepl is implemented in Go, ships as a single static binary, and is released under the MIT licence.
  Keywords: AI agents, deterministic replay, LLM debugging, reproducibility, MITM proxy, execution tracing, record/replay systems.

</details>


### [77] [Generative Ontology Induction: Domain-Agnostic Schema Discovery from Document Corpora Using Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16201)
*Sergei Sergienko*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: GOI induces structured ontologies (typed graphs) from example corpora, achieving 95-100% structural node coverage across four diverse domains, outperforming generic templates.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Ontology engineering is a bottleneck for knowledge-intensive AI; existing methods rely on predefined schemas, narrow domains, or produce unstructured outputs.

Method: GOI is a domain-agnostic framework that extracts a generative blueprint (entities, dimensions, properties, relationships, constraints) from examples and exports it as a typed graph (6 node types, 7 edge types) in YAML/JSON. A new metric (Node Coverage Score) measures structural ontology node coverage.

Result: GOI covers 95-100% structural backbone across four ontologies (Invoice, Job Description, Pain-Management, Professional Services Contract). A generic template drops from 97.8% (Invoice) to 52.2%-78.3% on less familiar domains.

Conclusion: GOI provides robust, domain-agnostic ontology induction with high structural coverage regardless of document familiarity, significantly outperforming generic templates.

Abstract: Ontology engineering remains a critical bottleneck in knowledge-intensive AI systems. Existing automated approaches either depend on predefined schemas, operate within narrow domains, or produce unstructured outputs unsuitable for downstream pipelines.
  We introduce Generative Ontology Induction (GOI), a domain-agnostic framework that induces a generative blueprint - entities, dimensions, properties, relationships, and constraints - from a corpus of examples and exports it as a typed graph (six node types, seven edge types) in YAML/JSON. We introduce the Node Coverage Score, a novel evaluation metric that measures the fraction of structural ontology nodes (classes, properties, and dimensions) appearing in generated outputs.
  A controlled generative validation on four contrasting ontologies - a familiar Software Services Invoice schema, a custom Job Description Ontology, a confidential Pain-Management Clinical Visit Record Ontology, and a Professional Services Contract & Statement of Work Ontology - shows that GOI-prompted generation covers 95-100% of the structural backbone in every case; a generic three-field template holds at 97.8% on the invoice schema but drops to 52.2% on the Job Description Ontology, 62.2% on the Pain-Management ontology, and 78.3% on the Professional Services Contract ontology. The structural coverage holds regardless of how familiar the document type is to the model.

</details>


### [78] [Democratizing AI with Small Language Models: Structured Benchmarking and Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning for Local Deployment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16202)
*Daniel Cersosimo*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper evaluates nine open-weight language models (135M–3B parameters) on a custom structured benchmark and shows that with parameter-efficient fine-tuning, small models like Qwen Coder 3B can achieve high accuracy for specialized local deployment, demonstrating that AI democratization is achievable via disciplined, low-cost workflows rather than frontier-scale models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To show that AI democratization depends not on matching frontier-scale generality, but on whether capable models can be selected, audited, and specialized under realistic hardware and governance constraints affordable to ordinary institutions.

Method: Controlled evaluation of nine open-weight LLMs (135M–3B) on a custom 1,085-example, 16-topic multiple-choice benchmark emphasizing symbolic precision, constrained formatting, and extraction; followed by parameter-efficient fine-tuning using 4-bit NF4 quantization with DoRA/LoRA adapters on an NVIDIA L4-class GPU.

Result: Qwen Coder 3B achieved top base accuracy (75.67%). Fine-tuning improved Qwen Coder 3B by +26.85 points, SmolLM2 1.7B by +25.92, and even sub-1B models like SmolLM2 135M by +5.55 points, making small models viable for niche structured workloads.

Conclusion: A disciplined workflow of benchmark construction, cross-model evaluation, and low-cost specialization makes a subset of sub-3B models viable as local experts for structured niche workloads, supporting democratized AI deployment.

Abstract: AI democratization is not primarily a question of matching frontier-scale generality; it is a question of whether capable models can be selected, audited, and specialized under hardware and governance constraints that ordinary institutions can actually satisfy. This paper studies that problem through a controlled evaluation of nine open-weight language models between 135M and 3B parameters on a 1,085-example, 16-topic multiple-choice benchmark designed for structured local deployment. The benchmark emphasizes symbolic precision, constrained formatting, extraction, and short-horizon semantic decision making under a strict one-letter output protocol. A shared parameter-efficient fine-tuning pipeline then adapts a subset of models using 4-bit NF4 quantization with DoRA/LoRA-style adapters on an NVIDIA L4-class budget. In base evaluation, Qwen Coder 3B leads at 75.67% strict accuracy, followed by Qwen2.5 1.5B at 67.10%, Qwen3.5 2B at 64.98%, and Granite 3.3 2B at 64.61%. On the shared 108-example held-out fine-tuning split, adaptation improves Qwen Coder 3B by +26.85 points, SmolLM2 1.7B by +25.92, Qwen2.5 1.5B by +19.44, SmolLM2 360M by +10.18, and SmolLM2 135M by +5.55. Across ranking, topic-level heterogeneity, difficulty strata, failure composition, efficiency frontiers, and topic-conditioned transfer, the same conclusion recurs: a disciplined workflow of benchmark construction, cross-model evaluation, and low-cost specialization already makes a subset of sub-3B models viable as local experts for structured niche workloads.

</details>


### [79] [Masked Diffusion Language Models are Strong and Steerable Text-Based World Models for Agentic RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16204)
*Darshan Deshpande*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces steerable text-based world models using masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) that outperform AR LMs 4x their size in coherence, groundedness, and rollout diversity. A GRPO training framework with deterministic state checks achieves up to 47% absolute gains on OOD environments without fine-tuning, supported by a curated dataset of 239,403 trajectories.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hand-curated RL environments with fixed difficulty become ineffective as models improve; sparse rewards over long horizons cause mode collapse. World models can scale diversity on-demand, but autoregressive models suffer from left-to-right bias, preventing conditioning on globally interdependent state anchors.

Method: (i) Formalizes text-based world modeling as steerable transition-dynamics with five components. (ii) Curates 239,403 trajectories across 9 environments and 12 model families. (iii) Compares AR LMs vs MDLMs using bidirectional anchor-aware denoising. (iv) Introduces plug-and-play GRPO training with deterministic state checks. (v) Zero-shot transfer ablations on 3 OOD environments with 3 agent backbones.

Result: MDLMs achieve better coherence, groundedness, and rollout diversity than LLMs 4x their size. Up to 47% absolute gains on OOD environments (ScienceWorld, ALFWorld, AppWorld) across 1.2B-7B backbones (LFM2.5, Qwen3, Mistral) without environment-specific fine-tuning.

Conclusion: Masked diffusion world models with bidirectional conditioning significantly outperform larger autoregressive models for steerable text-based RL training. The plug-and-play GRPO framework enables strong zero-shot generalization. The work is open-sourced to encourage further research.

Abstract: Recent growth in reinforcement learning (RL) has surfaced a need for diverse, specialized training environments. Hand-curated environments with fixed task and reward difficulties become ineffective signals as model performance improves, and sparse rewards over long horizons induce mode collapse on specific workflows or tool structures. World models that simulate environment states have matched pure rollout performance, making them promising for scaling diversity on-demand. However, autoregressive (AR) world models suffer from a left-to-right bias preventing conditioning on globally interdependent state anchors such as tool schemas, prior turns, and expected outcomes. We (i) formalize text-based world modeling as a steerable transition-dynamics problem decomposed into initial state, task context, tool schemas, domain rules, and steering directives, and (ii) curate 239,403 grounded state-action trajectories spanning nine open-source environments and twelve frontier model families. We compare AR LMs and masked diffusion language models (MDLMs), showing MDLMs, via bidirectional anchor-aware denoising, achieve better coherence, groundedness, and empirically validated rollout diversity than LLMs over 4x their parameter size, at comparable inference latency.
