We introduce a plug-and-play GRPO training framework with deterministic state checks, and perform zero-shot transfer ablations on three OOD environments (ScienceWorld, ALFWorld, AppWorld) across three 1.2B-7B agent backbones (LFM2.5, Qwen3, Mistral), achieving up to 47% absolute gains over baselines without environment-specific fine-tuning. We further conduct behavioral analysis of failure modes under adversarial scenarios and human evaluation on realism, outcome correctness, and training utility. We open-source our work to encourage research in this direction.

</details>


### [80] [It Takes 8 Tokens: Weak-to-Strong Off-Policy RL via Auxiliary Branches](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16205)
*Dayu Wang,Jiaye Yang,Weikang Li,Jiahui Liang,Liwei Qian,Xin Pei,Jizhou Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: W2SPO is an off-policy RL method that injects short segments from a weaker auxiliary model into target model trajectories to overcome limited exploration in reasoning tasks, achieving 64.2% Pass@1 (vs 62.3% for GRPO) with 3.55x training speedup on 4B-scale models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL with verifiable rewards suffers from a "support-limited bottleneck" where model samples converge into redundant erroneous reasoning basins with negligible reward contrast, hindering effective policy updates on challenging reasoning tasks.

Method: W2SPO is an off-policy RL method that uses a weaker but computationally efficient auxiliary model to inject short segments (as brief as 8 tokens) into intermediate target model trajectories. The target model then completes reasoning from these diverted states, and policy updates are restricted to only the short inserted segments based on final verifiable rewards.

Result: At 4B scale on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, W2SPO outperforms post-trained baselines, improving Pass@1 from 62.3% (GRPO) to 64.2% while achieving a 3.55x training speedup under the same sampling budget.

Conclusion: Weak auxiliary branches can induce stronger target reasoning policies by expanding local exploration support, effectively overcoming the support-limited bottleneck in RL-based reasoning enhancement.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards has emerged as a standard approach for enhancing reasoning in large language models, which typically optimizes the policy by contrasting multiple self generated rollouts. However, we identify a critical support limited bottleneck in this paradigm: on challenging reasoning tasks, the target model's samples often exhibit semantic redundancy, converging into the same erroneous "reasoning basins" that offer negligible reward contrast for policy updates. In this paper, we propose to overcome this limitation through a weak to strong learning paradigm, where a policy's exploration is informed by a weaker but computationally efficient auxiliary model. We introduce W2SPO, an off policy RL method that injects short auxiliary segments often as brief as 8 tokens into intermediate target model trajectories and the target model then completes the reasoning path from these diverted states. Policy updates are restricted to these short inserted segments based on final verifiable rewards. Empirically, W2SPO achieves superior performance among evaluated 4B scale models on mathematical reasoning benchmarks, outperforming evaluated post trained baselines. Compared with vanilla GRPO under the same sampling budget, W2SPO improves Pass@1 from 62.3% to 64.2% while achieving a 3.55 times training speedup. These results suggest that weak auxiliary branches can induce stronger target reasoning policies by expanding local exploration support.

</details>


### [81] [PPO-HSC: An Exploratory Reinforcement Learning Framework Based on Wide-Area Policy Coverage Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16206)
*Yujie Shen,Haowen Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PPO-HSC introduces a novelty-rewarding mechanism to prevent mode collapse in LLM fine-tuning by incentivizing diverse yet valid reasoning patterns, outperforming standard RLVR on math and code tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RLVR causes LLMs to over-optimize known solutions, sacrificing exploration and leading to mode collapse ("Invisible Shackles").

Method: Extends PPO with a High-order Sampling Coverage (HSC) reward that tracks a dynamic trajectory library, rewarding low-similarity yet high-validity reasoning patterns via a differentiable signal with a plausibility constraint.

Result: Enhances solution diversity and state-space coverage while maintaining or surpassing accuracy and syntax integrity on GSM8K, SVAMP, and code generation benchmarks.

Conclusion: PPO-HSC effectively breaks mode collapse in LLM fine-tuning, balancing exploration and exploitation for more robust reasoning.

Abstract: This paper introduces PPO-HSC (Proximal Policy Optimization with High-order Sampling Coverage), an exploratory reinforcement learning framework designed to address the "Invisible Shackles" of mode collapse in Large Language Model (LLM) fine-tuning. While standard Reinforcement Learning from Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) effectively reinforces high-reward trajectories, it often leads models to over-optimize known solutions, sacrificing curiosity and the ability to explore broader solution manifolds. To overcome this, PPO-HSC incorporates a High-order Sampling Coverage (HSC) reward that incentivizes the discovery of "low-similarity yet high-validity" reasoning patterns. By maintaining a dynamic trajectory library of verified unique solutions, the framework provides a differentiable signal that rewards semantic novelty while ensuring structural rationality through a plausibility constraint. Empirical evaluations on mathematical reasoning (GSM8K, SVAMP) and code generation tasks demonstrate that PPO-HSC significantly enhances solution diversity and state-space coverage while maintaining or surpassing the accuracy and syntax integrity of state-of-the-art RL baselines.

</details>


### [82] [JUMP: Single-Pass Membership Inference on Fine-Tuned Diffusion Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16207)
*Yeachan Jun,Albert No*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: JUMP is a new single-pass membership inference attack for fine-tuned discrete diffusion LLMs that exploits parallel and any-order decodability, improving ROC-AUC from 0.82 to 0.90 over SAMA on LLaDA-8B-Base across six domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MIA for dLLMs (SAMA) uses the any-order interface only as randomization, requiring many target/reference queries, and does not fully exploit the unique properties of discrete diffusion models for attack efficiency.

Method: JUMP uses a single-pass scoring attack with two key steps: (1) selector phase leverages any-order decodability to identify low-reference-confidence positions, (2) scoring phase jointly masks those positions and computes a clipped target/reference reconstruction-gap statistic using parallel decodability.

Result: On fine-tuned LLaDA-8B-Base across six MIMIR domains, JUMP improves mean ROC-AUC from 0.82 to 0.90 over SAMA, substantially improves low-FPR detection, and requires only one selector pass and one scoring pass through each model (target and reference).

Conclusion: By exploiting both any-order and parallel decodability of dLLMs, JUMP achieves more effective and efficient membership inference than prior approaches like SAMA.

Abstract: Membership inference attacks (MIAs) test whether a candidate example appeared in a model's training data. We study MIAs for fine-tuned discrete diffusion language models (dLLMs), where membership means inclusion in the target model's fine-tuning set. Unlike autoregressive language models, dLLMs allow an attacker to choose arbitrary mask sets and obtain token distributions for all masked positions in parallel. The prior dLLM attack, SAMA, follows a natural loss-mimicking strategy by averaging reconstruction signals over many randomly sampled masks, but it uses the any-order interface only as randomization and requires many target/reference queries. We propose JUMP (Joint Uncertainty-Guided Mask Probing), a single-pass scoring attack that exploits both distinctive properties of dLLMs: any-order decodability is used to select low-reference-confidence positions, and parallel decodability is used to score all selected positions through one joint masked query per model. JUMP masks the selected positions jointly and computes a clipped target/reference reconstruction-gap statistic. On fine-tuned LLaDA-8B-Base across six MIMIR domains, JUMP improves mean ROC-AUC from 0.82 to 0.90 over SAMA and substantially improves low-FPR detection, while requiring only one selector pass and one scoring pass through each of the target and reference models.

</details>


### [83] [ColGraphRAG: Late-Interaction Evidence Retrieval for Multimodal GraphRAG](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16208)
*Seonok Kim*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Replacing bi-encoder visual retrieval with late-interaction multi-vector scoring (ColBERT/ColPali-style) in graph-grounded multimodal QA improves graph-linked image candidate retrieval and downstream QA accuracy, especially for visual-dominant questions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current graph-grounded multimodal QA relies on single-vector bi-encoder similarity for visual candidate ranking, which loses fine-grained patch- and token-level structure needed for accurate alignment of graph-linked images.

Method: The authors replace the visual candidate-ranking operator over graph-linked image nodes with late-interaction MaxSim-style multi-vector scoring (from ColBERT/ColPali), while keeping all other components (offline graph construction, text/table retrieval, structured extraction, downstream reasoning) unchanged.

Result: On MultimodalQA, they observe improved retrieval-stage point estimates for graph-linked image candidates and downstream QA gains, with larger improvements on visual-dominant questions and mixed trends on text-dominant questions.

Conclusion: The pattern provides mechanism-level evidence for graph-linked visual evidence inclusion; broader validation and finer graph-level diagnostics are needed as future work.

Abstract: Graph-grounded multimodal question answering organizes text, tables, and images in a structured evidence graph, yet end-to-end accuracy depends on which multimodal assets are ranked highly enough to enter downstream reasoning; for graph-linked images, single-vector bi-encoder similarity can discard patch- and token-level structure needed for fine-grained alignment. We evaluate replacing the visual candidate-ranking operator over graph-linked image nodes with late-interaction MaxSim-style multi-vector scoring in the ColBERT/ColPali lineage, while keeping offline graph construction, text- and table-side retrieval, structured extraction, and downstream reasoning unchanged. On MultimodalQA, this change is associated with improved retrieval-stage point estimates for graph-linked image candidates and downstream QA gains, with larger movement where visual evidence matters most and mixed trends on text-dominant questions; we interpret the pattern as mechanism-level evidence for graph-linked visual evidence inclusion, while broader validation and finer graph-level diagnostics remain important future work.

</details>


### [84] [Shapley Context Pruning: A Cooperative Game Perspective for Context Reranking and Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16209)
*Yanqiao Chen,Dongsheng Hou,Yuhan Rui,Zhen Cao,Yepang Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Shapley Context Pruning (SCP), a cooperative-game-theory-based framework for context reranking in RAG systems, using Deep Sets architecture and Monte-Carlo sampling to achieve efficient, interpretable context pruning with theoretical guarantees.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing context reranking and pruning methods lack an interpretable and unified framework, relying heavily on heuristic loss functions and empirical attribution rather than principled approaches.

Method: SCP models context as a cooperative game using Shapley values for importance attribution, employs a Deep Sets architecture for permutation-invariant sentence-level value approximation, uses pairwise margin ranking loss, and leverages Monte-Carlo sampling with theoretical error bounds.

Result: Competitive downstream QA performance against robust baselines, validated through supporting-sentence recall, NIAH evaluations, long-context QA, multi-hop reasoning, and ablation studies.

Conclusion: The proposed SCP framework offers a mathematically grounded, interpretable, and scalable solution for context reranking and pruning in RAG systems.

Abstract: Context reranking and pruning have become essential for improving the efficiency of modern Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems, yet an interpretable and unified framework remains underexplored. Previous work has primarily emphasized lexical retrieval, cross-encoder architectures, model distillation, and Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA), mostly relying on heuristic loss functions and empirical attribution. This paper presents Shapley Context Pruning (SCP), a novel framework for context reranking that establishes a cooperative-game-theory perspective for importance attribution by modeling the context as a cooperative game. Balancing the trade-off between fine-grained and coarse-grained representations, we employ a Deep Sets architecture to approximate a permutation-invariant value function at the sentence level, utilizing pre-trained language models as sentence embedders and optimizing via a pairwise margin ranking loss. To ensure practical scalability without sacrificing mathematical rigor, we leverage Monte-Carlo sampling for efficient training and inference, providing formal theoretical error bounds and sample complexity guarantees for preserving Top-K subset rankings. Furthermore, we conduct comprehensive experiments-spanning supporting-sentence recall, Needle-in-the-Haystack (NIAH) evaluations, long-context QA, and multi-hop reasoning-alongside rigorous ablation studies on embedding quality and attribution strategies. The model achieves competitive downstream QA performance against robust baselines.

</details>


### [85] [A Survey on the Verification of Reinforcement Learning Policies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16210)
*Luca Marzari,Ezio Bartocci,Enrico Marchesini*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A survey on RL policy verification that provides a unifying taxonomy across three axes (verification paradigm, temporal scope, guarantee strength), unifies theoretical foundations, and identifies emerging research directions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RL is increasingly used in safety-critical domains, but neural network policies lack rigorous behavioral guarantees. The field is growing rapidly but conceptually fragmented, motivating the need for a unifying perspective.

Method: The authors introduce a three-axis taxonomy: (1) verification paradigm (formal vs. probabilistic), (2) temporal scope (step-wise vs. multi-step), and (3) guarantee strength. They also unify theoretical foundations and make implicit assumptions explicit.

Result: A structured taxonomy that clarifies relationships among existing RL verification approaches, along with explicit articulation of assumptions, limitations, and emerging directions in the field.

Conclusion: Provides a cohesive framework for understanding and comparing RL verification methods, helping researchers navigate the fragmented landscape and identify promising future work in safety-critical RL deployment.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is increasingly applied in complex, safety-critical domains, yet the lack of rigorous behavioral guarantees for neural network-based policies remains a major barrier to deployment. Recent advances in policy expressiveness and scale have intensified this challenge, leading to a rapidly growing but conceptually fragmented body of work on RL policy verification. This survey provides a unifying perspective on RL verification methods. We introduce a taxonomy that clarifies relationships among existing approaches along three axes: verification paradigm (formal versus probabilistic), temporal scope (step-wise versus multi-step), and guarantees strength. Beyond taxonomy, we unify underlying theoretical foundations, make implicit assumptions and limitations explicit, and identify emerging directions.

</details>


### [86] [Accurate and Efficient Long-Term Memory for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16211)
*Zicheng Zhao,Xinyang Guo,Luyao Lv,Menghan Wang,Ming Li,Shuaicheng Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: MOSAIC is a structured, conflict-aware long-term memory framework for LLM agents that uses entity-typed graph storage, hash-accelerated retrieval (0.58s latency), and active conflict detection, achieving 89.35% accuracy on LoCoMo (+27.21 pp) and detecting 66% of factual conflicts (4.7x better than baselines).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LLM agent memory systems use flat, unstructured storage that loses relational context and rely on expensive LLM-based classification, while silently accumulating contradictions without validation mechanisms.

Method: Three novel components: (1) entity-typed graph storage with semantic classification preserving relational structure; (2) hash-accelerated dual-path retrieval using locality-sensitive hashing instead of LLM classification; (3) active conflict detection at save time cross-referencing new info against graph neighbors.

Result: 89.35% accuracy on LoCoMo (+27.21 pp), best extraction F1 on HaluMem-Medium (86.77%) and Long (85.84%), best QA correctness (73.10% Medium, 70.75% Long), 66% conflict detection (4.7x baseline), 0.58s average latency.

Conclusion: MOSAIC demonstrates that structured graph-based memory with hash-accelerated retrieval and active conflict detection significantly improves both accuracy and efficiency over flat memory approaches for LLM agents.

Abstract: LLM agents augmented with persistent memory can recall past interactions, but existing systems suffer from two limitations: flat, unstructured storage loses relational context needed for multi-hop and temporal reasoning, and reliance on expensive LLM-based classification makes them impractical for latency-sensitive deployment. Without mechanisms to validate new information against stored knowledge, these systems silently accumulate contradictions. We present MOSAIC (Memory-Organized Structured Agent for Information Collection), a structured, conflict-aware long-term memory framework for LLM agents that is substantially more accurate and efficient. MOSAIC introduces three key capabilities: (1) entity-typed graph storage with semantic classification preserving relational structure across events, personas, and relationships, enabling multi-hop and temporal reasoning over conversation history; (2) hash-accelerated dual-path retrieval replacing LLM-based classification with locality-sensitive hashing, achieving near-instantaneous lookup with negligible accuracy loss; and (3) active conflict detection at save time that cross-references new information against existing graph neighbors, triggering updates or deletions for contradictory entries. Evaluated on LoCoMo (long-conversation QA), HaluMem, and a novel clinical-guideline error compounding test, MOSAIC achieves 89.35% accuracy on LoCoMo (+27.21 pp over the best baseline), best HaluMem-Medium extraction F1(86.77%) and HaluMem-Long extraction F1 (85.84%), best QA correctness on both Medium and Long (73.10%, 70.75%), and detects 66% of injected factual conflicts-4.7 times higher than the best baseline (14%)-while hash-accelerated retrieval keeps average search latency at 0.58 s per question.

</details>


### [87] [Symbolic Augmentation Closes a Canonical-Equivalence Blind Spot in Neural Fact-Checkers](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16212)
*Genpei Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes Symbolic Augmentation, a training-time framework that improves LLM robustness to physically equivalent but textually different numerical quantities (e.g., 95°C vs 368.15 K), lifting accuracy from 36.5% to 98.2% on such cases while maintaining in-distribution performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs hallucinate numbers and units when summarizing scientific text, a failure mode that can silently invert scientific claims. Existing fact-checkers have a sharp blind spot on canonical-equivalent rewrites of physically equivalent quantities.

Method: They create a five-class typed-quantity error taxonomy and a 1500-item benchmark from PMC/arXiv sources. They fine-tune a ModernBERT encoder and propose Symbolic Augmentation, a training-time framework that runs symbolic verifier modules in reverse to generate label-preserving augmented training data.

Result: Symbolic Augmentation lifts canonical-equivalence robustness from 36.5% to 98.2%, slightly improves in-distribution macro-F1 (0.899 to 0.902), transfers well to SciFact-Open (0.791 to 0.828), and matches a closed-frontier LLM at no inference cost. Symbolic features as auxiliary encoder inputs and symbolic silver labels under teacher noise both yield negative results.

Conclusion: Training-time augmentation is the right integration point between symbolic and learned components for quantity verification in scientific text.

Abstract: Large language models hallucinate numbers and units when summarizing scientific text, a failure mode that can silently invert a scientific claim. We recast the detection of such errors as typed verification: we introduce a five-class typed-quantity error taxonomy and a 1500-item benchmark, rewritten from PMC and arXiv sources and labeled by two independent LLM annotators with adjudication (Krippendorff's alpha = 0.882). A ModernBERT encoder fine-tuned on this benchmark reaches macro-F1 = 0.899, far above any off-the-shelf neural fact-checker, yet four probes expose a sharp structural blind spot: on canonical-equivalent rewrites of physically equivalent quantities (e.g., 95°C and 368.15 K) its accuracy collapses to 36.5%. We propose Symbolic Augmentation, a training-time framework that runs the modules of a symbolic verifier in reverse to generate label-preserving augmented training data. The augmentation lifts canonical-equivalence robustness to 98.2% while slightly improving in-distribution accuracy (macro-F1: 0.899 to 0.902); the augmented encoder matches a closed-frontier LLM at no inference cost and transfers to an external benchmark (SciFact-Open binary macro-F1: 0.791 to 0.828). Two negative results sharpen the claim: symbolic features as auxiliary encoder inputs add nothing, and symbolic silver labels scale negatively under teacher noise. Together these results identify training-time augmentation as the right integration point between symbolic and learned components.

</details>


### [88] [SelKV: Selective KV Cache Merging with Per-Token Merge-or-Drop and Attention Compensation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16213)
*Soumia Bouyahiaoui,Manel Kara laouar,Aicha Boutorh,Mohamed Hadj Ameur*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A training-free KV cache compression framework using soft cosine gating for selective token merging and attention-ratio compensation to correct softmax imbalance, achieving robust performance at 25% cache retention with 3.3x decoding speedup at 100k tokens.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing token merging methods for KV cache compression use indiscriminate aggregation that degrades representations and causes "attention sag" — a mismatch where merged tokens receive the same softmax mass as individual tokens despite encoding multiple inputs.

Method: A dual-component framework: (1) soft cosine gate adaptively modulates merging decisions based on value-vector similarity to preserve semantic fidelity; (2) attention-ratio compensation applies a decoding-time logit bias from prefill attention statistics to correct softmax imbalance.

Result: At 25% KV cache retention on LongBench, achieves strong performance against one-shot baselines; near-lossless on GQA models; outperforms full-cache baseline on multi-document QA; delivers 3.3x decoding speedup at 100k tokens.

Conclusion: The proposed training-free compression framework effectively addresses attention sag and semantic degradation from token merging, enabling efficient long-context LLM inference while maintaining generation quality, especially for GQA architectures.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) generate text autoregressively, relying on a key-value (KV) cache whose memory footprint grows linearly with context length, creating a major bottleneck. Recent compression methods mitigate this cost via token merging; however, these approaches often rely on indiscriminate aggregation, which degrades representations and introduces attention sag, a mismatch where merged tokens receive the same softmax mass as individual tokens despite encoding multiple inputs. We propose a training-free, dual-component framework for KV cache compression that addresses these limitations. First, a soft cosine gate adaptively modulates merging decisions based on value-vector similarity, suppressing or discarding dissimilar tokens to preserve semantic fidelity. Second, we introduce an attention-ratio compensation mechanism that applies a decoding-time logit bias derived from prefill attention statistics, correcting the softmax imbalance induced by merging. Evaluated on LongBench (16 English datasets) while retaining only 25% of the KV cache, our framework achieves strong compressed performance against representative one-shot baselines. It is especially robust on the evaluated grouped-query attention (GQA) models, maintaining nearlossless generation quality. Furthermore, the method outperforms the full-cache baseline on complex multi-document QA tasks and delivers a 3.3x decoding speedup at 100k tokens.

</details>


### [89] [RAIL Guard: Closing the Evaluation-to-Remediation Gap in Responsible AI for LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16215)
*Sumit Verma,Pritam Prasun,Pritish Kumar*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RAIL Guard introduces a closed-loop responsible AI pipeline that iteratively evaluates and rewrites LLM outputs across eight dimensions, achieving 96.9% convergence (vs. 49.1% for block-and-retry) and reducing unsafe agent executions by 33% with zero task completion impact.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing guardrail systems are binary classifiers that block unsafe content and force retries from scratch, which is inefficient. The authors aim to create a more effective, closed-loop remediation system.

Method: The paper proposes RAIL Guard, an evaluate-rewrite-reevaluate loop that assesses LLM outputs across eight measurable dimensions and iteratively fixes failing outputs rather than simply blocking them.

Result: Closed-loop remediation achieves 96.9% convergence vs. 49.1% block-and-retry. Feedback-driven self-repair reaches 86.6% convergence with no significant utility loss. Pre-tool-call evaluation reduces unsafe agent executions by 33% (p=0.007) with zero task impact. Structural dimensions (Transparency 93.0%, Accountability 92.8%, Inclusivity 82.5% failure) require architectural solutions.

Conclusion: RAIL Guard outperforms traditional block-and-retry systems for fixable dimensions, but structural dimensions need architectural changes rather than algorithmic remediation. The system is open-source.

Abstract: Existing guardrail systems for large language model agents operate as binary classifiers that block unsafe content, leaving organizations to discard failing outputs and retry from scratch. We introduce RAIL Guard, a closed-loop responsible AI pipeline that evaluates LLM outputs across eight measurable dimensions and iteratively remediates failing outputs through an evaluate-rewrite-reevaluate loop. We evaluate the pipeline across three experiments on four frontier LLMs and 4,276 content outputs plus 6,400 agent tool-call scenarios. Closed-loop remediation achieves 96.9% convergence versus 49.1% for block-and-retry, though the highest-convergence method reduces utility by 22.3%; feedback-driven self-repair achieves 86.6% convergence on fixable dimensions with no significant utility loss (p = 0.177). Pre-tool-call evaluation reduces unsafe agent executions by 33% (p = 0.007) with zero impact on task completion. We identify a key distinction between fixable dimensions that respond to remediation and structural dimensions (Transparency at 93.0%, Accountability at 92.8%, and Inclusivity at 82.5% failure) that require architectural rather than algorithmic solutions. The system is available as open-source SDKs.

</details>


### [90] [Generalist AI Control: Towards Multi-purpose Adaptive Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16313)
*Klinsmann Agyei,Pouria Sarhadi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A single neural network controller trained on 314K demonstrations from 25 diverse dynamical systems can control systems of varying orders and dynamics—stable, unstable, minimum-phase, non-minimum-phase, linear, and nonlinear—without system-specific tuning, achieving performance comparable to dedicated LQI controllers.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional controllers are designed for specific systems and do not transfer across different system orders and dynamics. The authors aim to create a single generalist controller that works across many systems without re-engineering.

Method: A dynamic state-space representation using attention mechanisms with masking, system tags for each system, multi-scale temporal processing, and a mixture-of-experts architecture. Trained in one shot on 314,630 demonstrations from 25 diverse systems.

Result: Achieves comparable performance to system-specific LQI controllers across all tested systems, including non-minimum-phase and unstable dynamics. Generalizes to unseen actuator saturation, noise, disturbance, and reference trajectories.

Conclusion: A significant step towards generalist control policies within a defined family of dynamical systems, demonstrating effective control across SISO systems using a single learned policy without system-specific tuning.

Abstract: Traditional controllers are designed for specific systems and do not transfer across different system orders and dynamics. We present a Generalist Controller, a learning-based controller capable of controlling systems of varying orders and dynamics. The approach introduces a novel dynamic state-space representation using attention mechanisms with masking, enabling a single neural network, trained in one shot, to handle systems with different dimensions without architectural modifications by assigning a system tag to each system. We generated 314,630 demonstrations from 25 diverse systems, including stable, unstable, minimum-phase, and non-minimum-phase dynamics, spanning linear and nonlinear systems from autonomous underwater and aerospace vehicles to mechanical systems and chemical processes. The model learns cross-system control strategies through multi-scale temporal processing and a mixture-of-experts architecture. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed generalist controller achieves comparable performance to system-specific LQI controllers across all tested systems, including challenging cases such as non-minimum-phase and unstable dynamics, whilst generalising to unseen operating conditions including actuator saturation, noise, disturbance, and reference trajectories not encountered during training. This work represents a significant step towards generalist control policies within a defined family of dynamical systems, demonstrating effective control across a range of single-input single-output (SISO) systems of varying order and dynamics using a single learned policy without system-specific tuning.

</details>


### [91] [LaCache: Exact Caching and Precision-Adaptive Inference for Diffusion Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16339)
*Xingru Chen,Zelang Liang,Yongjia Ma,Jiqing Zhan,Shuling Yang,Lian Wen,Kun Zhan*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LaCache is a training-free acceleration framework for Diffusion-based Large Language Models that eliminates operator-level redundancy through lossless caching (EmbedCache, RoPECache, FACache) and per-group FP8 quantization, achieving up to 40.2X speedup with comparable accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current DLLMs suffer from severe operator-level redundancy because they recompute the entire sequence during denoising steps, ignoring that the prefix and masked suffix remain invariant within a block.

Method: LaCache employs (1) Lossless State Memoization (LSM) with three caches (EmbedCache, RoPECache, FACache) to skip redundant computation on unchanged tokens, and (2) per-group FP8 quantization for FFN layers tailored to step-dependent activation distributions.

Result: LaCache alone achieves ~1.3X end-to-end speedup over vanilla DLLM; combined with existing methods, it reaches up to 40.2X speedup while maintaining comparable task accuracy.

Conclusion: LaCache effectively accelerates DLLMs without training or accuracy loss, and can be combined with other acceleration methods for substantial cumulative speedups.

Abstract: Diffusion-based Large Language Models(DLLMs) enable parallel generation via Semi-Autoregressive (SAR) decoding in text generation. However, current methods suffer from severe operator-level redundancy: they recompute the entire sequence during denoising steps, ignoring that the prefix and masked suffix remain invariant within a block. We propose LaCache, a training-free acceleration framework that alleviates this redundancy through lossless caching and mixed precision. Specifically, LaCache employs Lossless State Memoization (LSM) by caching three types of intermediate results: (i) EmbedCache for embedding outputs, (ii) RoPECache for token-wise pre-attention states, and (iii) FACache for the online softmax statistics within FlashAttention. These caches allow the model to skip redundant computation on unchanged tokens without altering the output. To further alleviate memory-bandwidth bottlenecks, LaCache inegrates a per-group FP8 quantization strategy for FFN layers, tailored to step-dependent activation distributions across the diffusion process. Experiments demonstrate that LaCache alone achieves approximately 1.3X end-to-end speedup over vanilla DLLM. When combined with existing acceleration methods, LaCache reaches up to 40.2X end-to-end speedup while maintaining comparable task accuracy.

</details>


### [92] [Interactive Task Alignment as a POMDP](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16412)
*Andy Dai,Zexue He,Zhenyu Zhang,Alex Pentland,Jiaxin Pei*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces task alignment—the ability of language models to infer user intent from ambiguous, underspecified requests. Using a POMDP framework, authors find current models achieve only 22-32% intent recovery vs. 48% for humans, showing models struggle with premature action, ineffective interaction, and resolving ambiguity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real user tasks are often ambiguous, incomplete, or exploratory, yet current benchmarks evaluate only fully specified tasks. This mismatch motivates studying how well models can align with users on intended tasks before execution.

Method: The authors formalize task alignment as a POMDP where the model infers a latent task from partial/evolving user intent. They convert specified tasks into underspecified interactions and validate with a human user study across shopping, coding, and professional settings.

Result: Models recover intended tasks only 22-32% of the time under ambiguity, while humans reach 48%. Post-training (SFT + RL) improves alignment, but models still lag behind humans in resolving uncertainty through interaction.

Conclusion: Current models lack key interaction abilities for reliable agency—they act prematurely, interact ineffectively, and fail to resolve ambiguous requests. Task alignment remains a significant gap between AI and human performance.

Abstract: Current benchmarks for language models primarily evaluate execution on fully specified tasks. However, real user tasks are often ambiguous. Users arrive with incomplete, exploratory, or even inconsistent goals, requiring the assistant to first determine the intended task before carrying it out. We study this problem as task alignment: the ability to align with a user on their intended task. We introduce a general framework for converting specified tasks into underspecified interactions, formalized as a POMDP in which the model must infer a latent task from partial and evolving user intent. We validate our user simulator post hoc with a human user study. Across shopping, coding, and professional work settings, we find that while models often perform well once the task is specified, models still struggle with task alignment: current models act prematurely, interact ineffectively, and fail to resolve ambiguous requests. Models on average recover the user's intended task only 22-32% of the time under ambiguity. In a human study in the same setting, humans reach 48%, outperforming all evaluated models. We show that post-training with supervised fine-tuning and reinforcement learning improves task alignment, but models still lag behind humans in resolving uncertainty through interaction. Together, our results suggest that current models still lack key interaction abilities required for reliable agency.

</details>


### [93] [When to Plan: Learning to Select Between Reactive Control and Deliberative Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16421)
*Adam Labiosa,Josiah P. Hanna*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces an RL method for training a meta-reasoning policy that learns when to switch between fast reactive control and slower deliberative planning, using an uncertainty score from the reactive policy to decide when planning is needed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Humans can switch between fast reactive decisions and slow deliberate planning, but artificial agents lack this ability. The paper aims to learn this meta-reasoning skill to balance speed and generalization in decision-making.

Method: The authors train a meta-reasoning policy using reinforcement learning, where the policy conditions on an uncertainty score from the reactive policy to decide whether to act reactively or engage in decision-time planning.

Result: Empirical studies on motion planning and navigation environments show that the meta-reasoning policy learns to appropriately allocate computation, and can shift toward fully reactive control as the reactive policy improves.

Conclusion: The proposed meta-reasoning framework effectively balances reactive efficiency and planning accuracy, and adapts dynamically as the reactive policy becomes more competent.

Abstract: It has long been recognized that humans have the ability to switch between fast, reactive decision-making and slower, deliberative planning. In this paper, we study the question of how to learn this ability, known as meta-reasoning, in artificial agents. We model reactive decision-making as a policy that directly maps state observations to actions. Such policies can be trained with reinforcement learning (RL) or imitation learning, but may generalize poorly outside of their training distribution. Alternatively, model-based decision-time planning is more likely to produce good actions across a broader set of states but requires additional computation time, which delays acting. In this work, we introduce an RL method for training a meta-reasoning policy that allocates computation by conditioning on a reactive-policy uncertainty score. This score enables it to predict when the reactive policy is likely to perform poorly and when planning is needed. We conduct an empirical study on motion planning and navigation environments, showing that this design enables the meta-reasoning policy to learn when the reactive policy provides a good-enough action versus when decision-time planning is needed. Additionally, we show that our design enables the meta-agent to shift toward fully reactive control as the reactive policy improves.

</details>


### [94] [Berkeley and Heiserman as an Unexhausted Architecture for Embodied Machine Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16465)
*Christopher A. Tucker*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper re-evaluates Edmund C. Berkeley's work, arguing he viewed symbolic logic as a practical design language for intelligent machines with memory, control, and environmental coupling, not just abstract symbol manipulation. It links his program to David L. Heiserman's adaptive creature architectures, suggesting their combined approach remains an under-tested path for embodied robotic cognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To correct an incomplete view of Berkeley as merely connecting symbolic logic to computing machinery, and to recover his broader vision of logic as a design language for intelligent, environment-coupled machines.

Method: Interpretive analysis of Berkeley's "Symbolic Logic and Intelligent Machines" and related projects, supplemented by comparison with Heiserman's machine-intelligence work, to reveal a coherent architectural program linking logical form, circuitry, control, and intelligent behavior.

Result: Berkeley defined intelligent machines in operational terms (inputs, outputs, memory, calculation, control, states, events) and moved beyond static logic toward temporally extended, environment-coupled behavior. Heiserman extended this toward adaptive creature architectures with memory, confidence, and generalization.

Conclusion: Berkeley and Heiserman are not obsolete historical figures but offer a still under-tested architectural approach to embodied robotic cognition worthy of further exploration.

Abstract: Edmund C. Berkeley is usually remembered as a writer who helped connect symbolic logic to computing machinery. That description is correct, but incomplete. Read across Berkeley's machine-oriented writings and projects, the central concern is broader: to show that symbolic logic can serve as a practical design language for machines that acquire information, retain it, respond appropriately to changing conditions, and organize their behavior over time. This paper takes Symbolic Logic and Intelligent Machines as the principal text in that effort, while treating it as representative of a larger Berkeley program that links logical form, circuitry, control, and intelligent behavior. On this reading, Berkeley does not merely treat intelligence as abstract symbol manipulation. He repeatedly defines intelligent machines in operational terms, ties logic to hardware realization, and describes machine behavior through the coordinated interaction of inputs, outputs, memory, calculation, control, states, and events. His treatment of robots and machine activities accordingly moves beyond static logical form toward temporally extended, environment-coupled behavior. David L. Heiserman's machine-intelligence work extends this program toward adaptive creature architectures built around memory, confidence, and generalization. Taken together, Berkeley and Heiserman can be read not as exhausted historical episodes but as contributors to a still under-tested architectural approach to embodied robotic cognition.

</details>


### [95] [SEER: Supervised Learning to Control Energetic Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16523)
*Sascha Van Cauwelaert,Michele Lombardi,Pierre Schaus*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes using Machine Learning to build an oracle that decides when to apply costly propagators like Energetic Reasoning in Constraint Programming, balancing pruning power versus computation time.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Propagation in Constraint Programming is a trade-off: stronger pruning reduces search space but increases computation time. Choosing the right propagator strength per constraint and instance is challenging, and existing static/dynamic approaches have limitations.

Method: The authors propose an ML-based oracle to decide dynamically whether to run complex (expensive) propagators for a given constraint. They specifically investigate building such an oracle for the Energetic Reasoning propagator, studying feature selection and prediction accuracy.

Result: Experiments demonstrate high prediction accuracy for the oracle, provide insights on effective classification features, and identify key challenges for building such dynamic selection mechanisms.

Conclusion: An ML-based oracle for propagator selection is feasible and shows promise; however, important practical issues must be addressed before embedding such oracles into state-of-the-art solvers.

Abstract: One of the main strengths of Constraint Programming is the ability to reduce the search space via propagation. However, propagation is a double-edged sword, with more pruning power coming at the price of larger computation time. For each problem constraint, the best propagator depends on the specific instance and may change at search time. In the literature, Machine Learning (ML) techniques and activity-based heuristics have been applied respectively for choosing (statically) the propagators for a batch of problems and to adapt (dynamically) the propagation strength. We propose to merge those efforts by using an oracle function, obtained via ML, to decide whether to run complex propagators for a target constraint. A combination of design choices makes the approach flexible and easy to embed in state-of-the-art solvers. In this paper, we focus on investigating the feasibility of building an oracle for the Energetic Reasoning propagator. Our experiments show that high prediction accuracy can be obtained, provide suggestions for classification features, and highlight important issues to address when building such an oracle.

</details>


### [96] [Nonuniformity Principle in Human-AI Coworking](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16530)
*An Luo,Jie Ding*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper explores how to optimally schedule human oversight in AI workflows, proposing a nonuniformity principle that oversight stages should be placed with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow, validated in literature review writing and website construction tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the tension between the need for human oversight in multi-step AI workflows and the efficiency demands of AI, specifically investigating how to optimally engage humans in human-AI coworking to improve satisfaction while reducing rework and token consumption.

Method: The authors formulate the problem of optimal placement of oversight stages in human-AI workflows, derive a nonuniformity principle under reasonable assumptions stating oversight gaps should be non-decreasing, and empirically validate this principle in two AI agent workflows (literature reviews and website construction).

Result: The nonuniformity principle is empirically validated: optimal scheduling of human oversight in AI workflows places oversight stages with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow, improving user satisfaction while reducing unnecessary rework and token consumption.

Conclusion: The nonuniformity principle offers a theoretically grounded and empirically validated approach to optimally allocate limited human oversight resources in human-AI collaborative workflows, balancing quality assurance with efficiency.

Abstract: As generative AI is increasingly applied to automate multi-step and high-stake workflows, human judgment and involvement remain essential for ensuring the quality of AI-generated outputs. In practice, while it is desirable for human experts to provide oversight on AI regularly, often by reviewing intermediate outputs, giving feedback, making corrections, and steering subsequent steps, such oversight is constrained by the time and resources that humans can afford. This creates a tension between the need for human oversight and AI's efficiency in delivering more output with less intervention. An important but underexplored question, then, is how to optimally engage humans in human-AI coworking. This work was originally motivated by our empirical observation that in long AI workflows, human oversight often improves user satisfaction while reducing unnecessary rework and token consumption. From there, we formulate the problem of where to place oversight stages in human-AI coworking. Under reasonable assumptions, we then develop the nonuniformity principle, which states that the optimal schedule places oversight stages with non-decreasing gaps along the workflow. We empirically validate this principle in two common AI agent workflows: writing literature reviews and constructing websites.

</details>


### [97] [From Modalities to Propositions: A Language-Centric Framework for Multimodal Intelligence](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16560)
*Nadine Chang,Maying Shen,Shizhe Diao,Jialiang Wang,Jingde Chen,Thomas Breuel,Pavlo Molchanov,Rafid Mahmood,Jose M. Alvarez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper proposes a language representation for multimodal data using atomic propositions, a shared semantic codebook that unifies images, videos, and text into interpretable, composable fact-based representations for reasoning and retrieval.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current multimodal systems lack a unified interpretable representation that can bridge different modalities while enabling compositionality, reasoning, and fine-to-coarse understanding.

Method: A global semantic codebook of canonical atomic propositions (simple statements about entities, actions, relations) is used to represent any observation—image, video, or text—as a bag of these propositions, creating a shared interpretable space.

Result: The framework enables cross-modal understanding and retrieval, compositionality for complex multimodal tasks, rich data curation, and structured retrieval, demonstrated on autonomous driving and open-world data.

Conclusion: Representing multimodal data via atomic propositions provides interpretability, reasoning, and compositionality across modalities, with practical benefits for complex understanding and retrieval tasks.

Abstract: We propose a language representation for multimodal data in which any observation, whether image, video, or text, is expressed as a bag of atomic propositions, simple statements about the entities, actions, and relations in a scene. A global semantic codebook unifies these into a shared vocabulary of canonical atomic propositions, placing every modality and observation into one interpretable space that spans fine grained facts to high level concepts and composes into richer ones. This brings interpretability with reasoning, cross-modal understanding and retrieval, and compositionality that enables complex multimodal understanding, rich data curation and complex structured retrieval. We demonstrate the framework on autonomous driving and open-world data.

</details>


### [98] [Exact Network Surgery: Functional Invariance and Gradient Plasticity in Reactive Computational Graphs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16568)
*Abdallah Khemais*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper formalizes Exact Network Surgery—a technique for inserting residual blocks into a live neural network while preserving the function bit-exactly (under floating-point hypotheses), keeping inserted parameters immediately trainable, and leaving optimizer states untouched. The authors prove theoretical results about gated residual blocks, reactive invalidation engines, and gradient dynamics, and validate all claims with empirical benchmarks showing bit-exact grafting, predictable gradient escape, and constant bookkeeping cost.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing function-preserving network growth methods (e.g., Net2Net, progressive stacking) either tolerate numerical perturbations or require a full rebuild of the training program. The authors aim to achieve exact, in-place network surgery that preserves the network function bit-exactly while enabling immediate training of newly inserted parameters.

Method: The authors formalize Exact Network Surgery, which inserts residual blocks into a live computational graph in-place. They prove three theorems: (1) an identity-morphism theorem for gated residual blocks, (2) a structural-locality theorem ensuring only the downstream cone is recomputed by a reactive invalidation engine, and (3) an escape-from-initialization proposition for the Gradient Shadowing gate alpha. They also identify a degenerate saddle-point configuration. Implementation is in NeuroDSL, a Julia-based reactive graph engine.

Result: Grafting is bit-exact on all 1600 logits tested (0 mismatches). The gate escapes zero at the first optimizer step and unlocks branch gradients at the second. The degenerate configuration exhibits identically zero gradients for 600 steps. Surgery cost correlates with downstream cone size (r=0.9992), while bookkeeping remains constant (~0.75ms). Training resumes bit-identically across process restarts.

Conclusion: Exact Network Surgery enables precise, in-place network growth with proven theoretical guarantees and empirically validated bit-exact behavior, constant overhead, and predictable gradient dynamics, advancing the state of dynamic neural network architecture modification.

Abstract: Function-preserving network growth techniques such as Net2Net and progressive stacking expand a model's capacity without destroying its learned function, but existing formulations either tolerate numerical perturbations or require a full rebuild of the training program. We formalize Exact Network Surgery: the in-place insertion of a residual block into a live computational graph such that (i) the network function is preserved -- bit-exactly under explicit floating-point hypotheses -- and (ii) inserted parameters remain trainable immediately after insertion. We prove an identity-morphism theorem for gated residual blocks, a structural-locality theorem showing that a reactive invalidation engine recomputes exactly the downstream cone of the insertion point, leaving every other node's value and optimizer state untouched, and an escape-from-initialization proposition showing that the Gradient Shadowing gate alpha, initialized at zero over a randomly initialized branch, receives a generically non-zero gradient at insertion time. We identify a degenerate configuration -- zero-initialized output projections combined with a zero gate -- that is an exact saddle point gradient descent cannot escape. Every claim is validated on the reference implementation in NeuroDSL, a reactive graph engine in Julia: grafting is bit-exact on every logit tested (0 mismatches out of 1600); the gate escapes zero at the first optimizer step and unlocks branch gradients at the second, exactly as predicted; the degenerate configuration exhibits gradients identically zero for the entire 600-step run; surgery cost tracks downstream cone size with r = 0.9992 while graft-plus-invalidation bookkeeping is constant (about 0.75 ms) across insertion depths; and training resumes bit-identically across a real process restart. A flagged preliminary appendix reports first single-seed observations on post-insertion gate dynamics.

</details>


### [99] [FST.ai 2.5: Explainable and Uncertainty-Aware AI for Olympic and Para-Taekwondo Decision Support, Athlete Digital Twins, and Federation-Scale Analytics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16597)
*Keivan Shariatmadar,Ahmad Osman,Ramin Rey*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FST·ai 2.5 is a unified, explainable, uncertainty-aware, and secure AI framework for Olympic and Para-Taekwondo, integrating athlete intelligence, competition analytics, digital twins, and federation-wide governance, with prototype deployments demonstrating feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI solutions in elite sport are fragmented, addressing isolated tasks (performance analysis, athlete monitoring, referee support) without a unified ecosystem.

Method: Introduces a unified digital ecosystem with athlete intelligence, competition analytics, federation-scale data management, AI-assisted decision support, athlete/event digital twins, explainable performance indicators, and adaptive training recommendations, governed by transparent, secure, federation-aware protocols.

Result: Prototype deployments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: The methodology is broadly applicable to explainable AI, digital twins, and trustworthy decision support in combat sports and other high-performance sporting environments.

Abstract: The rapid digitalisation of elite sport has created new opportunities for integrating artificial intelligence (AI), performance analytics, and decision-support systems into athlete development and competition management. However, existing solutions remain fragmented, typically addressing isolated tasks such as performance analysis, athlete monitoring, or referee support. This paper presents \textbf{FST$\cdot$ai~2.5}, an explainable, uncertainty-aware, and secure AI framework for Olympic and Para-Taekwondo.
  \textbf{FST$\cdot$ai~2.5} introduces a unified digital ecosystem integrating athlete intelligence, competition analytics, federation-scale data management, AI-assisted decision support, athlete and event digital twins, explainable performance indicators, and adaptive training recommendations. The framework supports World Taekwondo (WT), Member National Associations (MNAs), coaches, referees, analysts, and athletes through transparent, secure, and federation-aware governance.
  By combining multi-source competition data, athlete-performance information, and contextual evidence, \textbf{FST$\cdot$ai~2.5} provides tactical diagnostics, longitudinal athlete monitoring, performance forecasting, personalised development planning, and federation-wide benchmarking using explainable and uncertainty-aware AI. Prototype deployments demonstrate the feasibility of the proposed framework. Although developed for Olympic and Para-Taekwondo, the methodology is broadly applicable to explainable AI, digital twins, and trustworthy decision support in combat sports and other high-performance sporting environments.

</details>


### [100] [Just A Rather Very Intelligent Spoken Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16610)
*Chen Chen,Zhehuai Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces JarvisBench, a benchmark with two tracks (agent-collaboration and user-interaction) for evaluating a Jarvis-style mediator that enables real-time spoken interaction between users and long-horizon AI agents. Preliminary results on WildClaw tasks show mediation improves task performance and user responsiveness, but effectiveness depends heavily on the mediator's LLM capability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current long-horizon AI agents interact with users in a "thin" way—users give an initial instruction, receive only selective updates, and lack clear awareness of agent actions. There is a missing "always-on Jarvis-style mediator" to keep agents continuously reachable and enable real-time spoken interaction, proactive reporting, and user guidance injection.

Method: The authors propose JarvisBench with two tracks: (1) agent-collaboration track measuring whether mediation improves downstream task completion, and (2) user-interaction track measuring whether mediation makes execution more understandable and responsive. They build a modular reference Jarvis prototype and evaluate it on 34 WildClaw tasks executed in OpenClaw with GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini-based, and GPT-based workers.

Result: Preliminary results show Jarvis mediation enables trace-grounded user Q&A and improves task performance when sparse user guidance is injected at appropriate moments. Effectiveness strongly depends on the mediator's LLM capability.

Conclusion: Jarvis-style mediation is a promising "missing middle layer" in the AI agent ecosystem, but broader community effort is needed to realize its full potential.

Abstract: Long-horizon AI agents are becoming increasingly capable, yet their interaction with users remains surprisingly thin. In most workflows, users give an initial instruction, receive only selective textual updates, and lose a clear sense of what the agent is doing or when to step in. This leaves a missing part in the current agent ecosystem: an always-on Jarvis-style mediator that keeps the agent continuously reachable to the user. Such a mediator should support real-time spoken interaction with the user, answer questions without interrupting the worker, proactively report progress or confusion, and inject user guidance back into the agent's execution when useful. In this work, we introduce JarvisBench, a benchmark for measuring the dual value of mediation in long-horizon agent workflows. JarvisBench contains two complementary tracks: an agent-collaboration track that measures whether mediation improves downstream task completion, and a user-interaction track that measures whether mediation makes ongoing execution more understandable, responsive, and accessible to users. We instantiate the benchmark with a modular reference Jarvis prototype and evaluate it on 34 text-only WildClaw tasks executed in OpenClaw. Preliminary results with GPT-5.5, Claude Opus 4.7, Gemini-based, and GPT-based worker agents suggest that Jarvis-style mediation can provide trace-grounded responses to user questions and improve task performance when sparse user guidance is injected at appropriate moments. The results also show that effectiveness depends strongly on the mediator's LLM brain, highlighting both the promise of this missing middle layer and the need for broader community effort. Demo page https://cchen1436.github.io/jarvis

</details>


### [101] [A Research Prototype for Closed-Loop Generative Design of Customized Foot Orthoses via Semantic-Physics Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16631)
*Rui Wang,Byungwon Min,Suxing Liu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TANS-FO is a modular computational pipeline that uses a Text-Aligned Neural Surrogate (cross-attention) and a GNN surrogate to automate foot orthosis design from clinical text, achieving R²=0.94 agreement with FEA, 34.7% peak-pressure reduction, 0.42mm fit error, and preliminary observational comfort improvement (VAS 6.4→2.1, n=12).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the semantic-physical misalignment between unstructured clinical text and 3D geometry of foot orthoses, and to replace manual, expertise-dependent design workflows that lack instant biomechanical validation.

Method: TANS-FO pipeline: (1) Text-Aligned Neural Surrogate with cross-attention maps clinical text to lattice-density fields; (2) GNN surrogate predicts plantar stress in real-time (substituting FEA); (3) anchored on PicoFoot-5K database (5,230 subjects).

Result: GNN surrogate agrees with Abaqus FEA (R²=0.94); full pipeline synthesizes manufacturing-ready insoles in minutes; 34.7% peak-pressure reduction over parametric CAD; 0.42mm fit error; preliminary observational VAS 6.4→2.1 (n=12, no control).

Conclusion: TANS-FO demonstrates feasibility of automated, text-driven FO design with real-time biomechanical surrogate feedback, but the clinical data is explicitly preliminary observational evidence—not proof of clinical efficacy.

Abstract: Translating unstructured clinical prescriptions into patient-specific foot orthoses (FOs) is hindered by a semantic-physical misalignment: high-level clinical intent is not mapped deterministically onto the 3D geometric parameters of the orthosis, and existing design workflows remain dependent on manual expertise with no instantaneous biomechanical validation. We present TANS-FO, a research prototype-a modular pipeline with closed-loop feedback for computational design automation of customized FOs, not a clinically validated therapeutic device. A Text-Aligned Neural Surrogate (TANS) uses cross-attention to project clinical-text embeddings onto a continuous lattice-density field, while a Graph Neural Network (GNN) surrogate predicts plantar stress in real time as a substitute for Finite Element Analysis (FEA). The framework is anchored on the open-access PicoFoot-5K anthropometric database (5,230 subjects; 30+ anatomical parameters). Under standardized quasi-static loading, the GNN surrogate agrees with an Abaqus reference solver (R^2 = 0.94), and the full pipeline synthesizes manufacturing-ready lattice insoles within minutes. On the Male 18-40 cohort, the proposed system attains a surrogate-predicted peak-pressure reduction of 34.7% over parametric CAD, with a fit error of 0.42 mm. Separately, an exploratory feasibility observation (n = 12; 2-week follow-up; no control group) using VAS pain reporting indicates short-term comfort improvement (VAS 6.4 -> 2.1), but this data is explicitly classified as preliminary observational evidence only-not evidence of clinical efficacy.

</details>


### [102] [TopoTuner: Topological Finetuning of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16637)
*Abdulkadir Erol,Yash Mahajan,Vepaul Hariprashad,Baha Rababah,Santu Karmaker,Cuneyt G. Akcora,Mubarak Shah*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: TopoTuner is a framework that selectively freezes attention projection matrices based on topological analysis via Wasserstein distances, achieving competitive performance with full fine-tuning while training only 1-2% of parameters and reducing training time significantly across multiple LLMs and tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Full fine-tuning is computationally expensive as it updates all weights, while LoRA reduces parameters but doesn't identify which pretrained components should actually be trained or frozen. The authors aim to bridge this gap by introducing topology-guided selective freezing.

Method: TopoTuner treats each projection matrix as a row cloud, uses Wasserstein distances between persistence diagrams to measure topological changes during fine-tuning, learns a reusable freezing profile from a source dataset, and transfers it to efficiently fine-tune models on out-of-domain datasets.

Result: Across LLaMA-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, and Qwen3-8B-Base, TopoTuner matches full fine-tuning while training only 1-2% of parameters, outperforms LoRA in 7/9 settings, reduces training time by 20.4% vs full fine-tuning and 5.5% vs LoRA.

Conclusion: TopoTuner opens a new direction for reusable freezing profiles where fine-tuning behavior learned on one dataset can be shared across multiple tasks, demonstrating that task-specific topological drift generalizes across tasks like QA and sentiment analysis.

Abstract: Full fine-tuning remains a strong way to adapt pretrained LLMs, but it updates all weights and can be expensive. LoRA reduces the number of trainable parameters, but it does not directly answer which pretrained components should be trained and which can be frozen during adaptation. We introduce TopoTuner, a topology-guided fine-tuning framework for selective freezing of attention projection matrices. \method treats each projection matrix as a row cloud and uses Wasserstein distances between persistence diagrams to measure how its topology changes during fine-tuning.
  TopoTuner learns a reusable freezing profile from a source dataset and transfers it to efficiently fine-tune models on out-of-domain datasets, evaluating whether task-specific topological drift generalizes across question answering and sentiment analysis tasks.
  Across LLaMA-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B-v0.3, and Qwen3-8B-Base, TopoTuner is competitive with full fine-tuning while training only 1-2\% of the model parameters, and outperforms LoRA in 7 out of 9 model-dataset settings, which can change up to 39.57\% of the projection parameters. Along with minimized updates, TopoTuner reduces training time by 20.4\% relative to full fine-tuning and 5.5\% relative to LoRA on average. TopoTuner opens a new direction for reusable freezing profiles, where fine-tuning behavior learned on one dataset can be shared across multiple tasks.

</details>


### [103] [Diversity-Oriented Fine-Tuning for Uncertainty-Based Hallucination Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16643)
*Qiuyuan Li,Hongliang Dai,Piji Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes diversity-oriented fine-tuning (SFT and DPO) to reduce model certainty on hallucinated outputs, improving semantic-entropy-based hallucination detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing hallucination detection methods operate only at inference time without modifying the model. Many hallucinated outputs go undetected because models produce nearly identical incorrect answers across multiple runs (low semantic entropy for wrong answers).

Method: Two diversity-oriented fine-tuning strategies: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) to encourage varied generations, and (2) Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to reduce model confidence on hallucinated responses. The goal is to make models less likely to produce low semantic entropy responses for incorrect answers.

Result: After fine-tuning, models become less likely to produce low semantic entropy responses for hallucinated answers, improving hallucination detection effectiveness. Results are better than or comparable with state-of-the-art methods.

Conclusion: Fine-tuning for output diversity is an effective way to improve downstream hallucination detection, addressing a key limitation of inference-only detection approaches.

Abstract: Existing hallucination detection methods are typically conducted at the inference stage, without making any modifications to the model itself. In this paper, we are interested in exploring fine-tuning strategies that enhance the detectability of hallucinations in the resulting model. Focusing on semantic-entropy-based detection, we observe that many erroneous outputs remain undetected because the model produces nearly identical incorrect answers across multiple runs. To address this, we propose diversity-oriented fine-tuning to encourage more varied generations. We introduce two specific strategies: one based on Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) and the other on Direct Preference Optimization (DPO). Extensive experiments are conducted to evaluate our approach and analyze the behavior of the models before and after fine-tuning. We find that after adopting our fine-tuning methods, the models become less likely to produce low semantic entropy responses for hallucinated answers, thereby improving the effectiveness of hallucination detection, eventually yielding results better than or comparable with state of the art methods. The code will be publicly released.

</details>


### [104] [DS@GT ARC at eRisk 2026: Hybrid Multi-Agent LLM System with Structured Algorithmic Guidance for Conversational Depression Screening](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16712)
*Victor Gong,David Guecha*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: DS@GT developed a multi-agent pipeline for conversational depression screening that uses a hybrid architecture combining an open-source Gemma 27B interviewer with algorithmic components (dialogue tree, reliability-weighted consensus, cluster-based imputation), achieving 3rd place (ADODL=0.9063) in the eRisk 2026 Task 1 challenge at roughly one-quarter the cost of the paid baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To conduct conversational depression screening without directly asking sensitive mental health questions, and to test whether a weaker open-source model can compete with a stronger proprietary model when augmented with sufficient algorithmic supervision.

Method: Three-stage pipeline: (1) monolithic single-model prototype, (2) baseline multi-agent architecture separating interviewing from BDI-II scoring with orchestration, (3) hybrid configuration replacing GPT-5-nano with Gemma 27B plus a precomputed dialogue tree, reliability-weighted consensus aggregation (Weaver framework), and cluster-based imputation for unprobed symptoms. Three fully automated runs submitted across 20 personas.

Result: Hybrid Run 3 achieved ADODL of 0.9063, ranking 3rd among complete-submission runs and placing DS@GT 2nd overall among 21 teams, outperforming the paid baseline Run 1 (0.8841) at roughly one-quarter of the per-persona API cost.

Conclusion: With sufficient algorithmic supervision, a weaker open-source model can compete with a stronger proprietary model in the conversational interviewer role for depression screening.

Abstract: We describe DS@GT's submission to the eRisk 2026 Task 1 challenge on conversational depression screening, in which systems interview LLM personas that simulate individuals with varying depression profiles and produce a Beck Depression Inventory II (BDI-II) score plus four key symptoms per persona, without directly asking sensitive mental health questions. Our pipeline evolved through three stages: a monolithic single-model prototype to start off, a baseline multi-agent architecture that separates conversational interviewing from BDI-II scoring under a coordinating orchestration layer, and a final hybrid configuration that replaces the paid GPT-5-nano interviewer with the open-source Gemma 27B. To offset the model's weaker reasoning and instruction-following, the hybrid adds three algorithmic components: a precomputed dialogue tree that standardizes interview openers and follow-ups, a reliability-weighted consensus aggregation inspired by the Weaver framework, and a cluster-based imputation step for unprobed symptoms. We submitted three fully automated runs across all 20 personas, with Run 1 from the paid baseline and Runs 2 and 3 from the hybrid. Hybrid Run 3 achieved an ADODL of 0.9063, ranking 3rd among all complete-submission runs and placing DS@GT 2nd among the 21 teams overall, while outperforming our paid baseline Run 1 (0.8841) at roughly one-quarter of the per-persona API cost. These results support our central hypothesis that with sufficient algorithmic supervision, a weaker open-source model can compete with a stronger proprietary model in the conversational interviewer role. Our source code is available at https://github.com/dsgt-arc/erisk-task1-2026.

</details>


### [105] [Tractable Query Answering under Epistemic Confidentiality Policies in DL Ontologies (extended version)](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16715)
*Lorenzo Marconi,Daniela Rieti,Riccardo ROsati*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces a new semantics called Minimal Policy Violation (MPV) for Controlled Query Evaluation with Description Logic ontologies, which provides a sound approximation of existing semantics while ensuring indistinguishability confidentiality and polynomial-time data complexity for DL-LiteR ontologies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CQE semantics (GA- and IGA-entailment) are computationally intractable for DL-LiteR and fail to satisfy the indistinguishability property (specifically IGA), motivating the need for a new approach that balances efficiency and confidentiality.

Method: The authors propose a new CQE semantics based on Minimal Policy Violation (MPV), which approximates prior semantics while guaranteeing indistinguishability. They analyze query entailment (Boolean unions of conjunctive queries) under MPV for DL-LiteR ontologies.

Result: MPV semantics provides a sound approximation of GA- and IGA-entailment, satisfies indistinguishability, and achieves polynomial-time data complexity for DL-LiteR ontologies. The authors also implemented a software framework and evaluated it using an OWL 2 QL benchmark.

Conclusion: The MPV semantics offers a practical trade-off: it preserves confidentiality (indistinguishability) while being computationally tractable (polynomial in data) for DL-LiteR, unlike previous approaches that are intractable or fail to guarantee indistinguishability.

Abstract: We study Controlled Query Evaluation (CQE), a declarative approach to confidentiality-preserving data access, in the context of Description Logic (DL) ontologies, and for confidentiality policies expressed through Epistemic Dependencies (EDs). We first address the problem of answering queries (specifically, Boolean unions of conjunctive queries) under known semantics for CQE (GA- and IGA-entailment). Our results show that if the TBox is expressed in $\text{DL-Lite}_{\mathcal{R}}$, CQE is computationally intractable in general. Moreover, in the presence of EDs, the IGA semantics has recently been proven not to satisfy an important confidentiality preservation property known as indistinguishability. With the goal of defining computationally easier and confidentiality-preserving forms of CQE, we introduce a new semantics for CQE, based on the notion of minimal policy violation (MPV). We show that the new semantics provides a sound approximation of the previous ones, while satisfying the indistinguishability property. We also prove that, in the case of $\text{DL-Lite}_{\mathcal{R}}$ ontologies, query entailment under the MPV semantics can be decided in polynomial time in data complexity. Finally, we present a software implementation of our framework that we used to evaluate the feasibility of this new approach using an existing benchmark for OWL 2 QL.

</details>


### [106] [RECON: Benchmarking Agent Memory for Compositional Reasoning over Long Contexts](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16716)
*Mihir Shriniwas Arya*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RECON is a benchmark (24 case files, 50k-100k tokens each) testing LLM agents on compositional reasoning over long contexts across six memory-intensive tasks. The best non-Oracle system achieves only 22.4% accuracy, highlighting major limitations in current architectures.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory benchmarks only test fact retrieval or change detection, but not the downstream reasoning consequences after changes. RECON fills this gap by evaluating whether agents can trace cascading effects, invalidations, and alternative timelines.

Method: RECON consists of 24 case files across criminal, medical, and financial domains (50k-100k tokens each), testing six memory-intensive tasks: multi-hop evidence chains, cascading invalidations, source conflicts, counterfactual reasoning, temporal constraints, and temporal fact retrieval.

Result: The best non-Oracle system achieves only 22.4% accuracy. Retrieval and reasoning both emerge as significant challenges for current LLM architectures.

Conclusion: Current LLM agents have substantial limitations in compositional reasoning over long contexts, especially in tracing downstream effects of changes and handling complex temporal/counterfactual reasoning. RECON reveals that both retrieval and reasoning capabilities need significant improvement.

Abstract: Large language models and LLM-based agents are widely used as personal chat assistants, enterprise copilots, and autonomous workflow agents. In all these applications, memory (the ability to retain, access, and reason over information accumulated over long contexts and multiple interactions) plays a crucial role in determining the reliability of any agent.
