We introduce RECON (Reasoning over Extended Contexts with Obfuscated Narratives), a benchmark for evaluating compositional reasoning over long contexts. RECON spans 24 case files across three domains (criminal, medical, and financial), each ranging from 50k to 100k tokens, and tests agents on six memory intensive tasks: reconstructing multi-hop evidence chains, propagating cascading invalidations, resolving source conflicts, counterfactual reasoning, satisfying temporal constraints, and temporal fact retrieval. Recent memory benchmarks evaluate whether agents can retrieve scattered facts or detect if a fact has changed whereas RECON evaluates what happens after the change, whether agents can trace which downstream conclusions are affected, which survive through independent support, and how alternative timelines would have unfolded. Our evaluation reveals substantial limitations across current architectures: even the strongest non-Oracle system reaches only 22.4% Accuracy, with retrieval and reasoning each surfacing as challenges.

</details>


### [107] [Constraint-Anchored Reasoning Traces](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16727)
*Zehua Cheng,Wei Dai,Jiahao Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CART is a neuro-symbolic framework that reduces error snowballing in MLLMs by interleaving reasoning with machine-checkable constraint assertions and backtracking upon contradiction detection.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autoregressive MLLMs suffer from error snowballing (65% snowball rate) where a single early mistake corrupts all downstream CoT reasoning, and existing mitigations lack symbolic grounding, catch errors late, or sacrifice flexibility.

Method: CART trains MLLMs to interleave natural language reasoning with symbolic constraint assertions (e.g., count(red_objects)=3). A Constraint Propagation Module combines a neural grounding head with Boolean Constraint Propagation to verify assertions continuously. On contradiction, a backtrack controller reverts to the last consistent checkpoint. A variable-frequency emission mechanism adaptively controls anchor density to avoid bloat.

Result: CART reduces snowball rate from 0.65 to 0.14, improves GQA accuracy by +4.6 pp over training-only baselines, achieves 89.1 F1 on POPE-all with at most 18% inference overhead.

Conclusion: Neuro-symbolic constraint anchoring with backtracking effectively prevents error propagation in MLLMs while maintaining natural language flexibility and low overhead.

Abstract: Autoregressive multimodal large language models (MLLMs) suffer from error snowballing: a single incorrect inference early in a chainof-thought (CoT) trace corrupts all downstream reasoning. We find that in state-of-the-art open-source MLLMs, once the first error occurs, the reasoning cascades into failure across all remaining steps in 65% of such cases (a metric we term the snowball rate). Existing mitigations-sampling multiple chains, post-hoc self-verification, or full program synthesis-either lack symbolic grounding, catch errors too late, or sacrifice the flexibility of natural language reasoning. We propose Constraint-Anchored Reasoning Traces (CART), a neuro-symbolic framework that trains MLLMs to interleave natural language reasoning steps with symbolic constraint assertions: lightweight, machine-checkable statements about visual content (e.g., count(red_objects) = 3). A dual-pronged Constraint Propagation Module-combining a learned neural grounding head with Boolean Constraint Propagation-continuously verifies these anchors against extracted visual features and checks their mutual logical consistency. When a contradiction is detected, a backtrack controller halts generation and reverts to the last consistent checkpoint, preventing error propagation. A variable-frequency emission mechanism allows the model to adaptively control anchor density, avoiding trace bloat. We construct 218K training instances by augmenting GQA, CLEVR-CoGenT, and VCR with ground-truth constraint annotations derived from scene graphs, and fine-tune open-source MLLMs (LLaVA-NeXT, Qwen2-VL) via LoRA. On five benchmarks, CART reduces the snowball rate from 0.65 to 0.14, improves GQA accuracy by +4.6 percentage points over trainingonly baselines, and achieves 89.1 F1 on POPE-all with at most 18% inference overhead.

</details>


### [108] [Supporting Autonomous Process Execution within a Multi-Perspective Constraint Frame via Numeric Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16738)
*Paul Wittlinger,Giacomo Acitelli,Anti Alman,Fabrizio Maria Maggi,Andrea Marrella*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces a tool for what-if analysis in AI-Augmented BPMS that extends framed autonomy with multi-perspective constraints (data-aware and temporal) to recommend optimal, compliant process continuations, with scalability validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing framed autonomy research focuses mainly on control-flow constraints (declarative/procedural) using automata-based representations, lacking multi-perspective constraint support for autonomous decision-making in ABPMS.

Method: The proposed approach enriches process frames with multi-perspective constraints (data-aware, temporal) and exploits them to recommend optimal continuations given partial process executions, while ensuring compliance with process specifications.

Result: Empirical evaluation demonstrates scalability and effectiveness of the technique for autonomous, constraint-aware decision-making.

Conclusion: The tool shows potential for supporting autonomous and constraint-aware decision-making in ABPMS by extending framed autonomy beyond control-flow constraints.

Abstract: AI-Augmented Business Process Management Systems (ABPMS) enhance traditional BPMS by leveraging advanced AI techniques to define, execute, and monitor complex process structures. Within this landscape, Framed Autonomy denotes the capability of a system to autonomously advance the execution of a Business Process (BP) instance while strictly adhering to a predefined frame, i.e., a set of constraints that may span multiple perspectives. Existing research on framed autonomy has predominantly focused on control-flow constraints, either declarative or procedural, and typically relies on their transformation into automata-based representations. In this study, we extend this line of work by introducing a novel tool for what-if analysis that augments the process frame with multi-perspective constraints, including data-aware and temporal conditions. Given a partial process execution, the proposed approach exploits this enriched frame to recommend optimal continuations in compliance with the underlying process specifications. We additionally report an empirical evaluation demonstrating the scalability and effectiveness of the technique, thereby highlighting its potential for supporting autonomous and constraint-aware decision making in ABPMS.

</details>


### [109] [RELIC: Revealed Principles for Learning Interpretable Composable Skills in Multi-Agent Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16745)
*Nguyen Viet Tuan Kiet,Bui Dinh Pham,Duong Quoc Chinh,Dao Van Tung,Tran Cong Dao,Huynh Thi Thanh Binh*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: RELIC enables privacy-preserving multi-agent coordination where agents independently improve skills via private LLM search, with only abstract principles shared through a trusted orchestrator, avoiding executable policy exchange.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multi-agent planning becomes much harder when agents must improve specialized skills privately without sharing internal implementations, which prior work fails to address due to assumptions of centralized optimization or shared policy access.

Method: Each agent refines its own programmatic skill via private LLM-guided search; a trusted orchestrator evaluates updates based only on team performance; successful behaviors are abstracted into portable principles that agents can instantiate within their own interfaces.

Result: Successful behaviors are abstracted into portable principles that enable cross-agent transfer under heterogeneous skill signatures without sharing executable code.

Conclusion: RELIC introduces a new paradigm for privacy-preserving skill learning and coordination in multi-agent planning, separating coordination from implementation sharing.

Abstract: Multi-agent planning becomes substantially harder when agents must improve specialized decision-making skills while keeping their internal implementations private. This regime arises when agents are developed independently, expose different interfaces and capabilities, and must nevertheless coordinate without sharing executable policies. Prior research has largely assumed centralized optimization, shared policy access, or common skill representations, making it poorly suited to privacy-constrained cooperation. We introduce RELIC, a framework for learning interpretable and composable skills via revealed principles. Each agent refines its own programmatic skill through private LLM-guided search, while a trusted orchestrator evaluates proposed updates solely through team-level performance. Successful behaviors are not broadcast as code; instead, they are abstracted into portable principles that other agents can instantiate within their own interfaces and recombine with local strategies. This separates coordination from implementation sharing, enabling cross-agent transfer under heterogeneous skill signatures. RELIC thus introduces a new paradigm for privacy-preserving skill learning and coordination in multi-agent planning.

</details>


### [110] [FUSAR-R1: A Large-Scale Reasoning Model for Intelligent Interpretation of SAR Images](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16819)
*Yi Yang,Xiaokun Zhang,Yuxuan Li,Ruyi Zhang,Xinpeng Zhou,Haipeng Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: FUSAR-R1 is a large-scale reasoning model for SAR image interpretation that combines chain-of-thought reasoning data from human experts with reinforcement learning to enable step-by-step analysis, self-correction, and reliable reasoning, outperforming existing multimodal models on tasks like detection, counting, classification, and land-cover recognition.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing SAR vision-language models lack the step-by-step analysis, logical judgment, and self-correction capabilities of human experts, making reliable intelligent interpretation in complex SAR scenarios difficult due to coherent imaging, speckle noise, and target-background coupling.

Method: The model (1) constructs explicit chain-of-thought reasoning data by simulating human expert interpretation processes for instruction learning, then (2) introduces reinforcement learning to optimize outputs based on inference results, enabling self-correction and more reliable reasoning.

Result: FUSAR-R1 consistently outperforms existing multimodal large-scale models across SAR interpretation tasks, including target detection, target counting and classification, and land-cover category recognition.

Conclusion: Chain-of-thought reasoning combined with reinforcement learning effectively endows SAR vision-language models with human-like reasoning and self-correction capabilities, significantly improving reliability in complex SAR image interpretation.

Abstract: In recent years, large-scale vision-language models have been driving a paradigm shift in intelligent remote sensing image interpretation. By incorporating textual semantic information, the cognitive expression, semantic understanding, and human-computer interaction capabilities of interpretation models have been significantly improved, achieving initial progress in the field of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) image interpretation. However, SAR images are affected by factors such as coherent imaging mechanisms, complex scattering characteristics, speckle noise interference, and target-background coupling, resulting in complex and variable image features with significant uncertainties and specializations. Existing SAR vision-language models do not yet possess the step-by-step analysis, logical judgment, and self-correction capabilities of human experts, making it difficult to support reliable intelligent interpretation in complex scenarios. To address this issue, this paper proposes a large-scale reasoning model, FUSAR-R1, for intelligent interpretation of SAR images. The model first constructs explicit chain-of-thought reasoning data by simulating the interpretation process of human experts and uses this data to guide instruction learning, thereby endowing the model with basic reasoning capabilities. Subsequently, a reinforcement learning strategy is introduced to optimize the model's outputs based on inference results, enabling self-correction and more reliable reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that FUSAR-R1 consistently outperforms existing multimodal large-scale models across various SAR interpretation tasks, including target detection, target counting and classification, and land-cover category recognition.

</details>


### [111] [From Overload to Insights: How AI Agents Can Support Scientists in Analyzing Complex Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16845)
*Tim Fuchs,Luca Gelisio,Steffen Hauf,Walid Maalej*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: Researchers designed and evaluated an agentic AI system to help scientists at European XFEL navigate complex data analysis by retrieving knowledge and generating code, using a design science approach with interviews, evaluations, and user studies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Scientific data analysis at European XFEL is challenging because scientists must combine domain expertise with scattered, facility-specific knowledge across documentation, tools, and support channels.

Method: Used design science research approach: rapid literature review, systematic evaluation of 16 AI tools, interviews, focus group, and user study to develop and evaluate two prototypes.

Result: Identified key knowledge challenges in scientific data analysis, derived requirements for an AI agent supporting knowledge retrieval and source code generation, and proposed design recommendations for specialized, adaptable AI systems.

Conclusion: The findings offer guidance for developing maintainable AI support systems in highly specialized scientific environments.

Abstract: Scientists at European XFEL conduct experiments that generate very large and complex datasets. The subsequent data analysis is challenging as scientists must combine their domain expertise with facility- and software-specific knowledge scattered across documentation, tools, and support channels. To address this problem, we designed and evaluated an agentic AI system tailored to the scientists' needs and integrated with the high-performance computing environment of European XFEL. Using a design science research approach, we conducted a rapid literature review, a systematic evaluation of 16 AI tools, multiple interviews, a focus group, and a user study with experts at European XFEL to develop and evaluate two prototypes. Our study identifies key knowledge challenges in scientific data analysis, derives requirements for an AI agent that supports knowledge retrieval and source code generation, and proposes design recommendations for a specialized system adaptable to the evolving AI tool landscape. These findings provide guidance for developing maintainable AI support in highly specialized scientific environments.

</details>


### [112] [AgentBrew: Lifelong Knowledge Brewing from Strong Teachers to Weak LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16851)
*Yangqin Jiang,Chao Huang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AgentBrew distills a teacher LLM's interactive experience into a persistent external memory for a weaker student agent, requiring no weight updates, expert demonstrations, or test-time teacher access, achieving capable yet deployable agents across coding, math, and tool-use tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying LLM agents typically requires a compact test-time student, even if a stronger teacher is available during training. The goal is to enable knowledge distillation without weight updates, expert demonstrations, ground-truth labels, or test-time teacher access.

Method: AgentBrew has two components: (1) Ralph Loop—a failure-triggered teacher that transforms student failures into environment-validated notes to mitigate sparse binary feedback; (2) student-aware synthesis that calibrates teacher knowledge to the weak executor's operational granularity, yielding model-specific actionable guidance.

Result: Extensive evaluations and comprehensive ablations across coding, math, and tool-use tasks demonstrate that this asymmetric, training-free brewing paradigm produces highly capable yet deployable LLM agents.

Conclusion: The asymmetric, training-free brewing paradigm (AgentBrew) produces highly capable yet deployable LLM agents without requiring weight updates, expert demonstrations, or test-time teacher access.

Abstract: Deploying LLM agents typically requires a compact test-time student, even if a stronger teacher is available during training. We study knowledge brewing: distilling a teacher's interactive experience into a persistent external memory for the student. Crucially, this requires no weight updates, expert demonstrations, ground-truth labels, or test-time teacher access. This setting poses two challenges: environments provide only sparse, binary feedback, and teacher-authored notes must be inherently tailored to be concretely executable by a substantially weaker student. To address these hurdles, we propose AgentBrew, comprising two coupled components. First, a failure-triggered teacher--Ralph Loop mitigates sparse feedback by transforming student failures into environment-validated notes. Second, student-aware synthesis calibrates teacher knowledge to the weak executor's operational granularity, yielding model-specific, actionable guidance. Extensive evaluations and comprehensive ablations across coding, math, and tool-use tasks demonstrate that this asymmetric, training-free brewing paradigm produces highly capable yet deployable LLM agents.

</details>


### [113] [Beyond Semantic Equivalence: Logical Graphs for LLM Uncertainty Quantification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16868)
*Yanni Dong,Minghua Liu,Meiling Zhu,Xiaowei Huang,Lijun Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes Logical Graph Uncertainty (LGU), a framework that improves uncertainty estimation in LLMs by modeling logical relationships (implication and incompatibility) between answers, outperforming semantic entropy by up to +7.1% AUROC.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs produce confidently stated but unreliable outputs; existing uncertainty metrics like semantic entropy overestimate uncertainty by ignoring logical relationships between answers (e.g., answers differing in granularity but being logically compatible).

Method: LGU explicitly models implication and incompatibility among answers using a logical graph, aggregates probability mass along entailment chains, computes entropy over logically maximal hypotheses, and penalizes mutual incompatibility.

Result: Across multiple QA benchmarks, LGU consistently improves uncertainty estimation, outperforming semantic entropy baseline by up to +7.1% AUROC and +3.5% AUARC.

Conclusion: Modeling logical relationships between answers significantly improves uncertainty estimation for LLMs, reducing false hallucination flags caused by diverse yet compatible responses.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often produce confidently stated yet unreliable outputs, posing critical challenges for deployment in safety-sensitive applications. Existing uncertainty metrics such as semantic entropy capture agreement at the level of semantic equivalence, but largely ignore the logical relationships between distinct answers. As a result, they tend to overestimate uncertainty and falsely flag hallucinations in settings where generated responses are diverse in form yet logically compatible (e.g., differing only in granularity or specificity). We propose Logical Graph Uncertainty (LGU), a framework that explicitly models implication and incompatibility among answers. LGU aggregates probability mass along entailment chains, computes entropy over logically maximal hypotheses, and penalizes mutual incompatibility among them. Across multiple question-answering benchmarks, LGU consistently improves uncertainty estimation over existing methods, and outperforms the semantic entropy baseline by up to +7.1% AUROC and +3.5% AUARC across datasets.

</details>


### [114] [Environment-free Synthetic Data Generation for API-Calling Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16900)
*Seanie Lee,Sanjoy Chowdhury,Chao Jiang,Cheng-Yu Hsieh,Ting-Yao Hu,Alexander T Toshev,Oncel Tuzel,Raviteja Vemulapalli*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an environment-free synthetic data generation approach for training API-calling LLM agents, using LLMs as digital world models to simulate API responses and generate high-quality trajectories without needing fully implemented environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current methods for training API-calling LLM agents require fully implemented environments with executable APIs and pre-populated databases, which creates a scalability bottleneck for data collection at scale.

Method: The approach uses LLMs as on-the-fly digital world models to: (1) generate diverse tasks solvable with given APIs, (2) have a teacher agent iteratively solve tasks while an LLM simulator generates coherent synthetic API responses, and (3) use an LLM judge to filter trajectories for quality assurance.

Result: Fine-tuning models on the synthetic data yields significant performance gains on AppWorld and OfficeBench benchmarks, demonstrating that effective supervision can be generated without any executable environment.

Conclusion: LLM-based API simulation is a practical, scalable solution for training agents across diverse API ecosystems, overcoming the bottleneck of requiring fully implemented environments.

Abstract: Training API-calling large language model (LLM) agents demands massive amounts of high-quality trajectories. However, collecting such data at scale typically requires fully implemented environments with executable APIs and realistic, pre-populated backend databases, creating a major bottleneck for scalability. To overcome this, we propose an environment-free synthetic data generation approach that leverages LLMs as on-the-fly digital world models. Given only API specifications, our method generates trajectories mimicking interactions between an agent and a stateful environment. Specifically, an LLM first generates diverse tasks solvable with the provided APIs. A teacher agent then iteratively solves each task while an LLM simulator generates coherent synthetic API responses conditioned on the task context and simulation history. Finally, an LLM judge filters the trajectories to ensure the quality of the resulting dataset. We evaluate our approach on the challenging AppWorld and OfficeBench benchmarks, which include both information-retrieval and state-changing tasks. Fine-tuning models on our synthetic data yields significant performance gains, demonstrating that effective supervision for API-calling agents can be generated without any executable environment. Our results establish LLM-based API simulation as a practical, scalable solution for training agents across diverse API ecosystems.

</details>


### [115] [Lomekwi: Resource-Bounded Tool Discovery in LLM Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16961)
*Roshan Klein-Seetharaman,Daniel Wang,Andrew Xu*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes a cognitive science-inspired framework decomposing tool-use into curiosity, recognition, and efficiency, demonstrating that recognition ability inversely scales with model size—smaller models perform better than larger ones—across both existing benchmarks and novel combinatorial environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard tool-use benchmarks report a single success rate for complex tasks, which fails to capture the nuanced cognitive subprocesses involved. The authors aim to decompose tool-use into finer-grained components inspired by cognitive science.

Method: The authors decompose tool discovery into three components: curiosity (discovering parts), recognition (discovering the process), and efficiency (using the tool post-creation). They apply this framework to Voyager and introduce combinatorial games plus a real-world-emulating environment for analysis.

Result: Recognition ability inversely scales with model size—smaller models outperform larger ones. This inverse scaling is observed in both the combinatorial games and a separate real-world-emulating environment.

Conclusion: The proposed decomposition provides a more nuanced evaluation of tool-use capabilities in LLMs, revealing counterintuitive scaling patterns that standard aggregate success rates would obscure.

Abstract: Existing tool-use benchmarks report a single success rate for complex, multistep tasks. Inspired by ideas from cognitive science, we distinguish tool use from tool discovery and decompose the latter into curiosity (the model's ability to discover the parts needed to build the tool), recognition (the model's ability to discover the process of creating the tool), and efficiency (the model's use of the tool after creation). We show that this framework can be applied to existing discovery tasks, such as Voyager. In addition, we provide evidence that recognition inversely scales with model size, and we introduce and analyze a class of combinatorial games that demonstrates this. We further observe inverse scaling in a separate environment designed to emulate real-world tasks.

</details>


### [116] [Training Continuous Chain of Thought Models: A Tale of Two Regimes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16972)
*Varun Yerram,He He,Eunsol Choi*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: C-MTP is a faster, simpler direct supervision method for continuous Chain-of-Thought that models latent representations as averaged embeddings of compressed traces, outperforming prior direct methods and matching slower indirect methods on simple tasks, but reveals severe limitations (65% performance drop) on complex tasks with longer reasoning traces.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing continuous CoT methods require slow autoregressive generation during training due to indirect supervision of latent representations; the authors aim to develop a simpler, faster direct supervision approach.

Method: C-MTP directly supervises latents by modeling each as an average of embeddings from the CoT traces to be compressed, bypassing the need for autoregressive generation during training.

Result: C-MTP outperforms prior direct supervision methods and matches slower indirect methods on simplified CoT traces (under 100 tokens). However, on complex tasks with longer traces (hundreds of tokens), both direct and indirect methods suffer ~65% performance drop.

Conclusion: Current continuous CoT methods, including C-MTP, have significant limitations on complex reasoning tasks with long traces, revealing a critical gap for future research.

Abstract: Continuous Chain-of-Thought methods replace verbose reasoning traces with a short sequence of dense latent representations. Earlier continuous CoT methods indirectly supervise the latent representations such that its final state match that of verbose reasoning traces, requiring autoregressive, slow generation during training. We introduce C-MTP, a simpler, faster direct supervision approach that models each latent as an average of the embeddings in the CoT traces to be compressed. Our approach outperforms a prior direct supervision method that approximates the distribution of compressed tokens, and performs competitively to slower indirect supervision approaches in existing evaluation setup with simplified CoT traces (less than 100 tokens). Lastly, we extend the evaluation of Continuous CoT methods to complex tasks with longer reasoning traces ($\ge$ few hundreds reasoning tokens). We find both direct and indirect supervision training methods perform poorly (roughly 65\% performance drop) in this setting, revealing the limitations of current continuous CoT methods. The code and checkpoints are released at https://github.com/Varun221/cmtp_research

</details>


### [117] [Expected Free Energy as Belief-Dependent Utility for rho-POMDPs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16981)
*Patrick Cooper,Alvaro Velasquez*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proves that minimizing Expected Free Energy (EFE) in active inference is equivalent to solving a ρ-POMDP with a fixed exploration weight (w=1), removing the need for manual tuning of exploration parameters. The authors demonstrate this equivalence across observe-then-commit and factored observation POMDPs, validating experimentally on environments from Tiger to a 65,000-state Structural Inspection benchmark, where the untuned EFE matches or outperforms reward-only planning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard POMDPs value information only indirectly through reward, while ρ-POMDPs allow direct uncertainty reduction via belief-dependent utility ρ, but both ρ and its weight require manual tuning per task. The authors aim to eliminate this tuning burden by showing that active inference's EFE provides a principled, fixed-weight exploration objective.

Method: The authors prove equivalence between minimizing EFE and solving a ρ-POMDP where ρ is expected information gain and the exploration weight w=1 is fixed due to variational bounds expressing pragmatic and epistemic value in the same units (nats). They extend this from observe-then-commit POMDPs to factored observation POMDPs (covering interleaved observe-act problems like non-destructive testing). Experiments compare untuned EFE against reward-only planning and tuned exploration bonuses across Tiger, RockSample, and a Structural Inspection benchmark.

Result: The untuned EFE (w=1) matches or outperforms reward-only planning at the same horizon, avoids over-exploration of per-task tuned bonuses, and sits near the reward-maximizing knee of the success-reward Pareto frontier across all environments, including the 65,000-state Structural Inspection benchmark.

Conclusion: Expected Free Energy provides a derived (rather than tuned) belief-dependent utility that works out of the box for exploration-exploitation balance, with immediate practical value for applications like fault detection and medical screening where every test has a price and every missed fault has a cost.

Abstract: An agent acting under partial observability must decide when to gather information and which observations are worth their cost. Standard POMDPs value information only through its eventual effect on reward. The $ρ$-POMDP framework instead rewards uncertainty reduction directly, through a belief-dependent utility $ρ$, but in practice both the choice of $ρ$ and the weight placed on it are tuned by hand for every task. We show that active inference removes this tuning entirely. Minimizing Expected Free Energy (EFE) is exactly equivalent to solving a $ρ$-POMDP whose utility is expected information gain, and the exploration weight is fixed at $w=1$ because the variational bound expresses pragmatic and epistemic value in the same units (nats). We prove this equivalence for observe-then-commit POMDPs and extend it to factored observation POMDPs, a broader class that covers interleaved observe-act problems such as non-destructive testing and mobile sensing, where gathering information leaves the hidden state unchanged. Experiments support the theory. Across environments ranging from the classic Tiger problem to RockSample and a new Structural Inspection benchmark with over 65,000 states, the untuned weight matches or outperforms reward-only planning at the same horizon, avoids the over-exploration of bonuses tuned per task, and sits near the reward-maximizing knee of the success-reward Pareto frontier. The practical payoff is an exploration objective that works out of the box. In applications such as fault detection and medical screening, where every test has a price and every missed fault has a cost, EFE supplies a belief-dependent utility that is derived rather than tuned.

</details>


### [118] [PriorProof: A Point-in-Time Measure of Technique Novelty for Formal Proofs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16997)
*Neel Somani*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PriorProof scores proof-route nonstandardness in Lean by measuring surprisal of a proof's dependency footprint against a retrieval-conditioned prior from an earlier Mathlib snapshot, achieving 69.7% agreement with domain experts in a blinded topology study.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Mathematicians have informal intuitions about proofs being "nonstandard" (explain, simplify, use nonstandard routes), but these judgments are difficult to operationalize. The authors aim to formalize a narrower, time-relative notion of proof-route nonstandardness in formal mathematics.

Method: PriorProof extracts the dependency footprint of a Lean proof term and scores weighted surprisal under a retrieval-conditioned, hierarchically smoothed prior built from an earlier Mathlib snapshot. No hand-built ontology or human labels are needed; statement retrieval is learned from proof-derived contrastive pairs.

Result: In a blinded topology study with 76 underlying pairs, PriorProof agrees with majority expert ratings on 69.7% (53/76) overall, 91.7% (11/12) on canonical pairs, and 65.6% (42/64) on stratified pairs. Score-gap quartiles show endpoint-calibration tendency (84.2% in largest-gap bin vs 63.2% in smallest). A language model baseline agrees on 78.9%, but the difference is not statistically significant (p=0.210).

Conclusion: PriorProof is presented not as a replacement for expert or model judgment, but as a decomposable, time-anchored signal whose score gap provides an interpretable reliability indicator for proof-route nonstandardness.

Abstract: Mathematicians distinguish proofs that explain, simplify, or introduce a nonstandard route, but these judgments are difficult to operationalize. We study a deliberately narrower construct: time-relative proof-route nonstandardness in formal mathematics. For a Lean theorem, PriorProof extracts the dependency footprint of its elaborated proof term and scores the weighted surprisal of that footprint under a retrieval-conditioned, hierarchically smoothed prior built only from an earlier quarterly snapshot of Mathlib. The method requires no hand-built technique ontology and no human labels: statement retrieval is learned from proof-derived contrastive pairs, while the scored object is read mechanically from proof terms. In a blinded topology study, 100 presentations collapse to 76 distinct underlying pairs: 12 canonical contrasts shown three times for consistency screening and 64 distinct stratified pairs. Against the majority of three retained domain raters, PriorProof agrees on 53/76 pairs (69.7%, Wilson 95% CI 58.7-78.9%), including 11/12 canonical pairs (91.7%, 64.6-98.5%) and 42/64 stratified pairs (65.6%, 53.4-76.1%). Score-gap quartiles are nonmonotone after repeat collapse; the endpoints are 12/19 (63.2%, 41.0-80.9%) in the smallest-gap bin and 16/19 (84.2%, 62.4-94.5%) in the largest, supporting an endpoint-calibration tendency rather than a resolved staircase. The best language-model condition agrees on 60/76 pairs (78.9%, 68.5-86.6%); on paired outcomes, PriorProof alone is correct on 8 pairs and the model alone on 15 (exact two-sided McNemar p = 0.210), so the difference is not established at this sample size. We therefore present PriorProof not as a replacement for expert or model judgment, but as a decomposable, time-anchored signal whose score gap provides an interpretable reliability indicator.

</details>


### [119] [Reward-Driven LLM Agent Workflows: Synthesizing POMDP Routing and Self-Correction for Autonomous Decision-Making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17038)
*Amez Amanj Ali,Kuo-Kun Tseng*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes an LLM agent architecture integrating POMDP routing, self-correcting reward models, and multimodal inputs to improve long-horizon planning and sparse reward attribution. It achieves 24.5% improvement over ReAct on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address key technical challenges in LLM agent applications: long-horizon planning, sparse reward attribution, and dynamic environmental interaction, which conventional static prompting baselines fail to handle.

Method: Proposes a POMDP-based routing mechanism with an internal self-correcting reward model for evaluating decision trajectories before execution. Integrates multimodal inputs, proximal policy optimization, value function approximation, and long-term structural memory to dynamically adapt reasoning pathways.

Result: 24.5% absolute improvement in task success rate and trajectory efficiency over ReAct on ALFWorld and WebShop benchmarks. Ablation studies confirm the reward-driven critique module significantly reduces hallucination rates.

Conclusion: The architecture bridges reinforcement learning and graph-based memory with autonomous agents, offering a scalable framework for complex multi-step autonomous systems.

Abstract: This paper addresses key technical challenges in current large language model (LLM) agent applications, including long-horizon planning, sparse reward attribution, and dynamic environmental interaction, by designing and optimizing an intelligent agent workflow. The proposed architecture is based on the synthesis of core AI paradigms: Visual, Language, Generative, Graph, Multimodal, Reinforcement, and Agent Intelligence. Unlike conventional baseline models that rely on static prompting and lack robust perception-action loops, our approach introduces a Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) routing mechanism. This mechanism is augmented with an internal, self-correcting reward model that evaluates decision trajectories before execution. By integrating multimodal inputs and advanced reinforcement learning principles (such as proximal policy optimization and value function approximation), the agent maintains long-term structural memory and dynamically adapts its reasoning pathways to mitigate error accumulation. Empirical experiments on the ALFWorld embodied simulation environment and the WebShop online navigation benchmark demonstrate a 24.5% absolute improvement in task success rate and trajectory efficiency over mainstream baselines like the standard ReAct framework. Comprehensive ablation studies confirm the significant contribution of the reward-driven critique module in suppressing hallucination rates. This research bridges theoretical foundations of reinforcement learning and graph-based memory with autonomous agent workflows. Ultimately, the resulting architecture offers a practical, scalable reference framework for developing artificial intelligence technologies in complex, multi-step autonomous systems. Code is available at https://github.com/01Amez/RLAW_Implementation.

</details>


### [120] [When LLMs Over-Answer: Measuring and Mitigating Quality Issues in LLM-Based Hardware Description Language Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17063)
*Ziteng Hu,Jiachi Chen,Wenhao Lv,Huan Zhang,Yingjie Xia*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper investigates LLM-generated answers for hardware description language (HDL) questions, finding pervasive over-answering (65.7% redundant alternatives, 69.1% verbose padding) and proposing a multi-agent framework that improves answer quality significantly (+0.96 on core answers, +0.51 on non-core content).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While LLMs are increasingly used for HDL question answering, the quality of their responses compared to human experts remains unclear. Since HDL is synthesized into physical hardware, imprecise or redundant answers can cause late-stage timing violations or non-synthesizable logic, making quality assessment particularly important.

Method: The authors collect 6,246 HDL Q&A posts from Stack Overflow with accepted answers, organize them into a taxonomy (4 main categories, 10 subcategories), conduct a user study with 19 HDL engineers, and propose a multi-agent framework for improving LLM-based HDL QA evaluated via LLM-as-Judge and two structural metrics (core answer count and non-core content length).

Result: LLMs exhibit over-answering: 65.7% contain redundant alternatives, 69.1% verbose padding, 49.0% fail to fully align with expert answers, yet 58.3% participants preferred LLM responses for readability. The proposed multi-agent framework improves core-answer quality from 3.71 to 4.67 and non-core content quality from 3.72 to 4.23 on a 5-point scale across four LLMs.

Conclusion: LLM-generated HDL answers have a pervasive over-answering problem where correct content is buried under redundancy and verbosity. The proposed multi-agent framework effectively mitigates these issues and improves answer quality across multiple mainstream LLMs.

Abstract: The rapid advancement of large language models (LLMs) has led practitioners to increasingly rely on them for answering questions about hardware description languages (HDLs). Because HDL is ultimately synthesized into physical hardware, an imprecise or redundant answer can propagate into timing violations or non-synthesizable logic that surface only late in the design flow, making the quality of HDL answers especially consequential. However, the quality of LLM-generated responses, particularly in comparison with answers provided by human experts, remains unclear. To investigate this question, we collect 6,246 HDL Q&A posts with accepted answers from Stack Overflow and curate them into a dataset, organized into a taxonomy of four main categories (Conceptual, Debugging, Generation, and Optimization) and ten subcategories. Using this dataset, we design a user study conducted with 19 HDL engineers with one to three years of experience. Our findings reveal a pervasive over answering tendency: LLMs supply correct content but bury it under redundant alternatives (65.7%) and verbose padding (69.1%), while nearly half of answers (49.0%) fail to fully align with expert answers yet participants still preferred LLM responses for readability (58.3%). Motivated by these findings, we propose a multi-agent framework for improving LLM-based HDL question answering. We evaluate answer quality using an LLM-as-Judge and two structural metrics: the number of core answers, which reflects redundancy since LLMs often provide multiple alternative solutions, and the length of non-core content, which reflects verbosity. Evaluated on the four mainstream LLMs, our framework increases the average core-answer quality score from 3.71 to 4.67 (+0.96) and the non-core content quality from 3.72 to 4.23 (+0.51), on a five-point scale.

</details>


### [121] [Bridging the Information Gap: Semantic Densification and Hindsight Distillation for Cold-Start Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17070)
*Hao Duong Le,Yifei Gao,Huan Li,Lun Jiang,Chen Bai,Ke Xing,Chen Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: SemRaD is a distillation framework that uses structured semantic profiles and hindsight-aware knowledge transfer to solve the new-user cold-start problem in e-commerce, achieving significant LTV and CVR improvements over production baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: New-user cold-start is a critical bottleneck for e-commerce platforms due to sparse interaction history. Prior approaches (LLM augmentation and LUPI) have limitations: unstructured/noisy rationales from LLMs, and brittle student-teacher distillation due to heterogeneous information gaps.

Method: SemRaD has two components: (1) Structured Semantic Reasoning Pipeline with discover-curate-audit workflow producing Densified Semantic Profiles and Hindsight Distillation Targets; (2) Hindsight-Aware Distillation Network with Distillation Experts to handle heterogeneous information gaps.

Result: +1.9% LTV (Gini) and +1.0% CVR (AUROC) over production base on large-scale industrial dataset; online A/B test confirms +1.0% LTV / +0.43% CVR; matches production LTV using only 9% training data while improving CVR by 0.8%.

Conclusion: SemRaD effectively addresses the limitations of prior cold-start approaches by introducing structured semantic reasoning and hindsight-aware distillation, demonstrating significant and practical gains in both offline and online settings.

Abstract: New-user cold-start is a critical bottleneck for e-commerce platforms: predicting user lifetime value (LTV) and conversion rate (CVR) for users with sparse interaction history. Two prior directions -- LLM-based semantic augmentation and learning using privileged information (LUPI) -- each face a key limitation. First, LLM augmentation produces unstructured rationales that are noisy and hard to operationalize in production. Second, naive student-teacher distillation can be brittle due to an information gap between the privileged teacher and the sparse student; moreover, this gap is heterogeneous across users. We propose SemRaD, a Semantic Reasoning-aware Distillation framework addressing both limitations. First, a Structured Semantic Reasoning Pipeline replaces free-form rationales with a structured schema built via a discover-curate-audit workflow, producing per user a Densified Semantic Profile (consumed by the deployed student via a Semantic-Gated Encoder that focuses on the most informative dimensions) and a Hindsight Distillation Target reconciled from pre- and post-conversion reasoning (used only at training). Second, to bridge this gap and handle its heterogeneity, a Hindsight-Aware Distillation Network transfers privileged knowledge via the hindsight target, with Distillation Experts improving transfer under per-user variability. On a large-scale industrial dataset, SemRaD lifts +1.9% LTV (Gini) and +1.0% CVR (AUROC) over a production-grade base; a four-week online A/B at Keeta confirms +1.0% LTV / +0.43% CVR. SemRaD also matches the production system's LTV using only 9% of the training data while improving CVR by 0.8%.

</details>


### [122] [Otap:Structure-Aware Optimal Transport for Evaluating Planning and Execution in Agent Trajectories](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17082)
*Babak Barazandeh,Subhabrata Majumdar,George Michailidis*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper introduces OTAP (Optimal Transport for Agentic Planning), a new evaluation metric for LLM agent trajectories that uses optimal transport over dependency graphs to measure distances between agent execution graphs and valid solution graphs, providing a pseudo-metric invariant to dependency-preserving reorderings and robust to redundant, missing, or hallucinated steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current evaluation metrics for LLM agent trajectories (binary success flags or exact matching) are insufficient: success flags cannot distinguish luck from sound reasoning or explain failures, while exact matching penalizes valid but reordered or differently decomposed plans.

Method: The paper reframes trajectory evaluation as an unbalanced fused Gromov-Wasserstein optimal transport problem over attributed dependency graphs, which computes a distance between the agent's execution graph and a set of valid solution graphs.

Result: OTAP separates valid from invalid trajectories where semantics-only metrics score below chance, achieves highest accuracy with exact dependency graph recovery, and maintains robustness even with heuristic graph inference from free-text traces.

Conclusion: OTAP is a provably effective pseudo-metric for agentic planning evaluation that handles dependency-preserving reorderings, redundant steps, and varying plan granularity while providing interpretable failure analysis.

Abstract: Large language model agents solve tasks by generating trajectories that interleave planning, tool calls, and intermediate results. Current evaluation metrics reduce such a trajectory to a binary success flag or compare it against a reference by exact matching. A success flag cannot distinguish a sound solution from one that succeeds by luck, and says nothing about why a failed run went wrong. Exact matching penalizes plans that are valid but reordered or decomposed differently from the reference. We reframe trajectory evaluation as a distance between the agent's execution graph and a set of valid solution graphs, and instantiate it via an unbalanced fused Gromov-Wasserstein transport problem over attributed dependency graphs. The resulting score, termed \otap{} (Optimal Transport for Agentic Planning), is a pseudo-metric that is provably invariant to dependency-preserving reorderings and has bounded sensitivity to redundant steps. Its unbalanced marginals handle missing or hallucinated steps without forcing a match, and its soft coupling accommodates variation in plan granularity. On controlled perturbations and three public benchmarks, \otap{} separates valid from invalid trajectories in a regime where semantics-only metrics score below chance. Its accuracy is highest when the dependency graph is recovered exactly, and drops only when the graph is inferred heuristically from free-text traces.

</details>


### [123] [Fourier Geometric Wind Power Forecasting with Numerical Weather Prediction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17095)
*Shiyuan Piao,Fan Zehui,Yang Liu,Hong Cheng,Juepeng Zheng,Jie Zhou,Fugee Tsung*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: A multimodal framework integrating SCADA data with NWP forecasts, using geometric encoders and Fourier Neural Operators for rotation-invariant, long-range spatiotemporal modeling, achieving SOTA on three real-world wind farms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing methods fail to effectively combine weather forecasting data with wind turbine SCADA data due to heterogeneous inputs and complex physical wind-turbine interactions, leading to suboptimal wind power forecasting.

Method: (1) Decomposes inputs into scalar and vector features; (2) Geometric encoder extracts rotation-invariant features from wind vectors; (3) Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) performs global convolutions in frequency domain for long-range spatiotemporal relationships.

Result: Outperforms state-of-the-art baselines consistently across three real-world wind farms with weather forecasting data.

Conclusion: The physically-informed design of combining geometric encoding with FNO effectively captures heterogeneous wind turbine-weather interactions, setting a new SOTA for short-term wind power forecasting.

Abstract: Accurate short-term wind power forecasting is essential for grid stability and operational planning, yet remains challenging due to the complex interactions between atmospheric conditions and turbine dynamics. However, existing methods fail to effectively incorporate weather forecasting with wind turbine data (i.e., SCADA), leading to suboptimal solutions. To address this, we introduce a multimodal framework that integrates historical point-based SCADA data with grid-based Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) forecasts, which is challenging due to heterogeneous input and the complex physical wind-turbine interactions. Our approach first explicitly decomposes inputs into scalar and vector features to better capture both site-specific and geometric dependencies and then incorporates a geometric encoder to extract rotation-invariant features from wind vectors. We further leverages a Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) architecture, which performs global convolutions in the frequency domain to efficiently model long-range spatiotemporal relationships. Extensive experiments on three real-world wind farms, with weather forecasting data, demonstrate that our model consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, highlighting the effectiveness of its physically-informed design. The core implementation of our method is publicly available at: https://github.com/shawn-sypiao/GWPF.

</details>


### [124] [Evidence Interfaces Shape How Retrieval-Augmented Readers Use Support](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17108)
*Junchi Liao,Jiawen Deng,Fuji Ren*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces "evidence interface" as a reader-facing form of retrieved evidence in multi-hop RAG, showing that top-k answer scores conflate two distinct failures: missing support chains vs. poor reader adaptation of available evidence. Through support-annotated benchmarks, they demonstrate that reporting complete-support coverage alongside answer scores makes top-k evaluations interpretable.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard top-k answer scores in multi-hop RAG are opaque—they cannot distinguish whether performance loss stems from incomplete retrieval (missing support chain) or from the reader failing to leverage available evidence (evidence interface issue).

Method: Using three support-annotated multi-hop QA benchmarks, authors compare matched adapted readers trained with raw context, retrieval windows, and gold-support diagnostic renderings. They perform support-removal checks and use a support-supervised ranker to isolate support-availability effects from reader-interface effects.

Result: When complete support chains survive in top-k windows, short ranked windows match or improve over raw context. Gold support-first consistently improves readers. Support-supervised ranker on 2Wiki and MuSiQue raises coverage and recovers raw-context quality at lower prompt cost. Support-removal checks confirm gains rely on exposed evidence, not answer priors.

Conclusion: Top-k answer scores should be reported together with complete-support coverage in support-annotated evaluations to make multi-hop RAG performance truly interpretable.

Abstract: In multi-hop RAG evaluation, a top-k answer score can hide two different failures: the retrieval window may drop part of the support chain, or it may contain support in a form the adapted reader does not use well. We call this reader-facing form of retrieved evidence an evidence interface. Using three support-annotated multi-hop QA benchmarks, we compare matched adapted readers trained with raw context, retrieval windows, and gold-support diagnostic renderings. These comparisons distinguish support-availability failures from remaining reader-interface effects. Top-k windows become interpretable only after checking whether the complete annotated support chain survives: when it does, short ranked windows can match or improve over raw context; when it does not, missing support explains much of the loss. Gold support-first improves matched readers; on 2Wiki and MuSiQue, a support-supervised ranker raises coverage and recovers raw-context quality at lower prompt cost, while retaining gold headroom. Support-removal checks further show that the gains rely on exposed evidence, not only answer priors. On support-annotated evaluations, top-k answer scores should therefore be reported together with complete-support coverage.

</details>


### [125] [A Diagnostic Framework for AI Agent Behavior](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17149)
*Xichen Zhang,Yingjie Zhang,Tianshu Sun*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: The paper introduces "layer attribution," a diagnostic framework for AI agent behavior that distinguishes between the computational layer (what behaviors are possible) and the behavioral modulation layer (how capacities are expressed through roles, objectives, and governance), arguing that source-level diagnosis must precede explanation, validation, or governance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: AI agents increasingly operate within systems studied by behavioral scientists, yet the same behavioral patterns can arise from different sources (e.g., representational substrate vs. institutional rules). Current evaluation methods lack source-level diagnosis.

Method: Proposes a two-layer diagnostic framework: (1) the computational layer (architecture, memory, perception, attention, representation), and (2) the behavioral modulation layer (identity, resources, objectives, social interaction, institutional constraints, governance).

Result: Three consequences identified: surrogate validity is a model-task-layer relation; human-AI divergence provides diagnostic evidence; governance requires source attribution before intervention.

Conclusion: Treating AI agents as behavioral actors requires evaluation methods that determine where behavior originates before deciding how to explain, validate, or govern it.

Abstract: AI agents increasingly act within the same clinical, political, scientific, and social systems that behavioral scientists study. Evaluating these systems requires source-level diagnosis: the same behavioral pattern may arise from an agent representational substrate or from the roles, objectives, interaction structures, and governance rules that shape its expression. This Perspective proposes a diagnostic framework for AI agent behavior: layer attribution. The foundational computational layer defines what behaviors are possible through architecture, memory, perception, attention, and representation. The behavioral modulation layer shapes how those capacities are expressed through identity, resources, objectives, social interaction, institutional constraints, and governance. The framework clarifies three consequences: surrogate validity is a model-task-layer relation, human-AI divergence provides diagnostic evidence, and governance requires source attribution before intervention. Treating AI agents as behavioral actors therefore requires evaluation methods that determine where behavior originates before deciding how to explain, validate, or govern it.

</details>


### [126] [Is Your Model Thinking or Just Stagnating? PUMA: Diagnosing Reasoning Pathology via Phase-Momentum Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17188)
*Cheng Yan,Guangyang Ye,Wuyang Zhang,Fan Xu,Zhijun Fan,Xiang Xia,Yanyong Zhang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: PUMA is a training-free framework that improves test-time efficiency in Large Reasoning Models by distinguishing active exploration from passive stagnation via phase-momentum alignment, outperforming SOTA baselines across diverse benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the "overthinking" paradox in LRMs, where excessive CoT reasoning increases computational cost without guaranteed accuracy gain. Existing methods either suffer from deceptive convergence (information-theoretic) or lack real-time sensitivity (latent representation analyses).

Method: Proposes PUMA, a training-free framework with: (1) Phase-Momentum Alignment Hypothesis linking reasoning correctness to temporal synchronization of geometric momentum and uncertainty resolution; (2) Cognitive-Energy Model with two dimensions - Geometric Cognitive Effort (latent velocity, tortuosity) and Entropic Cognitive Uncertainty; (3) Tiered diagnostic architecture with lightweight phase monitoring + event-triggered geometric analysis for adaptive truncation or corrective interventions.

Result: PUMA consistently outperforms SOTA baselines across diverse benchmarks on LRMs ranging from 1.5B to 32B, achieving superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off and robust cross-domain generalization.

Conclusion: By effectively distinguishing active exploration from passive stagnation, PUMA enables precise, adaptive interventions that optimize test-time efficiency without sacrificing reasoning quality.

Abstract: Test-time scaling empowers Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) to tackle complex tasks via extensive Chain-of-Thought (CoT). However, this often induces the "overthinking" paradox, where redundant reasoning increases computational overhead without guaranteeing accuracy. Existing test-time efficiency optimization methods primarily fall into two categories: information-theoretic approaches, which are prone to "deceptive convergence" where low uncertainty masks hallucinations, and latent representation analyses, which are often post-hoc, lacking the real-time sensitivity for dynamic reasoning. To bridge this gap, we first posit the Phase-Momentum Alignment Hypothesis, asserting that reasoning correctness hinges on the temporal synchronization between geometric momentum and uncertainty resolution. We then theoretically formulate the Cognitive-Energy Model to characterize these dynamics through two orthogonal dimensions: Geometric Cognitive Effort, quantified by latent velocity and tortuosity, and Entropic Cognitive Uncertainty. To operationalize this, we introduce PUMA (Phase-Uncertainty Momentum Alignment), a training-free framework employing a tiered diagnostic architecture. By coupling lightweight phase monitoring with event-triggered geometric analysis, PUMA effectively distinguishes active exploration from passive stagnation, enabling precise interventions through adaptive truncation or corrective measures. Extensive experiments on LRMs spanning 1.5B to 32B demonstrate that PUMA consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks, achieving a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off and robust cross-domain generalization.

</details>


### [127] [Toward Anthropomorphic Dialogue: A Closed-Loop Framework for Human-Like Chat Generation, Evaluation, and Preference Alignment](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17191)
*Wentao Liu,Siyu Song,Xi Chen,Youjia Li,Xiaokun Wang,Min Ji,Ji Wang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: AnthroDial is a closed-loop framework for anthropomorphic dialogue that combines a role-conditioned runtime, executable benchmark with multi-dimensional evaluation, and post-training pipeline (SFT + GRPO with ZPD-aware Kalman-filtered rewards). Experiments on 55 personas/50 scenarios show Qwen3.6-27B-SFT+RL achieves 39% strict ACC and 98.5 overall score, significantly outperforming baselines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Human-like private chat requires preserving persona, relationship, memory, bounded knowledge, medium-specific timing, and coherent multi-turn arcs—capabilities beyond fluent response generation that existing systems lack.

Method: AnthroDial combines (1) role-conditioned scheduled dialogue runtime with persona cards, long-term memory, virtual time; (2) executable benchmark with L0 gate, 5 per-turn + 5 dialogue-level dimensions; (3) post-training pipeline using 16,436 filtered SFT examples + GRPO with cognitive-diagnostic, Kalman-filtered ZPD-aware reward that upweights weak behavioral dimensions.

Result: Strongest baseline (non-trained) reaches 32% strict ACC; Qwen3.6-27B-SFT+RL achieves 39% strict ACC and 98.5 overall score. In 9B no-think setting, SFT and RL improve strict ACC from 0% to 13% and 18.37%.

Conclusion: Anthropomorphic dialogue benefits when generation, evaluation, and reward shaping share the same behavioral dimensions, validating the closed-loop framework design.

Abstract: Human-like private chat requires more than fluent response generation: a system must preserve persona, relationship, memory, bounded knowledge, medium-specific timing, and a coherent multi-turn arc. We present AnthroDial, a closed-loop framework that formulates anthropomorphic dialogue as a joint problem of system architecture, executable evaluation, and diagnostic alignment. It combines (1) a role-conditioned scheduled dialogue runtime with persona and scenario cards, long-term memory, virtual time, and single-draft message decisions; (2) an executable benchmark with an L0 validity gate, five per-turn dimensions, and five dialogue-level dimensions; and (3) a post-training pipeline that filters 16,436 scheduled-decision examples for SFT and applies GRPO with a cognitive-diagnostic, ZPD-aware reward. The reward maintains Kalman-filtered capability estimates for each behavioral dimension, upweights dimensions with larger capability deficits, and uses rollout scores as task-level ZPD matches to focus optimization on learnable weak skills. On a benchmark with 55 personas, 50 scenarios, 50 persona-scenario bindings, and 100 role-conditioned cases per model, we evaluate 16 systems spanning frontier baselines, open models, thinking/no-think variants, and SFT/RL ablations. The strongest non-trained baseline reaches 32.00% strict ACC, while Qwen3.6-27B-SFT+RL reaches 39.00% strict ACC and a 98.5 overall score. In the 9B no-think setting, SFT and RL improve strict ACC from 0.00% to 13.00% and 18.37%. These results show that anthropomorphic dialogue benefits when generation, evaluation, and reward shaping share the same behavioral dimensions.

</details>


### [128] [A Systematic Evaluation of Trajectory Data Curation for LoRA Fine-Tuning of Code Agents](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17205)
*Yunze Han*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper systematically studies how trajectory quality and quantity jointly affect supervised fine-tuning of code agents, proposing a two-axis (Efficiency & Style) quality scoring framework and revealing a scale-dependent quality-quantity trade-off where quality filtering becomes beneficial only at larger dataset scales (≥2,000 trajectories).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The central yet underexplored question is how trajectory quality and quantity jointly shape model performance in supervised fine-tuning of code agents. The authors aim to fill this gap through systematic empirical study.

Method: The authors conduct 16 controlled experiments using LoRA fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct on the SWE-trajectory dataset (67,074 trajectories). They propose a two-axis quality scoring framework (Efficiency and Style) and evaluate at different scales (500, 1,000, 2,000 trajectories) using cross-entropy loss and ROUGE-L as metrics.

Result: Key findings: (1) Scale-dependent quality-quantity trade-off — at 500-1,000 samples, doubling dataset reduces CE loss by ~12.7% but TopQ-Random gap is <1% (insignificant); at 2,000 trajectories, the gap widens to 3.6% (significant). (2) Error-retry rate is the dominant sub-dimension, performing comparably to the full composite (Δ < 0.2%). (3) CE loss and ROUGE-L are perfectly rank-correlated (Spearman ρ = -1.00).

Conclusion: Trajectory-level quality scoring is a viable but scale-sensitive lever for code-agent SFT. The study offers a proxy-validated evaluation protocol (using CE loss) for the regime where end-to-end SWE-bench resolve rates are statistically infeasible for 7B-scale models.

Abstract: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) of open-weight LLMs on expert agent trajectories has emerged as a prominent approach to building capable code agents without reliance on proprietary models. A central yet underexplored question is how trajectory quality and quantity jointly shape model performance. We present a systematic empirical study of trajectory data filtering for LoRA fine-tuning of Qwen2.5-Coder-7B-Instruct on the SWE-trajectory dataset (67,074 trajectories, of which 32,161 are resolved). We propose a two-axis quality scoring framework -- Efficiency and Style -- and evaluate it through 16 controlled experiments spanning strategy, scale, and ablation analyses. Since 7B-scale models attain near-zero SWE-bench resolve rates, we adopt cross-entropy (CE) loss on held-out trajectories as the primary metric, validated via first-action generation: CE loss and ROUGE-L are perfectly rank-correlated (Spearman $ρ$ = -1.00), with limited-sample evidence supporting but not conclusively establishing this proxy. Our results reveal a scale-dependent quality-quantity trade-off: at small scales, doubling the dataset (500 to 1,000) yields ~12.7% CE-loss reduction whereas the TopQ-Random gap stays <1% (Mann-Whitney p > 0.10); at 2,000 trajectories this same gap widens to 3.6% (p = 0.016). Ablation further identifies error-retry rate as the dominant sub-dimension, performing comparably to the full composite ($Δ$ < 0.2%). Together, these findings establish trajectory-level quality scoring as a viable but scale-sensitive lever for code-agent SFT and offer a proxy-validated evaluation protocol for the regime where end-to-end resolve rate is statistically infeasible.

</details>


### [129] [Constrained Path Reasoning: Measuring When Committed Stages Earn Their Cost](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17240)
*Honglin Li*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CPR pairs path hypotheses with stage-level accounting, showing that task-compatible commitments improve reasoning efficiency when gains exceed error/cost, demonstrated across QCQPs and degenerate polynomial instances with measurable yield improvements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine when committed intermediate stages in LLM reasoning pipelines are worth their cost, introducing a framework (CPR) that balances constraint strength against error propagation and execution cost.

Method: Constrained Path Reasoning (CPR) with source-aware path hypotheses, stage-level accounting, soft/hard constraint separation, discrete/continuous commitment handling, and empirical evaluation on 1,180 QCQPs and 40 degenerate polynomial instances with 2,140 endpoints.

Result: Residual triage recovers 63.0% of repair-all's yield with 17.7% attempts; usable yields: 41.1% direct, 90.0% after formalization+deterministic execution, 20.0% after one-shot convexification, 21.1% full path; two-action rollback reaches 90% vs 36.7% for feedback-conditioned selector.

Conclusion: Model-generated states provide hypotheses while trusted execution earns constraint strength; CPR's framework effectively measures and demonstrates when intermediate commitments are beneficial in LLM reasoning.

Abstract: When does a committed intermediate stage in an LLM reasoning pipeline earn its cost? Constrained Path Reasoning (CPR) pairs a source-aware path hypothesis with stage-level accounting. Search generates provisional states; trusted or validated invariants can constrain hard, while other proposals remain soft and revisable. CPR predicts that task-compatible commitments can factor transitions, concentrate candidate mass, induce regularity, and expose feedback when their gains exceed propagated error and execution cost. The formalism covers discrete commitments and continuous flows and measures effective branching, endpoint concentration, and cost per usable output. Across 1,180 generated QCQPs and 40 engineered degenerate polynomial instances (2,140 endpoints), residual triage recovers 63.0% of repair-all's additional feasible yield with 17.7% of its attempts. Fixed-LLM accounting (270 unique calls shared across nested arms) finds usable yield of 41.1% direct, 90.0% after formalization and deterministic execution, 20.0% after one-shot convexification, and 21.1% for the full path. In 120 paired-condition calls, a two-action rollback rule reaches 90% usable yield versus 36.7% for the feedback-conditioned selector. Two endpoint probes separate source from validation: a 72-output cross-trajectory transplant reduces entropy and acceptable mass; a 24-output same-call self-proposal pilot gives unchanged two-repeat collision entropy, 25.0% versus 8.3% usable yield, and 1/8 deterministically confirmed endpoint checks. Model-generated states supply hypotheses; trusted execution earns constraint strength.

</details>


### [130] [LenGuard-GPC: Length Guarding with Guided-Prompt Consistency for Spatial Reasoning Reinforce Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17243)
*Xingjian Tao,Yiwei Wang,Yujun Cai,Jing Tang*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: LenGuard-GPC improves multi-view spatial reasoning in VLMs by using KL divergence-based dense rewards and staged length bonuses, outperforming GRPO with shorter responses.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard GRPO uses sparse outcome-level rewards that fail to pinpoint where reasoning goes wrong or control response length, leading to verbose yet inaccurate chain-of-thought in multi-view spatial reasoning.

Method: Proposes LenGuard-GPC: (1) computes token-wise KL divergence between predictive distributions of standard vs. guided prompts as dense reward, (2) introduces staged length bonus to regulate reasoning length without encouraging brevity.

Result: On six multi-view spatial reasoning benchmarks, LenGuard-GPC improves accuracy over vanilla GRPO while reducing average response length.

Conclusion: Dense reward with length control effectively addresses both reasoning quality and efficiency in multi-view spatial reasoning tasks.

Abstract: Multi-view spatial reasoning requires vision-language models to compare visual evidence across images, align object correspondences, and infer spatial relations over long visual contexts, a setting where chain-of-thought reasoning tends to grow verbose without becoming more accurate. Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards is a natural fit for this task, but standard GRPO reward relies on sparse outcome-level feedback and gives no signal about where a reasoning trajectory goes wrong, nor any control over its length. We propose LenGuard-GPC, a dense reward framework that addresses both problems together. For each sampled trajectory, it compares the token-wise predictive distributions under a standard prompt and a guided prompt, and uses the resulting token-sum KL divergence as a dense reward signal. Since this KL penalty accumulates over tokens and would otherwise reward shorter responses regardless of their quality, we introduce a staged length bonus that keeps reasoning length within a controlled range without simply encouraging brevity. On six multi-view spatial reasoning benchmarks, LenGuard-GPC improves accuracy over vanilla GRPO while reducing average response length.

</details>


### [131] [Coordinated Disentanglement with Iterative Mode Discovery Under Hidden Correlations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17264)
*Rong Hu,Ling Chen*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: CoDID is an end-to-end framework for disentangled representation learning that jointly discovers hidden modes and enforces mode-based conditional independence, using a dynamic architecture and coordination mechanism to prevent error amplification.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing disentangled representation learning methods address explicit attribute correlations but fail to handle hidden correlations where data under a certain attribute value exhibits underlying modes that are correlated with other attributes.

Method: Proposes Coordinated Disentanglement with Iterative mode Discovery (CoDID), an end-to-end framework with a dynamic architecture that adapts to evolving number of modes, and a coordination mechanism using meta-optimization to mitigate error amplification between mode discovery and disentanglement modules.

Result: Achieves state-of-the-art performance on diverse tasks compared to existing methods.

Conclusion: The proposed joint mode discovery and conditional independence enforcement, along with the coordination mechanism, effectively addresses hidden correlations in disentangled representation learning.

Abstract: Disentangled representation learning is a powerful paradigm for robust attribute prediction. While recent methods address attribute correlations, hidden correlations remain underexplored, where data under the value of a certain attribute exhibit underlying modes correlated with other attributes. To preserve mode information and achieve disentanglement, we jointly discover modes and enforce mode-based conditional independence. Yet, the interdependency between these two modules may lead to error amplification under naive iterations. We propose Coordinated Disentanglement with Iterative mode Discovery (CoDID), an end-to-end framework featuring a dynamic architecture that adapts to evolving number of modes, and a coordination mechanism that mitigates error amplification via meta-optimization. Empirical results demonstrate the state-of-the-art performance on diverse tasks.

</details>


### [132] [An Explicit World Model Based on Data-First Ontology: DaoQL Multimodal Storage Validation and Counterfactual Reasoning Evaluation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17269)
*Zhanbo Li,Shifeng Wu,Xiangjin Meng,Wenjie Cai*

Main category: cs.AI

TL;DR: This paper proposes data-first ontology where LLMs serve as reasoning engines while deterministic knowledge is stored in an explicit multimodal database (DaoQL), demonstrating superior composable counterfactual decomposability (94% vs 45% for GPT-4o alone) and addressing structural risks of implicit world models in high-precision domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Large language models encode world models implicitly in neural weights, which exposes four structural risks in high-precision domains (medicine, finance): hallucination, frozen knowledge, poor explainability, and poor modifiability.

Method: Proposes data-first ontology where LLMs are treated as reasoning/language engines while deterministic knowledge is moved into DaoQL, an explicit multimodal database integrating graph, column, vector, and full-text engines within one process. Formalizes explicit world model with rule independence, deterministic evaluation, and fixed conflict resolution.

Result: DaoQL reports graph BFS at 1.20 ms, HNSW at 83.1 us, Fluent hybrid query at 105.8 us. On LDBC SNB SF1 achieves 34/34 query coverage with mostly sub-millisecond queries but only 1.8 QPS overall. ANN-Benchmarks reaches Recall@10 >= 99% at thousand-level QPS. In counterfactual experiment (n=1250), DaoQL+GPT-4o achieves 94% composable counterfactual decomposability, 49 percentage points above GPT-4o alone.

Conclusion: Explicit models under specified conditions provide sufficient guarantee for composable counterfactual decomposability; implicit models lack atomic read/delta semantics and provide no comparable architectural guarantee. The paper explicitly separates provable structure, preliminary empirical evidence, and architectural roadmap claims, with KVCache graph nodes, expert hot updates, and DaoQL-Agent runtime as future work.

Abstract: Large language models encode world models implicitly in neural weights, which exposes four structural risks in high-precision domains such as medicine and finance: hallucination, frozen knowledge, poor explainability, and poor modifiability. This paper proposes data-first ontology: LLMs are treated as reasoning and language engines, while deterministic knowledge is moved into an explicit multimodal database, DaoQL. We formalize an explicit world model and show that, under rule independence, deterministic evaluation, and fixed conflict resolution, explicit models provide a sufficient condition for composable counterfactual decomposability; implicit models lack atomic read/delta semantics and therefore provide no comparable architectural guarantee. The implemented system focuses on DaoQL's verified storage layer and explicit Eval path, integrating graph, column, vector, and full-text engines within one process. KVCache graph nodes, expert hot updates, and the DaoQL-Agent runtime remain future work. On an embedded same-machine setup, DaoQL reports graph BFS at 1.20 ms, HNSW at 83.1 us, and a Fluent hybrid query at 105.8 us; these results indicate engineering potential but must be interpreted with deployment-shape differences from client-server systems.
