We implement KATA as fused Triton kernels at two operating points: a flash-attention-style forward up to ${\sim}1.6\times$ FlashAttention-2 throughput, and an exact $O(T)$ chunked-state form that reaches ${\sim}11\times$ FlashAttention-2 forward throughput at $131$k tokens. An associative scan of the first-order feature lowers the inter-chunk recurrence depth to $O(\log(T/C))$ for chunk size $C$ and averages ${\sim}2.4\times$ the throughput of a matched sequential linear-attention baseline. On long-range MQAR and repeated-key overwrite, several KATA variants outperform Gated DeltaNet, with parameter counts and state sizes reported alongside accuracy. Induction preserves near-perfect recall, while kernel benchmarks show that the maps can be implemented efficiently. KATA retains $0.985$ MQAR at a $16\times$ out-of-distribution length, approaching the softmax with roughly one quarter of the KV-cache entries. Experiments on 340M-parameter LLMs reveal a feature-dependent fluency trade-off and clarify how positional embeddings, delta rules, and decay gates interact with feature geometry.

</details>


### [212] [Learning Spatio-Temporal Foundation Models from Pure Synthetic Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16251)
*Yutong Feng,Shiyuan Piao,Yutong Xia,Xu Liu,Wenqi Fan,Fugee Tsung,See-Kiong Ng,Yuxuan Liang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: NeoST is the first spatio-temporal foundation model pre-trained entirely on synthetic data, using latent-space reasoning and multi-trajectory refinement to overcome distribution bias, error accumulation, and noise overfitting in existing STFMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing STFMs suffer from real-world distributional bias in pre-training data, structural bottlenecks in autoregressive/diffusion models, and objectives that overemphasize point-wise reconstruction in noisy observation spaces.

Method: NeoST is pre-trained solely on procedurally generated synthetic systems, features a latent-space reasoning architecture that generates and iteratively refines multiple future trajectories without sequential error accumulation, and uses latent-space objectives emphasizing structural dynamics with inference-time correction under distribution shifts.

Result: NeoST consistently outperforms existing STFMs across diverse real-world benchmarks, demonstrating superior long-horizon stability and inference efficiency.

Conclusion: Pre-training on synthetic data combined with latent-space reasoning can effectively address key limitations of current spatio-temporal foundation models, achieving better generalization and robustness.

Abstract: Spatio-Temporal Foundation Models (STFMs) aim to learn generalizable representations of complex dynamical systems across space and time. However, existing approaches suffer from distributional bias in real-world pre-training data, structural bottlenecks of autoregressive or diffusion-based paradigms, and objectives that overemphasize point-wise reconstruction in noisy observation space.We propose \textbf{NeoST}, the first spatio-temporal foundation model pre-trained solely on procedurally generated synthetic systems. NeoST introduces a scalable synthetic pre-training corpus to mitigate real-world bias, a latent-space reasoning architecture that generates and iteratively refines multiple future trajectories without sequential error accumulation, and latent-space objectives that emphasize structural dynamics and enable inference-time correction under distribution shifts.Extensive experiments across diverse real-world benchmarks show that NeoST consistently outperforms existing STFMs in diverse real-world spatio-temporal systems, achieves superior long-horizon stability and inference efficiency.

</details>


### [213] [One-step lowest-variance selection in a Gaussian random-field model motivated by masked diffusion: Total correlation and a square root collision threshold](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17522)
*Linjun Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a single selection step in masked discrete diffusion using a Gaussian random-field model, showing that the dependence cost of parallel decoding vanishes at sub-square-root scales but remains non-negligible at the square-root scale.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide a rigorous stochastic-geometry baseline for understanding how confidence-guided parallel unmasking works in masked discrete diffusion, particularly how budget size, score dependence, and spatial correlation affect selection.

Method: The authors model the selection step using a Gaussian random-field with a locally dependent nonnegative score field, selecting K positions with smallest scores, and analyze dependence via distance-dependent Gaussian correlation. They establish asymptotic results for conditional Gaussian total correlation.

Result: Two key results: (1) In the sub-square-root regime, the conditional Gaussian total correlation of the selected block vanishes in probability. (2) At the square-root scale, the correlation remains non-negligible with positive asymptotic probability and has a strictly positive lower bound. Synthetic experiments confirm the finite-size behavior.

Conclusion: The findings provide a rigorous framework quantifying how the geometry of low-score locations affects the dependence cost of factorized parallel decoding in masked discrete diffusion.

Abstract: Motivated by confidence-guided parallel unmasking in masked discrete diffusion, we study a single selection step in a stylized Gaussian random-field model. A locally dependent nonnegative score field represents position wise uncertainty, and the scheduler selects the K positions with the smallest scores. Dependence among the selected positions is measured through a distance-dependent Gaussian correlation model. This separation provides a tractable framework for quantifying how the geometry of low-score locations affects the dependence cost of factorized parallel decoding. We establish two complementary results. In a conservative sub-square-root regime, the conditional Gaussian total correlation of the selected block vanishes in probability. At the square-root scale, it remains non-negligible with positive asymptotic probability and admits a strictly positive expectation lower bound. Synthetic experiments support the predicted finite-size behavior. These results provide a rigorous stochastic-geometry baseline for understanding how budget size, score dependence, and spatial correlation jointly shape one-step confidence-based selection in masked discrete diffusion.

</details>


### [214] [SOS-LoRA: Static Orthogonal-Subspace Low-Rank Adaptation with Fixed Multi-Scale Scaling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16252)
*Yupeng Chang,Yuan Wu,Yi Chang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SOS-LoRA is a drop-in replacement for standard LoRA that splits a single low-rank update into multiple static orthogonal subspaces (experts), using multi-scale scaling and orthogonal initialization to reduce interference and improve optimization, achieving consistent gains across multiple benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard LoRA under a fixed rank budget uses a single low-dimensional input-side pathway, which can couple heterogeneous behaviors through shared input directions and induce interference during optimization.

Method: SOS-LoRA reparameterizes a rank-rtot update as a sum of K static (non-routed) low-rank experts. It (i) decomposes total rank across experts, (ii) applies fixed multi-scale scaling for scale-separated optimization, and (iii) promotes diverse input directions via cross-expert orthogonal initialization and a lightweight regularizer.

Result: Consistent gains over matched-budget LoRA baselines and recent variants on reasoning/knowledge benchmarks (Llama 2/3), GLUE, and math reasoning (GSM8K/MATH). Fully mergeable with no inference overhead.

Conclusion: SOS-LoRA effectively reduces interference in LoRA optimization by enforcing orthogonal, multi-scale subspaces, leading to improved fine-tuning performance without added inference cost.

Abstract: Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) is a widely used parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) method for large language models. Under a fixed rank budget, LoRA parameterizes each adapted weight through a single low-dimensional input-side pathway, which may couple heterogeneous behaviors through shared input directions and induce interference during optimization. We propose Static Orthogonal Subspace LoRA (SOS-LoRA), a drop-in extension that reparameterizes a rank-rtot update as a sum of K static (always-on, non-routed) low-rank experts. SOS-LoRA (i) decomposes the total rank across experts, (ii) applies a fixed multi-scale scaling scheme to encourage scale-separated optimization dynamics, and (iii) promotes diverse input-side directions via cross-expert orthogonal initialization and a lightweight regularizer. SOS-LoRA remains fully mergeable, adding no inference-time parameters or latency after merging. Experiments on reasoning and knowledge-intensive benchmarks (Llama 2/3), encoder-based NLU (GLUE), and math reasoning (GSM8K/MATH) show consistent gains over matched-budget LoRA baselines and recent variants. Code is available at https://github.com/llm172/sos-lora.

</details>


### [215] [Comprehensive Evaluation of Machine Learning for Type 2 Diabetes Risk Prediction: Large-Scale External Validation and Fairness Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16253)
*Rajveer Singh Pall,Sameer Yadav,Siddharth Bhalerao,Sourabh Sahu,Ritu Ahluwalia,Bhaskar Awadhiya*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: An XGBoost T2D risk prediction model showed good internal validation (AUC=0.794) but significant performance drops in external validation (AUC=0.717) and severe fairness issues, particularly age-related bias (elderly AUC=0.607 vs young AUC=0.742).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Machine learning-based T2D risk prediction models perform well internally but fail in real-world settings due to lack of external validation and fairness assessment; this gap motivates developing a comprehensive evaluation framework.

Method: Trained XGBoost on NHANES 2015-2020 (n=15,685) with 8 non-laboratory predictors; externally validated on BRFSS 2020-2022 (n=1,285,783); assessed discrimination, calibration, interpretability (SHAP), and algorithmic fairness across demographic subgroups.

Result: Internal AUC=0.794; external AUC=0.717 (−9.7%, p<0.001). Severe age bias (elderly AUC=0.607 vs young 0.742); obesity bias (obese AUC=0.698 vs normal 0.735); gender fair (p=0.142). Calibration showed risk overestimation (Brier=0.123).

Conclusion: Populations with highest diabetes risk receive the worst algorithmic performance, underscoring the need for fairness-aware, age-stratified deployment strategies before clinical use.

Abstract: Machine learning-based Type 2 diabetes risk prediction models obtain good internal validation results but lose effectiveness in real-world applications due to deficient external testing and fairness assessment. We developed a multi-dimensional framework evaluating discrimination, calibration, interpretability, and algorithmic fairness on nationally representative populations. An XGBoost model was trained on NHANES 2015-2020 (n=15,685) using eight non-laboratory predictors: age, sex, race/ethnicity, BMI, smoking status, physical activity, history of heart attack, and history of stroke. External validation was performed on BRFSS 2020-2022 (n=1,285,783) under realistic distribution shift. Internal validation showed good discrimination (AUC=0.794, 95% CI 0.788-0.800), with performance loss on external validation (AUC=0.717, relative decrease: -9.7%, p<0.001). Fairness analysis revealed severe bias: elderly adults (>=60) showed AUC=0.607 vs 0.742 for young adults (difference=0.135, p<0.001); obese individuals showed AUC=0.698 vs 0.735 for normal weight (difference=0.037, p<0.001). Gender showed comparable performance (male=0.723 vs female=0.712, p=0.142). Calibration revealed risk overestimation (Brier score=0.123). SHAP analysis identified age, BMI, and physical activity as primary risk drivers. Populations with highest diabetes risk receive the worst algorithmic performance, underscoring the need for fairness-aware, age-stratified deployment strategies before clinical use.

</details>


### [216] [More Than Memory: Task-Conditioned Signed FFN Writes in Long-Context Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16254)
*Zhibo Yang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FFN residual writes in LLMs exhibit signed, layer-specific, and task-conditioned roles (amplifier vs. suppressor) during retrieval, with write-gradient alignment serving as a compact diagnostic for these roles.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether FFN native residual writes push retrieval states toward or away from correct answers in long-context retrieval, beyond treating FFNs as mere parametric memories.

Method: Scale FFN writes one layer at a time without editing weights or injecting external vectors; compute local directional derivative to classify amplifiers vs. suppressors; validate on controlled retrieval suites and safety-filtered LongBench.

Result: Final FFN is a suppressor in 7/8 model-suite cases; 60% of layers switch roles between retrieval modes; suppressors have negative derivative (34/35) and amplifiers positive (18/18); diagnostic predicts attenuation damage with R²≈0.796-0.791 on Qwen models.

Conclusion: Native FFN scaling reveals a signed, task-conditioned residual-write structure; write-gradient alignment is an effective diagnostic for identifying monotone roles (amplifier/suppressor) and improving retrieval margins.

Abstract: FFNs are often treated as parametric memories. In long-context retrieval, however, the sharper question is not only what they store, but whether their native residual writes push the current retrieval state toward or away from the correct answer. We test this by scaling the model's own FFN write one layer at a time, without editing weights or injecting external steering vectors.
  Across controlled literal and semantic retrieval suites, native FFN response surfaces are signed, layer-specific, and task-conditioned: the final FFN is a suppressor in 7 of 8 model-suite cases, and 60% of layers switch role between retrieval modes (95% CI [50%, 69%]). A local directional derivative along the native write separates the two monotone roles: suppressors have negative derivative in 34/35 cases, and amplifiers have positive derivative in 18/18 cases, so the roles are not reducible to write size. On a safety-filtered LongBench retrieval-QA probe, the same diagnostic predicts attenuation damage with raw R^2=0.796 on Qwen2.5-7B and 0.791 on Qwen3.5-9B; a held-out suppressor-attenuation policy improves retrieval margins over random and norm-matched controls. These results show that native FFN scaling exposes a signed, task-conditioned residual-write structure in retrieval, and that write-gradient alignment is a compact diagnostic for the two monotone roles.

</details>


### [217] [Feature Generation Using LLMs: An Evolutionary Algorithm Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16255)
*Aria Nourbakhsh,Benoît Alcaraz,Christoph Schommer*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using LLMs to automatically generate new features from tabular data by applying mathematical and logical operations on existing attributes, with a selection algorithm to pick the best-performing feature sets, achieving improved classification results across eight diverse datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Feature engineering is a crucial but challenging step in ML pipelines, as finding meaningful relations among attributes that ML algorithms may not capture on their own is difficult. The authors aim to automate this process using LLMs' capabilities in coding, reasoning, and world knowledge.

Method: The authors build a pipeline that takes existing attributes and a prompt to generate new features via an LLM, then applies a selection algorithm to pick the best-performing feature sets. The method is evaluated on eight datasets from various domains.

Result: In most cases, the LLM successfully produces useful new features based on mathematical and logical operators, leading to improved classification results across diverse datasets.

Conclusion: LLMs can effectively automate feature generation from tabular data, and the generated features enhance classification performance, suggesting a promising direction for automated feature engineering.

Abstract: A crucial step in machine learning pipelines is to present each entity with features or attributes that are representative of the characteristics of the processed entities. Feature engineering is an important step in finding a relation among attributes that otherwise may not be processed by the ML algorithms. Meanwhile, Large Language Models have shown promising abilities in coding, mathematical reasoning, and processing world knowledge. In this work, we utilize an LLM for the problem of feature generation from tabular data based on the previously given features. We have created a pipeline that takes a set of attributes and a prompt to generate new features. Then, our selection algorithm selects the best-performing sets of attributes. We apply our method to eight datasets from different domains and data types. Our results show that, in most cases, the language model can produce new features based on mathematical and logical operators that are useful for the given tasks and can improve classification results.

</details>


### [218] [Discovery by Dreaming: Cross-Domain Recombination in Artificial Memory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16256)
*Oliver Zahn,James Evans,David Eagleman*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Dreams enable cross-domain knowledge recombination for discovery. Implementing this mechanism in neural (DREAMS) and symbolic (SAPIENCE) systems shows cross-domain consolidation creates value while within-domain rehearsal does not. The effect is weight-intrinsic, not prompt-based, and substrate-general, suggesting consolidation exists not for memory but for discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To challenge the dominant view that memory consolidation primarily serves to prevent forgetting. The authors hypothesize that the true function is recombinatory—synthesizing knowledge across domains that have never co-occurred—driving insight and creative discovery.

Method: Two architecturally unrelated systems implement recombinatory replay: (1) DREAMS, a LoRA fine-tuning pipeline, and (2) SAPIENCE, a symbolic engine replaying structured knowledge objects. Performance is measured on cross-domain transfer tasks (e.g., GSM8K math reasoning). A control experiment prepends the same material in-context to a 671B model to test if gains are weight-intrinsic.

Result: Symbolic system (SAPIENCE) achieves 85.7% novel cross-domain connections (+21 pp). Neural system (DREAMS) improves +5.64 pp overall, with +14.5 pp gains on cross-domain transfer subtasks. In-context prepending to a 671B model reverses the gain, confirming weight-intrinsic origin. Validated against 50,000 real papers.

Conclusion: Consolidation is fundamentally for discovery, not remembering. The recombinatory principle is substrate-general and tracks real discovery at scale. A falsifiable hippocampal-recording prediction is proposed to distinguish recombination from rehearsal.

Abstract: Dreams splice together people, places, and times that never met. Neuroscience suggests this recombination is not noise, but a function driving insight and creative discovery. This reframes memory consolidation: rather than merely defending against forgetting, its measurable value lies in recombining knowledge across experiences that have not yet co-occurred. We test this directly by isolating the recombinatory-replay mechanism and implementing it in two architecturally unrelated systems: a LoRA fine-tuning pipeline (DREAMS) and a symbolic engine replaying structured knowledge objects (SAPIENCE). Both systems converge on the same finding: cross-domain consolidation creates value, while within-domain rehearsal does not. The symbolic arm surfaces novel cross-domain connections at 85.7%, a +21 percentage point (pp) gain over baseline. The neural arm improves overall by +5.64 pp, but on subtasks explicitly requiring cross-domain transfer (like unseen math reasoning on GSM8K), gains reach +14.5 pp. This effect is a genuine property of the weights--not a prompt artifact--as prepending the same material in-context to a 671B-parameter model actually reverses the gain. We validate this prediction against documented discoveries across 50,000 real papers and state a falsifiable hippocampal-recording prediction to distinguish recombination from rehearsal. Ultimately, this principle is substrate-general, tracking real discovery at scale. Reading the literature teaches a model to recall what it has seen, but producing discovery requires a separate offline phase that recombines knowledge across domains--the computational analog of dreaming. Consolidation is not for remembering, but for discovering.

</details>


### [219] [From Outcomes to Actions: Leveraging Hindsight for Long-Horizon Language Agent Training](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16257)
*Zishang Jiang,Tingyun Li,Jinyi Han,Xinyi Wang,Sihang Jiang,Yizhou Ying,Xiaojun Meng,Jiansheng Wei,Jiaqing Liang,Yanghua Xiao*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HPO projects current and hindsight policy distributions into an intent space and uses Wasserstein distance to derive low-variance learning signals for long-horizon RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL methods struggle with long-horizon interactions due to high optimization variance from not distinguishing contributions of different actions.

Method: HPO projects the current policy distribution and hindsight distribution into an intent space, then extracts low-variance learning signals from the Wasserstein distance between them.

Result: Theoretically and empirically, aggregating semantically similar states/actions in the intent space yields a bounded-variance estimator and improves policy performance stably.

Conclusion: HPO provides a stable and effective policy gradient method for long-horizon RL tasks by reducing optimization variance through intent-space projection.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a widely adopted technique for improving large language models (LLMs) on complex tasks. Despite this progress, existing RL methods still face challenges in training agents with longer-horizon interactions. One major bottleneck is distinguishing the contribution of different actions in long-horizon interaction, leading to high optimization variance. To address this, we introduce a novel policy gradient method, Hindsight Policy Optimization (HPO), that projects both the current policy distribution and the hindsight distribution into an intent space and extracts low-variance learning signals from the Wasserstein distance between them. We theoretically and empirically show that aggregating semantically similar states and actions in the intent space yields a bounded-variance estimator and improves policy performance stably. Our code is available online.

</details>


### [220] [Neural Controlled Differential Equations for EMT-Level Surrogate Modeling of Grid-Forming Inverters](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16258)
*Jiagang Qu,Yong Tao,Dan Wang,Enyi Li,Jingjing Qi,Ding Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) framework for learning continuous-time surrogate models of grid-forming inverters, enabling multi-time-scale EMT simulation with physics-inspired regularization for improved stability and accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI methods for power electronic converter modeling face challenges including difficulties in multi-time-scale hybrid analysis and lack of physics-aware evaluation criteria and constraints, leading to poor performance.

Method: The authors propose (1) a Neural CDE framework for learning continuous-time surrogate models that relax fixed sampling rate constraints, (2) an affine-control formulation with dual slow/fast pathways to capture hierarchical multiscale behavior, and (3) a physics-inspired regularization method to enhance stability and coherence.

Result: The model accurately reproduces transient responses, preserves effective damping and dominant oscillatory characteristics, and maintains bounded long-horizon rollouts when evaluated on EMT-generated trajectories.

Conclusion: Neural CDE-based component modeling offers a physically consistent surrogate modeling approach for EMT-level simulation studies.

Abstract: The application of artificial intelligence methods in power electronic converter modeling is becoming increasingly widespread, but existing applications still face many challenges, such as difficulties in multi-time-scale hybrid analysis and the lack of physics-aware evaluation criteria and constraints, resulting in poor performance. This paper proposes a Neural Controlled Differential Equation (Neural CDE) framework for learning continuous-time surrogate models of grid-forming inverters for electromagnetic transient (EMT) simulation, which relaxes the constraint of fixed sampling rates and enables multi-time-scale control analysis. Then, an affine-control formulation with dual slow/fast pathways is proposed to capture the hierarchical and multiscale behavior of converter dynamics, and a physics-inspired regularization method is utilized to enhance stability and coherence. Evaluated on EMT-generated trajectories, the model accurately reproduces transient responses, preserves effective damping and the dominant oscillatory characteristics, and maintains bounded long-horizon rollouts. The results show that Neural CDE-based component modeling offers a physically consistent surrogate modeling approach for EMT-level simulation studies.

</details>


### [221] [Quantifying Ranking Uncertainty in LLM Benchmarks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16259)
*Bitya Neuhof,Yuval Benjamini*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes uncertainty in MMLU benchmark rankings for LLMs, showing that ranking variability across subjects is substantial and should be considered when comparing models.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To quantify and address uncertainty in multi-task leaderboard rankings of pretrained models, specifically focusing on the MMLU benchmark.

Method: The authors analyze sources of uncertainty in MMLU rankings and modify hypothesis tests to account for ranking variability across subjects.

Result: Ranking variability across MMLU subjects is substantial and should be accounted for when comparing LLMs or identifying top-performing models.

Conclusion: Hypothesis tests can be modified to account for uncertainty sources in MMLU, and ranking variability across subjects is a significant factor that must be considered for fair model comparison.

Abstract: Pretrained models are typically ranked on multi-task leaderboards to assess their effectiveness across diverse tasks. Rank confidence intervals were recently introduced as a method to quantify the uncertainty in these rankings by aggregating pairwise hypothesis tests. In this work, we analyze the sources of uncertainty in the knowledge evaluation benchmark MMLU and show how hypothesis tests can be modified to account for their effects. We demonstrate that ranking variability across MMLU subjects is substantial and should be considered when comparing LLMs or identifying the top-performing models.

</details>


### [222] [AdaSurvMamba: Dynamic Fusion and Semantic Scanning for Multimodal Survival Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16260)
*Jialong Zhong,Tingwei Liu,Baokun Yue,Jingjing Li,Yongri Piao,Miao Zhang,Leiye Liu,Jiahong Jiang,Wei Ji,Huchuan Lu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: AdaSurvMamba is a new framework for multimodal survival analysis that addresses limitations in static cross-modal fusion and rigid scanning in Mamba architectures, using Dual-Scale Importance-Aware Reconstruction and Semantic Aggregation Scanning to achieve state-of-the-art results across five TCGA cohorts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional fusion strategies assume static multimodal interaction strength, ignoring the fluctuating diagnostic importance of each modality across patients and local regions. Also, standard Mamba's rigid scanning along predefined physical paths disrupts semantic continuity and exacerbates long-range decay in scattered medical features.

Method: Proposes AdaSurvMamba with two key modules: (1) Dual-Scale Importance-Aware Reconstruction (DSIR) that dynamically modulates cross-modal interaction strength at sequence and token levels; (2) Semantic Aggregation Scanning (SAS) that reorganizes discrete tokens into semantically continuous sequences via a shared prototype pool and adaptively controls information absorption rate using global modality context and semantic priors.

Result: Consistent performance improvements over existing methods across five TCGA cohorts (cancer datasets) for multimodal survival analysis.

Conclusion: The adaptive fusion and dynamic scanning approach effectively addresses limitations of static fusion and rigid scanning, advancing multimodal survival analysis for cancer prognosis.

Abstract: Multimodal survival analysis utilizing whole slide images (WSIs) and genomic profiles is fundamental for cancer prognosis. Recently, state-space models like Mamba have emerged as powerful tools for sequence modeling. However, translating this success to complex multimodal tasks is hindered by two critical limitations. First, conventional fusion strategies assume a static multimodal interaction strength, ignoring the fluctuating diagnostic importance of each modality across different patients and local regions. Second, the standard Mamba architecture processes tokens along predefined physical paths. This rigid scanning disrupts the semantic continuity of spatially scattered medical features and exacerbates long-range decay. To address these challenges, we introduce AdaSurvMamba as a novel adaptive framework for multimodal survival analysis. The framework features a Dual-Scale Importance-Aware Reconstruction (DSIR) module to dynamically modulate cross-modal interaction strength. It evaluates diagnostic importance at both the sequence and token levels to reconstruct the input representations. Furthermore, we propose a Semantic Aggregation Scanning (SAS) module to overcome contextual fragmentation. The SAS module dynamically reorganizes discrete tokens into semantically continuous sequences via a shared prototype pool. It explicitly modulates the state transition step size using global modality context and semantic priors to adaptively control the information absorption rate. Experiments across five TCGA cohorts demonstrate consistent gains over existing methods. Code is available at https://github.com/zjlGO/AdaSurvMamba.

</details>


### [223] [Reducing Per-Sample Harm in Stochastic Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16261)
*Apostolos Avranas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper identifies "harm" in optimizers where batch averaging and historical state increase loss for individual samples, proposes a tractable proxy by reducing dimensionality to batch size and restricting optimization to the last linear layer, and shows improved generalization with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard optimizers (SGD, AdamW) aggregate gradients across batches and incorporate historical state (momentum, adaptive moments), which can inadvertently increase loss for individual samples. The authors aim to explicitly minimize this conflicting impact.

Method: Formalizes parameter updates as an optimization problem minimizing per-sample harm. Reduces dimensionality to batch size, then restricts optimization to the last linear layer (which captures second-order statistics of per-sample gradients). Integrates into SGD and AdamW with GPU-friendly iterations.

Result: The method exhibits favorable scaling (overhead shrinks with model/inference size). Experiments on image classification confirm reduced per-sample interference and improved generalization.

Conclusion: By explicitly addressing harm from batch averaging and momentum history, and leveraging the last linear layer for efficient proxy optimization, the approach improves optimizer behavior with minimal computational cost.

Abstract: Modern optimizers combine gradients from the current mini-batch with historical optimization state, such as momentum or adaptive moments. While highly effective, aggregating across the batch and incorporating this history can produce parameter updates that increase the loss of individual samples. We term this effect harm and formalize the parameter update as an optimization problem that explicitly minimizes the conflicting impact of both batch averaging and past optimization state on current data.
  Because the exact formulation is intractable, we introduce a highly efficient proxy. We first reduce the problem's dimensionality to the batch size, and then drastically cut memory and speed bottlenecks by successfully restricting the optimization to the last linear layer. This hinges on the unexpected finding that this layer alone reliably captures the second-order statistics of the per-sample gradients. The resulting surrogate problem integrates readily into standard optimizers like SGD and AdamW, and can be solved using a small number of GPU-friendly iterations. Crucially, the method exhibits favorable scaling properties, as the relative computational overhead shrinks as the model size or input grows. Experiments on image classification benchmarks confirm reduced per-sample interference and improved generalization.

</details>


### [224] [Autonomous mechanistic discovery of colorectal cancer vulnerabilities via multi-scale AI swarms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16262)
*Christopher Baker,Tianyu Ren,Karen Rafferty,Hui Wang,Simon McDade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Octopus is a neuro-symbolic framework combining LLM swarms with algorithmic physics engines to autonomously discover therapeutic vulnerabilities (IGF2 in colorectal cancer) that translate from in vitro data to in vivo tumor shrinkage and human survival, achieving statistically significant results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing multi-agent frameworks for automated discovery lack mathematically grounded causal constraints for clinical translation, while clinical digital twins sacrifice mechanistic interpretability for predictive accuracy.

Method: Octopus integrates zero-leakage local LLM swarms with algorithmic physics engines; it generates hypotheses from CCLE dependency data, traces causal cascades via XGBoost SHAP vectors, and translates vulnerabilities in silico to predict PDX and human overall survival (Marisa dataset).

Result: Identified IGF2 as a bounded vulnerability to 5-FU resistance in colorectal cancer (BH-corrected q=0.0292, Log-Rank p=0.0007); predicted in vivo tumor volume shrinkage validated in independent mouse cohort (Mann-Whitney p=0.0373).

Conclusion: Octopus establishes a verifiable, zero-leakage paradigm for automated end-to-end biomedical discovery, bridging multi-agent reasoning with mathematically bounded clinical survival.

Abstract: The acceleration of automated scientific discovery has been fundamentally bottlenecked by the epistemic gap between the semantic reasoning of large language models (LLMs) and the deterministic physics of mammalian biology. While recent multi-agent frameworks have achieved autonomous hypothesis generation and in vitro experimental analysis, they lack the mathematically grounded, causal constraints required for multi-scale clinical translation. Furthermore, while algorithmic clinical digital twins successfully forecast biological states, they rely on black-box latent spaces, sacrificing mechanistic interpretability for predictive accuracy. Here, we introduce the Multi-Scale Autonomous Discovery Engine (Octopus), a neuro-symbolic architecture that unites zero-leakage, local LLM swarms with strict algorithmic physics engines. Rather than stopping at isolated cellular assays, the system autonomously generated therapeutic hypotheses against in vitro CRISPR dependency data (CCLE), traced dynamic causal cascades using mechanistic interpretability (XGBoost SHAP vectors), and orthogonally translated the emergent vulnerabilities in silico to predict in vivo mammalian tumor trajectory (PDX) and human overall survival (Marisa). In a fully unsupervised sweep of colorectal cancer transcriptomes, the pipeline autonomously identified Insulin-like Growth Factor 2 (IGF2) as a strictly bounded vulnerability to 5-Fluorouracil resistance. The discovery maintained significance after rigorous Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate correction (q=0.0292, Log-Rank p=0.0007 ) and successfully predicted significant in vivo tumor volume shrinkage in an independent mouse cohort (Mann-Whitney p=0.0373). By bridging the chasm between multi-agent reasoning and mathematically bounded clinical survival, this framework establishes a verifiable, zero-leakage paradigm for automated, end-to-end biomedical discovery.

</details>


### [225] [Preference-based Antibody Expression Ranking: Scaling with Large-scale Weak Supervision](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16263)
*Josh Qixuan Sun,Morteza Babaie,Wenyang Hou,Mark Crowley,David Young*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a preference learning framework (using DPO adaptation) that integrates scarce quantitative expression data with large-scale weak positive supervision from immunization data to improve antibody expression ranking, outperforming baselines on internal datasets of 1,254 labeled sequences and 4 million unlabeled camelid antibodies.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Antibody expression ranking is critical in antibody design but suffers from scarcity of labeled experimental data, which hinders effective modeling.

Method: A unified preference-based learning framework that adapts Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to protein language models via union-masked log-likelihood approximation and IMGT-based alignment, combining scarce quantitative data with large-scale weak positive supervision from immunization data.

Result: On an internal dataset of 1,254 labeled sequences and 4 million unlabeled camelid antibodies, the method consistently outperforms baselines on most metrics.

Conclusion: Preference learning can effectively learn from weak supervision, offering a scalable solution for antibody expressibility optimization in data-constrained settings.

Abstract: Antibody expression ranking is a critical task in antibody design, yet its modelling is severely hindered by the scarcity of labeled experimental data. To address this, we propose a unified preference-based learning framework that integrates scarce quantitative expression data with large-scale weak positive supervision from immunization data. We adapt Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) to protein language models by introducing a union-masked log-likelihood approximation and IMGT-based alignment, enabling efficient training on variable-length sequences. Evaluating on a diverse internal dataset of 1254 labeled sequences and 4 million unlabeled camelid-derived antibodies, we show that our method consistently outperforms baselines on most metrics. Our results demonstrate that preference learning can effectively learn from weak supervision, providing a scalable solution for antibody expressibility optimization in data-constrained settings. Project page: https://kisoji-biotechnology-inc.github.io/Preference-Expression-Ranking/.

</details>


### [226] [PsiLogic: Chaos-Aware Active Cancellation for Adam with a Fair Cross-Domain Benchmark](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16268)
*Ali Sultonov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PsiLogic augments Adam with a chaos-detecting Active Cancellation Term gated by dual EMA of gradient norms, providing implicit warmup and improved validation metrics on NLP, ViT, and ResNet tasks vs. Adam/AdamW/Lion, with comparable memory and modest overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Adaptive optimizers like Adam apply the same update rule throughout training, ignoring differences between chaotic early-phase and stable near-convergence dynamics. The authors aim to introduce an optimizer that adaptively adjusts damping based on gradient stability without requiring hand-tuned warmup schedules.

Method: PsiLogic augments Adam with a dynamic Active Cancellation Term gated by a dual exponential moving average (EMA) of scale-normalized gradient norms. This "chaos detector" strengthens damping when gradients are unstable and decays to zero as training stabilizes, providing automatic implicit warmup.

Result: On FairBench (H100 80GB, 4 arenas, 3 seeds, 2000 steps, bf16 AMP): PsiLogic achieves best validation in 3/4 arenas — NLP perplexity 7.79 (vs. AdamW 8.17, p=0.049), ViT top-1 0.244 (vs. AdamW 0.223, p=0.015), ResNet top-1 0.222 (vs. Adam 0.172, p=0.001). Diffusion MSE statistically tied (p=0.49). ResNet vs. AdamW tied (p=0.44). Memory comparable; 1.2-1.8x wall-clock overhead on transformer tasks.

Conclusion: PsiLogic outperforms or matches Adam, AdamW, and Lion across multiple tasks, particularly significantly on NLP, ViT, and ResNet. The approach offers implicit warmup without manual scheduling, with acceptable computational overhead. Open-source release supports reproducibility.

Abstract: Adaptive optimizers such as Adam and AdamW apply the same update rule regardless of whether training is in a chaotic early phase or near convergence. We introduce PsiLogic, an optimizer that augments Adam with a dynamic Active Cancellation Term gated by a dual exponential moving average (EMA) of scale-normalized gradient norms. The resulting chaos detector strengthens damping when gradient statistics are unstable and fades to zero as training stabilizes, providing an implicit warmup without a hand-tuned schedule.
  We evaluate PsiLogic against Adam, AdamW, and Lion using FairBench -- a reproducible benchmark protocol with per-optimizer learning-rate sweeps, identical initialization per seed, and Welch t-tests. On an NVIDIA H100 80GB reference run (4 arenas, 3 seeds, 2000 steps, bf16 AMP), PsiLogic achieves the best validation metric in three of four arenas: NLP perplexity 7.79 +/- 0.18 vs. 8.17 +/- 0.08 (AdamW, p = 0.049), ViT top-1 accuracy 0.244 +/- 0.006 vs. 0.223 +/- 0.002 (AdamW, p = 0.015), and ResNet top-1 accuracy 0.222 +/- 0.001 vs. 0.172 +/- 0.004 (Adam, p = 0.001). On diffusion, validation MSE is statistically tied with Adam/AdamW (p = 0.49). ResNet accuracy vs. AdamW is a numerical tie without significance at three seeds (p = 0.44). Peak GPU memory is comparable across optimizers; PsiLogic incurs 1.2--1.8x wall-clock overhead on transformer-heavy arenas (implementation-bound).
  We release an open-source PyTorch implementation, the full FairBench harness, and all raw CSV outputs to support independent verification.

</details>


### [227] [A Predict-then-Correct Loop Based on Few-Shot Continuous Contextual Bandit for Demand Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16354)
*Zhiwei Lei,Benedict Jun Ma,Ilya Jackson*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A predict-then-correct (PtC) framework improves retail demand forecasting by combining a static ML forecast with a few-shot contextual bandit correction policy, achieving significant error reductions and lower inventory costs across multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retail demand shifts faster than static forecasting models can retrain, especially in early cycles with sparse labels, creating a gap between offline learning and real-time decisions.

Method: Proposes PtC framework: first-stage ML forecast + few-shot continuous contextual bandit correction policy with similar-SKUs augmentation and top-p masked updating.

Result: Statistically significant reductions in MAPE, MAE, RMSE across demand patterns; 9.52% average RMSE improvement over ML-only baseline; lower inventory costs than base-stock, PPO, and SAC policies.

Conclusion: Online forecast correction can bridge offline demand learning and real-time retail decision-making by adapting to sparse feedback without full model retraining.

Abstract: Retail demand forecasting remains difficult when demand shifts faster than static forecasting models can be retrained, especially in early demand cycles where newly observed labels are sparse. To address this, this study aims to improve adaptive retail forecasting by proposing a predict-then-correct (PtC) framework that retains a first-stage machine learning (ML) forecast and applies a few-shot continuous contextual bandit correction policy with similar-SKUs augmentation and top-p masked updating. Across Walmart retail data and an exclusive beverage dataset, PtC delivers statistically significant reductions in MAPE, MAE, and RMSE across stable & high volume, stable & low volume, and erratic & intermittent demand patterns, improves average RMSE by 9.52% over the ML-only baseline in the ablation study, and yields lower inventory costs than base-stock, proximal policy optimization, and soft actor-critic policies under the tested lead-time settings. These findings show that online forecast correction can bridge offline demand learning and real-time retail decision-making by adapting to sparse feedback without fully retraining the base forecasting model.

</details>


### [228] [Scaling Limits of Constant-Stepsize SGD at Flat Minima](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16384)
*Jingyi Zhang,Cheng Mao,Debankur Mukherjee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies SGD with constant stepsize for convex objectives with flat minima, showing that the invariant law scales as α^{1/m} (rather than √α) and converges to non-Gaussian stationary distributions when the flatness exponent m>2.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the long-term behavior of SGD beyond the standard strongly convex setting, specifically when the objective function has flat minima (higher-order stationary points), where the typical √α Gaussian limit breaks down.

Method: The authors study SGD with Markovian noise from a contractive driving chain. They prove existence, uniqueness, and geometric convergence to an invariant law using Wasserstein distance with an α-dependent metric. They analyze small-stepsize scaling limits via stochastic differential equation approximation.

Result: For flat minima with exponent m≥2, the invariant law scales as α^{1/m} (vs √α for m=2). The contraction factor is 1-cα^{m-1}. The rescaled iterates converge weakly to the stationary distribution of an SDE dY_t = -h_0(Y_t)dt + Σ^{1/2}dB_t, yielding non-Gaussian limits for m>2.

Conclusion: SGD's asymptotic behavior fundamentally changes for flat minima: slower concentration (α^{1/m} vs √α), weaker contraction (1-cα^{m-1} vs 1-cα), and non-Gaussian limiting distributions, with extensions to coordinate-separable objectives with unequal flatness.

Abstract: For stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with a constant stepsize $α$, the invariant law of the iterates, centered at a minimizer, describes the behavior of the algorithm over long time horizons. In the strongly convex case, this invariant law has the familiar $\sqrtα$ scaling and a Gaussian limit as $α\downarrow 0$. We show that this behavior changes fundamentally for convex objectives $H$ with flat minima and (sub)quadratic tails.
  More specifically, we study SGD with Markovian noise generated by a contractive driving chain. For every sufficiently small constant stepsize $α$, we prove existence, uniqueness, and geometric convergence to an augmented invariant law in a Wasserstein distance induced by an $α$-dependent metric. When the minimizer $x_\star$ has local flatness exponent $m\ge2$, meaning that $\nabla^2 H(x)\asymp \lVert x-x_\star\rVert^{m-2} I_d$ as $x\to x_\star$, we obtain a contraction bound with factor $1-cα^{m-1}$, where $c>0$ is a constant. This recovers the factor $1-cα$ in the quadratic case $m=2$. We then analyze the small-stepsize scaling limit. We show that the invariant law concentrates on the scale $α^{1/m}$ and that the rescaled iterates converge weakly to the stationary distribution of the stochastic differential equation $$
  dY_t=-h_0(Y_t)\,dt+Σ^{1/2}\,dB_t , $$ where $h_0$ is the limiting drift at the minimizer and $Σ$ denotes the asymptotic covariance. This recovers the Gaussian limit when $m=2$ and gives generally non-Gaussian stationary limits in the flat case $m>2$. Finally, we give corresponding results for coordinate-separable objectives with unequal flatness exponents.

</details>


### [229] [Retrieval is Enough: Training-Free Interpretability with a Tool-Using Agent](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16448)
*Sriram Balasubramanian,Soheil Feizi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: HARP is a training-free interpretability method that uses an LLM agent with a vector database of activations and textual contexts to discover concepts, outperforming expensive training-based methods like SAEs and activation oracles on multiple tasks, suggesting current training-based methods don't extract insights beyond their training data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether expensive training-based interpretability methods (SAEs, activation oracles) actually surface insights that go beyond what is recoverable from the training dataset itself, and to propose a cheaper alternative.

Method: HARP (Hypothesis-driven Agentic Retrieval and Probing): an LLM agent equipped with a vector database of activations paired with textual contexts and tools for activation manipulation (projecting out directions, computing differences/averages). The agent iteratively queries the database, forms hypotheses, and validates them by constructing linear probes, all without training.

Result: HARP outperforms both activation oracles and SAE-based agents on concept discovery, concept detection, model steering, and secret elicitation, while being substantially cheaper and more flexible due to its training-free design.

Conclusion: Current training-based methods do not yet extract insights beyond their training data, motivating benchmarks that explicitly require interpretability methods to demonstrate such insights. Training-free approaches like HARP can match or exceed their performance at lower cost.

Abstract: Interpretability methods for neural network activations span a wide cost spectrum, from cheap, training-free techniques (such as linear probes, PCA, SVD) to more expensive training-based ones (such as SAEs and activation oracles). Training-based methods are typically more powerful, in part because they leverage large activation datasets during training. This raises a natural question - do they actually surface insights that go beyond what is recoverable from the training dataset itself? To address this, we equip an LLM agent with a vector database of activations paired with their textual contexts, along with tools for manipulating activations - projecting out directions in latent space, computing activation differences and averages. The agent iteratively queries the database, forms hypotheses from the retrieved samples, and validates them by constructing linear probes. We call this method HARP, for Hypothesis-driven Agentic Retrieval and Probing. Despite not involving any training, HARP outperforms both activation oracles and SAE-based agents on concept discovery, concept detection, model steering, and secret elicitation. The training-free design also makes HARP substantially cheaper and more flexible: new datasets can be indexed on demand whenever existing ones prove insufficient. More broadly, our results suggest that current training-based methods do not yet extract insights beyond their training data, and motivate benchmarks that explicitly require interpretability methods to demonstrate such insights. We release our code at https://github.com/SriramB-98/HARP

</details>


### [230] [EA-RMENet -- Path Loss Prediction in Urban Environments using Deep Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16449)
*Jonathan O'Shea,Conor Brennan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EA-RMENet is a deep learning model using U-Net with EfficientNetB5 encoder, Attention Gates, and ASPP for efficient and accurate radio map estimation, achieving low RMSE and fast inference.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current path loss prediction methods struggle to balance accuracy and computational efficiency, motivating the need for a model that achieves both in radio map estimation.

Method: EA-RMENet employs a U-Net architecture with an EfficientNetB5 encoder (compound scaling), Attention Gated skip connections (feature suppression), and Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (multi-scale context capture).

Result: Test prediction RMSE of 0.0334 on RadioMapSeer3D dataset; inference time 0.022 seconds/sample; ranked 3rd in ICASSP 2023 challenge with RMSE 0.0406.

Conclusion: EA-RMENet demonstrates strong potential for real-world radio map estimation, effectively balancing accuracy and computational speed.

Abstract: Accurate path loss prediction is a critical component of wireless network planning. Current path loss prediction methods typically struggle to balance the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency. This paper proposes the Efficient Attention Radio Map Estimation Network (EA-RMENet) which is an image data-driven, deep learning (DL) model designed for radio map estimation (RME). EA-RMENet uses a U-Net framework with an EfficientNetB5 encoder, Attention Gated (AG) skip connections, and Atrous Spatial Pyramid Pooling (ASPP). The EfficientNet encoder uses compound scaling to balance accuracy and efficiency. AG skip connections suppress irrelevant features, and the ASPP captures a multi-scale context. The model has a test prediction RMSE of 0.0334 on the RadioMapSeer3D dataset with an inference time of 0.022 seconds/sample. In the ICASSP 2023 Radio Map Prediction Challenge, the model ranks third with a competitive RMSE of 0.0406 this highlights the models potential for real-world RME.

</details>


### [231] [Compact convolutional neural networks for AI-based drone detection system](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16455)
*Gábor Farkas,Gábor Fazekas,Karakai Patrik,András Németh,Gábor Farkas*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes using lightweight convolutional neural networks on rasterized time-domain images (instead of spectrograms) to detect FPV drone video signals from SDR captures, achieving high accuracy with low computational cost suitable for embedded systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The need for compact, reliable detection systems for first-person-view drones in complex electromagnetic environments, exploiting their continuous RF video transmissions for early detection.

Method: SDR-based capture → rasterized time-domain image conversion → lightweight CNN model training on ~40k labeled images → GNU Radio real-time integration.

Result: Compact models achieve high detection accuracy with low computational requirements, eliminating frequency-domain preprocessing while maintaining comparable accuracy to spectrogram-based methods.

Conclusion: Time-domain imaging with lightweight CNNs is a viable, computationally efficient alternative to spectrogram-based RF drone detection, well-suited for embedded warfare applications.

Abstract: The increasing use of first-person-view drones in modern conflicts has created a demand for compact and reliable detection systems capable of operating in complex electromagnetic environments. These drones continuously transmit video signals through onboard video transmitters, generating radio-frequency emissions that can be exploited for early detection. This study investigates the use of lightweight convolutional neural networks for automated detection of drone signals captured by a software-defined radio-based electronic warfare framework. Samples are converted into rasterized time-domain images, providing a computationally efficient input representation suitable for embedded systems. Several custom model architectures were designed and benchmarked in terms of accuracy, model size, and inference performance using a dataset containing approximately 40,000 labeled images. In addition to offline testing, the models were integrated into a GNU Radio signal processing chain for real-time evaluation. The results show that compact models can achieve high detection accuracy while maintaining low computational requirements, making them suitable for embedded radio-frequency monitoring applications. Compared with existing spectrogram-based RF detection methods, the proposed approach eliminates frequency-domain preprocessing and achieves comparable accuracy with significantly reduced computational cost.

</details>


### [232] [K-IPO: Kendall-constrained Importance Preserving Oversampling for Imbalanced Tabular Data](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16478)
*Marios Tyrovolas,Argiris Sofotasios,Dimitris Metaxakis,Georgios Mermigkis,George Georgoulas,Panagiotis Hadjidoukas,Chrysostomos Stylios*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: K-IPO is a "generate-then-select" oversampling framework that preserves feature importance rankings during data augmentation by accepting synthetic samples only if they maintain a minimum Kendall's τ correlation with the original ranking, achieving better preservation, consistency, and class separability across 20 datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing oversampling methods distort feature importance rankings in model explanations, and no prior work has actively tried to prevent this distortion.

Method: K-IPO iteratively generates minority-class candidates and only accepts those maintaining a user-defined minimum Kendall's τ correlation with the reference feature importance ranking, with optional stricter constraints on top-ranked features.

Result: Evaluated on 20 imbalanced binary classification datasets, K-IPO achieved best/tied-best in feature importance preservation, explanation consistency, and class separability, while generally improving predictive performance with competitive computational overhead.

Conclusion: K-IPO effectively preserves feature importance rankings during oversampling without sacrificing predictive performance or computational efficiency.

Abstract: Oversampling is widely used to address class imbalance in tabular classification, but existing methods can distort the feature importance ranking underlying model explanations. Although recent studies have quantified this distortion by comparing real and synthetic data, none have actively sought to prevent it. In this paper, we introduce Kendall-constrained Importance-Preserving Oversampling (K-IPO), a generator-agnostic, "generate-then-select" framework that preserves the original data's feature importance ranking during augmentation. K-IPO iteratively generates minority-class candidates and accepts them only if their inclusion maintains a user-defined minimum Kendall's tau (τ) correlation with the reference ranking. Optionally, stricter constraints can be applied to the highest-ranked features. We evaluated K-IPO on 20 imbalanced binary classification datasets using three classifiers and multiple explanation methods. In most cases, K-IPO achieved the best or tied-best results in feature importance preservation, explanation consistency, and class separability. It also generally improved predictive performance while maintaining competitive computational overhead.

</details>


### [233] [Leakage-Robust Evaluation and Data-Scale Sensitivity of Attention-Enhanced Multi-Task Learning for Joint Fault Diagnosis and Remaining Useful Life Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16493)
*Md Mahamudur Rahaman Shamim,Md. Nuruzzaman,Zannatul Ferdus,Md Rajib Ahmed,Abieer Nwshad Anward,Mohammad Tooneer,Johir Uddin Khan,Khalid Hossen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper reveals that naive train/test splitting of sliding-window sequences in multi-task predictive maintenance models can artificially inflate or deflate performance (e.g., 20-60% real accuracy vs. 99.9% fake). The authors propose a chunk-based, leakage-audited splitting protocol, evaluate AMTLNet on three benchmarks, and show that multi-task training stability depends on label provenance rather than task type, with the attention branch being the key contributor to regression stability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate how sliding-window sequence splitting biases multi-task learning performance in predictive maintenance, and to address the critical issue of data leakage that can produce misleading accuracy (0% to 99.9%).

Method: Proposes a chunk-based, leakage-audited splitting protocol for sliding-window data. Uses AMTLNet (attention-enhanced multi-task architecture) evaluated on C-MAPSS, IMS, and Hydraulic datasets with five seeds, one-way ANOVA, and Tukey HSD tests for robust comparison.

Result: Naive splitting inflates accuracy from 20-60% to 99.9%. On C-MAPSS, AMTLNet achieves 84.12% classification accuracy and R2=0.86, matching single-task baselines. Multi-task training is unstable on smaller datasets with asymmetric failures (classification degrades for Bearing, regression for Hydraulic). Removing attention branch reduces R2 from 0.861 to 0.766.

Conclusion: Task-specific stability in multi-task models depends more on label provenance than on task type. The authors contribute a reusable leakage-audit protocol, seed-transparent evaluation, and a framework for deciding when joint training is appropriate under data scarcity.

Abstract: Multi-task deep learning models that jointly perform fault classification and remaining useful life (RUL) regression are increasingly used in predictive maintenance, yet reported performance can be strongly affected by how sliding-window sequences are split into training and test sets. We investigate this issue using AMTLNet, an attention-enhanced multi-task architecture, on three public benchmarks: NASA C-MAPSS, NASA IMS, and the UCI Hydraulic System dataset. We show that naive splitting can inflate classification accuracy from a genuine 20-60 percent to 99.9 percent, or reduce it to 0 percent through degenerate class representation. To address this, we introduce a chunk-based, leakage-audited splitting protocol and evaluate all models using five seeds, one-way ANOVA, and Tukey HSD tests. On C-MAPSS, with 19,976 leakage-free training windows, AMTLNet matches a single-task CNN-LSTM baseline in classification, achieving 84.12 +/- 0.96 percent accuracy with Tukey p = 1.0, and reaches an R2 of 0.86 +/- 0.01 while significantly outperforming a naive multi-task baseline. On the smaller Bearing and Hydraulic datasets, multi-task training is unstable, but the failure mode differs: classification degrades for Bearing, whereas regression degrades for Hydraulic. We relate this asymmetry to label provenance and propose a practical framework for deciding when joint training is appropriate under data scarcity. Ablation results show that the multi-head attention branch is the main contributor to regression stability. Removing it reduces R2 from 0.861 to 0.766 and more than doubles classification variance, whereas the convolutional branch contributes little to regression despite using about one-third of the parameters. This study contributes a reusable leakage-audit protocol, seed-transparent evaluation, and evidence that task-specific stability depends more on label provenance than on task type.

</details>


### [234] [Feedback Attribution and Representation Geometry: Metrics for Comparing Individual and Shared Rewards in MARL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16524)
*Tasha Pais,Richard Higgins*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes two low-overhead diagnostics (EffRank/n and D_act) to measure whether team-averaged vs. individual rewards leave a geometric or behavioral signature on learned representations in cooperative multi-agent RL, finding that observation (not reward attribution) drives geometry, while reward attribution mainly affects behavior.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine whether the common practice of using team-averaged rewards in cooperative multi-agent RL leaves measurable geometric or behavioral signatures on learned representations, and to propose efficient diagnostics for detecting reward-attribution effects.

Method: Proposes EffRank/n (effective rank normalized by agent count) and D_act (mean pairwise KL divergence between agents' action distributions) as low-overhead diagnostics; tests on MAPPO agents in SMACv2 protoss_5_vs_5 with observation × reward-attribution comparison (unit type observed vs. masked; individual damage-contribution reward vs. shared team reward).

Result: Geometry follows observation rather than reward: shared and individual rewards have similar EffRank/n (0.31 vs 0.29) and probe accuracy (0.75 vs 0.73) with unit type observed. D_act is higher under individual rewards (1.23 vs 1.07). Masking unit type reduces above-chance probe signal by more than half (to 0.49).

Conclusion: Individually rewarded agents are competent and separable by role, but observation explains geometry while reward attribution mainly affects behavior. Geometric diagnostics must control for observed role information and test persistent roles not directly observed. EffRank/n and D_act add <5% overhead.

Abstract: Cooperative multi-agent RL systems routinely use team-averaged rewards, a feedback-attribution choice that gives each agent the team outcome regardless of its individual contribution. We ask whether this leaves a measurable signature, geometric or behavioral, on learned representations. We propose EffRank/$n$ (effective rank normalized by agent count) and $D_\text{act}$ (mean pairwise KL divergence between agents' action distributions) as low-overhead diagnostics for reward-attribution effects, then test them on competent MAPPO agents in SMACv2 \texttt{protoss\_5\_vs\_5}, where unit type is encoded in the observation. In an observation $\times$ reward-attribution comparison (unit type observed vs.\ masked; individual damage-contribution reward vs.\ shared team reward), geometry follows observation rather than reward. With unit type observed, shared and individual rewards have similar EffRank/$n$ ($0.31{\pm}0.03$ vs.\ $0.29{\pm}0.02$) and probe accuracy ($0.75{\pm}0.05$ vs.\ $0.73{\pm}0.05$, both $\gg 1/3$ chance), while $D_\text{act}$ leans higher under individual rewards ($1.23{\pm}0.06$ vs.\ $1.07{\pm}0.20$). Masking unit type cuts the above-chance probe signal by more than half, to $0.49$ in both reward arms. In short: individually rewarded agents are competent and separable by role, but on SMACv2 the observation explains the geometry and reward attribution shows up mainly in behavior. Thus geometric diagnostics must control for observed role information and test persistent roles that are not directly observed. EffRank/$n$ and $D_\text{act}$ add $<$5\% overhead.

</details>


### [235] [Hierarchical Domain Generalization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16528)
*Chenxiao Yang,Zhiyuan Li,Shai Ben-David,Nathan Srebro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper studies hierarchical domain generalization and shows that the train/test domain partition, not just hypothesis class complexity, fundamentally determines generalization failure, making domain structure a necessary consideration in generalization theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand why hierarchical domain generalization fails and to show that domain structure, not just hypothesis class complexity, is a fundamental cause.

Method: Theoretical analysis of hierarchical domain generalization as extrapolation from finite observed regions, considering arbitrary domain hierarchies and train/test domain partitions.

Result: Regardless of how small the hypothesis class or how large the training size, some domain partition will cause generalization failure for some target domain.

Conclusion: Modern generalization theory must treat domain structure as a first-class object.

Abstract: We study hierarchical domain generalization as a problem of extrapolation from finite observed regions to an entire instance space, replacing i.i.d. sampling with arbitrary domain hierarchies. We show that the central obstruction is not only the complexity of the hypothesis class, but the train/test domain partition through which evidence is revealed. In particular, no matter how small the class or how large the training size, some partition makes generalization fail for some target. These results suggest that modern generalization theory must treat domain structure as a first-class object.

</details>


### [236] [Building2Building: A Large Scale Benchmark for Generalizable Real-World Reinforcement Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16534)
*Vincent Taboga,Justin Veilleux,Doseok Jang,Anushree Rankawat,Pierre-Luc Bacon*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: B2B is a large-scale HVAC control benchmark suite built on EnergyPlus with a parametric building generator, featuring diverse configurations and heterogeneous observation/action spaces for studying transfer, multi-task learning, and meta-learning in RL.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing RL benchmarks lack diversity and complexity to rigorously study generalization and transfer, while learned policies remain brittle to real-world changes in dynamics, action spaces, observation spaces, or goals.

Method: B2B uses EnergyPlus with a parametric building generator to systematically create diverse building configurations with heterogeneous observation and action spaces, fully compatible with the Gymnasium interface.

Result: Defines benchmark tasks for goal adaptation, dynamics adaptation, action-space shifts, and cross-domain transfer, providing standardized evaluation protocols for systematic investigation of generalization in continuous control.

Conclusion: B2B enables rigorous study of RL generalization and transfer in a realistic, physically grounded setting, with societal impact potential through improved large-scale HVAC control in energy-intensive buildings.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has achieved strong results in control, yet learned policies remain brittle to changes in dynamics, action spaces, observation spaces, or goals, a critical limitation for real-world deployment. Existing benchmarks offer limited diversity and complexity, making it difficult to rigorously study transfer, multi-task learning, and meta-learning in RL. We introduce Building2Building (B2B), a large-scale suite of realistic Heating, Ventilation, and Air Conditioning (HVAC) control environments built on EnergyPlus, a state-of-the-art building simulator. B2B is fully compatible with the Gymnasium interface and features a parametric building generator, enabling the systematic generation of diverse building configurations with heterogeneous observation and action spaces. Based on this suite, we define benchmark tasks targeting key open challenges in RL, including goal adaptation, dynamics adaptation, action-space shifts, and cross-domain transfer. By providing a large-scale, diverse, and physically grounded testbed with standardized evaluation protocols, B2B enables systematic investigation of generalization and transfer in continuous control. Beyond advancing research on generalization in RL, this new benchmark also carries significant societal implications by enabling improved HVAC control at scale, one of the most energy-intensive systems in buildings.

</details>


### [237] [Discrete Ricci Curvature on Protein Contact Graphs for Lightweight Fold Classification](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16553)
*Jianru Shen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that lightweight Ricci curvature descriptors (22-dim) from protein contact graphs outperform mean-pooled ESM-2 (150M) embeddings for fold classification, and combining Ricci with persistent homology achieves best results (macro-F1 0.71 on CATH, 0.68 on SCOPe) with only 112 dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Direct comparisons between lightweight handcrafted structural descriptors and pretrained protein language model embeddings for fold classification remain limited.

Method: They compute Ollivier-Ricci and Forman-Ricci edge curvature distributions on Calpha contact graphs, then derive 22-dimensional fixed-length features from summary statistics and quantiles. These are evaluated on CATH and SCOPe benchmarks against geometry, contact-graph statistics, persistent homology, and mean-pooled ESM-2 (150M) baselines.

Result: Ricci curvature alone (22-dim) outperforms mean-pooled ESM-2 on both datasets. Combining Ricci with persistent homology achieves macro-F1 of 0.71 on CATH and 0.68 on SCOPe with 112 dimensions.

Conclusion: Lightweight interpretable graph descriptors offer a practical alternative to pretrained protein language model embeddings in the fold classification regime.

Abstract: Protein fold classification can be approached via sequence-based representations or structural descriptors, but direct comparisons between lightweight handcrafted descriptors and pretrained protein language model embeddings remain limited. We investigate discrete Ricci curvature on Calpha contact graphs as a lightweight structural descriptor for fold classification. Each protein domain is represented by a 22-dimensional fixed-length feature derived from summary statistics and quantiles of Ollivier-Ricci and Forman-Ricci edge curvature distributions. We evaluate on CATH top-10 Topology classification and on the ASTRAL 40%-identity SCOPe top-10 Fold benchmark, comparing against geometry, contact-graph statistics, persistent homology, and mean-pooled ESM-2 (150M) baselines. On both datasets, lightweight structural descriptors substantially outperform mean-pooled ESM-2 embeddings, with a larger performance gap on the ASTRAL 40% SCOPe benchmark. Ricci alone uses 22 dimensions, or 3.4% of the ESM-2 baseline dimensionality, and already outperforms mean-pooled ESM-2 on both datasets. Combining Ricci with persistent homology yields the strongest performance, achieving macro-F1 of 0.71 on CATH and 0.68 on SCOPe with a 112-dimensional feature vector. These results identify a regime where lightweight interpretable graph descriptors offer a practical alternative to pretrained protein language model embeddings.

</details>


### [238] [Learning from World Feedback: Why Model Uncertainty Fails as a Risk Signal in Model-Based RL](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16591)
*Zhaohui Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes RLxF principles for safe model-based control, showing that world-feedback signals (lidar, time-to-collision, outcome-supervised model) outperform internal model uncertainty proxies for safety, reducing collisions from 26-34% to 1-14%.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard MBRL uses internal model uncertainty as a safety proxy, but this paper shows it is anti-correlated with actual safety (r<0.15). The authors argue learning signals should come from world feedback rather than internal model proxies.

Method: The authors instantiate RLxF principles in safe model-based control using MPC across four world-model architectures. They compare model-internal uncertainty penalties against three world-feedback signals: minimum lidar (sensor margin), time-to-collision, and an outcome-supervised feedback model (g_psi) trained on prior collision labels.

Result: Model-internal safety proxies increased collisions from 26% to 34%. World-feedback signals reduced collisions to 1-14%. Statistical equivalence testing (TOST, n=200) showed MPC planning is equivalent across model architectures regardless of 2x MSE range. Model uncertainty and task risk have low empirical correlation (r<0.15).

Conclusion: Three RLxF principles are extracted: ground risk in world outcomes, validate proxies before deployment, and substitute outcome-trained feedback models when direct world signals are unavailable. These apply to both model-based control and LLM alignment methods like RLHF/verifier-based approaches.

Abstract: The RLxF programme argues that learning signals should come from world feedback rather than from internal model proxies. We instantiate this position in safe model-based control and distil it into three concrete design principles. Empirically, across four world-model architectures spanning a 2x MSE range, MPC planning is statistically equivalent (TOST, n=200), and dynamics-based uncertainty penalties increase collision rates from 26% to 34%: the standard MBRL safety proxy is anti-correlated with safety in this regime. Replacing the model-internal proxy with three world-feedback signals (a sensor-derived margin via minimum lidar, a temporal signal via time-to-collision, and an outcome-supervised feedback model g_psi trained on prior collision labels, structurally analogous to outcome-trained reward models in RLHF) reduces collisions to 1-14% without retraining the world model or the planner. The mechanism is structural: model uncertainty has support over state-prediction space, whereas task risk has support over constraint boundaries, with empirical correlation r < 0.15. From this we extract three RLxF principles (ground risk in world outcomes, validate proxies before deployment, and substitute outcome-trained feedback models when direct world signals are unavailable) and argue they apply equally to model-based control and to verifier-based or RLHF approaches in LLM alignment.

</details>


### [239] [Privacy Cost as Equity Input: A Group Fairness Criterion for Differentially Private Machine Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16620)
*Rakshit Naidu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper introduces the Privacy-Cost Equity Ratio (PCER), a fairness metric that measures a group's positive prediction rate normalized by its overfitting gap, treating privacy leakage as a form of harm that should be compensated by proportional benefit. Evaluated on benchmark datasets under DP-SGD, PCER reveals disparities (e.g., double disadvantage on COMPAS) missed by outcome-only fairness metrics.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing fairness audits focus on outcome disparities (e.g., accuracy gaps) but ignore that privacy cost itself is a harm. Groups may involuntarily bear greater membership inference risk, and fairness should account for who bears the cost of protection, not only who benefits from outcomes.

Method: Proposes PCER = (group's positive prediction rate) / (group's overfitting gap). The overfitting gap (train-test accuracy difference) upper-bounds membership inference vulnerability via a standard bound. PCER requires only per-group train/test accuracy, enabling post-hoc auditing without shadow models.

Result: Evaluated on six benchmark–attribute combinations (tabular and NLP) under DP-SGD at various privacy budgets. PCER reveals a persistent double disadvantage on COMPAS (protected group has higher privacy exposure and worse outcomes), which demographic parity gap masks. Under very strong privacy guarantees, overfitting collapses to a floor, making exposure-based audits uninformative.
