TL;DR: TAPAS is a throughput-adaptive perception strategy that uses Reinforcement Learning with RRM and GRU agent to dynamically adjust FPS targets and model-to-cluster mapping on mobile/edge platforms, achieving 93-100% throughput met rate with 76% energy savings on KITTI and 97% throughput met rate with 64% lower energy on unseen nuScenes dataset.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing perception strategies assume fixed FPS and static model-to-cluster mapping, causing over/under provisioning of throughput or unnecessary energy consumption across diverse dynamic scenes.

Method: Proposes TAPAS, a throughput-adaptive perception strategy using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with Reward Reasoning Model (RRM) and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU) agent to orchestrate perception tasks across heterogeneous mobile/edge platforms, enabling intelligent runtime resource allocation based on varying FPS targets.

Result: On KITTI: 93-100% throughput met rate, 76% energy savings. On unseen nuScenes: 97% throughput met rate, 64% lower energy compared to SOTA approaches.

Conclusion: TAPAS effectively addresses runtime throughput variation in autonomous perception by dynamically allocating resources, proving robustness and energy efficiency across both seen and unseen datasets.

Abstract: Autonomous systems rely on a perception module to navigate through dynamic environments. In real-world scenarios, the perception module's throughput requirements vary at runtime due to changes in scene complexity. However, existing perception strategies assume a fixed FPS and static model-to-cluster mapping, resulting in either over/under provision of throughput requirements or unnecessary energy consumption across diverse scenes. Addressing this challenge requires tightly coupled \textit{scene complexity awareness} to estimate an appropriate FPS target and \textit{dynamic model-to-cluster mapping} to deliver the required throughput at minimum energy. We propose a throughput-adaptive perception strategy for mobile/edge platforms, enabling intelligent runtime resource allocation based on varying FPS targets. We use Reinforcement Learning (RL) with RRM (Reward Reasoning Model) and a GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) agent to orchestrate perception tasks across heterogeneous mobile/edge platforms. We evaluate TAPAS on Jetson Orin NX across KITTI and unseen nuScenes. On the \textit{KITTI} dataset's test sequences, TAPAS achieves 93-100% throughput met rate while saving energy by 76%. On the unseen \textit{nuScenes} dataset, TAPAS maintains 97% throughput met rate with 64% lower energy compared to \textit{SOTA} approaches, proving its robustness.

</details>


### [293] [When Drift Detectors cry Wolf: False Alarm Rates in continuous ML Monitoring](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17336)
*Raj Shekhar Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper analyzes false positive behavior of five drift detectors (PSI, KS, MMD, LSDD, adversarial validation) under continuous monitoring conditions, revealing that small false positive rates accumulate into frequent alerts in production. PSI stabilizes with batch sizes >200, while KS, MMD, LSDD show persistent fluctuations. Bonferroni correction reduces false positives but hurts sensitivity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Drift detectors are typically evaluated on detection accuracy under synthetic shifts, ignoring false alarm accumulation under continuous monitoring in production, which leads to alarm fatigue.

Method: Empirically analyzes false positive behavior of five drift detectors (PSI, KS, MMD, LSDD, adversarial validation) under continuous monitoring across varying batch sizes, with and without Bonferroni correction.

Result: PSI is highly sensitive to batch size—frequent false alarms at small sizes but stabilizes >200 samples. KS, MMD, LSDD show persistent fluctuations but are more reliable than PSI in low-data regimes. Bonferroni correction reduces false positives at the cost of true positive sensitivity.

Conclusion: Provides systematic comparison of false positive behavior across drift detectors, identifies tradeoffs between stability and sensitivity, and offers practical guidelines for selecting and calibrating drift detectors in production ML systems.

Abstract: Drift detection is a core component of production machine learning monitoring systems, where detectors are used to compare incoming data with a reference distribution and trigger alerts when changes occur. However, these detectors are often evaluated in research settings that emphasize detection accuracy under synthetic shifts, while overlooking false alarms under continuous monitoring. In production environments, models are monitored repeatedly over time and across many features, and even small false positive rates can accumulate into frequent alerts, leading to alarm fatigue. We empirically analyze false positive behavior across five commonly used drift detectors: PSI, KS, MMD, LSDD, and adversarial validation. Consistent with existing literature, PSI exhibits strong sensitivity to batch size, producing frequent false alarms at small sample sizes; however, we further observe that its behavior stabilizes and improves substantially once batch sizes exceed approximately 200 samples. In contrast, KS, MMD, and LSDD display persistent fluctuations across batch sizes, while remaining comparatively more reliable than PSI in low-data regimes. Applying a Bonferroni correction reduces false positive rates, but often at the cost of reduced true positive sensitivity, reinforcing the well-known stability - sensitivity trade-off in drift detection. This work provides a systematic comparison of false positive behavior across multiple drift detectors under continuous monitoring conditions. We identify tradeoffs across detector families and provide practical guidelines for selecting and calibrating drift detectors in production ML systems.

</details>


### [294] [A multiverse-consensus pipeline for reproducible feature selection in untargeted LC-MS metabolomics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17345)
*Mohammed Saeed Al-Huraibi,Ihsan Yozgat,Ahmet Kaplan*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper applies multiverse analysis to untargeted LC-MS metabolomics, testing multiple preprocessing pipelines and feature-ranking methods to identify robust features that survive across different analytical choices.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Untargeted metabolomics relies on many preprocessing decisions, each with multiple defensible options. Analysts typically commit to one pipeline, making the dependence of results on unvaried choices invisible. The authors aim to expose and quantify analytical variability.

Method: An auditable, configuration-driven pipeline with (i) a ten-stage quality-control filter cascade logging each feature's fate, and (ii) a multiverse analysis over four preprocessing philosophies × four feature-ranking methods with bootstrap stability selection and label-permutation testing. Features recurring across paths enter a tiered consensus.

Result: On breast-cancer cell-line data (30,370 features), single pipelines returned 4-20 features with pairwise agreement as low as Jaccard = 0.05. Multiverse consensus retained 15 features (≥2/4 paths), with one recurring across all four. Pipeline-wide label-permutation test found no false discoveries in 50 null permutations.

Conclusion: Reporting only preprocessing-robust features with a complete audit trail converts hidden analytical degrees of freedom into explicit, inspectable output. Limitations include single-batch design and need for independent validation.

Abstract: Background: Untargeted LC-MS metabolomics requires a long chain of preprocessing decisions, each with several equally defensible options. Analysts typically commit to one pipeline and report the resulting feature shortlist. How strongly that shortlist depends on choices that were never varied stays invisible. Results: We adapt multiverse analysis to untargeted metabolomics feature selection. We present an auditable, configuration-driven pipeline that (i) applies a ten-stage quality-control filter cascade in which every feature's fate is logged, and (ii) runs the downstream analysis as a multiverse over four contrasting preprocessing philosophies, each combined with four feature-ranking methods under bootstrap stability selection and label-permutation testing. Only features recurring across paths enter a tiered consensus. On a demonstration dataset of five breast-cancer cell lines (30,370 detected features), the four single pipelines individually returned shortlists of 4-20 features whose pairwise agreement was as low as Jaccard = 0.05. The multiverse consensus retained 15 features (>=2/4 paths), of which one recurred across all four, although two paths (sharing normalization and drift-correction methods) dominate the consensus. A pipeline-wide label-permutation test found no false discoveries in 50 null permutations. Conclusions: Reporting only preprocessing-robust features, with a complete kept/dropped audit trail, converts hidden analytical degrees of freedom into an explicit, inspectable output. We discuss scope and limitations, including single-batch design and the need for independent validation.

</details>


### [295] [Chebyshev Manifold Adaptation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17377)
*Jiawen Li*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ChebyMA is a new parameter-efficient fine-tuning method that uses Chebyshev polynomial bases for weight adaptation, achieving superior performance over LoRA and other baselines across CV and NLP tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a more expressive parameter-efficient adaptation method that overcomes the limited expressivity of low-rank approximations (like LoRA) by leveraging function approximation theory.

Method: ChebyMA replaces standard linear projections with multi-surface superposition of Chebyshev polynomial bases evaluated on learnable coordinates, combined with trainable coefficient matrices for continuous function approximation.

Result: ChebyMA consistently achieves superior parameter-accuracy Pareto fronts compared to full fine-tuning, LoRA, TLoRA, and StelLA on CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, AG News, and SST-2 datasets.

Conclusion: ChebyMA provides a theoretically grounded (Approximation Expressivity Theorem, Kolmogorov n-width) and empirically superior alternative for parameter-efficient fine-tuning with purely vectorized computations.

Abstract: The paper presents a new parameter-efficient adaptation method called ChebyMA (Chebyshev Manifold Adaptation). ChebyMA adopts weight matrices through a multi-surface superposition of Chebyshev polynomial bases evaluated on learnable coordinates and combined via trainable coefficient matrices, replacing standard linear projections with highly expressive continuous function approximation. Theoretically, we establish an Approximation Expressivity Theorem, proving from the perspective of function approximation theory that single-manifold ChebyMA guarantees convergence in Frobenius norm error of reconstruction. Besides, drawing on Kolmogorov $n$-width intuition, we demonstrate the expressive advantages of multi-manifold superposition ($S > 1$) in decoupling high-dimensional complex features. Experimental results on Computer Vision CIFAR datasets(CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100)\cite{CIFAR} and Natural Language Processing (AG News, SST-2) datasets demonstrate that ChebyMA consistently achieves a superior parameter-accuracy Pareto front compared to standard full-parameter fine-tuning, LoRA\cite{LoRA}, TLoRA\cite{TLoRA}, and StelLA\cite{StelLA}. ChebyMA significantly outperforms other tested methods in tested datasets, validating its solid theoretical foundation for generality with purely vectorized computations.

</details>


### [296] [CoEvoP&R: Co-Evolving Placement Objectives with Routing Feedback via Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17398)
*Ruogu Chen,Weihua Xiao,Ramesh Karri,Jie Han*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoEvoP&R uses LLMs to automatically evolve analytical placement objectives, achieving significant improvements in routed wirelength (16.9%), congestion (36.7%), and timing slack across multiple chip design benchmarks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing analytical placers rely on differentiable surrogates (HPWL, density penalties) that misalign with actual downstream routed and timing quality. Prior solutions either require expert retuning (human-designed terms) or are difficult to debug/deploy (learned black-box surrogates).

Method: An LLM-based framework that iteratively proposes readable differentiable placement objectives. At each generation, the LLM receives prompts combining restricted objective interface, baseline context, archived candidates, and routing-related feedback. Proposed objectives are embedded in DREAMPlace, evaluated via timing proxy and actual router, and stored with feedback for subsequent generations.

Result: On ChiP-Bench Nangate45: 16.9% routed wirelength reduction, 36.7% congestion reduction, 0.70 ns WNS improvement, 912 ns TNS reduction over DREAMPlace. On ICCAD 2015 Superblue: 5.4% wirelength reduction, 23.2% congestion reduction.

Conclusion: CoEvoP&R effectively bridges the gap between analytical placement surrogates and actual routing/timing quality by automatically evolving objectives via LLMs, outperforming native baselines across diverse designs.

Abstract: Analytical placers rely on differentiable objective functions to guide placement, typically combining intermediate surrogate metrics such as half-perimeter wirelength (HPWL) and cell-density penalties. However, these placement-stage surrogates remain misaligned with downstream routed and timing quality. Prior work reduces this gap with human-designed terms or learned black-box surrogates, but the former requires expert retuning and the latter is difficult to explain, debug, or deploy in analytical placement flows. CoEvoP&R addresses these limitations with a large language model (LLM)-based framework that automatically evolves analytical placement objectives. At each generation, the prompt combines the restricted objective interface, baseline context, and archived prior candidates with routing-related feedback from placement, timing proxy, and routing tools. The LLM proposes readable differentiable objectives, which are embedded and validated in DREAMPlace, evaluated through a timing proxy and an actual router, and stored with their feedback to guide later generations. Across eight ChiP-Bench Nangate45 designs and three seeds, CoEvoP&R reduces post-route routed wirelength and congestion by 16.9% and 36.7%, with gains of 0.70 ns in worst negative slack and a 912 ns reduction in total negative slack magnitude over native DREAMPlace. Across eight ICCAD 2015 Superblue designs, it reduces post-route routed wirelength and congestion by 5.4% and 23.2%. Code is available at https://github.com/FCHXWH823/CoEvoP-R.git.

</details>


### [297] [CORAL: Learning Amyloid Fibril Ligand Docking with Cooperative Binding Rewards](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17412)
*Yasheng Sun,Bohan Li,Youqi Tao,Jürgen Schmidhuber*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CORAL is a reinforcement learning framework for docking ligands onto amyloid fibrils, addressing the scarcity of co-crystal structures and the unique cross-β groove binding geometry through cooperative ligand-ligand stacking rewards.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Amyloid fibrils are key targets in neurodegenerative diseases, but existing docking models fail because (1) resolved amyloid-ligand co-crystal structures are extremely scarce, hindering supervised training, and (2) amyloid binding involves intercalation into cross-β grooves with cooperative stacking along the fibril axis, a geometry not captured by standard docking models.

Method: CORAL uses reinforcement learning to train a generative docking model that produces ligand poses tailored to cross-β groove geometry. The reward function combines protein-ligand docking affinity with cooperative ligand-ligand stacking energy. A curated evaluation set was constructed from model-generated poses validated by domain experts.

Result: On both experimentally resolved structures and the expert-validated evaluation set, CORAL achieves improved pose quality and better binding affinity correlation compared to existing docking baselines.

Conclusion: CORAL effectively addresses the unique challenges of amyloid-ligand docking by directly modeling cooperative stacking in cross-β grooves, offering a promising tool for diagnostic and therapeutic development in neurodegenerative diseases.

Abstract: A hallmark of neurodegenerative diseases such as Alzheimer's and Parkinson's is the aberrant aggregation of proteins into amyloid fibrils, and small molecules that selectively bind to these fibrils hold promise as diagnostics, imaging probes, and therapeutics. Predicting how such ligands bind to fibril targets, however, presents two fundamental challenges. First, resolved co-crystal structures of amyloid-ligand complexes are exceptionally scarce; even with recent advances in cryo-EM only a handful have been structurally characterized, making supervised training of docking models impractical for this target class. Second, amyloid fibrils present a binding mode fundamentally different from globular proteins: ligands intercalate into longitudinal cross-$β$ grooves and stack cooperatively along the fibril axis, a geometry that existing docking models are not designed to capture. To address these challenges, we present CORAL (COopeRative Amyloid Ligand docking), a reinforcement learning framework that trains a generative docking model to produce ligand pose distributions tailored to the cross-$β$ groove geometry. Our reward explicitly incorporates cooperative ligand-ligand stacking energy alongside protein-ligand docking affinity, directly capturing the distinctive binding geometry of amyloid fibrils. We further introduce a curated evaluation set of amyloid-ligand complexes constructed from model-generated poses validated by domain experts. Experiments on both experimentally resolved structures and this evaluation set demonstrate improved pose quality and binding affinity correlation over existing docking baselines.

</details>


### [298] [Grounded verification of chemical and materials reasoning: detection is the bottleneck](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17417)
*Can Polat,Mustafa Kurban,Erchin Serpedin,Hasan Kurban*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a tiered verifier system that detects and repairs errors in LLM-generated chemical information by checking claims against authoritative databases, reducing formula errors from 22% to 4% with 3.2x fewer retrievals than retrieval-augmented approaches, though detection recall remains the key bottleneck.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs produce fluent but unreliable chemical information, especially for rare/long-tail entities where confidence is low, and existing blanket retrieval approaches are costly and inefficient.

Method: A tiered verifier that extracts checkable claims from LLM outputs, verifies them against authoritative databases and physics principles, then feeds verified references into a gated correction loop when errors are detected.

Result: Formula errors reduced from 22% to 4% with 3.2x fewer retrievals than blanket augmentation; repair success rate 80-97% when flags fire; accuracy improved from 83% to 90% when verifier scope reaches the deliverable; isotope half-life errors dropped from 11% to 0%.

Conclusion: Database-grounded verification is effective for correcting LLM chemical errors with high efficiency, but detection recall is the binding constraint—improving in-loop detection is more critical than repair capability.

Abstract: Large language models confabulate chemical objects (molecular formulas, space groups, formation energies) in fluent reasoning traces, concentrated on long-tail entities where confidence is least trustworthy. Deterministic, database-grounded verification can catch and repair such errors without the coverage cost of blanket retrieval; the binding constraint, we find, is detection, not repair. Our tiered verifier extracts each checkable claim, checks it against authoritative databases and physics, and feeds the reference into a gated correction loop. Across four models and 528 condition-pinned prompts, gated correction cuts committed-formula error from 22% to 4% at $3.2\times$ fewer retrievals than blanket augmentation, beating a conversational oracle. Repair succeeds wherever a flag fires (80--97%); the bottleneck is in-loop detection recall. Grounding improves the final answer only when the verifier's scope reaches the deliverable (83% to 90%), and the lift appears only where extractable long-tail error exists: absent on near-ceiling physical constants, large on isotope half-lives (11% to 0%).

</details>


### [299] [Decoder-Preserving Sparse Autoencoders: Which Readouts Survive Sparse Compression?](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17425)
*Aniket Deshpande*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) can preserve different linearly decodable signals even with equal reconstruction error and sparsity. The authors formalize this ambiguity and propose Decoder-Preserving SAEs (DPSAE) that combine reconstruction loss with a distortion metric between ridge-prediction operators. DPSAE reduces held-out decoder distortion by ~10.6-11.4% on GPT-2 while matching reconstruction quality, showing reconstruction quality alone doesn't determine which linear readouts survive sparse compression.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Formalize the ambiguity that equal reconstruction error and sparsity in SAEs can preserve different linearly decodable signals, showing reconstruction quality alone is insufficient to determine which linear readouts survive compression.

Method: Formalize ambiguity as matrix-valued distortion between optimal ridge-prediction operators; propose Decoder-Preserving SAEs (DPSAE) combining this distortion with reconstruction loss; apply rank relaxation with isotropic and structured task priors.

Result: DPSAE reduces held-out decoder distortion by 10.6-11.4% on GPT-2 small block 8 across three paired runs while matching reconstruction NMSE. Checkpoints pass average natural-text output-KL noninferiority test. One Pythia pair shows no improvement in probes restricted to few sparse features.

Conclusion: Reconstruction quality does not determine which refitted linear readouts survive sparse compression; readout preservation is distinct from learning cleaner benchmark concepts or preserving every frozen-model behavior.

Abstract: Sparse autoencoders (SAEs) compress model activations into sparse codes, but equal reconstruction error and sparsity can preserve different linearly decodable signals. We formalize this ambiguity as a matrix-valued distortion between optimal ridge-prediction operators and train decoder-preserving SAEs by combining this distortion with reconstruction loss. In a rank relaxation, an isotropic task prior saturates per-mode omission costs without changing PCA's ordering, whereas a structured prior can change which modes are retained. A controlled sparse experiment shows that a declared prior protects held-out combinations from its task subspace. On GPT-2 small block 8, DPSAE reduces held-out decoder distortion by 10.6--11.4% across three paired runs while matching reconstruction NMSE. The same checkpoints pass an average natural-text output-KL noninferiority test, but one matched Pythia pair shows no improvement in probes restricted to a few sparse features. These results show that reconstruction quality does not determine which refitted linear readouts survive sparse compression, and that readout preservation is distinct from learning cleaner benchmark concepts or preserving every frozen-model behavior.

</details>


### [300] [Abliteration Is Not a Scalpel: Off-Target Effects of Refusal Removal on Decision Disposition Across Model Families](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17427)
*Aleksander Fafuła*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Abliteration (removing refusal direction from model weights) creates measurable side effects beyond removing refusals: abliterated models become more optimistic, verbose, and use fewer uncertainty words, with inconsistent effects on confidence across model families.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To test whether "abliteration" - the standard technique for creating uncensored models by deleting a model's refusal direction - is a clean surgical operation or introduces unintended behavioral side effects beyond removing refusals.

Method: Compared base vs abliterated versions of two MoE model families (Gemma-4-26B and Qwen3-30B) on 21,600 forced up/down stock predictions (60 equities over 18 weeks), a task eliciting zero refusals. Used strict provenance controls (identical checkpoints, single abliteration author, identical serving stack, byte-identical prompts) and weeks-clustered bootstrap confidence intervals.

Result: Three replicated effects: (1) abliterated models are systematically more optimistic (+12.2pp Gemma, +7.4pp Qwen - preregistered), (2) produce longer justifications, (3) use fewer uncertainty words in self-critiques. One reversed sign: Gemma-abliterated became less confident, Qwen-abliterated more confident. No economic skill found (apparent edge was regime beta). Also discovered two contamination channels (mismatched-quantizer pilot, stale chat template).

Conclusion: Abliteration is not a clean surgery - it produces a measurably different decision-maker with systematic behavioral shifts, not simply "base model minus refusals." Deploying uncensored models as agents introduces unanticipated side effects. Toolchain artifacts are likely pervasive in studies of community-modified checkpoints.

Abstract: Abliteration - deleting a model's refusal direction from its weights - is the standard recipe behind popular "uncensored" open-weight models. We show the surgery is not clean. As a disposition probe we use 21,600 decisions under uncertainty - weekly up/down calls on 60 Warsaw Stock Exchange equities over 18 weeks, replayed through a frozen pipeline so the decision-layer model is the only variable. The task elicits no refusals at all, so any between-arm delta is pure side effect. Holding provenance constant (official BF16 checkpoints, a single abliteration author, an identical serving stack, one byte-identical frozen prompt), we compare base and abliterated arms of two Mixture-of-Experts families, Gemma-4-26B-A4B-it and Qwen3-30B-A3B-Instruct-2507. Three effects replicate across both families (weeks-clustered bootstrap CIs excluding zero): abliterated models are systematically more optimistic (+12.2 pp Gemma, +7.4 pp Qwen; the confirmed preregistered endpoint), justify themselves at greater length, and use fewer explicit uncertainty words in forced self-critiques (both exploratory). A fourth effect reverses sign: the same operation makes Gemma-abliterated less confident and Qwen-abliterated more (family CIs non-overlapping) - one weight surgery, opposite shifts in expressed confidence. Capability covariates rule out instruction-following degradation as the driver, and no arm shows economic skill: the apparent edge of abliterated arms is regime beta, not alpha. Our provenance audit also caught two independent contamination channels - a mismatched-quantizer pilot pair and a stale community chat template that silently mangled the rendered prompt - suggesting toolchain artifacts are the rule in studies of community-modified checkpoints. Whoever deploys an "uncensored" model as an agent is deploying a measurably different decision-maker, not the base model minus refusals.

</details>


### [301] [Calibrated Alzheimer's Conversion Risk in Mild Cognitive Impairment: Persistent Homology of Clinical Trajectories with Conformal Guarantees](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17442)
*Navin Bondade*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a leakage-audited, conformally calibrated model using persistent homology and topological features (H0 persistence entropy) to predict MCI-to-AD conversion, achieving AUC=0.840 with 90.4% cross-conformal coverage and demonstrating a novel topological biomarker for cognitive decline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MCI-to-AD conversion prediction models lack individual-level uncertainty estimates and rarely include transparent leakage audits, which inflate reported performance. The authors aim to address both gaps.

Method: 741 MCI subjects from ADNI with 4-year follow-up; five leakage sources corrected (naive AUC inflated +0.075). Vietoris-Rips persistent homology + sublevel-set proxies + trajectory slopes + engineered features (76 total) in a stacking ensemble with 5-fold CV. Cross-conformal prediction for individual risk guarantees.

Result: TDA features improved Cox C-index (+0.045) and RSF C-index (+0.014). Primary nested AUC=0.840; external AUC=0.879. H0 persistence entropy was top SHAP feature, significantly associated with APOE4 dosage (r=-0.191, p<0.0001). Cross-conformal coverage 90.4%±2.2% (target 90%); external 96.9%. Max fairness gap in FNR=0.092.

Conclusion: H0 persistence entropy is proposed as a topological biomarker of cognitive decline. The leakage-audited, conformally calibrated pipeline achieves competitive accuracy with individual-level uncertainty quantification, a first for this task.

Abstract: Background. Predicting conversion from mild cognitive impairment (MCI) to Alzheimer's disease (AD) is central to trial enrichment and care planning, yet existing models provide no individual-level uncertainty estimates and rarely include transparent leakage audits. We introduce the first application of persistent homology to longitudinal clinical trajectory point clouds for this task, and the first split-conformal individual risk guarantee for any AD-conversion model. Methods. We analysed 741 MCI subjects (240 converters, 32.4%) from ADNI with a uniform 4-year follow-up cap. Five leakage sources were corrected; without them a naive pipeline achieved AUC=0.934, inflated by +0.075. Vietoris-Rips persistent homology and sublevel-set proxies were combined with trajectory slopes and engineered features (76 total) in a stacking ensemble evaluated by 5-fold cross-validation. Results. Cox and Random Survival Forest models with TDA features achieved concordance C=0.799 and C=0.826 versus C=0.753 and C=0.812 without (+0.045 and +0.014). The primary nested AUC is 0.840 (same-fold bound 0.866); external AUC was 0.879 on a zero-overlap ADNI-2/GO/3 cohort. H0 persistence entropy was the top SHAP feature and significantly associated with APOE4 dosage (Spearman r=-0.191, p<0.0001, Bonferroni-corrected). Cross-conformal coverage was 90.4%+-2.2% (target 90%); empirical external coverage 96.9%. Maximum fairness gap in false-negative rate across seven subgroups was 0.092. Conclusions. We propose H0 persistence entropy as a topological biomarker of cognitive decline and demonstrate that a leakage-audited, conformally calibrated pipeline reaches competitive accuracy with individual-level uncertainty quantification not previously available for this task.

</details>


### [302] [Residual-Guided Multi-Resolution Refinement of Foundation Models: A Case Study in Drought Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17507)
*Wentao Gao,Jiuyong Li,Lin Liu,Thuc Duy Le,Jixue Liu,Yanchang Zhao,Yun Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RGMR is an inference-time framework that improves climate forecasting by applying multi-resolution refinement to frozen foundation models, reducing SPEI MSE by up to 18.9% across multiple sites.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expert climatologists use multi-scale temporal analysis and iterative error diagnosis, but current time series foundation models only do single-pass inference. The gap motivates a framework that bridges this discrepancy without retraining.

Method: RGMR is a residual-guided coarse-to-fine refinement wrapper applied at inference time to pre-trained TSFMs (TimesFM, TimeGPT, TabPFN). It does not update backbone parameters and is architecture-agnostic.

Result: Applied to drought forecasting with SPEI, RGMR consistently lowers test-set MSE across 3 South Australian sites and 3 additional regions. For TimesFM, one-month-ahead MSE is reduced by up to 18.9% (mean ~18.7%).

Conclusion: RGMR offers a practical, parameter-free way to deploy frozen TSFMs in regional climate forecasting, enhancing accuracy through structured refinement.

Abstract: Regional climate prediction presents unique challenges for time series foundation models, which typically process temporal patterns through single-pass inference. Expert climatologists, in contrast, employ multi-scale temporal analysis and iterative refinement based on systematic error diagnosis. We present RGMR (Residual-Guided Multi-Resolution Refinement), an inference-time framework that adapts pre-trained foundation models to perform structured coarse-to-fine refinement for climate forecasting without updating backbone parameters. Applied to drought forecasting using the Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI), RGMR is architecture-agnostic across the three TSFM backbones evaluated per site (TimesFM, TimeGPT, TabPFN) and consistently lowers test-set MSE on three South Australian sites and three additional regions outside South Australia. Applied to TimesFM, the wrapper reduces one-month-ahead SPEI MSE by up to 18.9\% across the three South Australian sites (mean reduction $\approx$18.7\%). Overall, RGMR provides a practical route for deploying frozen TSFMs in regional climate forecasting workflows.

</details>


### [303] [Retrieval-Augmented Interpretable Learning: Towards Task-Specific Zero-Shot Models in Healthcare](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17508)
*Sazan Mahbub,Caleb Ellington,Zhiyuan Li,Yixin Yang,Souvik Kundu,Ben Lengerich,Eric P. Xing*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RAIL is a probabilistic meta-learning framework that generates interpretable task-specific models from natural-language descriptions and a memory of prior models, achieving strong zero-shot (73.4%) and few-shot (73.2%) accuracy on long-tailed clinical prediction tasks while providing uncertainty estimates and feature-level explanations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Healthcare prediction tasks are highly long-tailed, new clinical targets arise frequently, and models must remain inspectable, uncertainty-aware, and compatible with human oversight. Existing supervised models fail in zero-shot or extreme few-shot settings (2-4 examples).

Method: RAIL uses a probabilistic meta-learning approach that retrieves related source tasks from memory, transfers structural information through coefficient space, and generates a new predictor in the original diagnostic-feature space. It synthesizes coefficient-space structure from natural-language task descriptions and a memory of previously learned task-specific predictors.

Result: 73.4% accuracy in zero-shot settings (where no supervised model can be trained), 73.2% accuracy in extreme few-shot regime (2-4 examples), while supervised models perform near chance. The framework also provides retrieval, uncertainty, and coefficient-level diagnostics.

Conclusion: RAIL suggests a path toward scalable clinical prediction systems that can adapt to new tasks while preserving interpretability and reliability, with support for human oversight through uncertainty-aware flagging.

Abstract: We introduce Retrieval-Augmented Interpretable Learning (RAIL), a probabilistic meta-learning framework for zero-shot generation of task-specific interpretable models that synthesizes coefficient-space structure from natural-language task descriptions and a memory of previously learned task-specific predictors. RAIL retrieves related source tasks, transfers structure through coefficient space, and generates a new predictor in the original diagnostic-feature space, enabling zero-shot and few-shot clinical procedure prediction with feature-level explanations. Its probabilistic formulation provides uncertainty over retrieval, model coefficients, and predictions, supporting reliability-aware deployment: uncertain predictions or unstable explanations can be flagged for additional clinical review rather than treated as automatic decisions. This makes RAIL particularly suited for healthcare settings, where prediction tasks are highly long-tailed, new clinical targets arise frequently, and models must remain inspectable, uncertainty-aware, and compatible with human oversight. Across long-tailed clinical procedure prediction tasks, RAIL maintains reliable performance across data-availability regimes: it achieves 73.4% accuracy in the held-out zero-shot settings, where no supervised task-specific model can be trained, and remains near 73.2% accuracy in the extreme few-shot regime with only 2-4 examples, where supervised task-specific models perform close to chance. RAIL further benefits from clinically informed task representations and yields retrieval, uncertainty, and coefficient-level diagnostics that make model behavior more transparent. These results suggest a path toward scalable clinical prediction systems that can adapt to new tasks while preserving interpretability and reliability.

</details>


### [304] [Lightweight Wrappers for Adapting Time Series Foundation Models to Regional Drought Forecasting](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17511)
*Wentao Gao,Jiuyong Li,Lin Liu,Thuc Duy Le,Jixue Liu,Yanchang Zhao,Yun Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: A lightweight, black-box adaptation framework (SMR² and MBB wrappers) improves frozen Time Series Foundation Models for regional climate forecasting via inference-time ensembling, achieving up to 26% MSE reduction on SPEI prediction without any backbone parameter access or fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: TSFMs show zero-shot promise but face practical barriers in regional climate forecasting: proprietary model weights, limited local training data, and constrained computational budgets make traditional fine-tuning infeasible.

Method: Two plug-and-play wrappers applied to frozen TSFMs at inference time: (1) SMR² decomposes input into multi-resolution temporal views, learns stride-specific residual corrections, and adaptively ensembles them; (2) MBB uses moving block bootstrap to create temporally coherent residual perturbations for ensemble-based point forecast stabilization. Both follow a bagging principle—diverse input/residual views forecast by same frozen backbone, then aggregated.

Result: Evaluated on 1-month-ahead SPEI prediction across multiple South Australia sites, the framework consistently improves forecasting across backbone models, achieving up to 26% MSE reduction over frozen backbones.

Conclusion: The approach enables practical, resource-constrained deployment of TSFMs for regional climate forecasting through inference-time ensembling, without needing access to model parameters or additional training.

Abstract: Large \emph{Time Series Foundation Models} (TSFMs) demonstrate strong zero-shot forecasting capabilities across diverse domains. However, their application to regional climate forecasting faces practical challenges: model weights are often proprietary, local training records are limited, and computational budgets are constrained, making traditional fine-tuning approaches infeasible. To address these constraints, we introduce a lightweight, black-box adaptation framework (requiring no access to backbone parameters and no backbone fine-tuning) that enhances frozen TSFMs at inference time through two plug-and-play wrappers: \textbf{SMR\textsuperscript{2}} (Stationarity aware multi-resolution Residual), which decomposes the input into multi-resolution temporal views, learns stride specific residual corrections that capture regional dynamics, then adaptively ensembles them into a single forecast, and \textbf{MBB} (Moving Block Bootstrap), which preserves temporal dependencies through block resampling and ensembles over temporally coherent residual perturbations to stabilize the point forecast. Both wrappers instantiate the same bagging style principle: they build diverse views of the input or its residuals, forecast each with the same frozen backbone, and aggregate, so all adaptation comes from inference time ensembling rather than any weight update. Evaluated on one month ahead Standardized Precipitation Evapotranspiration Index (SPEI) prediction across multiple sites in South Australia, our framework consistently improves forecasting performance across several backbone models, demonstrating up to 26\% mean squared error (MSE) reduction over the corresponding frozen backbone while enabling practical deployment in resource constrained regional forecasting systems.

</details>


### [305] [After the Euclidean Highway: Hyperbolic Expert AI as the Next Innovation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17513)
*Kwan Soo Shin,In Seok Kang,Munho Lee*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Hyperbolic losses should only be placed at the loss layer, not on adapters, to avoid training collapses; proven through theory and 220 GPU-hours of experiments across six SLMs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Expert domains (e.g., trees, hierarchies) are inherently hyperbolic, but applying hyperbolic geometry to adapters causes training collapses. The key question is not how much curvature to use, but where to place it.

Method: HySAT (Hyperbolic Structure-Aware Training) applies hyperbolic losses only at the loss layer. Tested on Llama 3.1 and EXAONE 3.5 with four adapter strategies, 18M corpus, using four-arm ablation and three propositions + lemma for theoretical proof.

Result: Loss-only placement trains stably (zero NaN over ~317K steps), while adapter-on-manifold collapses training (17 failures, ~220 GPU-hours). Four models deployed (one live consumer-facing, two open-weight).

Conclusion: Placement of hyperbolic geometry is a structural law, not a tunable knob; the preserved manifold invariant at the loss layer is the key to stable training.

Abstract: Expert domains are trees; the Euclidean transformer is not, diluting parent-child structure exponentially at depth. The hyperbolic turn left one question unasked: not how much of a network to curve, but where curvature may touch the gradient. Placement is a law, not a knob: the same geometry on a trainable adapter collapses training (seventeen training collapses, ~220 GPU-hours), yet at the loss layer alone it trains without one -- this is HySAT (Hyperbolic Structure-Aware Training), hyperbolic losses at the loss layer only. Across six expert SLMs we constructed and deployed (Llama 3.1 and EXAONE 3.5; four adapter strategies; 18.0M-sample corpus; zero NaN over ~317K optimizer steps), a matched four-arm ablation isolates the preserved manifold invariant, and three propositions and a lemma prove why loss-only placement is stable where adapter-on-manifold is not. Four models are operationally deployed (one live, consumer-facing), two open-weight, with per-step traces and a seventeen-incident failure ledger on Zenodo (CC-BY-4.0).

</details>


### [306] [FailureAtlas: A Taxonomy of Failure Modes in Multi-Provider LLM Serving Infrastructure](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17525)
*Vishal Pandey,Gopal Singh*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces \fa{}, a two-axis taxonomy for classifying failures in multi-provider LLM gateways by origin layer and detectability, revealing that the most severe failures are silent (return HTTP 200, pass health checks) and require semantic-level observability to detect.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Failure modes specific to the multi-provider LLM gateway architectural layer remain undocumented and scattered, with no unifying framework to understand them.

Method: The authors propose a two-axis taxonomy classifying failures by origin layer (Network/Transport, Streaming/Protocol, State/Session, Model Behavior, Governance/Cost) and detectability (Loud vs. Silent), populated with five verified catalog entries from public bug reports and stress testing.

Result: The most operationally severe failures are silent (HTTP 200, pass health checks, corrupt application state). Two silent failures were discovered first-hand: a concurrency race condition causing history loss and a streaming index collision corrupting tool-call payloads.

Conclusion: Silent failures in LLM gateways are the most dangerous, requiring semantic-level observability to detect rather than standard health checks.

Abstract: Multi-provider LLM gateways reverse proxies that route, load-balance, and rate-limit requests across foundation-model APIs have become critical production infrastructure. Yet the failure modes specific to this architectural layer remain undocumented, scattered across issue trackers and post-mortems with no unifying framework. We introduce \fa{}, a two-axis taxonomy that classifies failures by their \emph{origin layer} (Network/Transport, Streaming/Protocol, State/Session, Model~Behavior, Governance/Cost) and their \emph{detectability} (Loud vs.\ Silent). We populate this taxonomy with five verified catalog entries sourced from public bug reports and first-hand stress testing, each accompanied by a mechanistic root-cause analysis. Three entries include standalone reproduction scripts. Our principal finding is that the most operationally severe failures are \emph{silent}: they return HTTP~200, pass every standard health check, and corrupt application state in ways that require semantic-level observability to detect. Two such silent failures a concurrency race condition causing history loss and a streaming index collision corrupting tool-call payloads were discovered first-hand during \cb{} evaluation campaigns.

</details>


### [307] [Program Synthesis for Simulation-Based Inference: Joint Model Selection and Parameter Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17540)
*Siddharth Mishra-Sharma*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper introduces a framework that combines LLMs for program synthesis with neural simulation-based inference to jointly perform model selection and parameter estimation, enabling inference over model structures rather than just parameters within a fixed model.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Traditional simulation-based inference requires users to specify a simulator with a fixed model structure, limiting the ability to perform model selection or explore alternative model families.

Method: The framework uses LLMs to generate candidate simulator programs from natural language descriptions, iteratively refines them via feedback-driven mutation, and evaluates them using neural density estimation.

Result: On benchmarks (deterministic dynamics, stochastic epidemics, dark matter substructure inference), the method identifies plausible model families with accuracy reflecting data information content and model identifiability.

Conclusion: The approach extends simulation-based inference from parameter estimation within a fixed model to joint model selection and parameter estimation across a pool of candidate models.

Abstract: Neural simulation-based inference enables parameter estimation for complex models, but typically requires the user to specify a simulator encoding a fixed model structure. We present a framework for joint model selection and parameter estimation that combines large language models for program synthesis with neural simulation-based inference. Given a natural language description of the system and data under investigation, an LLM proposes candidate simulator programs which are iteratively refined via feedback-driven mutation and evaluated using neural density estimation. The approach enables simulation-based inference over a pool of models, not just parameters within a fixed model. On benchmarks spanning deterministic dynamics, stochastic epidemic models, and dark matter substructure inference from gravitational-lensing images, the method identifies plausible model families from open-ended prompts, with accuracy that reflects the information content of the data and identifiability of candidate models.

</details>


### [308] [Volatility-Aware Extreme Event Detection in High-Frequency Financial Markets](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17555)
*Maorufa Zaman,Haris Md Sahed*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a volatility-aware target formulation for detecting extreme price movements in high-frequency Bitcoin markets, using XGBoost on limit order book data. By incorporating volatility clustering into event labeling, the method improves PR-AUC from ~0.06 to ~0.40, demonstrating that target design is more impactful than model complexity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Extreme price movements in high-frequency financial markets are hard to predict due to non-stationarity, heavy-tailed returns, and class imbalance. Conventional models treat extreme events as isolated observations, failing to capture underlying market dynamics.

Method: The authors extend the target formulation to include both large future returns and high-volatility regimes, leveraging volatility clustering. They use XGBoost with time-series cross-validation and imbalance-aware evaluation on high-frequency Bitcoin LOB data.

Result: The proposed volatility-aware method achieves a PR-AUC of ~0.40, significantly outperforming the baseline formulation (PR-AUC ~0.06), representing a sixfold improvement in rare event detection.

Conclusion: Target design critically impacts financial ML performance, often exceeding the role of model complexity. Incorporating volatility structure into labeling provides a more effective and realistic framework for extreme event detection in high-frequency cryptocurrency markets.

Abstract: Predicting extreme price movements in high-frequency financial markets is a challenging task due to non-stationarity, heavy-tailed return distributions, and severe class imbalance. In particular, rare but impactful events are often difficult to detect using conventional modeling approaches, which typically treat extreme movements as isolated observations. This study proposes a volatility-aware approach for extreme event detection using high-frequency Bitcoin limit order book (LOB) data. Motivated by empirical evidence of volatility clustering, the target formulation is extended to incorporate both large future returns and high-volatility regimes. This redefinition increases the proportion of informative samples and aligns the learning objective with the underlying market dynamics. Using a tree-based model (XGBoost) with time-series cross-validation and imbalance-aware evaluation, the proposed method achieves a Precision-Recall AUC of approximately 0.40, significantly outperforming the baseline formulation with a PR-AUC of around 0.06. This represents more than a sixfold improvement in detecting rare events. The results highlight that target design plays a critical role in financial machine learning, often exceeding the impact of model complexity. By incorporating volatility structure into the labeling process, the proposed approach provides a more effective and realistic framework for extreme event detection in high-frequency cryptocurrency markets.

</details>


### [309] [CoCurve: Cross-Module Co-Pruning Curvature for Training-Free Structured LLM Pruning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17568)
*Zhiren Gong,Zihao Zeng,Zijie Wang,Tiantong Wang,Chau Yuen,Wei Yang Bryan Lim*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CoCurve introduces a training-free, calibration-only structured pruning method for LLMs that captures cross-module dependencies (attention heads and FFN channels) via a second-order Taylor expansion, enabling joint pruning decisions without fine-tuning.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing training-free pruning methods score units independently, failing to capture dependencies between attention and FFN units through the residual stream. Two individually weak units can be jointly indispensable, but independent scoring removes them together, causing excessive damage.

Method: CoCurve uses a second-order Taylor expansion of token-level KL divergence between the frozen model and its masked copy, producing a Fisher matrix whose diagonal is classical saliency and off-diagonals capture co-pruning interactions. Under a single-ablation additivity approximation, the matrix reduces to a Gram product, requiring only M forward passes. Pruning is then solved as a single budgeted quadratic program.

Result: The method recovers the full M x M interaction matrix without pairwise sweeps or gradients, and solves pruning as a one-shot quadratic program under a shared attention-FFN budget.

Conclusion: CoCurve demonstrates that cross-module dependencies are crucial for effective structured pruning, and that they can be captured efficiently without fine-tuning or labels, enabling better pruning decisions than independent scoring.

Abstract: Structured pruning compresses large language models (LLMs) by removing whole computational units, such as attention heads and feed-forward (FFN) channel groups. Most training-free methods, however, rank these units independently, implicitly treating the loss from pruning a set as the sum of its individual losses. This view fails for Transformers, whose sublayers are coupled through a shared residual stream. Two individually weak units can thus be jointly indispensable, yet independent scoring is blind to such dependence and removes them together. We introduce CoCurve (Cross-Module Co-Pruning Curvature), a calibration-only, fine-tuning-free method that prunes attention and FFN units jointly. A second-order Taylor expansion of the token-level KL between the frozen model and its masked copy yields a single Fisher matrix whose diagonal is classical node saliency and whose off-diagonal entries are co-pruning curvature edges: the extra damage of removing two units together. Under a single-ablation additivity approximation this matrix reduces to a Gram product of single-unit ablation features, so the full M x M interaction is recovered from M forward passes, with no pairwise sweeps or gradients. Pruning then reduces to one budgeted quadratic program, solved in a single shot under a shared attention--FFN budget, with no labels, fine-tuning, or recovery.

</details>


### [310] [A Weisfeiler-Leman Characterization of Global-Attention Graph Transformers for Mixed-Integer Linear Programs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17570)
*Md Abrar Jahin,Craig A. Knoblock,Jay Pujara*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proves that hierarchical graph transformers with global linear attention, edge-weighted cross-attention, and bipartite message passing are bounded by 1-WL test—they map 1-WL-equivalent MILP graphs to identical embeddings, limiting their expressive power.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the expressive power of graph foundation models (GFMs) with global attention when representing mixed-integer linear programs (MILPs), particularly whether they can distinguish non-isomorphic MILP instances beyond local graph neural networks.

Method: The authors prove that hierarchical graph transformers combining global linear attention, edge-weighted cross-attention, and bipartite message passing are bounded by 1-WL test via compositional proof showing each component is a symmetric multiset function. They validate empirically across 10 graph encoders including Graphormer, GraphGPS, Set-Transformer, and Gasse-style models.

Result: Every tested encoder maps 1-WL-equivalent non-isomorphic graph pairs to numerically identical embeddings. Expressiveness beyond 1-WL arises from input encoding (e.g., random-walk positional encodings) rather than attention mechanisms themselves.

Conclusion: The expressive power of global-attention GFMs is fundamentally bounded by 1-WL, providing an encoder-agnostic diagnostic for detecting representation equivalence in MILP graph learning.

Abstract: Graph foundation models (GFMs) with global attention are increasingly used to represent mixed-integer linear programs (MILPs), aiming to capture structure beyond the locality of standard graph neural networks. We study their expressive power through graph isomorphism testing, asking which MILP instances they map to identical representations. We prove that a broad class of hierarchical graph transformers combining global linear attention, edge-weighted cross-attention, and bipartite message passing is bounded by the one-dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) test: under any parameter setting, 1-WL-equivalent MILP graphs receive identical graph embeddings. Our compositional proof shows that each architectural component is a symmetric multiset function and thus preserves 1-WL equivalence. We validate this characterization across ten diverse graph encoders, including Graphormer-, GraphGPS-, Set-Transformer-, and Gasse-style models. Across model capacities, graph scales, and pooling operators, every tested encoder maps 1-WL-equivalent non-isomorphic graph pairs to numerically identical embeddings. Consequently, graph invariants that vary within a 1-WL equivalence class cannot be recovered from these representations. We further show that expressiveness beyond 1-WL arises from input encoding rather than attention: random-walk positional encodings separate the constructed pairs, while additional constructions expose the limits of this remedy. These results characterize the expressive power of global-attention GFMs and provide an encoder-agnostic diagnostic for detecting 1-WL-induced representation equivalence.

</details>


### [311] [AGG: Jacobian-Aggregated Group Gradient for Efficient GRPO Training of Diffusion Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17572)
*Ruiyi Ding,Jie Li,He Kang,Ziyan Liu,Chengru Song,Yuan chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: JAGG reduces the backward-pass cost of DiT RL training from W to 2 per group of W steps by exploiting the linearity of hidden states along the trajectory, achieving ~2x speedup with negligible quality loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: GRPO for diffusion/flow matching models requires backpropagation through the DiT backbone at every timestep, making high-resolution text-to-image training prohibitively expensive.

Method: JAGG approximates intermediate-step Jacobians via t-weighted interpolation of endpoint Jacobians, aggregates per-step upstream signals into two composite gradients, and applies them through a single joint backward pass. A cosine-similarity routing rule (jagg_frac) activates JAGG only where the linearity assumption holds.

Result: ~2x backward speedup on T2I benchmarks with negligible quality degradation.

Conclusion: The linearity of DiT hidden states along the trajectory can be leveraged to dramatically reduce RL training costs for diffusion/flow matching models.

Abstract: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) is a powerful reinforcement learning algorithm for aligning generative models with human preferences. While successful in large language models~\cite{shao2024deepseekmathpushinglimitsmathematical}, its extension to diffusion and flow matching models introduces a severe computational bottleneck: gradients must be back-propagated through the high-capacity DiT backbone at \emph{every} timestep of the sampling trajectory, making high-resolution text-to-image (T2I) training prohibitively expensive. Training-free DiT inference acceleration methods (e.g., $Δ$-DiT, ScalingCache) exploit the fact that DiT hidden states and velocity predictions vary \emph{smoothly and nearly linearly} along the trajectory. We ask whether the same linearity can reduce the backward-pass cost of DiT RL training, and answer affirmatively with \textbf{JAGG} (\textbf{J}acobian-\textbf{A}ggregated \textbf{G}roup \textbf{G}radient), which reduces full transformer backward passes from $W$ to $2$ per group of $W$ consecutive steps. JAGG approximates intermediate-step Jacobians via $t$-weighted interpolation of the endpoint Jacobians, then aggregates per-step upstream signals into two composite gradients applied through a single joint backward pass. We prove this interpolation is \emph{exact} when the velocity is linear in $(z,t)$, and a cosine-similarity routing rule (\texttt{jagg\_frac}) deploys JAGG only where the assumption holds. Experiments on T2I benchmarks show JAGG delivers $\sim$2$\times$ backward speedup with negligible quality degradation.

</details>


### [312] [ANNLib: A Development Framework for Efficient Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17582)
*Zheqi Shen,Jingbo Su,Zijin Wan,Yan Gu,Yihan Sun*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: ANNLib is a programming library for graph-based Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) that achieves both high performance and flexible functionality by decoupling algorithm and data structure components, allowing users to implement sophisticated settings like filter search, dynamic updates, and historical queries with minimal programming effort.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing ANNS systems struggle to simultaneously provide broad functionality and high performance. There is a gap that requires significant programming effort to achieve both goals.

Method: ANNLib decouples and independently optimizes algorithm and data structure components of graph-based ANNS. It integrates state-of-the-art algorithms and data structures as modular components, along with new designs, allowing users to select combinations for different applications.

Result: ANNLib achieves comparable or even better performance than previous work across various applications, including filter search, fully dynamic updates, and historical queries on snapshots.

Conclusion: ANNLib successfully bridges the gap between high performance and flexible functionality in ANNS systems with a simple, modular programming interface.

Abstract: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search (ANNS) plays a pivotal role in modern deep learning pipelines. Recently, many ANNS systems have been proposed to either provide broad functionality or reach high performance. However, it is yet difficult to achieve both with minimal programming efforts. We propose ANNLib to address the gap. ANNLib is a library that provides a programming framework for achieving high performance and flexible functionality in ANNS systems, based on popular graph-based ANNS algorithms. We carefully decouple and independently optimize both the algorithm and the data structure components of an ANNS system. In addition, we integrate state-of-the-art algorithms and data structures into ANNLib as modules, along with our new designs. Users can choose combinations of components to implement sophisticated settings with high performance, such as filter search, fully dynamic updates, and historical queries on snapshots. Our experiments show that our new solution provides a simple interface for various applications and achieves comparable or even better performance than previous work, specifically for each application.

</details>


### [313] [Concentration and Mean-Square Bounds for Contractive Stochastic Approximation: A Unified Elementary Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17595)
*Siddharth Chandak*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper presents a unified, elementary analysis yielding both mean-square and concentration bounds for stochastic approximation under arbitrary norm contractive mappings with multiplicative noise, achieving the first sub-Gaussian maximal concentration bound by using logarithmic stepsize dependence on confidence.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing analyses for stochastic approximation under arbitrary norm contractive mappings (common in RL) require complex techniques: smoothing the non-squared norm via generalized Moreau envelopes and multi-stage bootstrapping for concentration. The authors aim to provide a simpler, unified approach that simultaneously yields both mean-square and concentration bounds.

Method: Uses an averaged noise sequence and auxiliary iterates to derive a one-step Lyapunov drift inequality for the normed error directly (no norm smoothing). Mean-square bound: combinates drift inequality with induction showing bounded iterates in expectation. Concentration bound: develops probabilistic induction over "good" events controlling iterates, enabling standard Azuma-Hoeffding. Stepsize depends logarithmically on confidence level.

Result: First sub-Gaussian tailed maximal (all-time) concentration bound for SA under multiplicative noise. Unified analysis without Moreau envelope or multi-stage bootstrapping.

Conclusion: The techniques are generalizable to other noise models and iterative algorithms beyond the specific setting considered.

Abstract: We establish mean-square and concentration bounds for stochastic approximation (SA) with arbitrary norm contractive mappings, under a multiplicative noise model where the noise may scale affinely with the norm of the iterates, and the iterates are potentially unbounded. These settings arise in reinforcement learning, where operators are often contractive in the $\ell_\infty$ norm and the noise scales with the iterates. To address the arbitrary norm, earlier works replace the non-smooth squared norm with a smooth Lyapunov function constructed via the generalized Moreau envelope. For concentration analysis, these works handle multiplicative noise and unbounded iterates through a multi-stage bootstrapping argument that starts from a time-varying worst-case bound and iteratively refines it. We instead present a unified and elementary analysis that yields both bounds. Using an averaged noise sequence and corresponding auxiliary iterates, we obtain a one-step Lyapunov drift inequality for the normed error directly, without smoothing the norm or constructing an envelope. For the mean-square bound, we combine this drift inequality with an induction argument showing that the iterates remain bounded in expectation. For the concentration bound, we develop a probabilistic induction over a sequence of "good" events on which the iterates are controlled, allowing the standard Azuma-Hoeffding bound to be applied. Our approach yields the first sub-Gaussian tailed maximal (all-time) concentration bound for SA under multiplicative noise, by allowing the stepsize to depend logarithmically on the confidence level. Beyond the specific setting considered here, we discuss the generalizability of these proof techniques to other noise models and iterative algorithms.

</details>


### [314] [Trustworthy Protein-Ligand Binding Affinity Prediction via Reliability-Aware Multi-Engine Fusion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17601)
*Yongchan Hong,Defu Cao,Wenjin Liu,Thomas Ku,Jordy Homing Lam,Emily Nguyen,Willie Neiswanger,Vsevolod Katritch,Yan Liu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: RELIABLE-BA introduces an evidential framework for multi-engine protein-ligand binding affinity prediction that models each docking engine as an evidential expert, scales uncertainty via molecular context, and fuses experts through closed-form aggregation, achieving competitive accuracy with substantially improved uncertainty calibration and up to 25% error reduction through confidence-based filtering.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing docking engines often disagree without indicating which prediction to trust, and consensus/ensemble methods improve mean accuracy but lack interpretable confidence measures, uncertainty decomposition, and chemical context awareness for each protein-ligand pair.

Method: Three-step evidential framework: (1) each engine modeled as evidential expert via Normal-Inverse-Gamma distributions, (2) epistemic uncertainty scaled through learned reliability from molecular context while preserving predictive means, (3) experts fused via closed-form aggregation capturing individual uncertainty and inter-engine disagreement.

Result: Competitive point prediction on PDBBind and BDB2020+ benchmarks with substantially improved uncertainty calibration; validated on SARS-CoV-2 Mpro and 5HT2A receptor datasets; uncertainty-aware filtering reduces prediction error by up to 25%.

Conclusion: First multi-engine binding affinity prediction framework combining evidential fusion with context-dependent reliability, offering a principled approach toward trustworthy AI-guided drug discovery.

Abstract: Accurate protein-ligand binding affinity prediction is central to computational drug discovery, yet modern docking engines frequently disagree without indicating which prediction to trust. Consensus scoring and ensemble methods improve mean accuracy but treat all predictions identically without interpretable confidence measures or uncertainty decomposition, ignoring the chemical context of each protein-ligand pair. To address this limitation, we introduce RELIABLE-BA (RELIABiLity-aware Evidential fusion for Binding Affinity), an evidential framework for multi-engine binding affinity prediction. Our model comprises three steps: (1) modeling each engine as an evidential expert via Normal-Inverse-Gamma distributions, (2) scaling epistemic uncertainty through learned reliability from molecular context while preserving each expert's predictive mean, and (3) fusing experts through closed-form aggregation that captures both individual uncertainty and inter-engine disagreement. Experiments on the PDBBind and BDB2020+ benchmarks demonstrate competitive point prediction with substantially improved uncertainty calibration, and additional validation on the SARS-CoV-2 Mpro dataset and 5HT2A receptor demonstrates applicability to clinically relevant drug targets. Crucially, these uncertainty estimates enable reliable filtering of protein-ligand pairs, reducing prediction error by up to 25% when retaining only high-confidence pairs. To our knowledge, RELIABLE-BA is the first multi-engine binding affinity prediction framework to combine evidential fusion with context-dependent reliability, offering a principled path toward trustworthy AI-guided drug discovery. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/yongchand/RELIABLE-BA.

</details>


### [315] [Optimizing the Preconditioner: A Black-box Online-to-Nonconvex Conversion with Static Regret Minimization Oracles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17607)
*Haichen Hu,David Simchi-Levi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper presents a black-box reduction from stochastic nonconvex optimization to static-regret minimization in online convex optimization, achieving classical convergence rates and resolving an open problem posed by Chen and Hazan (2024).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To resolve the open problem of whether stochastic nonconvex optimization can be reduced to ordinary static regret minimization in online convex optimization in a black-box manner, and to provide a principled framework connecting adaptive optimizers (e.g., AdaGrad, Shampoo) to nonconvex settings.

Method: The reduction uses a predictable gradient tracker combined with a black-box OCO learner that selects preconditioners. The learner faces linear convex losses and is evaluated against a single fixed comparator. The framework extends to Lipschitz nonconvex objectives without smoothness via Goldstein stationarity.

Result: For smooth objectives, achieves O(1/√T) convergence rate when OCO oracle has O(√T) static regret. For non-smooth Lipschitz objectives, achieves optimal O(T^{-2/7}) convergence rate for Goldstein stationary points. Both rely only on ordinary static regret.

Conclusion: Stochastic nonconvex optimization can be reduced to static-regret OCO in a black-box manner, resolving the open problem. The framework separates optimizer design into gradient prediction and preconditioner selection, offering a new perspective on adaptive methods.

Abstract: We study whether stochastic nonconvex optimization can be reduced to ordinary static regret minimization in online convex optimization in a black-box manner. For smooth nonconvex objectives, our reduction maintains a predictable gradient tracker, while a black-box online learner selects a preconditioner that determines how this tracker is transformed into the update direction. The learner receives linear convex losses and is evaluated against a single fixed comparator over one undiscounted online game. For a $β$-smooth objective with range bounded by $M$ and an unbiased stochastic-gradient oracle with variance bounded by \(σ^2\), we establish $$\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T
  \mathbb E\!\left[\|\nabla f(x_t)\|_2^2\right]
  \lesssim
  \frac{σ\sqrt{Mβ}}{\sqrt T}
  +
  \frac{\sqrt{Mβ}\,
  \mathscr R_T(\mathcal A,I_d)}{T}
  +
  \frac{Mβ}{T}.$$ Consequently, any black-box OCO algorithm with $\mathscr R_T(\mathcal A,I_d)=O(\sqrt T)$ recovers the classical $O(\frac{1}{\sqrt{T}})$ convergence rate.
  We further show that the same black-box framework extends beyond the smooth setting to Lipschitz nonconvex objectives without Lipschitz continuous gradients. Importantly, this extension continues to rely only on an ordinary static-regret guarantee and requires no stronger notion of online regret. When the OCO oracle admits square-root static regret, the resulting conversion achieves the optimal $O(T^{-2/7})$ convergence rate for the corresponding Goldstein stationary point. These results resolve the open problem posed by Chen and Hazan (2024). More broadly, our framework separates optimizer design into gradient prediction and online preconditioner selection, providing a principled perspective on how adaptive optimization methods such as AdaGrad and Shampoo may be understood through static regret and applied in nonconvex optimization.

</details>


### [316] [PoLoRA: A Preconditioned Orthogonalized LoRA Optimizer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17620)
*Nikhil Ghosh,Tetiana Parshakova,Robert M. Gower*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: PoLoRA is a new optimizer for low-rank adaptation (LoRA) that incorporates product-aware spectral updates, curvature preconditioning, and a magnitude rule, achieving 1.2-1.7x faster convergence than Adam with lower learning rate sensitivity and minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard Adam optimizer treats LoRA's factor matrices as a flat vector, ignoring both the matrix structure and product structure, leading to suboptimal training. Prior attempts with matrix-aware optimizers (Muon) or product-aware variants failed to consistently improve over Adam.

Method: PoLoRA consists of three components: (1) product-aware spectral update direction, (2) curvature preconditioning controlling per-sample loss change, and (3) a magnitude rule controlling update sizes for both factors and merged weights.

Result: PoLoRA reaches Adam's held-out loss in 1.2-1.7x fewer steps across 1B-8B models on code and math instruction-tuning, with at most 3% per-step overhead, lower learning rate sensitivity, and stable optimal learning rate across ranks.

Conclusion: Explicitly incorporating the matrix and product structure of LoRA into optimization yields consistent improvements over standard Adam, making fine-tuning more efficient and robust to hyperparameter choice.

Abstract: Low-rank adaptation (LoRA) makes finetuning large language models cheaper by adding to each weight matrix a trainable low-rank update parameterized as the product of two matrices. These matrices are usually trained with Adam, which treats them as a single flat vector of parameters and ignores both the matrix and product structure of LoRA. Applying a matrix-aware optimizer such as Muon to each factor does not consistently improve over Adam, and neither do the product-aware Muon variants proposed in concurrent works. To realize consistent gains, we introduce PoLoRA, a Preconditioned Orthogonalized LoRA optimizer built from three ingredients: a product-aware spectral update direction, curvature preconditioning derived from controlling the per-sample loss change, and a magnitude rule that controls the sizes of both the factor and merged updates. We evaluate PoLoRA on instruction-tuning datasets for code and math across models from 1B to 8B parameters, and find that it reaches the final held-out loss achieved by tuned Adam in 1.2-1.7 times fewer steps, while adding at most 3% per-step overhead. Compared to Adam, PoLoRA is also less sensitive to the learning rate, and its optimal learning rate is stable across ranks.

</details>


### [317] [Can Transformers Really Do It All? On the Compatibility of Inductive Biases Across Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17624)
*Damien Teney,Liangze Jiang,Hemanth Saratchandran,Simon Lucey*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper proposes a method to optimize transformer architectures for specific datasets by replacing key non-linearities with learned functions, finding that task-specific designs can dramatically outperform standard transformers on algorithmic tasks (but not transfer well), while showing smaller but more transferable improvements on code/language tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether standard transformers are optimal for any given task/dataset and to understand task-specific inductive biases, with the broader goal of potentially finding improved architectures that support multiple capabilities simultaneously.

Method: Replace key non-linearities (GeLUs, softmax) with functions learned on held-out data, then train resulting architectures on other datasets to evaluate cross-task compatibility.

Result: New architectures show dramatic improvements on algorithmic toy tasks (learning speed, generalization, stability) but are highly task-specific. Smaller but more transferable improvements on code and language modeling datasets.

Conclusion: Standard transformers are rarely locally optimal; simple alternatives can perform much better but sacrifice universality, suggesting room for architectures that better support multiple capabilities simultaneously.

Abstract: Transformers are remarkably versatile and their design is largely consistent across a variety of applications. But are they optimal for any given task or dataset? The answer may be key for pushing AI beyond merely scaling current designs.
  *Method.* We present a method to optimize a transformer architecture for a given dataset, which we use as a tool to study optimal task-specific inductive biases. This method replaces the most important non-linearities (GeLUs,;softmax) with functions learned on held-out data. We then train the resulting architectures on other datasets, as a way to evaluate the compatibility between pairs of tasks.
  Findings. On algorithmic toy tasks, we identify new architectures with dramatic improvements in learning speed, in- and out-of-distribution generalization, and stability across seeds. The new designs prove very task-specific however, and indicate that these tasks require inductive biases very different from those of standard transformers. On code and language modeling datasets, we also find architectures with consistent, yet smaller improvements. These designs transfer much better across datasets and domains (English & computer code).
  Implications. Our results show that standard transformers are rarely a local optimum in the space of architectures. Simple alternatives can perform much better but sacrifice universality.
