### [345] [SGN: A Similarity-based Generative Network for Data Generation under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18072)
*Jiaqi Zhu,Xincheng Chen,Yuncheng Wu,Zhaojing Luo,Beng Chin Ooi*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SGN is a reusable generative framework trained once on source data that adapts to new target domains without parameter updates, using label-induced similarity structure to generate target-domain samples for data augmentation under distribution shifts.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Generative models trained on source domains produce poorly aligned samples under target domain shifts; existing adaptation methods require extra optimization and domain-specific parameters.

Method: SGN learns a latent space structured by label-induced pairwise similarities via encoder-decoder architecture; at generation, it encodes a small labeled target set and combines them in the learned space to produce target-specific yet class-consistent samples.

Result: Experiments on image and tabular datasets demonstrate effectiveness for target-guided data augmentation under source-to-target distribution shifts.

Conclusion: SGN enables reusable, parameter-update-free domain adaptation for data augmentation, with theoretical analysis on realizability and dimensionality of the similarity structure.

Abstract: Generative models trained on a source domain often produce samples that are poorly aligned with shifted target domains, limiting their effectiveness for target-domain data augmentation. Although target-specific adaptation can reduce this mismatch, it typically requires additional optimization and domain-specific parameters. We propose a Similarity-based Generative Network (SGN), a reusable framework that is trained once on labeled source data and applied to new target domains without parameter updates. SGN learns a latent space structured by label-induced pairwise similarities while preserving reconstructive information through an encoder-decoder architecture. At generation time, a small labeled representative set from the target domain is encoded and combined in the learned latent space, allowing the generated samples to inherit target-specific characteristics while maintaining class consistency. We further analyze the realizability and dimensionality requirements of the proposed similarity structure. Experiments on image and tabular datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of SGN for target-guided data augmentation under source-to-target distribution shifts.

</details>


### [346] [Sobek: Streaming Equivariant Tensor Product Convolutions](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18074)
*Vladimir Chorošajev,Cédric Bény*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Sobek reformulates equivariant graph neural network convolutions by streaming edge-local products directly into bounded receiver-side state, eliminating the need for edge-sized intermediates. This yields up to 49.7× speedup, 99% memory reduction, and handles workloads 100× larger than prior systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional equivariant GNN implementations materialize edge-specific weights, messages, and adjoints, causing workspace and memory traffic to scale rapidly with graph size and operator width, limiting feasible GPU workloads.

Method: Reassociates radial projection, spherical-harmonic coupling, and graph aggregation to consume edge-local products directly into bounded receiver-side state via a streaming formulation. Implemented in Sobek, a generated-CUDA backend.

Result: Across 75 capacity-matched comparisons (2 operator families, 3 differentiation orders), Sobek achieves 1.2× to 49.7× speedups and up to 99% peak memory reduction. It also executes workloads two orders of magnitude beyond OpenEquivariance's capacity with near-peak throughput.

Conclusion: Edge-scaled tensor-product workspace is an artifact of the conventional execution schedule, not a fundamental requirement of equivariant convolution itself.

Abstract: Equivariant graph neural networks repeatedly apply edge-conditioned tensor-product convolutions over graph edges. Conventional implementations materialize edge-specific weights, messages, and adjoints, causing tensor-product workspace and memory traffic to grow rapidly with graph size and operator width. This limits feasible workloads and can prevent larger problems from fully utilizing the GPU.
  We show that these edge-sized intermediates are artifacts of the execution schedule, not requirements of the equivariant operator. By reassociating radial projection, spherical-harmonic coupling, and graph aggregation, edge-local products can be consumed directly into bounded receiver-side state. The resulting streaming formulation preserves fully connected multiplicity mixing and extends through forward, backward, and double backward.
  We implement this formulation in Sobek, a generated-CUDA backend, and evaluate it across edge-scaling regimes and varied feature structures. Across two operator families and all three differentiation orders, Sobek is faster in all 75 capacity-matched comparisons, with speedups ranging from $1.2\times$ to $49.7\times$, and reduces peak allocated memory by up to 99\%. It also executes workloads up to two orders of magnitude beyond OpenEquivariance's capacity while retaining near-peak throughput. These results show that edge-scaled tensor-product workspace is a property of the conventional schedule, not of equivariant convolution itself.

</details>


### [347] [Generalised Bellman recurrence and three dualities in sequential decision-making](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18077)
*Fernando E. Rosas,David Hyland,Daniel Polani*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper shows that the Bellman equation arises from three conditions: decomposable dynamics through sufficient statistics, recursive return decomposition, and compatible uncertainty aggregation. It reveals three dualities (probability/return, return/aggregation, aggregation/probability) that unify methods across reinforcement learning, control, and decision theory.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the fundamental structural conditions that give rise to the Bellman equation and to unify seemingly separate methods across RL, control, and decision theory under a common framework.

Method: The authors identify three conditions (decomposable dynamics, recursive return, compatible aggregation) and show how their mutual consistency yields the Bellman equation. They explore cases where one condition fails and show how tractability can be recovered. They also derive three dualities from these conditions.

Result: The Bellman equation's form is determined by three specific conditions on state, dynamics, and return. When conditions fail, augmentation or deformation can restore tractability. Three dualities emerge from a single construction, unifying various methods across disciplines.

Conclusion: The recursive structure of optimal value functions has a precise mathematical foundation rooted in three conditions and their dualities, providing a unified lens for understanding methods in RL, control, and decision theory.

Abstract: What gives the Bellman equation its form? We show that the recursive properties of optimal value functions follow from three conditions: that the dynamics decomposes through sufficient statistics, that the return decomposes recursively, and that the aggregation of uncertainty is compatible with both. When all three conditions hold on a common state, the Bellman equation arises from their mutual consistency; when one fails, tractability can often be recovered by augmenting the state or by deforming return or dynamics. The same conditions are shown to give rise to three dualities: one between probability and return, one between return and aggregation, and one between aggregation and probability. Our framework reveals these dualities as arising from a single construction, unifying methods developed separately across reinforcement learning, control, and decision theory.

</details>


### [348] [SelectInfer: Selective Neuron Loading and Computation for On-Device LLMs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18081)
*Huzaifa Shaaban Kabakibo,Eric Schniedermeyer,Artem Burchanow,Lin Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: SelectInfer is a neuron-level optimization framework that enables efficient LLM inference on edge devices by selectively loading and computing only the most important neurons, reducing memory and computation while preserving performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLMs have high computational and memory demands that hinder deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing compression methods (pruning/quantization) often compromise accuracy or require costly re-training.

Method: SelectInfer uses an offline profiler to identify task-specific and general-purpose neurons. It then applies (1) selective loading (load only important neurons) and (2) selective computation (compute only relevant neurons at runtime).

Result: Significant reductions in memory footprint and computation across multiple datasets while preserving task performance.

Conclusion: SelectInfer is a practical step toward enabling LLM deployment on edge devices through fine-grained, neuron-level optimization without sacrificing accuracy.

Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities across a range of Natural Language Processing (NLP) tasks, but their high computational and memory demands pose significant challenges for deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. Existing approaches to model compression and optimization often rely on coarse-grained pruning or quantization, which can compromise accuracy or require re-training and fine-tuning. In this work, we introduce SelectInfer, a neuron-level optimization framework that enables efficient LLM inference on edge devices through selective neuron loading and computation. By profiling and identifying both task-specific and general-purpose neurons using an offline LLM profiler, SelectInfer implements two key optimizations: selective loading, which reduces memory footprint by selectively loading a subset of neurons that were identified to be most important during the offline stage, and selective computation, which dynamically computes only the most relevant neurons at runtime. Evaluation across multiple datasets shows that SelectInfer achieves significant reductions in memory footprint and computation while preserving task performance, making it a practical step towards enabling LLM deployment on edge devices

</details>


### [349] [Enhancing Rubric-based RL via Self-Distillation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18082)
*Mingxuan Xia,Yuhang Yang,Chao Ye,Shuai Zhu,Shenzhi Yang,Guangcheng Zhu,Yuhang Zhang,Cheng Peng,Haobo Wang,Siqing Wang*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: CriPO addresses two failure modes in rubric-based RL for LLMs: Unexplored Criteria (UC) and Suppressed Criteria (SC), using on-policy self-distillation without train-inference mismatch, achieving better performance with 2x fewer steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Rubric-based RL suffers from limited exploration (UC) where unsatisfied criteria get no signal, and the overlooked SC failure mode where satisfied criteria lose their learning signal due to non-positive aggregate advantages in scalar reward aggregation. SC affects over 57% of samples with an average of 1.8 SC per sample.

Method: CriPO enhances rubric-based RL via on-policy self-distillation. For UC: constructs a criterion-injection self-teacher and computes localized forward-KL loss to inject missing behaviors. For SC: employs a counterfactual self-teacher to locate criterion-relevant tokens in negative-advantage rollouts and flips their token-level advantages to positive values.

Result: CriPO consistently outperforms rubric-based RL on medicine and science benchmarks, achieving stronger final performance with approximately 2x fewer optimization steps.

Conclusion: CriPO effectively solves both UC and SC failure modes simultaneously without introducing train-inference mismatch, demonstrating superior efficiency and effectiveness over standard rubric-based RL approaches.

Abstract: Rubric-based RL has recently shown promise in improving LLMs on open-ended tasks. A widely recognized limitation of rubric-based RL is limited exploration: criteria that no rollout manages to satisfy (Unexplored Criteria, UC) receive no optimization signal. Recent methods address this by incorporating rubric information as external guidance during rollout, yet they introduce a train-inference mismatch: the policy is optimized on rollouts produced under external guidance while this guidance is absent at inference time, causing error accumulation through autoregressive decoding. Moreover, these exploration-focused approaches overlook a fundamentally different failure mode that we term Suppressed Criteria (SC) -- criteria that are satisfied by some rollouts yet whose learning signals are lost during optimization because scalar reward aggregation assigns them non-positive aggregate advantages. Our analysis reveals that SC are remarkably prevalent: over 57% of samples exhibit this failure mode throughout training, with an average of 1.8 SC per sample. To simultaneously address both UC and SC without introducing training-inference mismatch, we propose Criterion-Distilled Policy Optimization (CriPO), which enhances rubric-based RL via on-policy self-distillation. For UC, CriPO constructs a criterion-injection self-teacher and computes a localized forward-KL loss to inject missing behaviors into the policy. For SC, CriPO employs a counterfactual self-teacher to locate criterion-relevant tokens in negative-advantage rollouts and flips their token-level advantages to positive values, preserving useful patterns that would otherwise be suppressed. Experiments on medicine and science benchmarks demonstrate that CriPO consistently outperforms rubric-based RL, achieving stronger final performance with approximately $2\times$ fewer optimization steps.

</details>


### [350] [The Label Complexity of Class-Conditional Coverage under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18088)
*Weijia Han,Lisha Qu*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Standard evaluation benchmarks introduce distribution shifts that cause conformal prediction to maintain marginal coverage (90%) while per-class coverage collapses (some classes as low as 70%). The paper proves that per-class validity without target labels is impossible, quantifies the target labels needed for valid and efficient per-class coverage, and shows pseudo-labels from classifiers provide minimal efficiency gains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard conformal prediction methods maintain marginal coverage under distribution shifts but silently fail on per-class coverage, which is critical for fair and reliable recognition systems.

Method: The paper provides theoretical analysis: (1) proves impossibility of label-free per-class validity under joint covariate-label shift, (2) derives lower/upper bounds on target labels needed for per-class validity and efficiency, (3) analyzes prediction-powered inference with pseudo-labels. Validated on skeleton action recognition and natural-image corruption benchmarks.

Result: Per-class validity alone requires only a few labels per class; validity + efficiency requires labels proportional to inverse square of efficiency tolerance and log of number of classes. Pseudo-labels from classifiers improve efficiency only by a small constant factor. Per-class calibration using source labels helps only until marginal coverage itself breaks.

Conclusion: Restoring per-class coverage under distribution shift is theoretically costly: label-free methods cannot achieve valid and efficient per-class coverage, and even optimal use of classifiers' pseudo-labels yields limited gains. Source-only calibration recovers gaps only within the regime where marginal coverage holds.

Abstract: Standard evaluation of many recognition systems contains distribution shift by construction, since benchmarks place disjoint conditions in the training and test splits. Under such a shift, split conformal prediction keeps marginal coverage near the nominal level while per-class coverage fails silently: on a real cross-subject skeleton benchmark, marginal coverage stays near ninety percent, the worst action class is covered about seventy percent of the time, and ten of the sixty classes fall below eighty percent coverage.
  We characterize the cost of restoring per-class validity. First, an impossibility: once the shift acts jointly on the covariates and the labels, the target class-conditional score law is unidentified from source labels and an unlabeled target sample, so no label-free method attains per-class coverage that is at once valid and efficient. Second, we make the cost precise: per-class validity alone needs only a handful of target labels per class, while the label count necessary and sufficient for validity together with per-class efficiency grows as the inverse square of the efficiency tolerance and the logarithm of the number of classes, with matching upper and lower bounds. Third, within the evaluated prediction-powered inference family, even the most favorable use of the classifier's own pseudo-labels on an unbounded unlabeled target pool improves efficiency by at most a small constant factor where coverage collapses.
  Skeleton action recognition is our real-data case study. A per-class calibration using source labels alone recovers a substantial share of the per-class gap while the shift preserves marginal coverage, and stops helping exactly when marginal coverage itself breaks. Three real shifts of increasing severity trace this boundary, and the same collapse and recovery appears on a natural-image corruption benchmark, beyond any single modality.

</details>


### [351] [Empowering On-Device Model Adaptation with an Edge AI Inference Accelerator](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18101)
*Mateusz Piechocki,Alessandro Capotondi,Marek Kraft*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes a heterogeneous adaptation pipeline that uses a commercial edge AI inference accelerator (Hailo-8L) for frozen-backbone feature extraction during on-device training, while fine-tuning only a lightweight classification head on the host CPU. The method achieves up to 15.4x faster training vs Raspberry Pi 5 CPU baseline with reduced energy consumption.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: On-device model adaptation is needed for lifelong personalization on resource-constrained hardware, but compute, power, and memory limitations make end-to-end backpropagation impractical for modern deep neural networks.

Method: A heterogeneous adaptation pipeline that partitions the computational graph: the pre-trained backbone is quantized to INT8 and run on a Hailo-8L inference accelerator, while a lightweight FP32 classification head is fine-tuned on the host CPU.

Result: Achieves up to 15.4x faster wall-clock training time vs Raspberry Pi 5 CPU baseline, offers competitive throughput, and consistently reduces energy per sample. Post-training quantization restoration is crucial for preserving feature quality.

Conclusion: Demonstrates a practical approach to efficient on-device adaptation using inference-oriented edge accelerators, enabling frequent energy-efficient in-field updates with most weights remaining fixed.

Abstract: On-device model adaptation is essential to enable lifelong personalization on resource-constrained hardware, but compute, power, and memory limitations of such devices make end-to-end backpropagation impractical for modern deep neural networks. This work proposes a heterogeneous adaptation pipeline that repurposes a commercial edge AI inference accelerator, Hailo-8L, for frozen-backbone feature extraction during on-device training. The computational graph is partitioned so that the pre-trained backbone is quantized to INT8 and run on the accelerator, while only a lightweight FP32 classification head is fine-tuned on the host CPU, enabling frequent, energy-efficient in-field updates with most weights remaining fixed. Across multiple architectures and datasets, this pipeline achieves up to 15.4x faster wall-clock training time compared to a Raspberry Pi 5 CPU baseline, offers competitive throughput in favorable settings, and consistently reduces energy per sample. Post-training quantization restoration is shown to be crucial for preserving the quality of accelerator-generated features and mitigating accuracy loss in quantization-sensitive architectures. Overall, the results demonstrate a practical approach to efficient on-device adaptation using inference-oriented edge accelerators. The implementation is available at https://github.com/MatPiech/accelerator-training.

</details>


### [352] [LLM-as-a-Coach: Experiential Learning for Non-Verifiable Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18110)
*Tianzhu Ye,Li Dong,Guanheng Chen,He Zhu,Xun Wu,Shaohan Huang,Furu Wei*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: EL replaces scalar rewards in RL with dense textual feedback from an LLM-as-a-Coach, improving generalization and reducing reward hacking on open-ended tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RL on open-ended tasks compresses rubric-based evaluation into a scalar reward, discarding rich textual feedback and conflating responses with distinct quality profiles.

Method: Experiential Learning (EL) repurposes the feedback model from LLM-as-a-Judge into LLM-as-a-Coach, which distills assessment into transferable experiential knowledge to condition a teacher model and is internalized by the policy through on-policy context distillation.

Result: EL consistently outperforms rubric-based RL on held-out and unseen open-ended tasks across two policy families, generalizes better beyond training distribution, and mitigates reward hacking.

Conclusion: Experiential knowledge serves as a richer and more generalizable learning signal for post-training on non-verifiable tasks compared to scalar rewards.

Abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) on open-ended tasks compresses an LLM's rubric-based evaluation into a scalar reward, discarding rich textual feedback and conflating responses with distinct quality profiles. We propose Experiential Learning (EL), which repurposes the feedback model from an LLM-as-a-Judge into an LLM-as-a-Coach. The coach distills its assessment of each on-policy response into transferable experiential knowledge, which conditions a teacher model and is internalized by the policy through on-policy context distillation. Compared with scalar rewards, this higher-bandwidth feedback channel provides dense supervision and preserves fine-grained preferences among high-quality responses. Across two policy families, with feedback from the policy itself or a proprietary model, EL consistently outperforms rubric-based RL on held-out and unseen open-ended tasks. Notably, EL generalizes better beyond the training distribution, and mitigates reward hacking. These findings establish experiential knowledge as a richer and more generalizable learning signal for post-training on non-verifiable tasks.

</details>


### [353] [Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections for Parameter-Efficient Finetuning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18130)
*Valentijn Oldenburg,Floris de Kam,Bente Zuijdam,Lieve Eberson,Nicky van Zutphen,Stef de Wildt,Ivo Verhoeven*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper proposes Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC) as a PEFT method that learns residual routing modules. While mHC alone doesn't beat LoRA, combining mHC+LoRA improves language modeling loss and shows task-dependent gains at 1B and 7B scales.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing PEFT methods adapt weights or activations but leave residual connections unchanged; this paper investigates whether adapting residual connections can serve as a novel PEFT axis.

Method: mHC wraps frozen OLMo-2 backbones with learned residual routing modules, generalizing standard residual connections via manifold-constrained hyper-connections. The authors also explore fixing the residual mixing matrix to identity during finetuning.

Result: mHC alone does not consistently outperform LoRA. However, mHC+LoRA combinations at matched parameter budgets improve language modeling loss and show task-dependent benchmark gains at both 1B and 7B scales.

Conclusion: Residual routing is a distinct and promising new axis for PEFT, especially when combined with existing methods like LoRA.

Abstract: Most parameter-efficient finetuning (PEFT) methods adapt weights or activations, thus leaving one of the key Transformer components unchanged: residual connections. This paper investigates Manifold-Constrained Hyper-Connections (mHC), a generalisation of residual connections, as a novel PEFT approach, wrapping frozen OLMo-2 backbones with learned residual routing modules. We find that mHC can finetune frozen Transformers, but that its role differs fundamentally from the original pre-training setting: in finetuning, fixing the residual mixing matrix to identity often improves performance. As a standalone PEFT method, mHC does not consistently outperform LoRA. However, at matched trainable parameter budgets, mHC+LoRA combinations improve language-modelling loss and show task-dependent benchmark gains at both 1B and 7B scale. Overall, our results identify residual routing as a distinct and promising novel PEFT axis.

</details>


### [354] [Do Language Models Dream of Binding Molecules? Benchmarking LLMs under Spatial Constraints](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18144)
*Thomas MacDougall,Maksim Kuznetsov,Roman Schutski,Rim Shayakhmetov,Maxim Malkov,Vladimir Aladinskiy,Alex Aliper,Alex Zhavoronkov*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper evaluates whether general-purpose LLMs can handle 3D spatial constraints in structure-based drug design, introducing 3D-Fit benchmarking to compare them against diffusion models, finding LLMs lag behind but show promise in handling multiple spatial constraints simultaneously.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: While diffusion models dominate 3D molecule generation and LLMs are emerging in molecular design, their ability to reason about physics and 3D spatial environments in SBDD remains largely unexplored.

Method: The authors systematically analyze general-purpose LLMs on multi-conditioned 3D ligand generation (protein pockets + anchor fragments, pharmacophore points, mandatory interactions) and introduce 3D-Fit, a token-efficient benchmarking strategy for assessing LLM performance on spatial molecule generation.

Result: LLMs lag behind state-of-the-art diffusion models in spatial reasoning but show promising capabilities, particularly in handling multiple spatial constraints simultaneously and scaling to heterogeneous setups.

Conclusion: LLMs have promising but currently limited spatial capabilities for SBDD compared to specialized diffusion models, though their ability to handle multiple constraints suggests potential for heterogeneous drug design scenarios.

Abstract: Structure-based drug design (SBDD) leverages the 3D structure of protein targets, often complemented by other spatial constraints, to generate candidate binding molecules. While diffusion models have dominated as a leading paradigm for high-quality 3D molecule generation, LLM-based methods are rapidly emerging in molecular design and have shown competitive performance in pocket-conditioned molecular generation. However, their ability to reason about physics and 3D spatial environments is largely underexplored. In this work, we systematically analyze whether current general-purpose LLMs are capable of navigating complex 3D constraints compared to established baselines such as specialized diffusion models. We consider 3D ligand generation conditioned on protein pockets together with ligand- and interaction-derived spatial constraints, including anchor fragments, pharmacophore points, and mandatory pocket-ligand interactions. To enable this evaluation, we introduce 3D-Fit - a token-efficient benchmarking strategy for assessing LLM performance on multi-conditioned spatial molecule generation. Our findings reveal a clear pattern in LLM spatial capabilities: while they still lag behind state-of-the-art approaches, they are promising and can handle multiple spatial constraints simultaneously, enabling scaling to heterogeneous setups.

</details>


### [355] [Totally Positive Matrices and the Highest-Order Coefficients of the Characteristic Polynomial](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18148)
*Tiago Closs,Leandro Farina*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates whether totally positive matrices can be distinguished via the highest-order coefficients of their characteristic polynomials, finding that coefficients (a_{n-1}, a_{n-2}, a_{n-3}) provide strong nonlinear separation, describable by Mahalanobis ellipsoids, with distinct signatures for different structured families that become more separable in higher dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To determine the extent to which totally positive matrices can be distinguished from non-totally positive matrices using only the highest-order coefficients of their characteristic polynomials, and to identify which coefficients carry the most discriminatory information.

Method: Used neural-network classifiers with feature-attribution methods on datasets from structured totally positive families (products of positive bidiagonal matrices, Vandermonde matrices, Cauchy matrices) in dimensions 5, 10, and 30, analyzing the discriminatory power of characteristic polynomial coefficients.

Result: The three highest-order coefficients (a_{n-1}, a_{n-2}, a_{n-3}) contain strong discriminatory information for separating totally positive from non-totally positive matrices across all tested dimensions. The separation is nonlinear and described by Mahalanobis ellipsoids. Different structured families show distinct ellipsoidal signatures that become more separated as dimension increases.

Conclusion: A conjecture is formulated about the geometric separation of structured totally positive families in the space determined by the three highest-order characteristic coefficients.

Abstract: We investigate the extent to which totally positive matrices can be distinguished through the highest-order coefficients of their characteristic polynomials. To identify the most informative coefficients, we also employed neural-network classifiers together with feature-attribution methods. Using datasets built from several structured totally positive families, including products of positive bidiagonal matrices, Vandermonde matrices, and Cauchy matrices, we find that the coefficients (a_{n-1}, a_{n-2}, a_{n-3}) already contain strong discriminatory information for separating totally positive from non-totally positive matrices in dimensions 5, 10, and 30. The resulting separation is markedly nonlinear and admits a natural geometric description in the corresponding three-dimensional coefficient space by means of Mahalanobis ellipsoids. These ellipsoids enclose the totally positive samples while excluding most non-totally positive ones. Moreover, different structured totally positive families exhibit distinct ellipsoidal signatures, and the separation between these signatures increases with the dimension. These observations lead us to formulate a conjecture on the geometric separation of structured totally positive families in the space determined by the three highest-order characteristic coefficients.

</details>


### [356] [Differentiable Logic Gate Networks for Low-Latency EEG Classification on Edge Devices](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18149)
*Shyamal Y. Dharia,Stephen D. Smith,Camilo E. Valderrama*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Diff-Logic networks outperform/compete with MLPs and BNNs in EEG classification on edge devices, achieving up to 2.9× speedup and constant inference time across scales, with 80.2% Macro F1 on dementia detection while using pure Boolean circuits deployable on low-power CPUs.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time EEG classification on edge devices is bottlenecked by floating-point arithmetic in conventional neural networks, motivating the need for hardware-native alternatives.

Method: Investigated Differentiable Logic Gate Networks (Diff-Logic) that compile models into pure Boolean circuits executable via bitwise CPU operations, compared against MLP and BNN baselines across four EEG datasets and two tasks (dementia detection and emotion recognition) at four complexity tiers (50k-500k parameters).

Result: Diff-Logic achieved 80.2% Macro F1 on dementia screening (6.8% above MLP). On emotion recognition, MLP had moderate advantage but incurred 2.3× higher latency and 14× larger model size. Diff-Logic inference remained nearly constant across 10× model scale increase, with 2.9× peak speedup over MLPs.

Conclusion: Logic-based neural architectures are a practical paradigm for resource-constrained BCIs, offering competitive/superior performance while satisfying latency and memory constraints of portable edge deployment.

Abstract: Real-time EEG classification on edge devices is bottlenecked by the floating-point arithmetic of conventional neural networks. We investigated Differentiable Logic Gate Networks (Diff-Logic) as a hardware-native alternative that compiles models into pure Boolean circuits executable via bitwise CPU operations. Through rigorous iso-parameter experiments across four EEG datasets spanning two classification tasks, binary dementia detection and 3-class emotion recognition, we compared Diff-Logic against matched-capacity Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) and Binarized Neural Network (BNN) baselines at four complexity tiers (50k-500k parameters). On dementia screening, Diff-Logic achieved 80.2% Macro F1, outperforming the MLP baseline by 6.8%. On emotion recognition, the MLP retained a moderate performance advantage but incurred a 2.3$\times$ higher latency and 14$\times$ larger model size when deployed on a power-constrained (7W) Nvidia Jetson Orin Nano CPU (Single-core). Critically, Diff-Logic inference time remained nearly constant across a 10$\times$ increase in model scale, achieving a peak speedup of 2.9$\times$ over MLPs at the largest complexity tier. Our results establish logic-based neural architectures as a practical paradigm for resource-constrained brain-computer interfaces, achieving competitive or superior performance while natively satisfying the latency and memory constraints of portable edge deployment. Code is available on GitHub: https://github.com/Shyamal-Dharia/eeg-difflogic

</details>


### [357] [The Calibration Channel Determines the Bayes-Error Proxy: An Exact Law for Temperature-Induced Distortion](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18162)
*Shreyas Pradeepkumar Khandale*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper shows that temperature scaling distorts the soft-label Bayes-error estimator beta(z) in a predictable way, proving it's monotonic, bijective to (0, 1/2), and can produce any proxy value for a fixed classifier error, empirically varying by 56x-980x across tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Bayes-error estimator beta(z) of Ishida et al. is known to be fragile when probabilities aren't true posteriors, even with perfect calibration (Ushio et al.). This paper aims to characterize exactly how temperature scaling—the most common post-hoc calibration method—distorts this proxy.

Method: The authors prove an exact identity linking temperature-scaled proxy to the classifier's margin distribution, derive strict monotonicity and a continuous bijection to (0, 1/2), and obtain a closed-form two-parameter curve under a Gaussian logit model. They validate on CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST, and SVHN (eight binary tasks).

Result: Proxy varies by 56x to 980x at constant test error; the closed form reproduces empirical curves within 0.018; calibration temperature minimizing ECE does not align with any stable proxy value.

Conclusion: A proxy value is meaningful only together with the mechanism that produced its probabilities, reinforcing that calibration-based remedies must account for this distortion.

Abstract: The soft-label Bayes-error estimator beta(z) = E[min(z, 1-z)] of Ishida et al. estimates the irreducible error of a binary task directly from probability-valued labels. Recent work by Ushio et al. showed that this estimator is fragile when the probabilities are not the true posterior: even perfectly calibrated soft labels can yield a substantially inaccurate estimate, and they propose isotonic calibration as a consistent remedy. We complement that line of work by characterizing exactly how the most widely used post-hoc calibration map -- temperature scaling -- distorts the proxy. We prove an exact, model-free identity reducing the temperature-scaled proxy to the classifier's margin distribution, from which we obtain (i) strict monotonicity in the temperature and (ii) a continuous bijection from the temperature axis onto the open interval (0, 1/2), so that a fixed classifier -- with fixed decisions and fixed 0-1 error -- can be made to report any proxy value whatsoever. Under a Gaussian model of the logits we further derive a two-parameter closed form for the entire proxy-versus-temperature curve. Across CIFAR-10, Fashion-MNIST, and SVHN (eight binary tasks), the proxy varies by 56x to 980x at constant test error, the closed form reproduces the empirical curve to within 0.018, and the calibration temperature that minimizes the expected calibration error does not coincide with any stable proxy value. Our results give a precise, predictive account of the distortion whose existence motivates calibration-based remedies, and they reinforce the practical recommendation that a proxy value is meaningful only together with the mechanism that produced its probabilities.

</details>


### [358] [OR Else: A Differentiable Trust Region for Policy Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18163)
*Chinmay Rane,Kanishka Tyagi,Michael Manry*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: This paper investigates Output Reset (OR), a smooth one-sided saturation rule, as an alternative to clipped surrogate objectives in PPO and GRPO for LLM post-training. PPO-OR shows higher reward-model scores under GAE, while GRPO-OR shows different optimization behavior but no reward gain with small group size (G=2).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The abrupt derivative change caused by clipped surrogate objectives in PPO and GRPO motivates exploring a smooth alternative (Output Reset) for more favorable optimization in LLM post-training.

Method: Replaces clipped policy term with OR squared-margin loss in rollout-relative token log-ratio space; compares PPO-clip vs PPO-OR (with GAE) and GRPO vs GRPO-OR (with group-relative advantages) on Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct using Anthropic hh-rlhf.

Result: PPO-OR achieves 0.305 higher mean reward than PPO-clip under GAE but with larger spread. GRPO-OR shows no mean gain but smaller spread, near-zero terminal OR residual, and declining overshoot fraction. Both group-relative methods show larger log-ratio displacement than GAE methods.

Conclusion: OR changes optimization behavior in both comparisons, but reward effect differs; no gain observed for GRPO-OR at G=2, leaving open question for larger groups.

Abstract: PPO and the GRPO baseline studied here use clipped surrogate objectives whose favorable-direction saturation introduces an abrupt change in the scalar objective's derivative. We ask whether Output Reset (OR), a smooth one-sided saturation rule, offers a useful alternative for large language model post-training. PPO-OR and GRPO-OR replace the clipped policy term with an OR squared-margin loss in rollout-relative token log-ratio space; the advantage sign determines the update direction, and a token contributes zero direct OR residual after crossing the favorable margin. We compare PPO-clip with PPO-OR under generalized advantage estimation (GAE), and GRPO with GRPO-OR under group-relative advantages, using \texttt{Llama-3.2-1B-Instruct} on Anthropic \texttt{hh-rlhf} with one shared reward model and three seeds per method. Under GAE, PPO-OR has a mean final training-time reward-model score $0.305$ higher than PPO-clip, with a larger observed across-seed spread. Under group-relative advantages, GRPO-OR does not have a higher mean score, but shows a smaller observed spread, a near-zero terminal OR residual, and a declining overshoot fraction, while the matched GRPO clipped-objective trace remains variable. Both group-relative methods exhibit substantially larger rollout-to-current log-ratio displacement than the GAE methods, and OR does not consistently reduce it. Thus, OR changes optimization behavior in both matched comparisons, but the observed reward effect differs between them. At $G=2$, the GRPO-OR diagnostics do not translate into a reward-score gain. Whether larger groups change this outcome remains open. The reported scores are training-time reward-model measurements, not held-out human-preference performance.

</details>


### [359] [A Continual Validation, Updating, and Decision-Making Framework for Self-Adaptive Digital Twins via Robust Model Predictive Control: A Case Study in Additive Manufacturing](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18164)
*Yi-Ping Chen,Ying-Kuan Tsai,Vispi Karkaria,Seul Lee,Daniel Apley,Wei Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: Proposes an adaptive Digital Twin framework using Fisher score drift detection, LoRA for efficient fine-tuning (<1% params), and Mann-Whitney U test for statistical validation, successfully maintaining surrogate model fidelity under concept drift.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Surrogate models in Digital Twins degrade over time due to concept drift, and existing frameworks lack principled detection, efficient adaptation, and statistical certification of improvements.

Method: Integrates (1) Fisher score-based multivariate drift detector, (2) Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for parameter-efficient continual learning, and (3) Mann-Whitney U test for online statistical validation of predictive improvements.

Result: Applied to stochastic linear system and directed energy deposition additive manufacturing; detects distribution shifts with short delays, restores predictive accuracy and uncertainty quantification under both abrupt and incremental drift.

Conclusion: Establishes a statistically rigorous and computationally tractable approach for maintaining trustworthy neural-network-based Digital Twins throughout their operational life cycle.

Abstract: Digital Twins rely on surrogate models to mirror physical systems in real time, yet these models can degrade as operating conditions evolve, a phenomenon known as concept drift. Maintaining surrogate fidelity under drift, particularly when models must also capture aleatoric uncertainty, remains an open challenge. Existing adaptive frameworks lack principled mechanisms for detecting when updates are needed, for efficiently adapting models from limited streaming data, and for certifying that updates genuinely improve predictive performance. Here we present an adaptive Digital Twin framework that integrates a Fisher score--based multivariate drift detector, Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) for parameter-efficient continual learning, and a Mann--Whitney $U$ test for online statistical validation. The framework monitors surrogate-model confidence via Fisher score vectors, triggers targeted fine-tuning of fewer than 1% of model parameters upon drift detection, and statistically certifies predictive improvement before deploying the updated surrogate. Applied to a stochastic linear system and a directed energy deposition additive manufacturing process as case studies, the framework successfully detects distributional shifts with short delays and restores both predictive accuracy and uncertainty quantification under abrupt and incremental drift. These results establish a statistically rigorous and computationally tractable pathway for sustaining the trustworthiness of neural-network--based Digital Twins throughout their operational life cycle.

</details>


### [360] [FlashRT: Agent Harness for Guiding Agents to Deploy Real-Time Multimodal Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18171)
*Krish Agarwal,Zhuoming Chen,Yanyuan Qin,Zhenyu Gu,Atri Rudra,Beidi Chen*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: FlashRT is an agent harness that uses a chain-of-program paradigm to automatically transform simple developer-written reference implementations of multimodal AI pipelines into optimized multi-GPU deployments, achieving up to 70x latency reduction and 3.6x throughput improvement across NVIDIA and AMD GPUs, even outperforming expert implementations like vLLM-Omni on AMD hardware.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-time multimodal applications (voice agents, interactive video generation) compose heterogeneous models into pipelines, but existing serving systems and auto-parallelism compilers are limited to fixed transformations and workload assumptions, requiring hand-crafted implementations for each new application.

Method: FlashRT uses a chain-of-program paradigm: (1) a coding agent transforms a reference implementation into an intermediate representation (IR) capturing data dependencies and persistent-state scopes, (2) validates the IR via a sequential interpreter, (3) performs static analyses to identify candidate transformations, and (4) iteratively implements, verifies, and benchmarks each candidate under a measurement-gated optimization loop.

Result: Up to ~70x latency reduction and 2.8x throughput improvement on NVIDIA B200 GPUs; on AMD MI355X, matches peak latency reduction while increasing throughput improvement to 3.6x; for Qwen3-Omni text-to-audio inference, 65% response latency reduction vs. expert vLLM-Omni implementation on AMD MI355X.

Conclusion: Agent-driven optimization can be more scalable on platforms with less mature expert optimization, effectively automating deployment decisions that previously required significant manual effort.

Abstract: Real-time multimodal applications, including voice agents and interactive video generation, compose heterogeneous models into pipelines whose efficient deployment requires application-specific decisions about placement, streaming, and intra-model parallelism. Existing serving systems and auto-parallelism compilers commit to limited transformations and fixed workload assumptions, so achieving high performance on a new application requires hand-crafting an efficient implementation. We present FlashRT, an agent harness that guides coding agents to lift simple developer-written reference implementations into optimized multi-GPU deployments that flexibly weigh target metrics like latency and throughput. Using a new chain-of-program paradigm, FlashRT directs a generic coding agent through a multi-pass transformation process where an agent transforms the reference into an intermediate representation (IR) to capture data dependencies and persistent-state scopes, validates this IR via a sequential interpreter, and performs static analyses to identify candidate transformations. Then, the agent iteratively implements, verifies, and benchmarks each candidate under a measurement-gated optimization loop to produce effective deployments that span different hardware budgets. Across various applications, including video world models and multimodal LLMs, FlashRT converts reference implementations into highly efficient deployments, delivering up to ~70x latency reduction and 2.8x throughput improvement on NVIDIA B200 GPUs. On AMD MI355X GPUs, FlashRT matches the peak latency reduction while increasing peak throughput improvement to 3.6x, demonstrating that agent-driven optimization can be more scalable on platforms with less mature expert optimization. In fact, for Qwen3-Omni text-to-audio inference, FlashRT reduces response latency by 65% compared to the expert vLLM-Omni implementation on AMD MI355X.

</details>


### [361] [Three-Body Scattering for Generative Modeling](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18198)
*Peng Sun,Zhenglin Cheng,Deyuan Liu,Jun Xie,Xinyi Shang,Tao Lin*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: TBSM introduces a novel one-step generative framework using distributional energy and three-body scattering, achieving competitive FID scores on ImageNet-256 without adversarial critics, autoregressive factorization, or prescribed noise-to-data paths.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing generative models rely on adversarial critics, prescribed noise-to-data paths (diffusion), or autoregressive factorization. This paper aims to develop a simpler, non-adversarial one-step generator using distributional energy as direct regression supervision.

Method: Three-Body Scattering Modeling (TBSM): each generated sample (projectile) is attracted toward one real source and repelled from one independently generated source, producing O(B) sample-level losses per batch. The conditional expectation of this interaction equals the 2-Wasserstein gradient-flow velocity. Tracking this expectation online reduces field noise.

Result: On ImageNet-256, TBSM achieves FID=2.23 with pixel-space PixelDiT-XL and FID=1.63 with latent-space DiT-XL at NFE=1, outperforming prior one-step methods.

Conclusion: Tracked three-body scattering provides a viable route to high-dimensional one-step generation, with a design map connecting diffusion-related supervision, Drift-like dynamics, and GAN-like objectives.

Abstract: Modern generative models typically rely on an adversarial critic, a prescribed noise-to-data path, or an autoregressive factorization. Instead, we show that a proper distributional energy can induce sample-level motion and provide direct regression supervision for a one-step generator. Three-Body Scattering Modeling (TBSM) for generation turns the energy distance into a constant-size per-projectile interaction: each projectile is attracted toward one real source and repelled from one independently generated source. Conditioned on the projectile and its condition, its expectation equals the $2$-Wasserstein gradient-flow velocity of $\frac12D_E^2(P_θ,Q)$. A batch of $B$ frozen-target events yields $O(B)$ sample-level losses, each using one reference for its condition instead of the minibatch-wide all-pairs field used by methods such as Drifting Models. Tracking this conditional expectation online can reduce field noise. Using scattering in frozen image features, TBSM trains one-step generators on ImageNet-256, achieving FID${}=2.23$ with pixel-space PixelDiT-XL and FID${}=1.63$ with latent-space DiT-XL at NFE${}=1$. We provide a design map relating diffusion-related supervision, Drift-like dynamics, and GAN-like objectives. These results establish tracked scattering as a route to high-dimensional one-step generation. Code: https://github.com/sp12138/TBSM.

</details>


### [362] [Causal Discovery on Irregular Time Series](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18226)
*Martim Penim,Ricardo Ribeiro Pereira,Jacopo Bono,Hugo Ferreira,Mário A. T. Figueiredo,Pedro Bizarro*

Main category: cs.LG

TL;DR: The paper extends PCMCI+ to handle irregularly sampled time series data by aggregating causal influences over temporal windows, outperforming standard PCMCI+ on synthetic irregular data.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-world tasks involve irregularly sampled event streams (sensors, healthcare, finance), but existing causal discovery methods assume regular and discrete lag structures, limiting applicability.

Method: Extends PCMCI+ by replacing fixed-lag dependencies with aggregation of causal influence over predefined temporal windows to handle irregular time series.

Result: The method consistently recovers underlying causal graphs and substantially outperforms standard PCMCI+ on synthetic irregular event streams under various signal-to-noise ratios.

Conclusion: The proposed extension effectively enables causal discovery for irregularly sampled temporal data, broadening the applicability of PCMCI+ to real-world scenarios.

Abstract: Causal discovery methods have shown strong performance in temporal systems, but they typically rely on regular and discrete lag structures, limiting their applicability to regularly sampled data. However, many real-world tasks require dealing with irregularly sampled streams of events, such as sensor streams, healthcare data, and financial transactions. In this work, we propose an extension of PCMCI+, a state-of-the-art method for causal discovery on regular multivariate time series, to allow for handling irregular time series. Instead of modelling causal relations through fixed-lag dependencies, our method aggregates causal influence over predefined temporal windows. We evaluate our method on synthetic irregular event streams with known causal structures under different signal-to-noise ratios, showing that it consistently recovers the underlying causal graph and substantially outperforms the standard PCMCI+ on irregularly sampled data.

</details>


<div id='cs.IT'></div>

# cs.IT [[Back]](#toc)

### [363] [EML-AirComp: Layered Over-the-Air Computation from a Single Nomographic Gate](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16360)
*Onur Günlü*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a reusable two-input AirComp gate for the exp-minus-log (EML) operation, enabling function computation via a single gate type across EML trees. It provides AWGN and fading implementations under peak-power constraints, and analyzes error propagation, latency, and positivity preservation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To avoid node-specific nonlinear gate designs in over-the-air computation by reusing a single EML gate across all internal nodes of a real-admissible EML tree.

Method: Derives AWGN and coherent flat fading implementations for the EML gate under peak-power constraints; analyzes error propagation, positivity preservation, and feasibility across the tree.

Result: Characterization of gate evaluations, dependency depth, latency, node-wise feasibility, deterministic error propagation, positivity preservation, and high-probability AWGN error bound for the complete tree.

Conclusion: The reusable EML gate enables efficient and analyzable over-the-air function computation with guaranteed positivity and bounded error propagation.

Abstract: Over-the-air computation (AirComp) exploits multiple-access superposition to compute functions of distributed data without separately decoding all terminal messages. We study a reusable two-input AirComp gate for the exp-minus-log (EML) operation $\eml(u,v)=\exp(u)-\log(v)$, $v>0$. Thus, all internal nodes of a prescribed real-admissible EML tree reuse one gate type, avoiding node-specific nonlinear gate designs. Given an explicit EML tree whose intermediate logarithm arguments remain positive on a given compact domain, we derive additive white Gaussian noise (AWGN) and coherent flat fading implementations under peak-power constraints. We then characterize the number of gate evaluations, the dependency depth, evaluation latency, node-wise feasibility, deterministic error propagation, positivity preservation, and a high-probability AWGN error bound for the complete tree. A four-terminal two-hop example gives explicit positivity and end-to-end error conditions, and a digital interface propagates quantization and gate errors across the tree.

</details>


### [364] [AISAC: Closing the Loop Between AI and Integrated Sensing and Communication for 6G](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16507)
*Mehdi Karbalayghareh,Abhishek Rajasekaran,Xiaoyan Ma,David J. Love,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces AISAC (AI-integrated sensing and communication) as a closed-loop framework merging ISAC and AIAC for 6G networks, where AI uses ISAC as a physical substrate and ISAC configurations must be optimized for learning alignment rather than classical sensing accuracy or communication rate alone.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAC and AIAC are currently treated as separate usage scenarios in 6G, but they are beginning to merge in practice. The paper motivates a unified closed-loop framework that combines sensing, communication, and AI learning.

Method: The paper presents a conceptual framework (AISAC) with a closed-loop architecture. It proposes a new physical-layer design principle where waveforms, beams, power, bandwidth, and sensing modes are configured for learning alignment rather than for sensing distortion or communication rate individually.

Result: Key insight: the most accurate sensing configuration from a classical estimation viewpoint is not necessarily the most useful for AI training or inference. Identifies a three-way sensing-communication-learning tension in the closed-loop architecture.

Conclusion: AISAC requires a paradigm shift in physical-layer design. The paper outlines a vehicular edge-intelligence use case and identifies open problems for theory, implementation, and standardization.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) and AI-and-communication (AIAC) are identified as separate usage scenarios in the ITU IMT-2030 vision for sixth-generation (6G) networks. In practice, however, these two directions are already beginning to merge. ISAC gives the network a way to observe the physical world, while AI gives the network a way to learn from those observations and act on them. This article introduces AI-integrated sensing and communication (AISAC) as a closed-loop framework for this merger. In AISAC, AI is not only a tool used to optimize an ISAC system. ISAC is also the physical substrate through which AI receives data, context, and connectivity. The key technical message is that AISAC requires a new physical-layer design principle, in which the ISAC waveform, beam, power, bandwidth, and sensing mode should be configured for learning alignment, not for sensing distortion or communication rate alone. In particular, the sensing configuration that is most accurate from a classical estimation viewpoint need not be the one that is most useful for training or inference. We present the AISAC landscape, explain why imperfect sensing changes the learning problem, develop the closed-loop architecture and its three-way sensing-communication-learning tension, and outline a vehicular edge-intelligence use case together with open problems for theory, implementation, and standardization.

</details>


### [365] [Variable Rate Lossy Source-Channel Coding over Channels with Feedback](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16600)
*Timothy Liu,Fady Alajaji,Tamás Linder*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A variable-rate lossy joint source-channel coding scheme for burst-noise channels with feedback is proposed, using multi-stage channel-optimized vector quantization and greedy bit allocation. It outperforms fixed-rate schemes by up to 4.5 dB on Markov noise channels.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve upon existing fixed-rate joint source-channel coding schemes for burst-noise communication channels with feedback, by introducing variable-rate allocation to better adapt to channel conditions.

Method: A multi-stage channel-optimized vector quantization system with greedy dynamic bit allocation among variable-rate residual quantizers at each stage, guided by channel output feedback received at the encoder.

Result: The variable-rate scheme consistently outperforms the fixed-rate baseline across all tested channel bit error rates and noise correlations, achieving SNR gains up to ~4.5 dB on M-th order Markov noise Polya contagion channels.

Conclusion: Variable-rate allocation with feedback significantly enhances robustness against burst-noise without requiring higher transmission power or bandwidth.

Abstract: A variable-rate lossy joint source-channel coding scheme for burst-noise communication channels with noiseless feedback is introduced. The scheme comprises a multi-stage channel optimized vector quantization system that dynamically allocates bits via a greedy algorithm among the variable-rate residual quantizers at each stage, based on the channel output sequence received at the encoder through the feedback link, thereby generalizing a prior fixed-rate scheme. Simulations over an $M$-th order Markov noise Polya contagion channel demonstrate that the proposed variable-rate scheme consistently outperforms the fixed-rate scheme, regardless of the channel bit error rate and noise correlation, achieving signal-to-noise ratio gains of up to about 4.5 dB.

</details>


### [366] [Leveraging von Mises Message-Passing for Massive MIMO Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16686)
*Sweta Suresh,Arthur Michon,Charly Poulliat,Maxime Guillaud,Claire Goursaud*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a belief propagation detector for mMIMO with PSK modulations using von Mises distributions, achieving low complexity independent of modulation order.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Optimal detectors like MAP have exponential complexity in mMIMO systems, motivating the need for low-complexity approximate detection methods.

Method: Uses belief propagation with directional statistics: continuous relaxation of PSK to the unit circle, von Mises parametric message representation for sparse/infinite-dimensional message handling.

Result: Low complexity independent of PSK modulation order; performance quantified and compared with Gaussian approximation-based detectors.

Conclusion: The proposed BP detector offers efficient approximate detection for mMIMO PSK systems, with extensions to imperfect channels.

Abstract: Detection in massive multiple input multiple output (mMIMO) systems suffers from exponential complexity while using optimal decoders like maximum a posteriori (MAP). We propose a belief propagation-based detector based on directional statistics, applicable to mMIMO systems relying on PSK modulations. Thanks to a continuous relaxation of the PSK modulation to the unit circle, and to the use of von Mises parametric representations of the messages to obtain sparse representations of the (generally infinite dimensional) messages, the proposed method allows for approximate detection with a low complexity which does not depend on the PSK modulation order. Extensions of the algorithm to imperfect channel realizations are also present. We quantify the performance and complexity of the proposed approach and compare it with detection algorithms based on Gaussian approximation.

</details>


### [367] [Revisiting the Stability of the Ingleton Inequality: A Tropicalization-Free Approach](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16773)
*Laszlo Csirmaz*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper simplifies the stability analysis of the Ingleton inequality using a tropicalization-free approach, improving error estimates, streamlining proofs, and resolving an open problem by deriving a new infinite family of entropy inequalities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To revisit and extend the stability results of the Ingleton inequality under small conditional mutual information violations, while avoiding the complex framework of tropical probability spaces used in prior work (Matveev & Romashchenko, 2026).

Method: Develops an alternative, tropicalization-free framework that simplifies proofs, derives explicit error terms, and constructs a new infinite family of entropy inequalities to establish stability of the sum of two Ingleton expressions.

Result: Improved error estimates and streamlined proofs for the stability of the Ingleton inequality; resolution of an open problem via a new infinite family of entropy inequalities demonstrating stability for summed Ingleton expressions.

Conclusion: The proposed tropicalization-free approach is simpler and more explicit than previous methods, successfully addressing open questions and enhancing the theoretical understanding of Ingleton inequality stability.

Abstract: The classical Ingleton inequality is known to hold for entropic points under specific exact conditional independence constraints. Recently, Matveev and Romashchenko (2026) investigated the stability of these implications, quantifying the extent to which the Ingleton inequality can be violated when a group of conditional mutual information terms is small but non-zero. While their proofs relied fundamentally on the complex framework of tropical probability spaces, we revisit these stability results using a completely tropicalization-free approach. By developing an alternative framework, we significantly streamline the underlying concepts and proofs, derive explicit error terms, and improve some estimates. Furthermore, we resolve an open problem posed in prior work by exhibiting a new infinite family of entropy inequalities that establishes the stability of the sum of two Ingleton expressions.

</details>


### [368] [Sustainable Air-Ground Integrated Coverage Networks: ISCC Architecture, Technologies, and Testbed](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16812)
*J. Liu,X. Zhang,M. Sheng,R. Zhang,N. Zhao,J. Wang,J. Li*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: A comprehensive survey on sustainable air-ground integrated coverage networks (AGICNs) for 6G, proposing an ISCC-driven architecture that balances coverage with carbon efficiency, achieving 20% power reduction with >90% coverage probability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AGICN studies focus on coverage enhancement while ignoring sustainability, and pursuing sustainable AGICNs introduces new challenges due to multidimensional resource coupling across heterogeneous air-ground segments.

Method: Proposes an ISCC (Integrated Sensing, Communication, and Computation)-driven architecture with closed-loop control for dynamic resource orchestration; introduces a multi-dimensional sustainability metric system covering operational efficiency, task-oriented performance, and full lifecycle carbon emissions.

Result: Experimental results on a real-world testbed demonstrate a 20% reduction in power consumption while achieving over 90% coverage probability.

Conclusion: Demonstrates the feasibility of sustainable AGICNs for future green networks, balancing coverage capacity with carbon efficiency in low-altitude economies.

Abstract: The rapid emergence of sixth-generation (6G) networks and the low-altitude economy has accelerated the evolution of wireless infrastructures toward air-ground integrated coverage networks (AGICNs), which seamlessly fuse terrestrial and aerial communication resources. However, existing AGICN studies primarily focus on coverage enhancement, while ignoring sustainability. Pursuing sustainable AGICNs introduces new challenges due to the multidimensional resource coupling across heterogeneous air-ground segments. In view of this, this paper presents a comprehensive survey and tutorial on sustainable AGICNs, aiming to balance coverage capacity with carbon efficiency in low-altitude economies. An integrated sensing, communication, and computation (ISCC)-driven architecture, which enables dynamic resource orchestration through closed-loop control, is proposed. We thus introduce a multi-dimensional sustainability metric system, which covers operational efficiency, task-oriented performance, and full lifecycle carbon emissions, to quantify energy and carbon footprints. We review enabling technologies, including artificial intelligence, hybrid precoding, integrated sensing and communication, and simultaneous wireless information and power transfer, and discuss their integration into the ISCC framework to minimize energy consumption while maintaining robust coverage. Experimental results on a real-world testbed demonstrate a 20% reduction in power consumption while achieving over 90% coverage probability, highlighting the feasibility of sustainable AGICNs for future green networks.

</details>


### [369] [Decoding Desarguesian spread codes beyond half minimum distance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16890)
*Ermes Franch,Chunlei Li,Angelica Piccirillo*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies decoding capabilities of Desarguesian spread codes, showing that unique decoding is possible beyond half the minimum distance. A new decoding algorithm is developed for handling both insertions and deletions, even when their total dimension exceeds half the minimum distance, provided deletions are at most k-2 dimensions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Spread codes are important constant-dimension subspace-metric codes with minimum distance 2k. The authors want to understand if decoding beyond the unique decoding radius (half the minimum distance) is possible, and to develop practical algorithms for handling both insertions and deletions in the subspace metric.

Method: The authors study the Nearest Neighbor Decoder for Desarguesian spread codes analytically. They then propose a new decoding algorithm that can uniquely decode under both insertions and deletions, with two refinements that empirically approach the performance of the Nearest Neighbor Decoder.

Result: The proposed algorithm succeeds with small decoding failure when the sum of insertion/deletion dimensions exceeds half the minimum distance, as long as deletions are at most k-2. The refined versions handle nearly as many insertions as the Nearest Neighbor Decoder empirically.

Conclusion: Desarguesian spread codes can be decoded beyond the unique decoding radius, and the proposed algorithm provides a practical way to achieve this for both insertion and deletion errors.

Abstract: Spread codes are a well-known family of constant-dimension subspace-metric codes. For constant dimension $k$ and ambient space dimension $n$ being a multiple of $k$, these codes have minimum distance $2k$ and a rich geometric structure. In this paper, we study the decoding capabilities of the Nearest Neighbor Decoder for Desarguesian spread codes, establishing that unique decoding is still achievable beyond half the minimum distance. Motivated by this, we develop a new decoding algorithm to uniquely decode Desarguesian spread codes in the presence of both insertions and deletions, which increase and decrease, respectively, the dimension of the transmitted codeword. Even when the sum of the dimensions of insertions and deletions exceeds half the minimum distance, provided that deletions are of dimension at most $k-2$, the algorithm succeeds with a small decoding failure. We also propose two refinements to this algorithm that, empirically, can handle nearly as many insertions as the Nearest Neighbor Decoder.

</details>


### [370] [Tight Sample Bounds for Renyi and Min-Entropy Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16966)
*Arman Adibi,Piotr Krysta*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper characterizes the sample complexity of estimating min-entropy and Rényi entropy (order α > 1) for k-symbol alphabets, proving min-entropy estimation requires Θ(k log k) samples (more than Shannon entropy's Θ(k/log k)), and establishing tight bounds for integer α with an unbiased falling-factorial estimator and hidden-heavy-coordinate lower bounds.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To precisely characterize the sample complexity of min-entropy and Rényi entropy estimation, correcting a previously stated incorrect bound for min-entropy and providing tight bounds for all integer α > 1 (and non-integer α ≥ 1.001).

Method: Upper bounds use largest empirical frequency with dyadic grouping (for min-entropy) and unbiased falling-factorial estimators based on α-way collisions (for Rényi entropy). Lower bounds use a hidden-heavy-coordinate construction where a slightly heavier symbol is placed at a random location.

Result: Min-entropy estimation: Θ(k log k) samples. Integer α (2 ≤ α ≤ c₀ log k): Θ_{c₀}(α k^{1-1/α}). Real α ≥ 1.001: Ω_{c₀}(α k^{1-1/α}) lower bound. Min-entropy approximates H_α when α is a large multiple of log k, giving Θ_ε(k log k) in the high-order regime.

Conclusion: Min-entropy requires Θ(log² k) more samples than Shannon entropy, correcting prior work. The bounds for Rényi entropy are tight up to constant factors, with the factor α proven unavoidable.

Abstract: Estimating entropy from samples is fundamental in information theory and property testing. Shannon entropy measures average uncertainty and can be estimated to constant additive accuracy over a $k$-symbol alphabet using $Θ(k/\log k)$ samples. Min-entropy depends only on the most likely symbol. Both are special cases of order-$α$ R'{e}nyi entropy, $H_α$.
  We characterize the sample complexity of estimating min-entropy and R'{e}nyi entropy for $k$ and integer $α>1$; our lower bounds also hold for noninteger $α\ge1.001$. We prove that min-entropy estimation to constant additive accuracy has sample complexity $Θ(k\log k)$. The upper bound uses the largest empirical frequency and concentration via dyadic grouping. The matching lower bound hides a slightly heavier symbol at a uniformly random location. Thus, min-entropy requires $Θ(\log^2 k)$ more samples than Shannon entropy and corrects a previously stated $Θ(k/\log k)$ characterization.
  For every integer $2\leα\le c_0\log k$, we prove the matching fixed-accuracy bound $Θ_{c_0}(αk^{1-1/α})$. Previous results gave $Ω_α(k^{1-1/α})$ for fixed integer $α>1$ and $O_{c_0}(α^2k^{1-1/α})$ for all integer $α>1$. Our upper bound analyzes an unbiased falling-factorial estimator based on $α$-way collisions, while a hidden-heavy-coordinate construction gives the matching lower bound and shows that the factor $α$ is unavoidable. For every real $1.001\leα\le c_0\log k$, we prove the uniform lower bound $Ω_{c_0}(αk^{1-1/α})$. Finally, since $0\le H_α(p)-H_\infty(p)\le\log k/(α-1)$, min-entropy uniformly approximates $H_α$ when $α$ is a sufficiently large multiple of $\log k$. Combining this reduction with our min-entropy bounds gives $Θ_\varepsilon(k\log k)$ sample complexity in the high-order regime.

</details>


### [371] [Transmit Coefficients and Receive Combining Vector Design for OTA-FL with Imperfect CSI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16983)
*Xiaoyan Ma,Shahryar Zehtabi,Yinan Zou,Taejoon Kim,Christopher G. Brinton*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper addresses CSI uncertainty in over-the-air federated learning by proposing a Lyapunov-based optimization framework that jointly designs transmit coefficients and receive combining vectors to minimize long-term MSE, demonstrating significant test accuracy improvements on multiple datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: OTA-FL suffers from imperfect model aggregation due to channel state information (CSI) uncertainty, which distorts learning performance. The authors aim to minimize the long-term mean squared error (MSE) under imperfect CSI conditions.

Method: The authors establish an upper bound for time-averaged MSE through convergence analysis, then develop an optimization framework jointly designing transmit coefficients (devices) and receive combining vectors (PS). For causal CSI scenarios, a Lyapunov-based optimization with virtual queues handles temporal dependencies and energy constraints.

Result: Evaluations on Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10, and CIFAR-100 show significant reduction in test accuracy degradation caused by imperfect CSI, with superiority over benchmark schemes.

Conclusion: The proposed joint optimization and Lyapunov-based methods effectively mitigate CSI uncertainty in OTA-FL, improving learning performance under realistic channel conditions.

Abstract: Over-the-air (OTA) computation has recently gained significant attentions as an effective approach to enhance the communication efficiency of wireless federated learning (FL). By enabling simultaneous transmission and aggregation of local model updates, OTA-FL can substantially reduce both latency and bandwidth consumption. However, a key challenge lies in the imperfect aggregation of global models caused by channel state information (CSI) uncertainty, which introduces distortion to the final learning performance. To address this issue, we study the long-term mean squared error (MSE) minimization problem for OTA-FL under imperfect CSI conditions. Through convergence analysis, we establish an upper bound for the time-averaged MSE, thereby revealing the effect of aggregation errors accumulated throughout multiple communication rounds on the overall training performances. Based on this analysis, an optimization framework is developed to minimize the long-term MSE via the joint design of (i) transmit coefficients at the local devices and (ii) receive combining vectors at the parameter server (PS). Since this alternating optimization approach requires non-causal CSI, a Lyapunov-based optimization method is further introduced to handle causal CSI scenarios. By incorporating virtual queues to characterize long-term energy consumption, the proposed method effectively decouples temporal dependencies and allows transmit coefficients to be optimized based on the causal CSI of each aggregation round. Comprehensive evaluations on Fashion-MNIST, CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 datasets have demonstrated that the proposed algorithms can significantly reduce the degradation of test accuracy caused by imperfect CSI. Comparisons with other benchmark schemes further verify the superiority of our proposed algorithms.

</details>


### [372] [Cofilling Shattering: A Syndrome-Support Hierarchy for Check Erasures](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17028)
*Joshua Steier*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces Shat(q,s)(A) as a new invariant for binary linear codes that measures the minimum common check support needed for a q-dimensional syndrome subspace where all nonzero elements have coset-leader weight at least s. It connects this to generalized Hamming weights, code length bounds, and demonstrates behavior through examples like repetition codes and simplicial coboundary maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To define and study a hierarchy of invariants (Shat(q,s)) that generalizes the concept of generalized Hamming weights, capturing finer information about the structure of linear codes and their realizations, particularly distinguishing between releasing independent syndromes versus releasing a subspace without easy linear combinations.

Method: The authors define Shat(q,s)(A) as the least common check support of a q-dimensional syndrome subspace whose every nonzero element has coset-leader weight at least s. They prove finiteness properties, relate to the binary Griesmer bound (N₂(q,s)), and analyze behavior under row-equivalent realizations. Examples include pair-repetition codes and simplicial coboundary maps.

Result: R_q(C_A) ≥ N₂(q,s). For pair-repetition codes, the standard realization achieves Shat(q,s)(H₀)=N₂(q,s), while a row-equivalent realization can have value q. The hierarchy is coordinate-relabeling invariant but changes under check basis changes. For s=1, reduces to generalized Hamming weights; for s≥2, even identical labeled cut codes can differ.

Conclusion: The Shat hierarchy provides a more refined invariant than generalized Hamming weights for binary linear codes, with sensitivity to the specific realization (check basis) and the ability to distinguish codes that generalized Hamming weights cannot, particularly for s≥2.
