Abstract: Let $A:\mathbb{F}_2^n\to\mathbb{F}_2^m$ be a binary linear map with fixed coordinate bases, let $C_A=\ker A$, and let $λ_A(y)$ be the minimum Hamming weight of a preimage of the syndrome $y$. We define $\operatorname{Shat}_{q,s}(A)$ as the least common check support of a $q$-dimensional syndrome subspace whose every nonzero element has coset-leader weight at least $s$. It therefore distinguishes release of $q$ independent syndromes from release of a subspace with no easy linear combination. Deleting check coordinates $F$ releases $\ker A_{\bar{F}}/\ker A$, canonically isomorphic to $(\operatorname{im} A)[F]$.
  Finiteness implies $R_q(C_A)\ge \mathsf{N}_2(q,s)$, where $\mathsf{N}_2(q,s)$ is the shortest length of a binary code of dimension $q$ and distance at least $s$; profile-Griesmer bounds independently control common check support. The hierarchy is coordinate-relabeling invariant but can change under a change of check basis. For the pair-repetition code $C_n=\{(x,x):x\in\mathbb{F}_2^n\}$, the standard realization $H_0=[I_n\ I_n]$ has $\operatorname{Shat}_{q,s}(H_0)=\mathsf{N}_2(q,s)$ whenever feasible. For every $q\ge 1$ and $s\ge 2$, with $n=\mathsf{N}_2(q,s)$, a row-equivalent realization of the same code has value $q$.
  For a simplicial coboundary map $A=δ_k$, check erasure is top-face erasure and the released quotient is emergent cohomology. At $s=1$ the hierarchy reduces to generalized Hamming weights and is Tutte-determined; for $s\ge 2$, even identical labeled cut codes can have different values.

</details>


### [373] [Dynamic Channel Knowledge Map: Fundamentals, Construction, and Applications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17133)
*Wenjun Jiang,Xiaojun Yuan*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces dynamic Channel Knowledge Map (CKM) as a middle layer linking quasi-static environmental priors to physical-layer signal processing, addressing time-varying channel components from dynamic scatterers, terminal attitude changes, and RF impairments for 6G systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing CKM research focuses only on quasi-static propagation features (buildings, terrain), leaving time-varying channel components (dynamic scatterers, terminal attitude changes, RF impairments) unaddressed. The paper aims to fill this gap.

Method: The paper presents the concept of dynamic CKM as a middle layer, clarifies its relationship with quasi-static CKM and physical layer, surveys representative construction methods, and discusses applications in pilot design, interference suppression, and ISAC.

Result: Dynamic CKM provides time-evolving channel representations that link quasi-static environmental priors to physical-layer processing, supporting pilot design, interference suppression, and integrated sensing and communications.

Conclusion: Key open research directions lie in co-design of dynamic CKM construction and physical-layer signal processing, with dynamic CKM playing an important architectural role in emerging 6G systems.

Abstract: Wireless communication networks are evolving toward extremely large antenna arrays, millimeter-wave and terahertz bands, and dense heterogeneous deployments, all of which increase channel dimensionality and make channel acquisition increasingly costly. Channel knowledge map (CKM) establishes a mapping from geographical locations to channel characteristics, providing location-specific prior information to reduce the overhead of channel acquisition. Most existing CKM research, however, has focused on quasi-static propagation features shaped by quasi-static environmental structures such as buildings and terrain, leaving unaddressed the time-varying channel component introduced by dynamic scatterers, terminal attitude changes, and radio-frequency (RF) impairments. This article presents a new concept of dynamic CKM as a middle layer that links quasi-static environmental priors to physical-layer signal processing by providing time-evolving channel representations. We first introduce the fundamentals of dynamic CKM, clarifying its relationship with the quasi-static CKM and the physical layer. We then survey representative construction methods and discuss how dynamic CKM can support pilot design, interference suppression, and integrated sensing and communications. Finally, we outline key open research directions in the co-design of dynamic CKM construction and physical-layer signal processing. These discussions offer an architectural perspective on the role of dynamic CKM in emerging 6G systems.

</details>


### [374] [Joint Channel Estimation and Dynamics-Aware Grouping for Time-Varying RIS-Assisted OTA Federated Learning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17169)
*Ziqi Li,Shuangzhi Li,Uchechukwu Awada,Jiankang Zhang*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: Proposes a unified framework integrating GRU-based temporal channel estimation and dynamics-aware user grouping for reliable RIS-assisted OTA federated learning under imperfect CSI and user heterogeneity.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: RIS-assisted OTA-FL suffers from time-varying channels, imperfect CSI, and user heterogeneity, which degrade aggregation accuracy and cause model update cancellation.

Method: A closed-loop framework combining GRU-based temporal modeling for CSI evolution, OTA federated aggregation with personalization, and a dynamics-aware grouping strategy using long-term path-loss and short-term channel dynamics.

Result: Substantial gains in CSI estimation accuracy and OTA aggregation performance in low-pilot and high-mobility regimes, with improved convergence speed and robustness under strong user heterogeneity.

Conclusion: The proposed dynamics-aware joint channel estimation and user grouping framework effectively addresses key limitations in RIS-assisted OTA-FL systems.

Abstract: Reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted over-the-air federated learning (OTA-FL) enables efficient distributed intelligence but suffers from time-varying channels, imperfect channel state information (CSI), and strong user heterogeneity, which jointly degrade aggregation accuracy and cause severe model update cancellation. To address these issues, we propose a unified framework for joint channel estimation and dynamics-aware user grouping in RIS-assisted OTA-FL systems, enabling reliable learning under imperfect CSI and heterogeneous dynamics. The framework integrates gated recurrent unit (GRU) for temporal modeling to capture time-varying CSI evolution, OTA-based federated aggregation with personalization, and RIS-aware physical-layer optimization in a closed loop. In addition, we design a dynamics-aware grouping strategy based on long-term path-loss and short-term channel dynamics to reduce inter-user conflicts under heterogeneous conditions. Simulation results show that the proposed method achieves substantial gains in CSI estimation accuracy and OTA aggregation performance in low-pilot and high-mobility regimes, while improving convergence speed and robustness under strong user heterogeneity.

</details>


### [375] [Rate-Distortion-Perception Theory: Redefining the Fundamental Limits of Information Representation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17232)
*Photios A. Stavrou,Giuseppe Serra,Marios Kountouris*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This tutorial provides a structured overview of rate-distortion-perception (RDP) theory, extending classical rate-distortion theory by incorporating perceptual quality as a third axis. It covers coding principles, achievability results, optimization methods for computing the RDP function (including alternating minimization, Newton-based methods, and convex optimization), analytical cases (Gaussian sources, perfect-realism regime), and future research directions at the intersection of information theory, neural compression, and perception-aware systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Classical rate-distortion theory uses distortion measures like MSE that fail to capture perceptual quality and semantic validity. As modern learning-driven applications demand better perceptual quality, there is a need to extend the RD framework by introducing perception as a fundamental axis, quantified via distributional similarity between source and reconstructed signals.

Method: The tutorial provides a structured overview of perception-aware lossy compression principles, surveys achievability results under different randomness assumptions, and presents a unifying optimization viewpoint for computing the RDPF. It covers computational tools including alternating minimization schemes, Newton-based methods, and convex optimization formulations, along with analytically tractable cases like Gaussian sources and the perfect-realism regime.

Result: The tutorial systematically characterizes the rate-distortion-perception function (RDPF) as defined by Blau and Michaeli for both discrete and continuous sources under broad families of perceptual constraints (f-divergences, alpha-divergences, Wasserstein-based metrics). It demonstrates computational approaches for finding RDP limits.

Conclusion: The tutorial outlines promising research directions at the intersection of information theory, neural compression, robust source coding, and perception-aware networked control systems, emphasizing the coding-theoretic and computational machinery for characterizing, computing, and interpreting RDP limits.

Abstract: Classical rate-distortion (RD) theory has long established the fundamental limits of lossy compression by quantifying the minimum number of bits required to represent a source under a prescribed distortion constraint. However, widely used distortion measures such as mean-squared error often fail to capture perceptual quality or semantic validity, which are increasingly central in modern learning-driven applications. Rate-distortion-perception (RDP) theory extends the RD framework by introducing perception as a third fundamental axis, quantified via distributional similarity between the source and reconstructed signals, leading to the rate-distortion-perception function (RDPF). This tutorial provides a structured overview of the coding principles underlying perception-aware lossy compression and surveys recent achievability results under different randomness assumptions. It then presents a unifying optimization viewpoint for computing the RDPF as defined by Blau and Michaeli, for both discrete and continuous sources under broad families of perceptual constraints, including f-divergences, alpha-divergences, and Wasserstein-based metrics. Special attention is given to computational tools such as alternating minimization schemes, Newton-based methods, and convex optimization formulations, as well as to analytically tractable cases such as Gaussian sources and the perfect-realism regime. Unlike recent broad surveys that emphasize generative architectures and AI-empowered communication systems, this tutorial focuses on the coding-theoretic and computational machinery needed to characterize, compute, and interpret the RDP limits. Finally, the tutorial outlines promising research directions at the intersection of information theory, neural compression, robust source coding, and perception-aware networked control systems.

</details>


### [376] [Critical-Set-Aided Simplified Blind SCL Recognition of Polar Codes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17480)
*Changwei Tu,Cheng Yang,Xianzhao Feng,Kai Niu*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a critical-set-aided simplified blind successive cancellation list (SBSCL) recognition method for polar codes that reduces complexity by performing two-hypothesis path expansion only at critical positions, using density-evolution-based bounds for improved reliability.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Blind recognition of polar codes from noisy observations is essential for non-cooperative signal processing, but existing BSCL recognition suffers from high complexity due to two-hypothesis path expansion at every source-bit position.

Method: The authors first analyze recognition-error positions in BSC recognition and relate them to Bhattacharyya-parameter-based upper bounds. They propose SBSCL, which selects critical-set positions and performs two-hypothesis path expansion only there, while using BSC recognition elsewhere. They further refine performance analysis using density-evolution-based bounds with optimized Chernoff and overlap coefficients.

Result: DE-based bounds are tighter than Bhattacharyya-parameter-based bounds, with a gap within 1 dB at recognition-error probability of 10^{-2}. SBSCL achieves nearly the same recognition success rate as BSCL, and the critical set size decreases rapidly as SNR increases.

Conclusion: The proposed SBSCL method significantly reduces complexity while maintaining recognition performance, and the DE-based bounds provide more accurate performance analysis for blind polar code recognition.

Abstract: Blind recognition of polar codes from noisy observations is a key problem in non-cooperative signal processing. Although existing blind successive cancellation list (BSCL) recognition exploits channel soft information, it performs two-hypothesis path expansion at every source-bit position, resulting in high complexity. In this paper, we first analyze the first recognition-error positions in the blind successive cancellation (BSC) recognition and observe that they are closely related to the corresponding contribution terms in the existing Bhattacharyya-parameter-based upper bounds. Based on this observation, a critical-set-aided simplified blind successive cancellation list (SBSCL) recognition method is proposed. SBSCL performs two-hypothesis path expansion only at the selected critical-set positions and keeps BSC recognition at the remaining positions, thereby reducing complexity. To improve the reliability of critical-set selection and refine the performance analysis, density-evolution (DE)-based bounds are further developed. Under the ideal SC-consistent condition, the synthetic log-likelihood-ratio (LLR) distributions obtained from density evolution are used to compute the optimized Chernoff coefficient for the upper bound and the overlap coefficient for the lower bound. Simulation results show that the DE-based bounds are tighter than the Bhattacharyya-parameter-based bounds. In the considered settings, the gap between the DE upper and lower bounds is within $1$ dB around a recognition-error probability of $10^{-2}$. Furthermore, SBSCL achieves nearly the same recognition success rate as BSCL, and the size of critical set decreases rapidly as the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) increases.

</details>


### [377] [Generalized BCH Codes and Twisted Goppa Codes Attaining Their Designed Distances](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17517)
*Yaqi Chen,Hao Chen,Cunsheng Ding,Huimin Lao,Chao Liu,Conghui Xie*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper studies minimum distances of generalized BCH codes and twisted Goppa codes via parity-check matrices, providing conditions for achieving designed distances and characterizing infinite families that attain these distances.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Determining the true minimum distance of alternant codes is notoriously difficult in coding theory; the paper aims to bridge the gap between designed and actual minimum distances for specific subclasses.

Method: The authors derive necessary and sufficient conditions for alternant codes to attain their designed distance using parity-check matrix analysis, then apply these conditions to generalized BCH codes and twisted Goppa codes.

Result: Broad classes of generalized BCH codes are proven to have minimum distances equal to their designed distances, yielding explicit infinite families. For twisted Goppa codes Γ(L,g,η) with deg g = t, the condition for d(Γ(L,g,η)) = t+1 is characterized, also producing structured infinite families.

Conclusion: The paper provides novel theoretical characterizations and explicit infinite families for both generalized BCH and twisted Goppa codes where the minimum distance meets the designed distance, advancing the understanding of alternant code distances.

Abstract: Determining the true minimum distance of an alternant code remains a notoriously difficult problem in coding theory. In this paper, we study the minimum distances of generalized BCH codes and twisted Goppa codes through their parity-check matrices. We first give a necessary and sufficient condition for an alternant code to attain its designed distance and apply it to generalized BCH codes. As applications, we prove that broad classes of generalized BCH codes have minimum distances equal to their designed distances. These classes provide explicit infinite families rather than isolated examples. We characterize when a twisted Goppa code $Γ(L,g,η)$ with $\operatorname{deg} g=t$ satisfies $d(Γ(L,g,η))=t+1$, and derive structured classes and infinite families attaining this distance.

</details>


### [378] [Enhanced ODMA for Massive Sparse Access: Hybrid Data-Frozen Bit Transmission and Fixed-Point Analysis over Block Fading Channels](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17553)
*Guanghui Song,Jianxiang Yan,Ying Li,Zhaoji Zhang,Jun Cheng*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces ODMA, a scheme using minimal frozen symbols (5-20 per user in 300-user systems) for joint multi-user channel estimation and iterative decoding, achieving performance equivalent to perfect channel knowledge, along with a fixed-point analysis method for iterative convergence prediction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable efficient joint multi-user channel estimation and iterative decoding in massive access systems while minimizing pilot overhead and computational complexity.

Method: Proposes on-off division multiple access (ODMA) with sparsely distributed frozen symbols in codewords, using low-complexity single-user estimation/decoding with iterative interference cancellation, plus a fixed-point analysis for convergence estimation.

Result: Achieves performance equivalent to perfectly known user channels with only 5-20 frozen symbols per user in 300-user systems; fixed-point method accurately estimates iterative convergence without extensive Monte Carlo simulation.

Conclusion: ODMA with frozen symbols is an efficient, low-complexity solution for massive multi-user systems, and the fixed-point analysis provides a practical design tool for arbitrary code parameters.

Abstract: This paper proposes a novel on-off division multiple access (ODMA) transmission scheme that enables efficient joint multi-user channel estimation and iterative decoding by inserting a small number of frozen symbols into the codewords. Functionally analogous to pilots, these symbols are sparsely distributed within the codeword. Unlike conventional pilot-based methods, our approach requires only a minimal number of frozen symbols (e.g., $5\sim20$ symbols per user in a 300-user system), which serve dual purposes as both estimation references and decoding aids. By employing low-complexity single-user channel estimation and decoding, combined with simple iterative interference cancellation, the scheme achieves performance equivalent to that with perfectly known user channels, even when accounting for the additional energy and bandwidth costs of the frozen symbols. Furthermore, for the large-scale ODMA sparse multiple access system, this paper proposes a fixed-point analysis method, which can accurately estimate the iterative convergence performance over multi-user block fading channels by only leveraging the decoding functions under the single-user AWGN channel. This method is applicable to performance analysis for arbitrary code lengths, code rates, and decoding algorithms. It eliminates the need for extensive Monte Carlo simulation time, and provides an efficient tool for the design of multi-user codes.

</details>


### [379] [Skew CRT codes and their decoding in poly skew metric](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17737)
*Kayode Epiphane Nouetowa,Olivier Ruatta*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: The paper introduces skew CRT codes and poly-skew metric with error model, plus a decoding algorithm.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To introduce a new family of codes (skew CRT codes) with a novel metric (poly-skew metric) and associated error model, and to provide a decoding algorithm.

Method: The paper introduces skew CRT codes and the poly-skew metric with an associated error model, and develops a decoding algorithm for this code family under that metric.

Result: A new family of codes (skew CRT codes) and a decoding algorithm under the poly-skew metric.

Conclusion: Skew CRT codes with poly-skew metric are introduced and a decoding algorithm is provided.

Abstract: In this paper, we introduce skew CRT codes and the poly-skew metric with associated error model, and we provide a decoding algorithm for this family of codes under the poly-skew metric.

</details>


### [380] [Autonomous Discovery of Wireless Communications Algorithms](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17762)
*Fayçal Aït Aoudia,Jakob Hoydis,Sebastian Cammerer,Gian Marti,Merlin Nimier-David,Nicolas Roussel,Alexander Keller*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: AITE is an LLM-driven evolutionary search framework that autonomously designs wireless communication algorithms. It outperforms baselines on OTFS equalization (3.6x latency reduction) and matches neural receivers on pilot-free OFDM, demonstrating strong potential for autonomous algorithm discovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: LLM-driven evolutionary search has shown promise in other scientific fields but remains unexplored for wireless communications algorithm design, motivating the need to bridge this gap.

Method: The AITE framework employs LLM-driven evolutionary search to autonomously evolve algorithms for complex wireless communication problems while navigating performance-complexity tradeoffs.

Result: (1) OTFS equalizer: outperforms best-known solutions with 3.6x computational latency reduction. (2) Pilot-free OFDM receiver: achieves performance parity with state-of-the-art neural receivers using explicit, explainable algorithms.

Conclusion: LLM-driven evolutionary search shows strong potential for autonomously discovering next-generation wireless communications algorithms.

Abstract: Large language model (LLM)-driven evolutionary search is an emerging algorithm-discovery paradigm that has already produced novel results in several scientific fields. Yet its application to wireless communications remains largely unexplored. To bridge this gap, we introduce The AI Telco Engineer (AITE), a framework to autonomously design algorithms for complex communication problems, while navigating performance-complexity tradeoffs. We showcase AITE on two challenging physical-layer problems: designing an equalizer for an orthogonal time-frequency space (OTFS) system, and constructing a receiver algorithm for an orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) system using a custom constellation and operating without pilots. For the first task, AITE develops algorithms that outperform the best-known solutions while reducing computational latency by a factor of 3.6 compared to the strongest baseline. For the second task, it discovers the first explicit, explainable algorithms that achieve performance parity with state-of-the-art neural receivers. These results demonstrate the strong potential of LLM-driven evolutionary search for the autonomous discovery of next-generation wireless communications algorithms.

</details>


### [381] [Task-Oriented Precoding for Edge Inference over Large-Scale MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17877)
*Hongru Li,Zeyan Zhuang,Zixin Wang,Hengtao He,Shenghui Song,Jun Zhang,Khaled B. Letaief*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a statistical CSIT-based MIMO precoder for multi-device distributed inference, using random matrix theory to approximate a task-aware (MCR²) utility, avoiding costly instantaneous channel feedback.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing task-oriented precoding requires instantaneous CSIT, which incurs high overhead (feedback, reoptimization per coherence block) in multi-device MIMO systems.

Method: Develops a random-matrix-theoretic framework using statistical CSIT (channel covariance + feature statistics). Derives a deterministic approximation of the MCR² utility via random matrix theory, and optimizes it with a projected block-coordinate ascent and successive convex approximation algorithm under per-device power constraints.

Result: Experiments on ModelNet10 validate the deterministic approximation and show that the statistical precoder outperforms competitive benchmarks in task-aware mode allocation and inference performance.

Conclusion: Statistical CSIT-based precoding can effectively reduce overhead while preserving task-relevant information for distributed inference in multi-device MIMO systems.

Abstract: Future wireless networks are expected to support networked artificial intelligence (AI) services, where multiple devices transmit learned features to an edge server for distributed inference. This setting calls for task-oriented physical-layer optimization, where wireless transmission should preserve useful information for inference rather than only maximize the rate or reconstruct the transmitted signals. A key physical-layer control variable is the multiple-input multiple-output precoder, which determines how device features are shaped and combined over wireless channels. Existing task-oriented precoding methods typically adapt the precoder to instantaneous channel state information at the transmitter (CSIT). However, in multi-device MIMO systems, acquiring the aggregate channel, feeding back CSI or optimized precoders, and reoptimizing across coherence blocks introduce substantial overhead. This paper develops a random-matrix-theoretic framework based on statistical CSIT that designs a slow-timescale precoder from channel covariance statistics and training-set feature statistics, without requiring instantaneous CSIT. We adopt maximal coding rate reduction (MCR${^2}$) to measure the class separability of the received features, yielding a task-aware utility for MIMO precoder design. Since this utility still depends on random small-scale fading, we derive a deterministic approximation that converts it into a fixed-point objective depending only on long-term statistics and large-system dimension ratios via random matrix theory. A projected block-coordinate ascent and successive convex approximation algorithm is developed to optimize this deterministic objective under per-device power constraints. Experiments on ModelNet10 verify the approximation and show that the proposed statistical precoder improves task-aware mode allocation and inference performance over competitive benchmarks.

</details>


### [382] [Optimal Domain-Aware Privacy Mechanisms for Synthetic Data Generation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18021)
*Sajani Vithana,Sangwon Jung,Haoyang Hu,Viveck R. Cadambe,Flavio P. Calmon,Haewon Jeong*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper introduces PubMix, a public-data-aware differential privacy mechanism for histogram-based synthetic data generation that theoretically and empirically outperforms domain-agnostic DP mechanisms by incorporating public data directly into the privacy mechanism itself rather than just in pre/post-processing steps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing approaches use public data only indirectly (pre/post-processing) with domain-agnostic DP mechanisms. This work aims to theoretically and practically incorporate public data directly into DP mechanisms to improve privacy-fidelity trade-offs.

Method: The authors establish a theoretical framework for domain-aware DP mechanisms using normalized histograms as distribution estimators, characterize the asymptotically optimal mechanism, and propose PubMix—a public-data-aware DP mechanism for histogram-based data synthesis.

Result: PubMix significantly improves synthetic data generation quality over domain-agnostic privacy mechanisms in experimental evaluations.

Conclusion: Directly embedding public data into DP mechanisms (domain-aware approach) yields better privacy-utility trade-offs than indirect use of public data, with PubMix serving as an effective instantiation for histogram-based pipelines.

Abstract: Differential privacy (DP) imposes fundamental trade-offs between privacy and statistical fidelity in synthetic data generation. While access to public data has been shown to improve these trade-offs empirically, existing approaches use public data only indirectly, through pre-processing (e.g., using pre-trained generative models) or post-processing steps (e.g., matching target statistics estimated from public datasets), while relying on domain-agnostic DP mechanisms. In this work, we lay the theoretical framework to study the principled incorporation of public data into DP mechanisms themselves. We consider normalized histograms as distribution estimators and characterize the asymptotically optimal domain-aware privacy mechanism within a specific class of DP mechanisms. We introduce PubMix, a public-data-aware DP mechanism that can be used in histogram-based data synthesis pipelines. Our experiments demonstrate that PubMix significantly improves synthetic data generation quality compared to domain-agnostic privacy mechanisms.

</details>


### [383] [When Phase Doesn't Matter: Self-Coherent Over-the-Air Computation at Sub-THz](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18052)
*Sherif Ghozzy,Mohamed Seif,H. Vincent Poor,Kaushik Sengupta*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a self-coherent, synthesizer-free over-the-air computation (OAC) framework using Kramers-Kronig (KK) reception, which eliminates carrier synchronization requirements and CFO sensitivity by employing biased aggregate waveforms with direct detection and KK phase reconstruction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Practical OAC deployment is severely challenged by reliance on accurate carrier synchronization and coherent reception, which are costly and fragile in short-range, low-complexity systems.

Method: The authors propose a self-coherent OAC framework based on Kramers-Kronig (KK) reception: transmit a biased aggregate waveform, use direct detection at the receiver, and reconstruct the phase via KK algorithm, eliminating explicit carrier recovery while preserving coherent-like signal aggregation.

Result: The architecture fundamentally removes CFO sensitivity between transmitters and receiver, reduces control overhead, improves scalability, and approaches theoretical baseband OAC performance limits under practical conditions—especially suitable for mmWave and sub-THz systems.

Conclusion: The self-coherent KK-based OAC framework eliminates carrier synchronization bottlenecks, making OAC practical for scalable multi-user aggregation in millimeter-wave and sub-THz systems where oscillator phase instability is a fundamental challenge.

Abstract: Over-the-air computation (OAC) enables efficient function aggregation in wireless networks by exploiting the superposition property of the multiple-access channel. However, practical deployment of OAC is severely challenged by the reliance on accurate carrier synchronization and coherent reception, which are costly and fragile, especially in short-range and low-complexity systems. In this work, we propose a \emph{self-coherent, synthesizer-free over-the-air computation framework} based on \emph{Kramers--Kronig (KK) reception}. By transmitting a biased aggregate waveform and employing direct detection followed by KK phase reconstruction at the receiver, the proposed scheme eliminates the need for explicit carrier recovery while preserving coherent-like signal aggregation.
  We develop a signal-domain system model for multi-user OAC under KK reception and provide a synchronization-relaxation analysis demonstrating that the proposed architecture fundamentally removes carrier-frequency offset (CFO) sensitivity between transmitters and receiver. By shifting synchronization complexity away from strict carrier-phase tracking and eliminating distributed phase alignment requirements, the framework reduces control overhead and improves scalability in multi-user aggregation. A detailed per-symbol mean-squared error (MSE) characterization isolates the impact of channel mismatch and KK reconstruction noise, showing that the proposed self-coherent architecture approaches the theoretical performance limits of baseband OAC under practical operating conditions. Finally, we demonstrate that the approach is particularly well suited for mmWave and sub-THz systems, where oscillator phase instability otherwise represents a fundamental bottleneck to scalable coherent OAC.

</details>


### [384] [Compositional Semantic Communication for Physical AI: Category Theory Meets Game Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18115)
*Christo Kurisummoottil Thomas,Walid Saad,Emilio Calvanese Strinati*

Main category: cs.IT

TL;DR: This paper proposes a compositional semantic communication (CSC) framework for physical AI systems, using category theory and game theory to enable efficient multi-device semantic coordination, achieving up to 17% bandwidth reduction and 53% lower latency while maintaining 85% inference accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing semantic communication approaches face limited adaptability, poor out-of-distribution generalization, and scalability challenges in physical AI systems where distributed agents must coordinate in networked environments. Transmitting raw sensor data incurs significant communication overhead and latency.

Method: The authors develop a category-theoretic measure of compositional semantics, formalize semantic composition using Grothendieck topologies and presheaves, and formulate multi-device coordination as a Stackelberg game solved via an ADMM-based algorithm.

Result: The proposed approach achieves 17% bandwidth reduction and 53% lower end-to-end latency compared to baselines, while maintaining 85% inference accuracy across diverse autonomous driving scenarios.

Conclusion: Equilibrium existence is established under mild conditions and is Pareto optimal when compositional information yields increasing collective benefit, demonstrating the effectiveness of the CSC framework for multi-device coordination in physical AI.

Abstract: Physical artificial intelligence (AI) systems involve distributed sensing agents with embedded AI models that must coordinate to perceive, reason, and act in networked environments. Transmitting raw sensor data incurs significant communication overhead, latency, and redundancy. While semantic communication (SC) mitigates these challenges by transmitting task-relevant information, existing deep learning-based joint source-channel coding approaches exhibit limited adaptability, poor out-of-distribution generalization, and scalability challenges. To address these limitations, this paper proposes a framework for compositional semantic communication (CSC), enabling heterogeneous physical AI sources to transmit semantic representations (SRs) that compose meaningfully at a base station (BS) or edge server for remote inference. First, a category-theoretic measure of compositional semantics is developed to quantify each device's contribution to inference tasks beyond mutual information. Second, Grothendieck topologies and presheaves formalize semantic composition across devices, ensuring consistency and task relevance. Building on these foundations, multi-device coordination is formulated as a Stackelberg game in which devices commit to encoding strategies and the BS optimally composes received SRs. An ADMM-based algorithm computes equilibrium signaling strategies. Equilibrium existence is established under mild conditions and is Pareto optimal when compositional information yields increasing collective benefit. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed approach achieves up to 17% bandwidth reduction and 53% lower end-to-end latency than cooperative multi-agent, distributed gradient descent, and uniform-selection CSC baselines while maintaining 85% inference accuracy across diverse autonomous driving scenarios.

</details>


<div id='eess.IV'></div>

# eess.IV [[Back]](#toc)

### [385] [When Do Multimodal and Graph-Augmented RAG Help? A Controlled Evaluation for Document Question Answering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16604)
*Sokipriala Jonah*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: This paper evaluates a multimodal graph-RAG system combining text, knowledge graphs, and visual evidence (figures/tables) on scientific documents. The key finding: graph augmentation provides no reliable accuracy gain, text-only systems fail on pixel-only questions (0% accuracy), and multimodal systems show limited success (5.7-11.4%) constrained by retrieval and generator capabilities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard RAG systems lose information contained in figures, tables, layout, and cross-passage relationships. The authors aim to determine whether multimodal and graph-based augmentations can meaningfully improve retrieval and question answering over text-only baselines.

Method: A four-way ablation on 1,000 PubLayNet pages using: (1) text-only baseline, (2) LLM-extracted subject-relation-object triples (graph), (3) CLIP-based figure/table retrieval (visual), (4) fusion at generation. Tested on single-passage, multi-hop, and figure questions with 4 multimodal generators (2 closed-weight, 2 open-weight). Compared caption-answerable vs. pixel-only figure questions.

Result: Graph augmentation showed no reliable accuracy improvement. Text-only systems achieved 0% accuracy on pixel-only questions. Multimodal systems reached 0.057-0.114 accuracy, constrained by low image retrieval recall (Recall@3=0.371) and limited generator reasoning. Caption-derived questions inflated text-only VQA estimates. Token count varied 11x across generators for the same image.

Conclusion: Multimodal and graph augmentation value depends heavily on retrieval design, corpus structure, benchmark construction, and generator efficiency. The findings caution against overclaiming the benefits of these augmentations without careful ablation and highlight the critical bottleneck of image retrieval and generator visual reasoning capability.

Abstract: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems commonly operate on text extracted from documents, potentially losing information contained in figures, tables, layout, and relationships distributed across passages. We present an explainable multimodal graph-RAG architecture that augments a text-only baseline with LLM-extracted subject--relation--object triples and CLIP-based retrieval of figures and tables. The three evidence sources are retrieved independently and fused only at generation time, allowing the effects of graph evidence, visual evidence, and generator choice to be evaluated separately. We conduct a controlled four-way ablation on 1,000 PubLayNet pages across single-passage, multi-hop, and figure questions using two closed-weight and two open-weight multimodal generators. We also compare matched caption-answerable and pixel-only figure question sets to distinguish caption recovery from genuine visual question answering. Knowledge-graph augmentation provides no reliable accuracy improvement across generators or question types in this corpus. On pixel-only questions, text-only systems achieve zero accuracy, while multimodal systems reach 0.057--0.114 and are constrained by both image retrieval (Recall@3 = 0.371) and the generators' ability to interpret dense scientific figures and tables. Caption-derived questions substantially overestimate text-only visual question-answering capability. Processing the same image also produces an elevenfold variation in input tokens across generators, showing that image tokenisation can dominate deployment cost. Source code, experiment configurations, and evaluation resources are publicly available at https://github.com/sokistar24/multimodal-graph-rag. These findings show that the value of multimodal and graph augmentation depends on retrieval design, corpus structure, benchmark construction, and generator efficiency.

</details>


### [386] [A Unified Multisensor Machine-Learning Framework for Live Fuel Moisture Content Retrieval](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16714)
*Valerio Pampanoni,Emilio Chuvieco,Alvise Ferrari,Giovanni Laneve,Simone Saquella*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A unified machine-learning framework estimates live fuel moisture content from multi-sensor satellite data (MODIS, VIIRS, Landsat, Sentinel-2/3) combined with weather, topography, and seasonal predictors, achieving R² values of 0.693-0.715 across grass, shrub, and tree vegetation types.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Live fuel moisture content is critical for fire management but remains difficult to estimate over large areas due to sparse field observations concentrated in specific regions.

Method: Uses Random Forest and XGBoost regressors with predictors from satellite vegetation indices, meteorological variables, topography, and cyclic day-of-year; harmonizes multiple optical sensors (MODIS, VIIRS, Landsat, Sentinel-2/3) through common spectral feature space, reflectance calibration toward Sentinel-2 reference, and screening for remote-sensing suitability.

Result: Best models achieve pooled R² values of 0.715 (Grass), 0.693 (Shrub), and 0.700 (Tree) under primary validation design with held-out observation dates.

Conclusion: The framework successfully enables large-scale live fuel moisture estimation by integrating multi-sensor data, with weather/topography/seasonal predictors providing most predictive gain; it can incorporate additional optical sensors given compatible bands and sufficient overlap data.

Abstract: Live fuel moisture content controls vegetation flammability and is a high-importance variable in fire management. Nevertheless, it remains difficult to estimate and map over large areas due to the concentration of field observations in specific regions. We develop a unified machine-learning framework that estimates live fuel moisture content from satellite vegetation indices, meteorological variables, topography and seasonal predictors. GlobeLFMC 2.0 measurements are matched to Terra and Aqua MODIS, VIIRS, Landsat 8/9, Sentinel-2 and Sentinel-3 surface-reflectance products, combining the long MODIS record with finer-resolution recent observations. To account for differences among sensors, optical predictors are restricted to a common red, near-infrared and shortwave-infrared feature space; site--product combinations and field time series are screened for remote-sensing suitability; and spectral response function diagnostics are combined with target-independent empirical reflectance calibration toward a Sentinel-2 reference domain. Preliminary single-product experiments show that weather, topography and cyclic day-of-year provide most of the predictive gain beyond vegetation indices, whereas optional product-specific predictors do not justify their additional dependencies. Separate Grass, Shrub and Tree models are trained with Random Forest and XGBoost regressors. Under the primary validation design, which withholds observation dates from sites represented in training, the best models achieve pooled R2 values of 0.715, 0.693 and 0.700 for Grass, Shrub and Tree, respectively. The framework can incorporate additional optical sensors when compatible reflectance bands, documented spectral responses and sufficient overlap observations are available for calibration and validation.

</details>


### [387] [Luminosity-Adaptive Contrast Enhancement Using CLAHE for Retinal Fundus Images with Quantitative Validation and Comparative Analysis](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17691)
*K. Mithra,Prem Kumar Santhanam*

Main category: eess.IV

TL;DR: A two-stage retinal fundus image enhancement pipeline (HSV luminosity correction + CLAHE on V channel) outperforms standard HE and AHE methods on the DRIVE dataset, achieving PSNR=29.3dB, SSIM=0.91, CNR=3.12 with fast processing (0.14s per image).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Retinal fundus images suffer from non-uniform illumination, motion blur, and low contrast, which increase diagnostic error risk. Reliable enhancement is needed before computer-aided ophthalmic diagnosis.

Method: A two-stage pipeline: (1) luminosity correction via HSV color space decomposition, (2) CLAHE applied only to the Value (V) channel. Binary masking isolates hyper-reflective pathology regions. Evaluated on DRIVE dataset (40 images) against HE and AHE baselines.

Result: Proposed method: PSNR=29.3dB, SSIM=0.91, CNR=3.12; outperforms HE (21.4dB, 0.74) and AHE (23.1dB, 0.79). Processing time: 0.14s per image.

Conclusion: The luminosity-CLAHE pipeline provides superior contrast and structural fidelity over baselines, with clinical-compatible speed. Deep learning comparisons are noted as future work.

Abstract: Background: Retinal fundus imaging is central to the early diagnosis of sight-threatening conditions including diabetic retinopathy, glaucoma, and retinal vein occlusion. Clinical utility of fundus images is routinely compromised by non-uniform illumination, motion blur, and low contrast - artefacts that increase the risk of diagnostic error. Effective image enhancement is therefore a prerequisite for reliable computer-aided ophthalmic diagnosis. Methods: This study proposes a two-stage image enhancement pipeline combining luminosity correction via HSV colour space decomposition with Contrast Limited Adaptive Histogram Equalization (CLAHE) applied exclusively to the Value (V) channel. Experiments are conducted on the publicly available DRIVE dataset (40 retinal fundus images, 584 x 565 pixels, Canon CR5 camera, ophthalmologist-annotated ground truth). Quantitative evaluation employs Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), Structural Similarity Index (SSIM), and Contrast-to-Noise Ratio (CNR). Baseline comparisons include standard Histogram Equalization (HE) and Adaptive Histogram Equalization (AHE). A binary masking step is subsequently applied to isolate hyper-reflective regions consistent with vascular pathology. Results: The proposed method achieves PSNR = 29.3 dB, SSIM = 0.91, and CNR = 3.12 - outperforming HE (PSNR = 21.4 dB, SSIM = 0.74) and AHE (PSNR = 23.1 dB, SSIM = 0.79) across all metrics, with an average processing time of 0.14 seconds per image. Conclusions: The combined luminosity-CLAHE pipeline yields measurably superior contrast and structural fidelity compared to established baseline methods, with processing speed compatible with clinical screening workflows. Limitations and directions for deep-learning-based comparison are discussed.

</details>


<div id='eess.SP'></div>

# eess.SP [[Back]](#toc)

### [388] [Overcoming the BCI Calibration Bottleneck: A Clinically-Grounded Architecture using Riemannian Alignment and Stochastic Weight Averaging](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16225)
*Immanuvel Prathap Sagayaraju*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A deep learning pipeline combining ICA, Riemannian alignment, and EEGNet with SWA achieves 90.97% accuracy in zero-calibration BCI on the MOABB benchmark, with 74.31% mean accuracy across 9-fold LOSO cross-validation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To overcome the calibration bottleneck in BCIs caused by cross-subject spatial covariance shifts and physiological artifacts, enabling zero-calibration motor imagery classification.

Method: A pipeline combining Per-Session Independent Component Analysis, Riemannian Euclidean Alignment, and EEGNet stabilized by Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA).

Result: 90.97% accuracy (AUC: 0.976, Cohen's κ: 0.819) for Subject 1; 74.31% mean accuracy across 9-fold LOSO cross-validation.

Conclusion: The proposed architecture achieves hardware-agnostic zero-shot efficacy for binary motor imagery, proving clinically robust and globally stable performance.

Abstract: Brain-Computer Interfaces (BCIs) face a severe calibration bottleneck due to cross-subject spatial covariance shifts and physiological artifacts. To enable zero-calibration BCI, a deep learning pipeline was engineered combining Per-Session Independent Component Analysis, Riemannian Euclidean Alignment, and EEGNet stabilized by Stochastic Weight Averaging (SWA). Evaluated on the strict MOABB BNCI2014-001 benchmark, the proposed architecture successfully isolates true sensorimotor rhythms. For the primary case study (Subject 1), a clinically robust SWA stable accuracy of 90.97% (AUC: 0.976, Cohen's $κ$: 0.819) was achieved. Furthermore, expanded 9-fold Leave-One-Subject-Out (LOSO) cross-validation yielded a globally stable mean accuracy of 74.31%, proving hardware-agnostic zero-shot efficacy for binary motor imagery.

</details>


### [389] [From Intent to Infrastructure: LLM-Driven Agent Compilers for ISAC Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16269)
*Lijie Zheng,Xudong Zhong,Baoquan Ren,Xiangwu Gong,Xinghui Zhu,Ji He*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces the Agent Compiler, an LLM-enabled compilation layer that translates high-level engineering intent into complete ISAC system configurations for 6G networks, using a four-stage process and time-scale separation where LLMs handle slow strategic decisions while algorithms manage real-time control.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAC system design involves too many tightly coupled choices (waveform, sensing, scheduling, deployment) that manual tuning or isolated optimizers cannot handle effectively, motivating the need for an automated compilation approach.

Method: The Agent Compiler operates in four stages: intent parsing, task decomposition, policy graph synthesis, and infrastructure mapping, producing an ISAC Policy Graph (IPG) as intermediate representation. A runtime engine supports three-level closed-loop adaptation: fast parameter updates, partial recompilation, and full workflow recompilation, with strict time-scale separation between LLM strategic decisions and algorithmic real-time control.

Result: A UAV-assisted disaster rescue example illustrates the complete compilation process, demonstrating how the Agent Compiler can generate executable ISAC configurations from high-level intent.

Conclusion: The paper identifies open issues including compilation latency, output reliability, constraint verification, and pipeline security as directions for future research in LLM-enabled ISAC system configuration.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communications (ISAC) is moving from proof-of-concept demonstrations to system-level deployment in sixth-generation (6G) networks. Because sensing and communication share hardware, spectrum, and waveform resources, ISAC design now involves many tightly coupled choices, including waveform selection, sensing algorithm setup, resource scheduling, and deployment planning. This design space is already too large to manage well through manual tuning or isolated optimizers. This article introduces the \textit{Agent Compiler}, a large language model (LLM)-enabled compilation layer that translates high-level engineering intent into complete and executable ISAC system configurations. The Agent Compiler works in four stages: intent parsing, task decomposition, policy graph synthesis, and infrastructure mapping. It produces a verifiable intermediate representation called the ISAC Policy Graph (IPG). A runtime engine then deploys the compiled configuration and supports closed-loop adaptation at three levels: fast parameter updates, partial recompilation of affected subgraphs, and full workflow recompilation. The core design principle is strict time-scale separation: the LLM handles slow-loop strategic decisions, while proven algorithms retain real-time control in the fast loop. A UAV-assisted disaster rescue example illustrates the full compilation process. We also discuss open issues, including compilation latency, output reliability, constraint verification, and pipeline security, to guide future research.

</details>


### [390] [Physics-Informed Feature Engineering 1D-CNN for Multilayer Cloud Detection from Geostationary Satellites](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16270)
*Fu Wang,Chi Yang,Qi-Feng Lu,Rui-Xia Liu,Xiao-Fei Yang,Xiao-Fang Liu,Bo Li,Lin Chen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A physics-informed 1D-CNN incorporating threshold-based feature-engineering priors outperforms conventional threshold algorithms for multilayer cloud detection from active-passive satellite observations.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Multilayer cloud detection is vital for numerical weather prediction, but conventional threshold-based algorithms suffer from lower detection probability and higher false alarm rates.

Method: Channel selections from threshold-based algorithms are embedded as feature-engineering priors into a 1D-CNN, using ML to learn latent physical relationships from radiative transfer theory.

Result: 1D-CNN achieves POD_mul=0.620 and FAR_mul=0.240, outperforming the threshold algorithm (POD_mul=0.558, FAR_mul=0.369). Replacing AGRI C12 with C13 improved POD_mul to 0.609 without materially affecting FAR_mul.

Conclusion: Prior physical knowledge from radiative transfer theory serves as effective feature-engineering priors; ML-revealed mechanisms can enhance traditional algorithms, but sensor-specific characteristics (e.g., SRF mismatches, on-orbit radiometric stability) must be considered during operational transfer.

Abstract: Multilayer cloud detection from active--passive observation is vital for numerical weather prediction. In this study, channel selections derived from threshold-based algorithms are embedded as feature-engineering priors into a 1D-CNN, and machine learning (ML) is used to learn latent physical relationships to simplify physical retrievals for operational deployment. The results show that the 1D-CNN achieves a multilayer-cloud probability of detection ($\mathrm{POD}{\mathrm{mul}}$) of 0.620 and a false alarm rate ($\mathrm{FAR}{\mathrm{mul}}$) of 0.240, outperforming the conventional threshold algorithm ($\mathrm{POD}{\mathrm{mul}} = 0.558$, $\mathrm{FAR}{\mathrm{mul}} = 0.369$). These results demonstrate that prior physical knowledge derived from radiative transfer theory can serve as an effective feature-engineering prior. Further experiments show that ML-revealed physical mechanisms can also enhance traditional algorithms. Replacing AGRI channel 12 (C12, centered at $10.8~μ\mathrm{m}$) with channel 13 (C13, centered at $12.0~μ\mathrm{m}$) increased $\mathrm{POD}{\mathrm{mul}}$ from 0.558 to 0.609 without materially affecting $\mathrm{FAR}{\mathrm{mul}}$. However, for AHI, substituting the $11.2~μ\mathrm{m}$ channel with the $12.3~μ\mathrm{m}$ channel yielded negligible improvement. In addition to spectral response function (SRF) mismatches, a primary contributing factor is the channels' on-orbit radiometric stability. Hence, physics-informed machine-learning methods appear promising for advancing remote-sensing AI, while sensor-specific characteristics must be considered during operational transfer.

</details>


### [391] [Efficient EEG Seizure Detection Using INT8 Quantization, Channel Pruning, and Spiking Neural Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16296)
*Kartikey Ahlawat*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper explores three brain-inspired efficiency strategies (SNN conversion, channel pruning with 2:4 sparsity, and INT8 quantization) to reduce the computational cost and model size of a 1D CNN seizure detector for deployment on wearable/implantable EEG devices, achieving up to 64% energy reduction and 2.8x speedup with minimal accuracy loss.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continuous EEG monitoring for epilepsy is constrained by limited power and memory budgets of wearable and implantable devices, making it difficult to deploy high-accuracy deep neural networks on such platforms.

Method: Using a single 1D CNN seizure detector on CHB-MIT scalp EEG as baseline; investigating three strategies: (i) CNN-to-SNN conversion via parameter transfer, (ii) EEG channel pruning with 2:4 structured weight sparsity, and (iii) INT8 quantization via FX/ONNX workflows with quantization-aware training and operator fusion.

Result: Quantized CNN reduced model size from 1.63 MB to 0.44 MB, lowered estimated energy per inference by up to 64%, and achieved up to 2.8x speedup in CPU latency while preserving AUC (slightly improved in one case). Pruned CNN halved input channels and non-zero weights with modest accuracy drop. SNN conversion provided spiking implementation with sparse temporal activity.

Conclusion: These three complementary efficiency directions characterize viable paths for deploying seizure detection on resource-constrained wearable/implantable devices.

Abstract: Continuous EEG monitoring for epilepsy is constrained by the limited power and memory budgets of wearable and implantable devices. Deep neural networks can detect seizures with high accuracy, but their computational cost and model size make them difficult to deploy on such platforms. In this work we use a single 1D CNN seizure detector on the CHB-MIT scalp EEG dataset as a common baseline, and then investigate three brain-inspired efficiency strategies: (i) conversion of the CNN into a spiking neural network (SNN) via parameter transfer, (ii) EEG channel pruning combined with 2:4 structured weight sparsity, and (iii) INT8 quantization using FX- and ONNX-based workflows, including quantization-aware training and operator fusion. The quantized CNN variants reduce stored model size from 1.63 MB to 0.44 MB, lower estimated energy per inference by up to 64%, and achieve as much as 2.8 times speedup in CPU latency while preserving, and in one case slightly improving, AUC. The pruned CNN halves the number of input channels and non-zero weights with only a modest accuracy drop, and the SNN conversion provides a spiking implementation with sparse temporal activity. Together, these experiments characterize three complementary efficiency directions for seizure detection.

</details>


### [392] [ECG-LLM: Foundation Model for ECG-Based Cardiac Reasoning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16323)
*Alexander Selivanov,Friederike Jungmann,Jan Kehrer,Karl-Ludwig Laugwitz,Eimo Martens,Daniel Rueckert*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: ECG-LLM is a large language model trained on 679,112 ECG studies that can answer diverse cardiovascular questions from a 12-lead ECG alone, including predicting phenotypes not directly visible on standard ECGs, such as ventricular volumes and aortic stenosis, outperforming existing baselines on ECG understanding tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current ECG-AI systems are limited to fixed diagnostic labels or automated reports, lacking the ability for patient-specific clinical reasoning. Front-line triage often lacks immediate access to definitive imaging like ECHO or CMR, creating a need for a more flexible, question-driven ECG interpretation tool.

Method: ECG-LLM uses a multimodal-to-language supervision strategy, trained on clinically structured question-answer pairs derived from ECG signals, clinical context, CMR, and ECHO across four cohorts (679,112 ECG studies from 186,409 patients).

Result: ECG-LLM recovers conventional ECG measurements (e.g., heart rate), strongly predicts complex CMR-derived phenotypes (ventricular/atrial volumes, ventricular function), detects vital echocardiographic phenotypes (increased LV wall thickness, aortic stenosis, RV systolic dysfunction), and matches/exceeds existing baselines for diagnostic report generation and ECG-QA benchmark.

Conclusion: By moving beyond fixed-label prediction, ECG-LLM provides clinically valuable, question-driven cardiovascular reasoning to support general practitioner and front-line triage decisions when specialist review is delayed.

Abstract: Electrocardiography (ECG) is an inexpensive, standard-of-care test for cardiac symptoms, but front-line triage often lacks immediate access to definitive imaging such as echocardiography (ECHO) or cardiac magnetic resonance (CMR). Furthermore, most existing ECGAI systems are limited to fixed diagnostic labels or automated reports, constraining their use for patient-specific clinical reasoning. To address this gap, we introduce ECG-LLM, an ECG-conditioned large language model trained across four cohorts comprising 679,112 ECG studies from 186,409 patients. Using a novel multimodal-to-language supervision strategy, ECG-LLM is trained on clinically structured question-answer pairs derived from ECG signals, clinical context, CMR, and ECHO. This unified approach enables the model to answer diverse cardiovascular questions from a 12-lead ECG alone, spanning both conventional interpretation and phenotypes not directly visible on standard ECGs. ECG-LLM successfully recovers conventional ECG measurements, such as heart rate, and strongly predicts complex CMR-derived phenotypes, including ventricular and atrial volumes and ventricular function. Crucially, it detects vital echocardiographic phenotypes, including increased LV wall thickness, aortic stenosis, and right-ventricular systolic dysfunction. On standard ECG understanding tasks, ECG-LLM matches or exceeds existing baselines for diagnostic report generation and the ECG-QA benchmark. By moving beyond fixed-label prediction, this multimodal framework provides clinically valuable, question-driven cardiovascular reasoning to support general practitioner and front-line triage decisions when specialist review is delayed.

</details>


### [393] [Robust and Feasible QoS-Aware mmWave Massive MIMO Hybrid Beamforming](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16306)
*Mohsen Tajallifar,Ahmad R. Sharafat,Halim Yanikomeroglu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a robust hybrid beamforming framework for millimeter-wave 5G/6G networks that mitigates imperfect CSI by using a smaller effective channel uncertainty region, detects infeasible streams, and employs a cutting-set iterative method for QoS-aware baseband beamforming with MSE constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Hybrid beamforming with QoS provisioning is essential for 5G/6G mmWave networks, but practical CSI errors make robust design challenging. Existing methods lack efficiency, feasibility guarantees, or robustness under uncertainty.

Method: The authors mitigate CSI uncertainty via baseband beamforming using the effective channel's (smaller) uncertainty region, steer RF beamformer with estimated channel eigenvectors, use a cutting-set iterative method for baseband beamforming with limited MSE constraints, derive a sufficient feasibility condition, diagonalize the baseband precoder, and apply MMSE combining.

Result: Simulations validate the formulations and theoretical derivations, showing that using the effective channel's uncertainty region reduces transmit power, and the low-complexity scheme effectively satisfies QoS constraints while detecting infeasible streams.

Conclusion: The proposed robust HB scheme efficiently handles CSI uncertainty with lower complexity and power consumption, providing feasible QoS-aware stream provisioning for practical mmWave 5G/6G deployments.

Abstract: Hybrid beamforming (HB) with quality-of-service (QoS) provisioning per stream in millimeter waves is indispensable in 5G/6G networks. HB includes baseband and radio frequency (RF) beamforming, and requires error-free channel state information (CSI), which is erroneous in practice. So there is a need for efficient, feasible, robust, and QoS-aware HB. To achieve this, we mitigate CSI uncertainty via baseband beamforming, and we steer the RF beamformer by using the estimates of the channel's eigenvectors. In doing so, we consider the effective channel's uncertainty region instead of the uncertainty region of the channel itself, as the former is smaller than the latter, requiring less transmit power to satisfy the QoS constraint. We also detect and eliminate the infeasible data streams. Our iterative scheme (which is based on the cutting-set method) for baseband beamforming satisfies the mean-squared error (MSE) constraint per stream, where a limited number of constraints are considered instead of infinitely many constraints. In our low-complexity scheme, we derive a simple sufficient condition to check the feasibility of each stream, we diagonalize the effective channel at the baseband precoder, and we use minimum MSE combining at the baseband combiner. Extensive simulations validate our formulations and theoretical derivations.

</details>


### [394] [Joint-Embedding Predictive Architecture for Sensor-based Activity Recognition](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16350)
*Mohd Halim Mohd Noor,Abdulrahman M. A. Baraka*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes HAR-JEPA, a self-supervised framework for sensor-based human activity recognition that learns robust representations from unlabeled data using an encoder that models both local and long-term temporal patterns, combined with an improved VICReg objective to prevent representation collapse, showing strong performance especially on minority transitional activities.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Supervised HAR models require large amounts of labeled data, which is costly and labor-intensive to collect and annotate. The paper aims to address this by learning from unlabeled data.

Method: Proposes a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture (JEPA) for HAR with an encoder that captures both fine-grained local temporal patterns within a window and long-term temporal sequences across adjacent windows. Introduces an improved VICReg objective with a lightweight norm term to stabilize pre-training and prevent representation collapse.

Result: The HAR-JEPA framework learns high-quality representations from unlabeled sensor data and demonstrates superior generalization on minority, high-variance transitional activities (e.g., sit-to-stand, sit-to-lie) where supervised models overfit.

Conclusion: Self-supervised pre-training with HAR-JEPA effectively reduces reliance on labeled data and improves robustness, especially for underrepresented transitional activities in HAR.

Abstract: Sensor-based human activity recognition (HAR) has achieved significant progressed in fully supervised learning settings. However, these supervised learning models rely on large amount of labeled data, which require labor-intensive collection and meticulous annotation. To address these challenges, this paper proposes a Joint Embedding Predictive Architecture framework tailored for sensor-based HAR, designed to learn robust and generalizable representations from unlabeled datasets. The proposed framework features an encoder designed to explicitly model both the fine-grained local temporal representations within individual window and the long-term temporal sequence of adjacent windows. Furthermore, we introduce an improved Variance-Invariance-Covariance Regularization (VICReg) objective function that incorporates computationally lightweight norm term to stabilize the JEPA pre-training phase. This term balances variance, invariance and covariance constraints to prevent representation collapse. The proposed HAR-JEPA framework is evaluated using two benchmark continuously performed activity datasets. The results show that high-quality representations are successfully learned by the proposed framework. Furthermore, the representations learned by HAR-JEPA demonstrates superior generalization on minority, high variance transitional activities such as sit-to-stand and sit-to-lie where supervised learning tend to overfit due to limited support.

</details>


### [395] [Scalable Attention for 5G NR Channel Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16462)
*Mahdi Abdollahpour,Marco Bertuletti,Yichao Zhang,Luca Benini,Alessandro Vanelli-Coralli*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: CHEA is a low-complexity attention-based channel estimator for 5G NR MU-MIMO that uses multi-resolution windowed attention and per-PRB upsampling to achieve linear complexity, state-of-the-art MSE, and support for varying resource allocations without retraining.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing attention-based neural channel estimators suffer from quadratic computational cost scaling with the number of subcarriers and are tied to a single resource allocation, making them inefficient for practical 5G NR MU-MIMO systems.

Method: CHEA replaces global attention with a multi-resolution windowed design: a high-resolution encoder captures local pilot details, a low-resolution encoder captures wide frequency context, a local cross-attention decoder transfers coarse context back, and a per-PRB upsampling module reconstructs the full channel. All attention operations use fixed-size windows, enabling linear complexity.

Result: CHEA achieves the lowest MSE among conventional and state-of-the-art neural estimators on standard-compliant PUSCH, while requiring 2.8× to 22.0× fewer operations than existing attention-based estimators.

Conclusion: The linear complexity and ability to support different PRB allocations without retraining make CHEA a practical and efficient solution for 5G NR MU-MIMO channel estimation.

Abstract: Attention-based neural estimators achieve strong channel-estimation accuracy, but the computational cost of global attention over the time-frequency resource grid grows quadratically with the number of subcarriers, and these estimators are typically tied to a single resource allocation. This paper proposes Channel Estimation Attention (CHEA), a low-complexity channel estimator for 5G New Radio (5G NR) multi-user multiple-input multiple-output (MU-MIMO). CHEA replaces global attention with a multi-resolution windowed design: a high-resolution encoder preserves local pilot detail, a low-resolution encoder captures wider frequency-domain context, and a local cross-attention decoder transfers this coarse context back to the high-resolution pilot tokens. A per-Physical Resource Block (PRB) upsampling module then reconstructs the channel over the full slot. Because every attention operation is confined to a fixed-size window and reconstruction is performed per PRB, the cost of CHEA scales linearly with the number of subcarriers, and a single trained model supports different PRB allocations without retraining. On a standard-compliant Physical Uplink Shared Channel (PUSCH), CHEA achieves the lowest Mean Squared Error (MSE) among conventional and state-of-the-art neural estimators, while requiring 2.8\(\times\) to 22.0\(\times\) lower operations than existing attention-based estimators.

</details>


### [396] [Polynomial Chaos Expansion Based Nonlinear Filtering of Stochastic Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16504)
*David Bordenkircher,Ruixin Niu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A new polynomial chaos expansion-based filter (CD-PCE-CF) is proposed for Dynamic Data Driven Applications Systems, outperforming the CD-Extended Kalman Filter in tracking ballistic objects with radar measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop an alternative non-linear continuous-discrete tracking filter that improves upon existing PCE-based filters and the CD-EKF for DDDAS applications, particularly in terms of estimation accuracy and stability.

Method: The filter uses polynomial chaos expansion with Galerkin projection to derive PCE coefficient-wise prediction and update steps, leveraging orthogonality properties. Unlike previous PCE filters, it avoids independent collocation point propagation and recomputation at each time step.

Result: In a ballistic object tracking case study using radar measurements with process noise, the CD-PCE-CF significantly outperformed the CD-EKF in both estimation accuracy and stability.

Conclusion: The CD-PCE-CF is a superior alternative to the CD-EKF for non-linear continuous-discrete tracking problems in DDDAS, offering better performance and stability.

Abstract: In Dynamic Data Driven Applications Systems (DDDAS), non-linear continuous-discrete (CD) tracking algorithms have been proposed to recursively estimate stochastic processes which follow continuous-time stochastic differential equations (SDE) using non-linear discrete-time measurement sequences. In this paper, a new filter in this class which is based on the polynomial chaos expansion (PCE) is proposed as an alternative solution to these tracking problems. Using the orthogonality properties of the PCE basis, PCE coefficient-wise prediction and update steps are derived via the Galerkin projection. This differs from previous PCE based filters where collocation points are independently propagated through the motion model and the PCE is recomputed at each time step. For this reason, we call the proposed filter the CD-PCE coefficient filter (CD-PCE-CF). A case study is provided where the CD-PCE-CF and the CD-Extended Kalman Filter (CD-EKF) are used to track a ballistic object undergoing process noise using radar measurements. It is shown that the proposed method significantly outperforms the CD-EKF in terms of estimation accuracy and stability.

</details>


### [397] [RIS-Aided ISAC in Cluttered Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16611)
*Yosefine Triwidyastuti,Tri Nhu Do,Ngo Hoang Tu,Georges Kaddoum*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper analyzes a communication-optimized RIS-assisted ISAC system in cluttered environments, deriving exact and approximate capacity expressions for communication links and average SCNR for sensing, showing that zero-Doppler clutter leakage dominates the SCNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To analyze how RIS-assisted ISAC systems perform in cluttered environments where multiple scatterers interfere with reflected sensing signals, and to understand the trade-offs between communication optimization and sensing performance.

Method: Derives exact ergodic-capacity expression for no-RIS baseline, Gamma approximation and Jensen bound for RIS-assisted link; analyzes average SCNR by separating RIS-induced clutter from environmental clutter using range and slow-time leakage responses.

Result: Communication capacity scales with RIS elements; sensing powers scale as constant, linear, linear, quadratic, and constant for different return types; zero-Doppler clutter leakage dominates SCNR.

Conclusion: RIS phases optimized for communication cause misaligned radar returns; static scatterers near radar act as environmental clutter affecting only sensing; zero-Doppler clutter is the main limiting factor for sensing performance.

Abstract: In this paper, we analyze the performance of a communication-optimized reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS)-assisted integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system operating in a cluttered environment where multiple scatterers may interfere with the different types of reflected sensing signals. The RIS phases coherently combine the direct and reflected communication paths at the user equipment, whereas the corresponding radar returns remain generally misaligned. In addition, static scatterers near the radar act as environmental clutter that affects only the sensing function. For the communication link over small-scale fading, we derive an exact ergodic-capacity expression for the no-RIS baseline, a moment-matched Gamma approximation for the RIS-assisted link, and a Jensen upper bound, all of which are interpreted as upper bounds on the rate of the underlying binary phase-shift keying waveform. For sensing, our analysis focuses on the average signal-to-clutter-plus-noise ratio (SCNR) at the direct range-Doppler cell. Specifically, we derive the average powers of the direct, RIS-related, and scatterer returns, which scale as constant, linear, linear, quadratic, and constant, respectively, with the number of RIS elements. We then weigh them by the range and slow-time leakage responses to obtain the SCNR, thereby separating RIS-induced clutter from geometry-governed environmental clutter. Range and velocity estimation are evaluated using resolution-normalized metrics. Our Monte Carlo simulation results validate the analysis and show that zero-Doppler clutter leakage dominates the SCNR.

</details>


### [398] [Toward a Stable and Deployable Adaptive Chirplet Transform: Residual Projection, Hybrid GPU Acceleration, and Multi-Channel Scalability](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16629)
*Nishant Kumar,Steve Mann*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper addresses instability and computational cost issues in the Adaptive Chirplet Transform by introducing unit normalization, residual-based projection, a hybrid CPU-GPU architecture, multichannel batching, and hierarchical coarse-to-fine search, achieving stable decomposition and significant speedups (up to 8.22x) across platforms.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The Adaptive Chirplet Transform is powerful for decomposing non-stationary signals but suffers from algorithmic instability (divergent decompositions) and high computational cost due to high-dimensional parameter search, hindering practical deployment.

Method: 1) Unit normalization and residual-based projection aligned with Matching Pursuit Theory to ensure stability; 2) Hybrid CPU-GPU architecture (CPU generates chirplet families, GPU parallelizes search); 3) Multichannel batching for simultaneous multi-signal processing; 4) Hierarchical coarse-to-fine search (inspired by Logon Expectation Maximization) to reduce memory usage.

Result: Achieved elimination of divergence, substantial residual error reduction; speedups of 6.6-7.38x on desktop (3.94x single-channel to 8.22x at 10 channels); peak memory below 1 GB with maintained reconstruction quality.

Conclusion: The proposed contributions establish a correct, stable, and practically deployable foundation for chirplet-based signal decomposition across various domains (EEG, EMG, radar, etc.).

Abstract: The Adaptive Chirplet Transform is a flexible framework that can decompose non-stationary signals into sparse chirplets; it has been applied to signals such as electroencephalography, electromyography and radar. However, the practical deployment of this transform has been hindered by two challenges: algorithmic instability in prior implementations, which can lead to divergent decompositions, and the computational cost of searching over a high-dimensional parameter space. This paper addresses both by a sequence of contributions. Firstly, unit normalization and residual-based projection are introduced to align the decomposition with Matching Pursuit Theory, thereby eliminating divergence and substantially reducing residual error across all signal domains, as demonstrated on three representative signal types. A hybrid CPU-GPU architecture offloads chirplet family generation to the CPU while parallelizing the search on the GPU, removing bottlenecks in CPU-only search and GPU-only generation, achieving speedups of 6.6-7.38 on desktop hardware, with consistent gains observed across laptop and embedded platforms. Multichannel batching enabled simultaneous multi-signal processing, amplifying the speedup, which scaled from 3.94 for a single channel to 8.22 at 10 channels. Finally, a hierarchical coarse-to-fine search, inspired by Logon Expectation Maximization, is introduced.
