This reduced peak memory usage below 1 GB while maintaining similar reconstruction quality, at the cost of longer runtime. Together, these contributions establish a correct, stable and practically deployable foundation for chirplet-based signal decomposition. Index Terms: Chirplet Transform, GPU Computing, Matching Pursuit, Signal Decomposition, Sparse Representation, Time-Frequency Analysis

</details>


### [399] [EEGPrep: a validated Python implementation of the EEGLAB preprocessing pipeline](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16647)
*Arnaud Delorme,Suraj Ranganath,Christian Kothe,Aman Jaiswal,Scott Makeig,Bruno Aristimunha*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: EEGPrep is a Python package that reproduces the default EEGLAB preprocessing pipeline with high numerical precision (RMS < 2.1×10⁻⁵ µV) across ARM and x86 architectures, supporting BIDS data and cloud deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Automated EEG preprocessing is widely used, but few pipelines have been systematically tested. The default EEGLAB pipeline was the only one to significantly outperform simple high-pass filtering in a recent benchmark, yet its MATLAB implementation complicates Python/cloud workflows.

Method: EEGPrep implements the default EEGLAB workflow (average rereferencing, clean_rawdata, Picard ICA, ICLabel, channel interpolation, epoching) in Python. Validation compared each stage against MATLAB reference on ARM64 and x86_64 using 64-channel P300 oddball data from 13 participants, measuring MAE, RMS, AMARI distance, and ICLabel agreement.

Result: On ARM64: exact match for import/rereferencing; clean_rawdata and ICA at numerical zero (max RMS 1.5×10⁻¹² µV, AMARI ≤ 0.000001, mean correlation = 1.000). ICLabel introduced the only measurable error (max RMS 2.0×10⁻⁵ µV), but rejection decisions agreed fully. End-to-end max RMS ≤ 2.1×10⁻⁵ µV. x86_64 matched to same precision.

Conclusion: EEGPrep faithfully reproduces the validated EEGLAB pipeline in Python with negligible numerical differences, enabling cloud-native and Python-based EEG workflows. Available via PyPI and Docker.

Abstract: Objective. Automated EEG preprocessing is common in research and clinical work, but few pipelines have been tested systematically. In a recent benchmark, the default EEGLAB pipeline was the only pipeline that significantly outperformed simple high pass filtering. Its MATLAB implementation, however, complicates deployment in Python and cloud workflows. We developed EEGPrep to reproduce this pipeline in Python while supporting BIDS data. The main technical problem was numerical: small floating point differences can accumulate during recursive filtering and ICA. Approach. EEGPrep covers the default EEGLAB workflow: average rereferencing, artifact removal with the clean rawdata plugin, Picard ICA, ICLabel component classification, channel interpolation, and epoching. We compared each stage with MATLAB reference output on ARM arm64, the primary analysis, and Intel x86 64, the supplementary analysis. The test dataset contained 64 channel P300 auditory oddball recordings from 13 participants. We measured maximum absolute difference, RMS error, AMARI distance for ICA, and agreement between ICLabel decisions. Main Results. On ARM arm64, import and rereferencing matched exactly for all 12 analysed subjects. The clean rawdata and Picard ICA stages remained at numerical zero, with maximum RMS equal to 1.5 x 10^-12 microvolts, AMARI distance less than or equal to 0.000001, and mean correlation equal to 1.000. ICLabel neural network inference introduced the only measurable difference, with maximum RMS equal to 2.0 x 10^-5 microvolts. Rejection decisions nevertheless agreed for every subject, and the difference did not increase through interpolation and epoching, with end to end maximum RMS less than or equal to 2.1 x 10^-5 microvolts. The Intel x86 64 analysis matched to the same precision. Significance. EEGPrep reproduces the validated EEGLAB pipeline and can be installed from PyPI or run in Docker.

</details>


### [400] [Joint Optimization of Uplink and Downlink Resources under QoS Constraints of AR](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16722)
*Shiyong Chen,Shengqian Han*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a learning-based framework for joint UL/DL resource optimization in interactive AR services, using a tandem queuing model to transform probabilistic QoS constraints into a tractable service-time condition, and employs GNN-based policies (exploiting permutation equivariance) for UL power allocation and DL beamforming to minimize weighted transmit power while meeting end-to-end latency and reliability requirements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Interactive AR services require stringent end-to-end latency and reliability guarantees, where UL (video upload) and DL (augmented video download) are tightly coupled. Existing approaches optimize UL and DL resources separately, failing to capture their interdependence and thus wasting power.

Method: The authors model the AR transmission as a tandem queuing system, derive a tractable service-time condition from probabilistic QoS constraints, formulate a weighted UL-DL transmit-power minimization problem, and propose a learning-based framework with GNN-based policies (UL-GNN with PE; DL-GNN with PE and optimal wideband beamforming structure) using a differentiable upper bound for gradient-based training.

Result: The proposed joint optimization method satisfies the AR reliability requirement and achieves lower weighted transmit power compared to baselines that optimize UL and DL resources separately.

Conclusion: Joint UL/DL resource optimization with GNN-based policies effectively reduces power consumption while meeting AR QoS requirements, demonstrating the advantage of modeling the interdependence between uplink and downlink service processes.

Abstract: This paper studies joint uplink (UL) and downlink (DL) resource optimization for interactive augmented reality (AR) services, where the live video captured by an AR device is uploaded to the network edge, and then the augmented video is subsequently downloaded. By modeling the AR transmission process as a tandem queuing system, we derive an upper bound for the probabilistic quality of service (QoS) requirement concerning end-to-end latency and reliability. The derived bound transforms the probabilistic QoS requirement into a tractable service-time condition that jointly characterizes the UL and DL service processes. Based on this condition, we formulate a weighted UL-DL transmit-power minimization problem and propose a learning-based framework to jointly optimize UL power allocation and DL beamforming. To enable gradient-based training, we further derive a differentiable upper bound for the service-time condition. Moreover, we design GNN-based policies for UL power allocation and DL beamforming, where the UL GNN exploits permutation equivariance (PE) and the DL GNN incorporates both PE and the optimal structure of wideband DL beamforming. Simulation results show that the proposed method satisfies the AR reliability requirement and reduces the weighted transmit power compared with baselines that optimize UL and DL resources separately.

</details>


### [401] [Task-Oriented Communication with Hybrid-Precision Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16766)
*Songjie Xie,Wei Guo,Shenghui Song,Jun Zhang,Ying-Jun Angela Zhang,Khaled B. Letaief*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hybrid-precision task-oriented communication framework for edge inference that uses a binarized front-end on devices and a full-precision back-end on servers, achieving an optimal balance among communication efficiency, on-device computation, and inference accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing edge inference approaches address communication and computation separately, struggling to achieve a balanced trade-off among transmission efficiency, on-device processing cost, and inference accuracy.

Method: Proposes a hybrid-precision framework with binarized front-end on devices (binary feature extraction via OFDM) and full-precision back-end on servers. Includes on-device binarization for split inference, channel-aware transmission with subcarrier-based feature calibration, and a KD-based training strategy with specialized gradient estimators.

Result: Experiments on ImageNet demonstrate superiority over existing solutions, achieving optimal trade-off among communication efficiency, computational cost, and inference accuracy.

Conclusion: The hybrid-precision framework holistically balances communication, computation, and utility, outperforming existing edge inference approaches.

Abstract: Edge inference has emerged as a promising solution for the proliferation of artificial intelligence (AI) services by deploying models at the network edge to circumvent cloud-routing latency. Existing edge inference approaches mainly focused on either cooperative inference to reduce latency or lightweight model design to fit resource-constrained devices. These solutions often address the communication and computation challenges separately, and thus struggle to achieve a balanced trade-off among transmission efficiency, on-device processing cost, and inference accuracy. To bridge this gap, this paper proposes a hybrid-precision task-oriented communication framework for edge inference to holistically balance communication, on-device computation, and utility. In this framework, a binarized front-end is deployed on the edge device to extract and transmit binary features via orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) signals, while a full-precision back-end on the edge server performs the final inference. To ensure model consistency, we introduce an on-device binarization method tailored for split inference and develop an integrated channel-aware transmission scheme featuring subcarrier-based feature calibration. Furthermore, a knowledge distillation (KD)-based training strategy, supported by specialized gradient estimators, is developed to optimize the end-to-end system and inherit semantic knowledge from a full-precision teacher model. Extensive experiments on the large-scale ImageNet dataset demonstrate the superiority of the proposed hybrid system. Our analysis confirms that this design achieves an optimal trade-off among communication efficiency, on-device computational cost, and inference accuracy, outperforming existing edge inference solutions.

</details>


### [402] [Demodulation of chaotic signals using convolutional neural network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16788)
*Mykola Kozlenko,Emrullah Demiral,Anton Yudhana*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper introduces a deep learning-based demodulation method using CNN for chaotic bifurcation parameter keying, achieving BER of 0.0819 with 1.34% parameter deviation at -13 dB SNR, and demonstrating generalization to unseen chaotic patterns.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To develop a deep learning-based demodulation method for chaotic modulation that can detect bifurcation parameter keying, addressing limitations of traditional detection approaches in low SNR conditions.

Method: A convolutional neural network (CNN)-based demodulator is designed and trained to detect keying of the bifurcation parameter in chaotic logistic map signals. Performance is evaluated under AWGN conditions.

Result: Bit error rate of 0.0819 was achieved for binary signals with only 1.34% bifurcation parameter deviation at -13 dB SNR (normalized SNR of +20 dB). The model generalized to chaotic patterns not seen during training.

Conclusion: Deep learning-based demodulation is effective for chaotic communication, offering robust detection even with tiny parameter deviations and low SNR, with promising generalization capability.

Abstract: Chaotic modulation is an effective communication technique that exploits deterministic chaos to produce pseudo-random signals. A widely adopted approach involves modulation of the chaotic bifurcation parameter. This paper introduces a deep learning-based demodulation method for keying of the bifurcation parameter. It describes the architecture of the convolutional neural network and evaluates performance metrics for signals generated using the chaotic logistic map. The study assesses the bit error rate for binary signals and reports a bit error rate of 0.0819 for a bifurcation parameter deviation of 1.34% under additive white Gaussian noise at a signal-to-noise ratio of -13 dB (corresponding to a normalized signal-to-noise ratio of +20 dB). The results demonstrate the capability to detect chaotic patterns even when the specific patterns were not included in the training dataset.

</details>


### [403] [Power-Domain-Multiplexed Precoded Faster-Than-Nyquist Signaling for NOMA Downlink](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16853)
*Prakash Chaki,Shinya Sugiura*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a precoded FTN signaling scheme with power-domain NOMA to achieve high spectral efficiency and simultaneous multiuser connectivity by combining non-orthogonality in both time and power domains.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To jointly achieve high spectral efficiency (via FTN signaling) and simultaneous multiuser connectivity (via NOMA) by introducing non-orthogonality in both the symbol-interval (time) and multiple-access (power) domains.

Method: Uses eigendecomposition of the FTN-specific ISI matrix for efficient ISI cancellation, and successive interference cancellation (SIC) to eliminate NOMA-induced multiuser interference.

Result: Derives an achievable analytical rate bound and demonstrates bit error rate (BER) performance results through numerical simulations.

Conclusion: The proposed precoded FTN-NOMA scheme enables flexible resource allocation by merging non-orthogonality in both time and power domains, offering improved spectral efficiency and multiuser connectivity.

Abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel precoded faster-than-Nyquist (FTN) signaling scheme with power-domain-multiplexing in non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) downlink for tapping the joint benefits of high spectral efficiency and simultaneous multiuser connectivity. Non-orthogonality is introduced in both the symbol-interval (time) domain as well as in the multiple-access (power) domain to achieve a flexible resource allocation. Eigendecomposition of the FTN-specific intersymbol interference (ISI) matrix is used to achieve efficient cancellation of ISI, while successive interference cancellation is used to eliminate multiuser interference induced by NOMA. We derive the achievable analytical rate bound and demonstrate the numerical results of the bit error rate performance for the proposed scheme.

</details>


### [404] [Hierarchical Wireless Foundation Model for Multi-Task Optimization](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16877)
*Yangjing Wang,Ouya Wang,Shenglong Zhou,Geoffrey Ye Li*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a hierarchical wireless foundation model (WFM) that combines a foundation channel encoder (FCE) and foundation optimization decoder (FOD) via geometry-aware cross-attention to achieve multi-task optimization in wireless networks, demonstrating strong generalization across diverse tasks and channel conditions with reduced inference latency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing AI-for-wireless approaches develop task-specific deep learning models for single scenarios, lacking generalization across diverse tasks, channel conditions, and system configurations.

Method: The WFM couples an upstream FCE (self-supervised masked reconstruction for task-agnostic channel representations) with a downstream FOD (multi-task optimization via differentiable output heads), using geometry-aware cross-attention and a hybrid supervised-to-unsupervised training strategy.

Result: The WFM learns high-fidelity channel representations, achieves competitive multi-task optimization performance, substantially reduces inference latency compared to numerical baselines, and exhibits robust generalization to unseen environments, varying constraints, and heterogeneous configurations.

Conclusion: The proposed foundation model paradigm effectively overcomes the generalization bottleneck in AI-enabled wireless communications, offering a scalable and adaptable solution for multi-task optimization.

Abstract: The increasing complexity of next-generation wireless networks has driven the integration of artificial intelligence (AI) into wireless communications. However, most existing studies focus on developing task-specific deep learning techniques for single scenarios, which limits their ability to generalize across diverse tasks, channel conditions, and system configurations. To address this generalization bottleneck, we propose a hierarchical wireless foundation model (WFM) for multi-task optimization. The proposed WFM couples an upstream foundation channel encoder (FCE) with a downstream foundation optimization decoder (FOD) via geometry-aware cross-attention. Specifically, the FCE extracts task-agnostic channel representations via self-supervised masked reconstruction while the FOD generates multi-task optimization decisions through differentiable output heads. Moreover, a hybrid supervised-to-unsupervised training strategy is employed to overcome the performance ceiling of purely supervised learning, and the modular architecture of the WFM enables efficient adaptation to unseen communication tasks with minimal parameter overhead. Simulation results show that the proposed WFM learns high-fidelity channel representations and achieves competitive multi-task optimization performance while substantially reducing optimization inference latency relative to numerical baselines. Furthermore, it exhibits robust generalization to unseen propagation environments, varying constraint parameters, and heterogeneous system configurations.

</details>


### [405] [Outage Analysis of Backscatter-Based Ambient IoT Device Classes with Energy Buffering](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16919)
*Azzam Al-nahari,Riku Jäntti,Yi Zhou,Deepak Mishra,Masoud Kaveh,Abhishek Mondal*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops an analytical framework to compare outage probabilities of two A-IoT device classes—passive backscatter (Device 1) vs. supercapacitor-buffered with amplification (Device 2)—showing Device 2 excels in energy-rich conditions while Device 1 is more robust under energy constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand the interplay between energy availability and communication reliability for different A-IoT device types, and to determine which device class is optimal under varying operating regimes.

Method: An analytical framework jointly modeling carrier-detection sensitivity, energy harvesting constraints, supercapacitor energy dynamics, and an energy-aware amplification policy; energy evolution is modeled via a discrete-time Markov chain (DTMC).

Result: Device 2 (buffered + amplification) outperforms in energy-rich regimes; Device 1 (passive backscatter) is more robust in energy-constrained regimes, especially at larger distances or high payloads.

Conclusion: Optimal device choice depends critically on the operating regime and application demands.

Abstract: This paper presents an analytical framework for evaluating the outage probability of ambient Internet of Things (A-IoT) device classes communicating directly with a base station. Device 1 is a passive backscatter device with minimal storage, while Device 2 is equipped with a supercapacitor that enables energy buffering and optional amplification. The proposed framework jointly accounts for carrier-detection sensitivity, energy harvesting constraints, supercapacitor energy dynamics, and an energy-aware amplification policy, while the energy evolution of buffered devices is modeled using a discrete-time Markov chain (DTMC). The results highlight the interplay between energy availability and communication reliability. Device 2 achieves superior performance in energy-rich regimes due to buffering and amplification gains, whereas Device 1 becomes more robust in energy-constrained regimes, particularly at larger distances or under high payload requirements. These findings highlight that the optimal device choice depends critically on the operating regime and application demands.

</details>


### [406] [Intelligent Code-Division Multiplexing for Resilient Underwater Optical Wireless Communications](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16925)
*Gaurav Mahadik,Ananya Hazarika,Mehdi Rahmati*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes an intelligent chaotic-based CDMA scheme for underwater optical wireless communication, using logistic map-generated chaotic sequences and a Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning framework to dynamically optimize parameters. It demonstrates superior performance over conventional schemes in a 2-meter water tank testbed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Address critical performance degradation in UOWC caused by severe scattering and multipath dispersion, where conventional modulation techniques like on-off keying suffer from high sensitivity to channel distortions.

Method: Uses unpredictable deterministic chaotic sequences (logistic map) for CDMA, with a MARL framework enabling distributed agents to dynamically adapt chaotic map parameters (initial conditions, bifurcation parameters) based on real-time environmental feedback.

Result: Experimental validation in a 2-meter water tank with controlled turbidity shows superior performance versus conventional schemes, with rapid convergence and relaxed synchronization requirements.

Conclusion: The adaptive framework is highly suitable for distributed underwater networks where centralized coordination is impractical, offering enhanced robustness against scattering-induced impairments.

Abstract: This paper presents a novel intelligent chaotic-based code-division multiple access (CDMA) scheme for underwater optical wireless communication (UOWC), addressing critical performance degradation caused by severe scattering and multipath dispersion in underwater environments. Unlike conventional modulation techniques such as on-off keying, which depend on precise pulse timing and show high sensitivity to channel distortions, the proposed approach leverages unpredictable deterministic chaotic sequences generated by the logistic map to enhance robustness against scattering-induced impairments. A Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning (MARL) framework enables distributed agents to dynamically adapt chaotic map parameters, including initial conditions and bifurcation parameters, based on real-time environmental feedback, optimizing sequence generation to maintain low cross-correlation properties and improve resilience to multipath effects. Experimental validation using a 2-meter water tank testbed with controlled turbidity demonstrates superior performance compared to conventional schemes. The adaptive framework exhibits rapid convergence and relaxed synchronization requirements, making it highly suitable for distributed underwater networks where centralized coordination is impractical.

</details>


### [407] [Efficient Audio-Visual Event Recognition via Knowledge Distillation and Dynamic INT8 Quantization of a Hybrid Cross-Attention Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16980)
*Parinaz Binandeh Dehaghani,Danilo Pena,A. Pedro Aguiar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes an efficient compression framework for audio-visual event recognition that combines architectural compression, knowledge distillation, and INT8 quantization, achieving 59% parameter reduction in the fusion module and model size reduction from 10.71 MB to 2.04 MB with minimal accuracy loss (2.14%).


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Transformer-based audio-visual event recognition models suffer from high computational complexity, large memory footprint, and high inference cost, making them unsuitable for deployment on edge and resource-constrained devices.

Method: The framework combines three techniques: (1) architectural model compression (reducing hidden feature dimensions, attention heads, and FFN size), (2) knowledge distillation from a high-capacity teacher model (VideoMAE + AST + hybrid cross-attention fusion), and (3) dynamic INT8 post-training quantization for further size reduction.

Result: On the AVE dataset, the framework reduces trainable parameters in the multimodal fusion module by 59.06% with only 2.14% accuracy drop, and INT8 quantization further reduces model size from 10.71 MB to 2.04 MB while maintaining competitive performance.

Conclusion: The proposed framework offers an effective trade-off between recognition accuracy and computational efficiency, making it a promising solution for deployment on resource-constrained edge AI platforms.

Abstract: Audio-visual event recognition (AVER) has achieved significant performance improvements through transformer-based multimodal architectures. However, the high computational complexity, large memory footprint, and inference cost of these models hinder their deployment on edge and resource-constrained devices. This paper presents an efficient compression framework for hybrid cross-attention-based audiovisual event recognition by combining architectural model compression, knowledge distillation, and dynamic INT8 quantization. A high-capacity teacher model integrates VideoMAE for visual representation learning, the Audio Spectrogram Transformer (AST) for audio feature extraction, and a hybrid cross-attention fusion network for multimodal feature integration. A lightweight student model is constructed by reducing the hidden feature dimension, the number of attention heads, and the feedforward network size while preserving the overall network architecture. The student model is trained using knowledge distillation to effectively transfer discriminative knowledge from the teacher. Finally, dynamic INT8 post-training quantization is applied to further reduce the model size for efficient deployment. Experimental results on the Audio-Visual Event (AVE) dataset show that the proposed framework reduces the number of trainable parameters in the multimodal fusion module by 59.06%, with only a 2.14% decrease in classification accuracy compared with the teacher model. Furthermore, dynamic INT8 quantization reduces the model size from 10.71 MB to 2.04 MB while maintaining competitive recognition performance. These results demonstrate that the proposed framework provides an effective trade-off between recognition accuracy and computational efficiency, making it a promising solution for deployment on resource-constrained edge AI platforms.

</details>


### [408] [Off-grid Variational Bayesian Parameter Estimation for Fractional Delay-Doppler OTFS-ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17001)
*Van-Chung Luu,Duy H. N. Nguyen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes an off-grid variational Bayesian (OVB) method for fractional delay-Doppler estimation in OTFS-based ISAC systems, using von Mises distributions and closed-form variational updates for automatic path-number estimation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable accurate off-grid fractional delay-Doppler parameter estimation in OTFS-based integrated sensing and communication systems.

Method: Reformulates OTFS channel using separable delay/Doppler steering vectors, models phase variables with von Mises distributions, and derives closed-form variational Bayesian updates for posterior statistics.

Result: Achieves higher channel and parameter estimation accuracy than conventional fractional DD estimation approaches.

Conclusion: The proposed OVB method effectively enables automatic path-number estimation and outperforms existing methods in accuracy.

Abstract: This letter proposes an off-grid variational Bayesian (OVB) method for fractional delay-Doppler (DD) estimation in OTFS-based integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems. To enable off-grid parameter estimation, the OTFS channel is reformulated using separable delay and Doppler steering vectors, and the corresponding phase variables are modeled by von Mises distributions. Closed-form variational updates provide posterior statistics for identifying significant paths and pruning redundant candidates, enabling automatic path-number estimation. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves higher channel and parameter estimation accuracy than conventional fractional DD estimation approaches.

</details>


### [409] [Movable Antenna for Integrated Sensing and Communication in Air Sea Ground Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17041)
*Ahmed A. Al-habob,Octavia A. Dobre,Yindi Jing*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a movable antenna framework for ISAC in air-sea-ground networks, using K-means clustering for orientation optimization and particle swarm optimization for antenna placement, achieving better trade-off between communication and sensing performance than stationary antennas.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAC faces conflicting beamforming goals and resource contention; movable antennas can exploit spatial degrees of freedom through dynamic position/orientation control to enhance performance.

Method: A multi-objective optimization problem is formulated maximizing both communication rate (for aerial, sea, ground devices) and sensing rate (for targets). Solutions include: K-means clustering for sub-array orientation, particle swarm optimization for sub-array placement, successive convex approximation for transmit beamforming, and generalized eigenvector method for receive beamforming.

Result: Simulation results show the movable antenna framework improves the ISAC objective and achieves a remarkable trade-off between communication data rate and targets' sensing rate compared to conventional stationary antenna arrays.

Conclusion: Movable antennas significantly enhance ISAC system performance in air-sea-ground networks by resolving conflicting beamforming goals through optimized spatial resource allocation.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is a new paradigm for efficiently combining sensing and communication functionalities by leveraging shared hardware and radio resources. Despite its promise, ISAC yields conflicting beamforming goals and competition over the same resources. Movable antennas enable effective exploitation of spatial degrees of freedom through dynamic position/orientation control, thereby enhancing the performance of ISAC systems. This paper proposes a movable antenna framework for ISAC in air sea ground networks. A multi-objective optimization problem is formulated with the objectives of maximizing the communication rate of a set of aerial, sea, and ground devices and the sensing rate of a set of targets. The location and orientation of the antenna sub-arrays, as well as the transmit/receive beamforming, are optimized under practical constraints on the movable antennas' location and orientation. A solution is developed based on a $K$-means clustering approach to optimize the sub-arrays' orientation and a particle swarm optimization to place the sub-arrays in optimized locations. The transmit and receive beamforming are designed using a successive convex approximation and a generalized eigenvector method, respectively. Simulation results illustrate that the developed movable antenna framework improves the ISAC objective and provides a remarkable trade-off between the communication data rate and the targets' sensing rate when compared with the conventional stationary antenna array scenario.

</details>


### [410] [Fourth-Order Cyclostationary Analysis of Power-Based Nonlinear Gardner Timing Error Detectors in Coherent Optical Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17135)
*Zhongxing Tian,Zeyu Feng,Huan Huang,Dongdong Zou,Gangxiang Shen,Yi Cai*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper develops a fourth-order cyclostationary framework for power-based Gardner timing error detectors, using square-Gardner TED as a case study, revealing their cyclic-statistical origins and characterizing effects of pulse shaping, probabilistic shaping, polarization rotation, and PMD.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing second-order cyclostationary analysis cannot fully characterize power-based Gardner TEDs because their nonlinear power-domain operations depend on higher-order cyclic statistics, motivating the need for a fourth-order analytical framework.

Method: The authors develop a fourth-order cyclostationary framework for the square-Gardner TED (SG-TED), using moment-cumulant decomposition to separate the clock-tone component into a Wick-reducible term and a cumulant-related non-Gaussian term.

Result: The framework shows that SG-TED clock-tone originates from the symbol-rate cyclic component of the power-process autocorrelation function; it characterizes effects of pulse shaping, probabilistic shaping, polarization rotation, and PMD, revealing fundamentally different CT-response characteristics from second-order TEDs.

Conclusion: The framework provides a unified statistical basis for SG-TED and related power-based Gardner-type TEDs, validated by numerical evaluations and Monte Carlo simulations.

Abstract: Power-based nonlinear Gardner timing error detectors (TEDs) can enhance clock-tone (CT) extraction in low-roll-off and bandwidth-limited coherent optical systems. However, their nonlinear power-domain operations make the extracted CT components depend on higher-order cyclic statistics, which cannot be fully characterized by second-order cyclostationary analysis. In this paper, we develop a fourth-order cyclostationary analytical framework for power-based Gardner-type TEDs, using the square-Gardner TED (SG-TED) as a representative case. We show that the SG-TED CT originates from the symbol-rate cyclic component of the power-process autocorrelation function (CAF), revealing its fourth-order cyclic-statistical origin in the received complex field. Through moment-cumulant decomposition, the CT component is separated into a Wick-reducible term and a cumulant-related non-Gaussian term, which respectively explain its connection to the conventional Gardner/Godard mechanism and its modulation- and distribution-dependent behavior. The framework further characterizes the effects of pulse shaping, probabilistic shaping, polarization rotation, and polarization-mode dispersion (PMD), revealing CT-response characteristics fundamentally different from second-order TEDs. Numerical evaluations and waveform-level Monte Carlo simulations validate the analysis and demonstrate the framework as a unified statistical basis for SG-TED and related power-based Gardner-type TEDs.

</details>


### [411] [Inverse-Reinforcement Learning Enabled Digital Twin for Intent-based Drone Networks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17186)
*Jiahao Wang,Ruimin Yang,Hanzhi Yu,Huaiyu Dai,Ye Hu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a digital twin (DT) and inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) framework for intent-based drone base station (DBS) trajectory design in dynamic wireless networks, where the DT suggests trajectory adjustments to maximize prioritized user service without full knowledge of the drone's evolving intent. The IRL-based solution achieves near-optimal performance with ~85% less loss than traditional RL and up to 2.5x improvement over standard drone networks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To design optimal trajectories for an intent-based drone base station (DBS) that serves prioritized ground user clusters (e.g., high-risk highway sections) in resource-constrained, dynamic wireless environments, where the DBS's service priorities evolve over time and cannot be communicated to a central digital twin system due to intermittent connectivity.

Method: The problem is formulated as an optimization to maximize the fraction of prioritized users served. An inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) based digital twin actuation solution is proposed, where the DT simulates the environment, predicts changes, and suggests trajectory adjustments without explicit knowledge of the DBS's underlying intent.

Result: The proposed IRL-based DT solution provides near-real-time, near-optimal trajectory adjustment with approximately 85% less performance loss across environmental changes compared to traditional on-board RL control. The DT framework also improves drone network performance by up to 2.5 times over standard drone networks with erroneous and delayed sensing.

Conclusion: The digital twin coupled with inverse reinforcement learning effectively handles unknown, evolving drone intent and dynamic environmental changes, enabling robust and efficient trajectory planning for intent-based drone operations.

Abstract: In this paper, the problem of the trajectory design for an intent-based drone operating in resource-constrained, dynamic wireless network environments is studied. In the considered model, the drone acts as a supplementary base station that navigates among ground user clusters to provide on-demand uplink data access. Given its intended application (e.g traffic monitoring), the drone base station (DBS) prioritizes serving certain clusters (e.g. high-risk highway sections). A digital twin (DT) system, hosted on a central server, creates a virtual representation of the physical wireless network environment to simulate and predict related changes, in which case the DBS trajectory should also be adjusted. Then, the DT system suggests adjustments to DBS trajectories without guaranteed access to the underlying DBS intent (i.e., service priorities), as this intent evolves over time and cannot be updated to the DT system in a timely manner due to intermittent connectivity between the DBS and the DT server. Such adjustment is posed as an optimization problem whose goal is to find the trajectories with which the fraction of prioritized users served by the DBS is maximized. To solve this problem under unknown DBS intent and unpredictable environment changes, an inverse reinforcement learning (IRL) based DT actuation solution is proposed. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed solution provides near-real-time, near-optimal trajectory adjustment, with approximately 85\% less performance loss across environmental changes, compared to traditional reinforcement learning based on-board DBS control. The DT framework also enhances drone network performance by up to 2.5 times, compared to standard drone networks where a DBS operates with its erroneous and delayed environmental sensing.

</details>


### [412] [Broadband Content-Adaptive Moiré Meta-spectrometer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17256)
*Arnab Ghosh,Johannes E. Fröch,Arka Majumdar,Vishwanath Saragadam*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: A content-adaptive spectrometer using Moiré meta-optics and recursive sampling achieves efficient, high-resolution spectral reconstruction across 405-980 nm with up to 7x fewer measurements.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional spectrometers face a rigid trade-off between footprint, spectral range, and resolution, limiting their applicability in compact, real-time sensing scenarios.

Method: Co-designs dispersive Moiré meta-optics (hyperchromatic metasurfaces) with a recursive sampling algorithm that exploits spectral sparsity: coarse sweep first, then fine angular refinement in high-information regions.

Result: Prototype spanning 405-980 nm reconstructs diverse spectra with ~3x fewer measurements on average (up to 7x for sparse lines), achieving 30 dB reconstruction 6.7x faster than uniform sampling.

Conclusion: Establishes a framework for intelligent, task-adaptive meta-optical sensors that tightly integrate physical dispersion with computational signal processing for real-time spectrometry.

Abstract: Optical spectroscopy underpins material characterization, chemical sensing, and astronomy, but conventional instruments face a rigid trade-off between footprint, spectral range, and resolution. We demonstrate a content-adaptive spectrometer that overcomes this by co-designing dispersive Moiré meta-optics with a recursive sampling algorithm. Instead of using Moiré metalenses solely for varifocal tuning, we harness the strong chromatic aberration arising from phase-wrapping in their subwavelength metasurface architecture. This hyperchromaticity enables a deterministic, one-to-one mapping between the metasurfaces' mutual rotation angle and the sharply focused wavelength, repurposing the pair as a high-resolution spectral scanner. To accelerate data acquisition, we introduce a content-adaptive recursive sampling protocol that exploits the structural sparsity of physical spectra: a fast coarse sweep identifies high-information regions, followed by successively finer angular refinement only where needed. Using a laboratory prototype spanning 405-980 nm, we reconstruct diverse spectra -- from smooth broadband to sparse multi-line laser emissions -- with nearly 3x fewer measurements on average at matched fidelity (up to 7x for sparse line spectra), achieving 30 dB reconstruction 6.7x faster than conventional uniform sampling. This establishes a framework for intelligent, task-adaptive meta-optical sensors that tightly integrate physical dispersion with computational signal processing for real-time spectrometry.

</details>


### [413] [An Enhanced MNOMP for Line Spectrum Estimation and Detection](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17308)
*Yulin Jiang,Jiang Zhu,Fengzhong Qu,Yonina C. Eldar*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes EMNOMP, an enhanced version of MNOMP for line spectrum estimation, which improves upon MNOMP by solving the GLRT over the continuous frequency domain rather than restricting to DFT grid frequencies, using chi-squared random field theory and the Lambert W function to derive a closed-form false alarm threshold.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: MNOMP approximates the GLRT by restricting the search to discrete DFT grid frequencies, which introduces off-grid inaccuracies. The authors aim to improve detection accuracy by solving the GLRT over the continuous frequency domain.

Method: EMNOMP derives the false alarm probability for continuous-domain GLRT using chi-squared random field theory and obtains a closed-form threshold via the Lambert W function. It also analyzes the SNR gain relative to MNOMP.

Result: Numerical simulations validate the theoretical analysis and demonstrate the effectiveness of EMNOMP compared to MNOMP, with derived and analyzed SNR gains.

Conclusion: The continuous-domain GLRT approach in EMNOMP outperforms the DFT-grid-restricted GLRT in MNOMP, providing more accurate line spectrum estimation with a theoretically grounded false alarm threshold.

Abstract: Multisnapshot Newtonized orthogonal matching pursuit (MNOMP) incorporates Newton's method into OMP to avoid the off-grid issues, achieve high accuracy, high resolution and fast line spectrum estimation with multiple measurement vectors. It employs the generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) with a constant false alarm rate (CFAR) criterion to determine the number of sinusoids. In this paper, we develop an enhanced MNOMP (EMNOMP) by analyzing the statistical distribution of the exact GLRT statistic. In contrast to MNOMP, which approximates the GLRT by restricting the search to discrete Fourier transform (DFT) grid frequencies, EMNOMP instead solves the GLRT over the continuous frequency domain. The key technical novelty is the derivation of the false alarm probability for this continuous-domain GLRT using chi-squared random field theory and the resulting closed-form threshold via the Lambert W function. The signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) gain of EMNOMP relative to MNOMP is derived and analyzed in depth. Numerical simulations validate the theoretical analysis and the effectiveness of EMNOMP compared to MNOMP.

</details>


### [414] [Transmissive RIS Transceiver-Empowered ISAC Systems: Energy Efficiency Optimization for Perfect and Imperfect CSI](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17475)
*Yuan Guo,Wen Chen,Yang Liu,Kunlun Wang,Zhendong Li,Qiong Wu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a transmissive reconfigurable intelligent surface (TRIS) transceiver for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, and develops energy efficiency maximization algorithms under both perfect and imperfect CSI conditions using FP and MM techniques.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve energy efficiency in ISAC systems by introducing a novel TRIS transceiver architecture that can simultaneously support communication and sensing functions.

Method: For perfect CSI: applies fractional programming (FP) to reformulate rate functions, then uses majorization-minimization (MM) framework. For imperfect CSI: uses S-Procedure to convert semi-infinite constraints into LMIs, then develops MM-based algorithm with slack variables.

Result: Numerical results demonstrate convergence and effectiveness of the proposed algorithms, and validate energy efficiency gains of the TRIS transceiver-enabled ISAC system.

Conclusion: The TRIS transceiver architecture combined with the proposed optimization algorithms can achieve significant energy efficiency improvements in ISAC systems under both perfect and imperfect CSI.

Abstract: In this paper, a novel transmissive reconfigurable intelligent surface (TRIS) transceiver is employed to enable an integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) system supporting both communication and sensing. Under both perfect and imperfect channel state information (CSI), we study the transmit beamforming design for the TRIS transceiver to maximize the system energy efficiency (EE), subject to per-user minimum-rate guarantees, a minimum beampattern gain toward the sensing target, and per-antenna power constraints. The corresponding EE maximization problems are challenging to solve due to the fractional objective and non-convex constraints. In particular, under imperfect CSI, the resulting semi-infinite constraints further complicate the problem. For the perfect CSI case, we first apply the fractional programming (FP) methodology to obtain more tractable reformulations of the rate functions, and then propose an iterative algorithm based on the majorization-minimization (MM) framework. For the imperfect CSI case, we utilize the S-Procedure to transform the semi-infinite inequality constraints into linear matrix inequalities (LMIs), and further develop an efficient MM-based algorithm with the aid of slack variables. Numerical results demonstrate the convergence and effectiveness of the proposed algorithms and validate the EE gains of the TRIS transceiver-enabled ISAC system.

</details>


### [415] [Radio Map Updating from Streaming Spectrum Measurements via Memory-Based Online Gaussian Processes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17954)
*Yuanyuan Deng,Bo Zhou,Tian Chen,Shijian Gao,Jia Yan,Lantu Guo,Qiuming Zhu,Qihui Wu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes M-OSVGP, an online sparse variational Gaussian process method with grid-assisted inducing point selection, for efficient radio map reconstruction from streaming spectrum measurements, achieving good accuracy and computational efficiency.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Conventional batch methods for radio map reconstruction are computationally prohibitive when measurements arrive continuously, as they require reprocessing all accumulated data for each update.

Method: M-OSVGP uses sparse variational inference with online posterior updates via a hybrid objective (new measurements + memory subset) to avoid catastrophic forgetting, plus GOIPS for dynamic inducing point selection.

Result: Extensive simulations show M-OSVGP outperforms batch and online baselines in reconstruction accuracy, computational efficiency, and uncertainty quantification.

Conclusion: The proposed online method enables efficient, scalable radio map updates from streaming data while maintaining high reconstruction quality and uncertainty estimates.

Abstract: Radio maps, which estimate spatial radio-frequency characteristics from spectrum measurements, are essential for applications such as spectrum management and network planning. With the continuous arrival of spectrum measurements, conventional batch processing methods for radio map reconstruction become computationally prohibitive, as they require reprocessing all accumulated measurements for each radio map update. To address this, we propose a memory-based online sparse variational Gaussian process (M-OSVGP) method that efficiently updates radio maps from streaming spectrum measurements. Our method employs sparse variational inference and updates the posterior online by minimizing a hybrid objective that integrates newly received measurements and a memory subset of previous ones to mitigate catastrophic forgetting. To further improve posterior approximation as measurements accumulate over spatially diverse regions, we extend M-OSVGP with a grid-assisted online inducing point selection (GOIPS) algorithm. GOIPS dynamically adapts the number and locations of inducing points based on measurement density and spatial correlation, providing a more informative inducing set while maintaining computational efficiency. Extensive simulations demonstrate the effectiveness of our proposed methods in reconstruction accuracy, computational efficiency, and uncertainty quantification, compared to existing batch and online baselines across various scenarios.

</details>


### [416] [Transmit Beamforming Design for Integrated Sensing and Communication Using Transmissive RIS Transceiver](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17485)
*Yuan Guo,Wen Chen,Yang Liu,Qiong Wu,Weiren Zhu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a transmissive reconfigurable intelligent surface (TRIS) transceiver for ISAC systems and develops low-complexity beamforming algorithms using FP, MM, and ADMM frameworks to maximize sum-rate or beampattern gain under sensing/communication constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: ISAC is key for future wireless networks but requires hardware-efficient architectures. TRIS transceivers offer a promising solution to jointly support communication and sensing, but beamforming design under practical per-unit power constraints is challenging.

Method: The authors formulate two optimization problems (sum-rate maximization and beampattern gain maximization) with non-convex objectives/constraints. They combine FP and MM to develop SOCP-based solutions, then apply ADMM to split coupling constraints and derive analytic low-complexity algorithms for beamformer updates.

Result: Simulations demonstrate convergence and effectiveness of the proposed algorithms. The low-complexity ADMM-based algorithms achieve performance close to SOCP-based benchmarks with substantially reduced computational complexity.

Conclusion: The proposed TRIS-based ISAC system with ADMM-based beamforming algorithms achieves efficient trade-offs between communication and sensing performance while significantly reducing computational complexity compared to SOCP benchmarks.

Abstract: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) is a key technology for future wireless networks, calling for hardware-efficient architectures to jointly support communication and sensing. In this paper, a transmissive reconfigurable intelligent surface (TRIS) transceiver is leveraged to enable an ISAC system. Under the considered system model, we investigate transmit beamforming design for the TRIS transceiver to maximize the sum-rate/beampattern gain, subject to the predefined sensing beampattern gain/communication rate thresholds and the per-unit power constraints of the TRIS transceiver. Since the objective functions and constraints are non-convex, the above two optimization problems are highly challenging. To resolve the difficult optimization problems, we combine the fractional programming (FP) method and the majorization-minimization (MM) framework to develop second-order cone programming (SOCP)-based solutions. Since the per-element power constraints introduce a large number of constraints, this increases the complexity of solving the optimization problems. By splitting the coupling constraints and applying the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) framework, we propose two analytic-based algorithms for efficiently updating the beamformer configurations in the sum-rate and beampattern gain maximization problems, respectively. Simulation results demonstrate the convergence and effectiveness of the proposed algorithms, and show that the low-complexity algorithms achieve performance close to the SOCP-based benchmarks with substantially reduced computational complexity.

</details>


### [417] [Constrained Tensor Decomposition-Based Target Sensing for Sparse Non-Uniform Array-Enabled AFDM ISAC Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17589)
*Linchu Chen,Zhendong Li,Lin Chen,Zhou Su,Wenjie Wang,Wen Chen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: Proposes a constrained tensor decomposition-based sensing framework using manifold-constrained ALS and golden section search for accurate delay, Doppler, and angle estimation in sparse non-uniform array AFDM ISAC systems, achieving near-CRB performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The performance of sparse non-uniform array-enabled AFDM for ISAC critically depends on accurate target parameter estimation, which existing methods fail to achieve effectively.

Method: Develops a manifold-constrained alternating least squares (ALS) algorithm exploiting sparse array geometry for robust factor matrix extraction and direct angle estimation, followed by iterative one-dimensional golden section search to refine delay and Doppler shift.

Result: The proposed algorithm nearly attains the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) and significantly outperforms unconstrained ALS and conventional methods.

Conclusion: The constrained tensor decomposition-based approach is validated as effective for sparse non-uniform array-enabled AFDM ISAC systems.

Abstract: Sparse non-uniform array-enabled affine frequency division multiplexing (AFDM) is a promising candidate for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC), while its performance critically depends on accurate target parameter estimation. In this paper, we propose a constrained tensor decomposition-based sensing framework for delay, Doppler, and angle estimation. Specifically, a manifold-constrained alternating least squares (ALS) algorithm is developed by exploiting the sparse array geometry structure, enabling robust factor matrix extraction and direct angle estimation. From the decomposed factor matrices, we further apply an iterative one dimensional golden section search to refine delay and Doppler shift. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed algorithm nearly attains Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) and significantly outperforms unconstrained ALS and conventional methods, validating its effectiveness for sparse non-uniform array-enabled AFDM ISAC systems.

</details>


### [418] [Tensor-Based Dynamic Channel Estimation for mmWave Movable Antenna MIMO Systems](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17591)
*Zhendong Li,Linchu Chen,Lin Chen,Zhou Su,Ruoyu Zhang,Guangji Chen,Ying Wang,Wen Chen*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: The paper proposes a tensor decomposition-based channel estimation algorithm for mmWave movable antenna MIMO systems, achieving high accuracy by exploiting channel sparsity and fourth-order tensor modeling, with results approaching the Cramér-Rao bound.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To achieve highly accurate dynamic channel estimation in mmWave movable antenna (MA) MIMO systems, which is challenging due to the movable antenna configuration and mmWave channel characteristics.

Method: The channel is transformed into a superposition of sparse paths using a path response model. The received signal is constructed as a fourth-order tensor to capture high-dimensional structural information. Two tensor decomposition schemes extract factor matrices from which propagation loss, frequency offset, AoA/AoD, and time delay are estimated, and the channel matrix is rebuilt.

Result: The proposed algorithm achieves estimation accuracy nearly approaching the Cramér-Rao bound (CRB). Simulation results show a notable reduction in normalized mean square error (NMSE) compared to baseline algorithms.

Conclusion: The tensor decomposition-based approach effectively exploits the sparsity and high-dimensional structure of mmWave MA MIMO channels, providing superior estimation accuracy with guaranteed decomposition uniqueness.

Abstract: This paper investigates the dynamic channel estimation algorithm in mmWave movable antenna (MA) multiple-input multiple-output (MIMO) systems. To achieve highly accurate channel estimation, we propose a tensor decomposition-based channel estimation algorithm. First, by leveraging the path response model and utilizing the intrinsic sparsity of mmWave channels, the channel corresponding to MA pairs at the base station and mobile station is transformed into a superposition of channels from sparse paths. Next, the received signal is constructed as a fourth-order tensor to fully capture the high-dimensional structural information of the MA MIMO channel. Then, two tensor decomposition schemes are adopted to extract the factor matrices, and our analysis reveals that the uniqueness of the decomposition can be guaranteed in our model. Subsequently, the propagation loss, frequency offset, angle of arrival/departure, and time delay are obtained based on these factor matrices and the channel matrix can be rebuilt. Additionally, Cramér-Rao bound (CRB) is also derived as a performance evaluation standard, proving that the proposed algorithm achieves a higher estimation accuracy and nearly approaches this minimum bound. Moreover, normalized mean square error (NMSE) is selected as the evaluation metrics for estimation accuracy. Finally, simulation results reveal a notable reduction in the estimation error of the proposed algorithm when compared to the baseline algorithms, confirming its estimation advantage.

</details>


### [419] [Waveform Design for OTFS Assisted Simultaneous Acoustic Information and Power Transfer](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17631)
*Jinheng Kang,Yizhe Zhao,Kun Yang,Jun Liu*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an OTFS-based SAIPT waveform design for dynamic underwater acoustic channels that outperforms traditional OFDM-based schemes in energy harvesting efficiency while meeting data rate constraints.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing OFDM-based SAIPT systems are vulnerable to severe multipath propagation and Doppler effects in dynamic underwater acoustic channels, limiting their effectiveness for self-sustainable IoUT networks.

Method: The paper jointly designs AIT and APT symbols, incorporates transducer conversion efficiencies and nonlinear rectifier characteristics into the system model, and develops an SCA-based algorithm to solve the non-convex waveform optimization problem.

Result: The proposed OTFS-based design outperforms the OFDM-based scheme in terms of DC output in dynamic transmission scenarios, with analysis confirming the effectiveness in improving acoustic energy transfer efficiency.

Conclusion: OTFS-based SAIPT is a promising approach for robust underwater IoT energy and data transmission in dynamic channels, with key system parameters affecting performance.

Abstract: Simultaneous acoustic information and power transfer (SAIPT) is a promising technique for supporting self-sustainable Internet of Underwater Things (IoUT) networks through concurrent data transmission and energy supplement. However, existing OFDM-based SAIPT studies are vulnerable to severe multipath propagation and Doppler effects in dynamic underwater acoustic channels. To address this issue, this paper proposes an orthogonal time frequency space (OTFS)-based SAIPT waveform design for dynamic underwater acoustic channels. The acoustic information transfer (AIT) and acoustic power transfer (APT) symbols are jointly designed, while the transducer conversion efficiencies and nonlinear rectifier characteristics are incorporated into the system model. Based on the derived achievable data rate and DC output expressions, a waveform optimization problem is formulated to maximize the harvested DC output under transmit power and minimum data-rate constraints. To solve the resulting non-convex problem, a successive convex approximation (SCA)-based algorithm is developed. Simulation results show that the proposed OTFS-based design outperforms the OFDM-based scheme in terms of DC output in the dynamic transmission scenarios. The effects of key system parameters are also analyzed, confirming the effectiveness of the proposed design in improving acoustic energy transfer efficiency.

</details>


### [420] [DMSNet: Cross-Band Learning for Multi-Target Sensing in Multi-Band ISAC](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17655)
*Haotian Liu,Zhiqing Wei,Quanjiang Zhao,Lin Wang,Yunxin Geng,Xingwang Li,Zhiyong Feng*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: DMSNet is a neural network for dual-band ISAC that jointly estimates target number and parameters (range, velocity, angle) with higher accuracy and 68.7% less runtime than existing methods.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing dual-band ISAC sensing methods cannot effectively exploit deep complementary information across heterogeneous frequency bands and suffer from high computational costs.

Method: Proposes DMSNet, a Dual-Band Multi-Target Sensing Neural Network for joint target number and parameter estimation using complementary high- and low-frequency echo information.

Result: Target count accuracy: 89.01% → 91.74%; Macro-F1: 90.80% → 93.07%. Median absolute errors reduced by 82.2% (range), 56.9% (velocity), 73.2% (angle). Runtime reduced by 68.7%.

Conclusion: DMSNet significantly improves both accuracy and efficiency for dual-band multi-target ISAC sensing, outperforming baselines across all metrics.

Abstract: Multi-band integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) offers complementary high- and low-frequency echo information for multi-target sensing. However, existing dual-band ISAC sensing methods have a limited ability to exploit deep complementary information across heterogeneous bands and often incur high computational costs. To address these limitations, we propose a Dual-Band Multi-Target Sensing Neural Network (DMSNet) for joint target number and parameter estimation. Under representative simulation conditions, DMSNet outperforms the best baseline in target number estimation, increasing count accuracy from 89.01 % to 91.74 % and Macro-F1 from 90.80 % to 93.07 %. For parameter estimation, compared with the best baselines, DMSNet reduces the median absolute errors of range, velocity, and angle by 82.2%, 56.9%, and 73.2%, respectively. Moreover, DMSNet reduces runtime by 68.7 % relative to the fastest existing dual-band ISAC sensing method.

</details>


### [421] [Ambiguity Function Analysis of Pilot-Embedded Random OFDM Signals](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17663)
*Jialin Wu,Fan Liu,Ying Zhang,Yifeng Xiong,Jie Yang,Shi Jin*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper derives analytical expressions for the mean squared ambiguity functions (DP-AF and FST-AF) of OFDM waveforms with deterministic pilot symbols and random data payloads for ISAC, showing that DP-AF depends on pilot patterns/symbols/number while FST-AF depends only on pilot count.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To understand how pilot-embedded OFDM signals behave in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems, particularly regarding their ambiguity functions which are critical for sensing performance.

Method: Analytical derivation of mean squared discrete periodic ambiguity function (DP-AF) and fast-slow-time ambiguity function (FST-AF) for OFDM waveforms with deterministic unit-modulus pilots and random data payloads, validated by numerical simulations.

Result: The mean squared DP-AF depends jointly on pilot patterns, pilot symbols, and number of pilots; the mean squared FST-AF depends only on the number of pilots. Different pilot patterns yield distinct DP-AF characteristics.

Conclusion: The findings provide relevant design considerations for pilot design in communication-centric ISAC systems, enabling tailored waveform design for dual-functional radar-communication.

Abstract: This paper investigates the statistical ambiguity functions (AFs) of orthogonal frequency division multiplexing (OFDM) waveforms that incorporate deterministic unit-modulus pilot symbols and random data payloads for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC). We derive analytical expressions for the mean squared discrete periodic ambiguity function (DP-AF) and fast-slow-time ambiguity function (FST-AF) of such pilot-embedded OFDM signals. Our analysis demonstrates that, under a fixed signal length and constellation scheme, the mean squared DP-AF depends jointly on the pilot patterns, pilot symbols and number of pilots, while the mean squared FST-AF relies only on the number of pilots. Numerical simulations closely match the theoretical expressions. Furthermore, in numerical results, we show that different pilot patterns correspond to DP-AF with distinct characteristics, offering relevant considerations for pilot design in communication-centric ISAC systems.

</details>


### [422] [On the Detection Optimality and Exact BER Analysis of NOMA](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17755)
*Tasneem Assaf,Hamad Yahya,Arafat Al-Dweik*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper derives optimal per-user MAP detectors for NOMA, proves that conventional SIC equals JML, and provides exact BER analysis accounting for SIC-induced statistical changes, showing consistent BER gains at low-to-moderate SNR.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current NOMA detection methods (SIC and JML) lack established optimality for individual-user BER minimization, and existing BER analyses ignore statistical changes caused by SIC.

Method: Derives optimal per-user MAP detectors, analyzes when near-user detector reduces to conventional SIC, proves equivalence of SIC and JML, and presents exact average BER analysis accounting for SIC-induced statistical changes.

Result: The proposed MAP detector achieves modest but consistent BER gains at low-to-moderate SNR compared to conventional approaches.

Conclusion: Conventional SIC is equivalent to JML, and the optimal per-user MAP detector provides BER improvements especially in low-to-moderate SNR regimes.

Abstract: Non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) detection using successive interference cancellation (SIC) and joint multiuser maximum likelihood (JML) has been extensively studied, although their presumed optimality for individual-user bit error rate (BER) minimization is unestablished. Moreover, existing BER analyses overlook the SIC-induced statistical changes. This letter derives optimal per-user maximum a posteriori (MAP) detectors, identifies when the near-user detector reduces to conventional SIC, and proves the equivalence of conventional SIC and JML. An exact average BER analysis accounting for SIC-induced statistical changes is presented. The results show that the proposed detector achieves modest but consistent BER gains at low-to-moderate signal-to-noise ratio (SNR).

</details>


### [423] [Deep Recurrent Q-Learning Based Beam Steering Strategy for Throughput Maximization in WPCNs](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17838)
*Samannaya Adhikari,Navchetan Awasthi,Siddhartha Sarma*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a deep learning-based blind adaptive beam-steering policy using ADRQN to regulate energy harvesting device access in wireless powered communication networks, achieving up to 68% throughput improvement over non-learning schemes without requiring channel estimation or device tracking.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing MAC protocols for harvest-then-transmit WPCNs fail to meet requirements of being distributed, low-overhead, and handling irregular transmissions, leading to inefficient use of scarce harvested energy.

Method: Identifies beam steering as a charging rate regulator; formulates joint energy beam steering and slotted ALOHA access problem; uses action-specific deep recurrent Q-Network (ADRQN) to learn policy from ternary slot outcomes (idle, success, collision).

Result: Up to 68% throughput increase over non-learning schemes; achieves 75-80% of oracle policy performance; operates without channel estimation, charge reporting, or device-state tracking.

Conclusion: Blind adaptive beam-steering via deep reinforcement learning is effective for distributed, low-overhead MAC in WPCNs, significantly improving energy efficiency and throughput.

Abstract: In wireless powered communication networks, medium access control protocols for devices using the harvest-then-transmit strategy must be distributed, low-overhead, and capable of handling irregular and infrequent data transmissions to ensure efficient energy utilisation. However, most existing protocols fail to meet one or more of those requirements, leading to wasted scarce harvested energy. We address this by identifying beam steering as a potential mechanism to regulate the charging rate of energy harvesting devices and thus control their access to the shared wireless medium. After formulating a joint problem of energy beam steering and slotted ALOHA-based random access, we leverage a deep learning framework based on an action-specific deep recurrent Q-Network (ADRQN) to learn a beam-steering policy only from the macro-level ternary slot outcomes, namely, idle, success and collision. Additionally, we design an oracle policy with global knowledge of the network to benchmark our proposed blind adaptive beam-steering approach. The numerical results demonstrate that our approach achieves up to 68\% increase in throughput compared to non-learning schemes, while also reaching 75-80\% of the oracle policy's performance, all without requiring channel estimation, charge-level reporting, or device-state tracking.

</details>


### [424] [Massive MIMO-OFDM ISAC for Sparse ISAR Imaging: Joint Power and Subcarrier Allocation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17862)
*Hamid Reza Hashempour,Yanjiao Li,Jie Zhang,Hyundong Shin,Hien Quoc Ngo*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes an mMIMO-OFDM ISAC framework with ISAR imaging, using an adaptive reweighted ADMM algorithm for sparse-aperture reconstruction and a SAC-based resource allocation method to optimize communication and sensing performance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable simultaneous downlink communication and ISAR imaging in IoT applications, while addressing the challenge of incomplete measurement data from sparse pilot transmission and sensing-subcarrier activation.

Method: Dual-function architecture combining communication precoding and sensing beamforming; adaptive reweighted 2D ADMM for sparse ISAR reconstruction; joint resource-allocation optimizing subcarrier assignment, selection, and power allocation; SAC-based learning for sparse-sensing resource allocation; analytical full-band sensing benchmarks using statistical CSI with MR and ZF precoding.

Result: The adaptive ADMM outperforms conventional methods in sparse ISAR reconstruction. The SAC-based design achieves substantial sum spectral efficiency gains over full-band benchmarks while meeting communication and sensing constraints, revealing the tradeoff between reconstruction accuracy and spectral efficiency.

Conclusion: The proposed framework effectively integrates mMIMO-OFDM ISAC with ISAR imaging, demonstrating that intelligent resource allocation via SAC can balance the tradeoff between sensing quality and communication performance.

Abstract: This paper investigates a massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) orthogonal frequency-division multiplexing (OFDM) framework for integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) with inverse synthetic aperture radar (ISAR) imaging, supporting applications such as the Internet of Things (IoT). A dual-function architecture combines communication precoding and dedicated sensing beamforming to enable simultaneous downlink communication and ISAR imaging. Due to intermittent pilot transmission and sparse sensing-subcarrier activation, the received echoes provide incomplete measurements, resulting in a sparse-aperture ISAR reconstruction problem. To address this issue, an adaptive reweighted two-dimensional alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM) algorithm is developed for high-resolution image recovery from sparse observations. A joint resource-allocation framework is also proposed to optimize communication-subcarrier assignment, sensing-subcarrier selection, and transmit power allocation subject to communication quality-of-service and sensing constraints. Exploiting channel hardening, analytical full-band sensing benchmarks based solely on statistical channel state information (CSI) are derived for maximum-ratio (MR) and zero-forcing (ZF) precoding, while a soft actor-critic (SAC)-based method is developed for sparse-sensing resource allocation. Numerical results show that the proposed adaptive ADMM algorithm improves sparse ISAR reconstruction over conventional methods. The SAC-based design also achieves substantial gains in sum spectral efficiency over the full-band benchmarks while satisfying communication and sensing constraints, thereby revealing the tradeoff between ISAR reconstruction accuracy and communication spectral efficiency.

</details>


### [425] [System-Level Evaluation of LEO Satellite Communications Under Service-Driven Traffic Dynamics](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17993)
*Miyeon Lee,Dong-Hyun Jung,Sucheol Kim,Hwanjin Kim,Junil Choi*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper evaluates LEO satellite communication performance under realistic (non-full-buffer) traffic models using a 3GPP-compliant system-level simulator, analyzing UE throughput, RB allocation, and packet delay across diverse traffic scenarios.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing LEO satellite studies rely on full-buffer assumptions, which ignore the heterogeneity and intermittency of real user traffic, leading to unrealistic performance assessments.

Method: Developed a system-level simulator following 3GPP TR 38.811 and 38.821 standards, incorporating non-full-buffer traffic models to evaluate three key metrics: UE throughput, RB allocation ratio, and packet delay.

Result: Throughput results define practical operating boundaries; RB allocation analysis reveals resource consumption patterns with varying UE density; delay analysis characterizes latency behavior crucial for satellite feasibility.

Conclusion: Non-full-buffer modeling provides a more realistic performance outlook for LEO satellite communications and offers insights into user experience under practical conditions.

Abstract: As low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite communications take shape on a global scale, system-level evaluation has become essential for a rigorous understanding of their performance characteristics. Most existing studies have relied on a full-buffer assumption, which obscures the heterogeneity and intermittency that characterize actual user equipment (UE) traffic. This article evaluates the performance of LEO satellite communications under realistic service conditions through the incorporation of non-full-buffer traffic models. To this end, we develop a system-level simulator that complies with the channel modeling and evaluation methodologies specified in 3rd Generation Partnership Project (3GPP) technical reports 38.811 and 38.821. Three key system-level performance metrics are considered: UE throughput, resource block (RB) allocation ratio, and packet delay, which are evaluated across 3GPP LEO satellite study cases under diverse traffic models. The throughput results characterize the distribution of achievable data rates, which delineates the practical operating boundaries of the system. The RB allocation analysis reveals patterns of resource consumption as UE density varies, which provides a quantitative basis for assessing system capability. Furthermore, the delay analysis characterizes latency behavior, which is of particular importance in satellite environments where substantial propagation delays are inherent and must be examined to ensure service feasibility. These evaluations provide a realistic performance outlook for non-full-buffer LEO satellite communications and provide insight into the user experience under practical operating conditions.

</details>


### [426] [Self-Interference-Aware AS-Assisted Tri-Hybrid Beamforming for Full-Duplex Massive MIMO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18036)
*Yuanzhe Gong,Cheng-Jie Zhao,Tho Le-Ngoc*

Main category: eess.SP

TL;DR: This paper proposes a tri-hybrid beamforming (tri-HBF) scheme with antenna-selection-based reconfigurable sub-arrays for full-duplex massive MIMO systems, achieving up to 45.1% sum-rate improvement and 63 dB beam-level isolation via joint optimization of antenna subsets, analog/digital beamformers, and power allocation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the challenges of self-interference (SI), multi-user interference, and desired-signal enhancement in full-duplex massive MIMO systems through joint optimization of antenna selection and beamforming.

Method: Proposes a tri-HBF scheme with group-wise AS-based reconfigurable sub-arrays, optimized via alternating optimization (AO) that jointly optimizes: i) SI-aware antenna subset selection, ii) analog beamformers via projected gradient ascent (PGA), iii) digital precoders/combiners via SI-aware RZF and MMSE, and iv) DL/UL power allocation via successive convex approximation (SCA). Experimental SI channels from an 8x8 Tx-8x8 Rx FD array prototype are used.

Result: Selective activation outperforms full-array with 21.3% higher average sum-rate; compared to baselines, achieves 45.1% sum-rate improvement, with DL and UL rate gains of 36.9% and 82.9% respectively; beam-level isolation better than 63 dB is achieved.

Conclusion: The proposed AS-aided tri-HBF optimization scheme exhibits robust convergence, effectively balances desired-signal enhancement, SI mitigation, and multi-user interference suppression, and provides power-efficiency benefits by reducing active paths.

Abstract: This paper proposes a tri-hybrid beamforming (tri-HBF) scheme with antenna-selection (AS)-based reconfigurable sub-arrays for full-duplex (FD) massive multiple-input multiple-output (mMIMO) systems. A sub-connected HBF architecture is adopted, where AS is performed in a group-wise manner to avoid excessive switch-network and routing complexity. An alternating optimization (AO) algorithm is developed to jointly optimize the i) active antenna subsets considering a self-interference (SI)-aware utility, ii) analog beamformers through projected gradient ascent (PGA), iii) digital precoders/combiners via SI-aware regularized zero-forcing (RZF) and minimum mean-square error (MMSE) updates, and iv) DL/UL power allocation by successive convex approximation (SCA).
