To capture realistic electromagnetic coupling in FD mMIMO operation, experimental SI channels based on an 8x8 Tx-8x8 Rx FD array prototype are incorporated into the study. The proposed AS-aided tri-HBF optimization scheme exhibits robust convergence across various base station configurations and effectively balances desired-signal enhancement, SI mitigation, and multi-user interference suppression in FD mMIMO operation. Illustrative results show that selective activation can outperform full-array activation, achieving a 21.3% higher average sum-rate and a more consistent performance across user realizations, with power-efficiency benefits by reducing the active paths. A comprehensive study is conducted to characterize how the number of activated antennas affects the achievable rate, user-channel coherence, and SI suppression gain. Compared with various selection baselines, it achieves a 45.1% improvement in average sum-rate, with average DL and UL rate gains of 36.9% and 82.9%, respectively. In addition, beam-level isolation better than 63 dB is achieved, further confirming the effectiveness of the proposed SI-aware design.

</details>


<div id='cs.RO'></div>

# cs.RO [[Back]](#toc)

### [427] [HyperDCM: Dynamic Cluster Memory Replay in Hyperbolic Space for Continual Robotic Navigation Across Scenes](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16267)
*Zhengfei Lu,Jian Yang,Muyu Wang,Shaowen Chen,Jinpeng Mi,Ke Li,Xiong You,Qi Wu,Xuan Tang,Xian Wei*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: HyperDCM is a hyperbolic memory mechanism with scene graph modeling and dynamic clustering that mitigates catastrophic forgetting in continual visual navigation, outperforming baselines on multi-scene datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Continual learning in visual navigation suffers from catastrophic forgetting and difficulty adapting to diverse, evolving environments.

Method: HyperDCM extracts semantic triples via LVLMs, encodes them with R-GCN, projects into hyperbolic space for structural separability, and uses dynamic clustering for representative memory replay.

Result: Superior retention of past navigation capabilities and improved generalization on multi-scene indoor/outdoor datasets.

Conclusion: HyperDCM effectively preserves knowledge diversity and mitigates catastrophic forgetting for continual navigation.

Abstract: Continual learning in visual navigation remains challenging due to catastrophic forgetting and the difficulties associated with adapting to diverse and evolving environments. To address these issues, we propose Hyperbolic Dynamic Cluster Memory (HyperDCM), a structure-aware memory mechanism that enhances diffusion policy-based navigation through scene graph modeling and principled memory replay. HyperDCM extracts semantic scene triples from RGB observations using large vision-language models, encodes them into scene graph embeddings via a Relational Graph Convolutional Network (R-GCN), and projects the embeddings into hyperbolic space to enhance structural separability and retention in continual navigation. A dynamic clustering and structure-sensitive update strategy selects representative samples for memory replay, thereby preserving knowledge diversity and mitigating catastrophic forgetting. Experiments on multi-scene indoor and outdoor datasets demonstrate that HyperDCM achieves superior retention of past navigation capabilities and improved generalization compared to representative continual learning baselines adapted to diffusion policy navigation.

</details>


### [428] [Control Design for a Rideable Animatronic Two-Wheeled Robot with Quadruped Form](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16302)
*Tadashi Sumioka,Kazushi Akimoto,Takuya Tsujimura,Sho Takayanagi,Yuya Horiuchi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A rideable two-wheeled robot with four limbs is developed as a future mobility partner, using a self-balancing base for primary locomotion while limbs provide auxiliary support and expressive movements, with focus on robust self-balancing control and natural quadruped-like motion.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To rekindle interest in motorcycles among younger generations by creating a character-inspired, rideable two-wheeled robot that offers an engaging and dynamic riding experience.

Method: The robot uses a self-balancing two-wheeled base for primary locomotion, with limbs providing auxiliary support during mounting/dismounting and synchronized movements. The focus includes robust self-balancing control during rapid limb motions and motion control for natural quadruped-like gait.

Result: Achieved an animatronic robot capable of intuitive weight-shift-based control for free and natural movement while maintaining character fidelity in weight, size, and expressiveness.

Conclusion: The hybrid approach of wheeled base locomotion with coordinated limb movements enables expressive, safe, and natural motion suitable for public events and rider interaction.

Abstract: In recent years, motorcycle popularity has been declining, particularly among the younger generations. To rekindle interest in motorcycles among this demographic, we developed a rideable two-wheeled robot equipped with four limbs as a future partner mobility concept and intended for use in public events. The character reproduced by this robot carries the protagonist on its back and exhibits a dynamic quadrupedal gait. To maximize the riding experience, we aimed to match the robot's weight and size to the character's specifications while ensuring rider safety and enabling expressive movements of limbs, wrists, ankles, and facial features. However, achieving locomotion solely through limb movement would require excessive motor output and increased limb strength, resulting in higher weight and extremely slow gait, thereby reducing character fidelity. To overcome these challenges, the robot performs its primary locomotion using a self-balancing two-wheeled base, while the limbs provide auxiliary support during mounting and dismounting and move in coordination with the wheeled locomotion speed. This approach enables an animatronic robot capable of intuitive, weight-shift-based control for free and natural movement. In this paper, we focus on a robust selfbalancing control method that maintains stability even during rapid limb movements, as well as a motion control strategy that generates natural quadruped-like behavior.

</details>


### [429] [Real-Time sEMG-Based Telecontrol of an Assistive Robotic Arm Using a 1D Convolutional Neural Network](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16310)
*Edgar Manacorda,Mena Samir Kama Abouseffien,Olivier Lecompte,Amandine Gesta,Abolfazl Mohebbi*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a real-time sEMG-based interface using a 1D CNN to teleoperate an assistive robotic arm, achieving over 90% test accuracy and ~0.32s latency in both simulation and real-world settings.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motor impairments affect upper limb function and autonomy. Assistive robotic arms require intuitive, reliable, and responsive control interfaces. sEMG offers non-invasive access to muscle activity and motor intentions.

Method: Four-channel sEMG acquisition → signal preprocessing → sliding window segmentation → 1D CNN classification. Investigates threshold-based onset detection, two-stage classification (rest vs movement → gesture), and single classifier for rest+5 gestures.

Result: CNN test accuracy >90%, strong generalization, average latency ~0.32s, stable real-time behavior, coherent/smooth robot movements in simulated and real environments.

Conclusion: sEMG-based telecontrol for assistive robotics is feasible. A unified real-time framework integrating signal processing, deep learning, and control strategies is key. Future work may explore hybrid control with additional sensing modalities.

Abstract: Motor impairments affecting the upper limb significantly reduce autonomy in daily activities, particularly for tasks involving object manipulation. Assistive robotic arms offer a promising solution, provided they can be controlled in an intuitive, reliable, and responsive manner. Among human--machine interface approaches, surface electromyography (sEMG) enables non-invasive access to muscle activity and thus to the user's motor intentions. This work proposes a real-time sEMG-based interface for the teleoperation of an assistive robotic arm. The system relies on four-channel sEMG acquisition, signal preprocessing, segmentation into sliding windows, and classification using a one-dimensional convolutional neural network (CNN). Several real-time strategies are investigated, including threshold-based onset detection, a two-stage classification approach (rest vs movement followed by gesture recognition), and a single classifier handling both rest and five gestures. The complete pipeline is implemented and evaluated both in simulation and on a real robotic platform. The CNN-based approach achieves high classification performance, with a test accuracy above 90\% and strong generalization on experimentally acquired signals. The system exhibits stable real-time behavior, with an average latency of approximately 0.32 s consistent with the chosen windowing strategy, and the robot can be controlled reliably using discrete gestures, producing coherent and smooth movements in both simulated and real environments. These findings demonstrate the feasibility of sEMG-based telecontrol for assistive robotics and highlight the importance of integrating signal processing, deep learning, and control strategies within a unified real-time framework. Future work may explore hybrid control approaches combining sEMG with additional sensing modalities to further improve robustness and usability.

</details>


### [430] [S.E.A.G.R: A Socially and Emotionally Aware Greeting Robot Framework with Dual-Layer Cultural and Affective Modulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16341)
*Sajjad Hussain,Ranveer Bhura,Amandip Dutta,Shwetangshu Biswas*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SEAGR is a robotic greeting framework that adapts greetings based on cultural background and emotional state, using a dual-layer modulation system combining cultural mapping and affective gesture modulation, implemented as a low-cost prototype with future user studies planned.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing greeting robots use static routines that ignore cultural variation, emotional context, and interpersonal distance, leading to socially inappropriate interactions in public spaces.

Method: A dual-layer modulation framework where cultural identity determines greeting type and affective cues modulate execution, integrated via a Sense-Think-Act architecture with context-aware cultural mapping, emotion-based gesture modulation, and proxemic regulation.

Result: A low-cost proof-of-concept prototype using USB camera, ultrasonic sensor, Arduino servos, and Python processing; no empirical user study results are provided.

Conclusion: The system demonstrates a feasible design for culturally and emotionally adaptive greetings, but requires future user studies to validate its effectiveness empirically.

Abstract: This paper presents SEAGR (Socially and Emotionally Aware Greeting Robot), a robotic greeting framework designed for human-robot interaction environments involving users from diverse cultural backgrounds and different emotional states. Since greeting behaviour strongly influences first impressions, user comfort, and trust, robots operating in public spaces must be able to interact in a socially appropriate and adaptive manner. However, many existing systems still rely on static greeting routines that do not account for cultural variation, emotional context, or interpersonal distance. SEAGR introduces a dual-layer modulation framework in which cultural identity determines the appropriate greeting type, while affective cues influence how that greeting is executed. The system combines context-aware cultural mapping, emotion-based gesture modulation, and proxemic regulation within a unified Sense-Think-Act architecture. A low-cost prototype is implemented using a USB camera, ultrasonic sensor, Arduino-controlled servos, and a laptop-based Python processing system. This work is presented as a system design and proof-of-concept; empirical validation through user studies is explicitly acknowledged as a current limitation and is identified as the primary direction for future work.

</details>


### [431] [PRISM: Multimodal Terrain Mapping for Rover Navigation in Unstructured Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16366)
*Raul Castilla-Arquillo,Carlos Perez-del-Pulgar,Levin Gerdes,Alfonso Garcia-Cerezo,Miguel A. Olivares-Mendez*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PRISM is a multimodal perception system using RGB-D-T (thermal) imagery and a vision transformer (OmniUnet) for semantic terrain segmentation and traversability mapping in unstructured environments, validated on two new datasets and deployed on embedded hardware for autonomous rover navigation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robotic navigation in unstructured environments requires robust situational awareness to safely traverse hazards like steep slopes and rocky terrain, motivating the need for improved multimodal perception and terrain mapping.

Method: PRISM uses a custom sensor suite capturing aligned RGB, depth, and thermal (RGB-D-T) imagery, with OmniUnet—a vision transformer-based network for multimodal semantic terrain segmentation—deployed on a resource-constrained embedded computer.

Result: Validated on two newly annotated datasets (BASEPROD and LAENTIEC) and through physical field experiments, demonstrating efficient traversability map generation for autonomous navigation.

Conclusion: Integrating thermal imagery with optical and depth sensors improves terrain mapping reliability; PRISM enables autonomous rover navigation via its GNC subsystem in unstructured environments.

Abstract: Robotic navigation in unstructured environments requires robust situational awareness to safely traverse hazards such as steep slopes and rocky terrain. To address this challenge, perception systems increasingly rely on multimodal sensor fusion. Specifically, integrating thermal imagery with standard optical and depth sensors enhances terrain differentiation, directly improving the reliability of mapping algorithms. This paper presents PRISM, a multimodal perception system for terrain mapping in unstructured settings. PRISM leverages a custom sensor suite to capture aligned RGB, depth, and thermal (RGB-D-T) imagery. At its core is OmniUnet, a novel vision transformer-based network specifically designed for multimodal semantic terrain segmentation. We validated the proposed system using two newly annotated datasets (BASEPROD and LAENTIEC) and demonstrate its real-world applicability through physical field experiments. Deployed on a resource-constrained embedded computer, PRISM efficiently generates traversability maps that directly enable autonomous navigation via a rover's Guidance, Navigation, and Control (GNC) subsystem.

</details>


### [432] [Articulated Humanoid Head for a Robot Receptionist Capable of Natural Human Interaction](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17042)
*Tharusha Fonseka,Charuka Bandara,Moshintha Hewavitharana,Melisa Arukgoda,Wageesha N. Manamperi,Udaya S. K. Perera Miriya Thanthrige,Peshala Jayasekara*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an affordable, articulated humanoid robot head with 21 DoF, realistic silicone skin, and integrated AI (face recognition via SCRFD/ArcFace/ByTetrack, NLP via Llama/Whisper) designed for receptionist roles, achieving a 4.13/5 human-likeness score.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Current humanoid heads are limited by high costs, mechanical complexity, and poor adaptability across environments, requiring a more practical solution for natural human-robot interaction.

Method: A 21-DoF mechanical head with silicone skin was built, integrating SCRFD, ArcFace, and ByTetrack for face recognition/re-identification, and Llama/Whisper for conversational NLP, all running in real-time on hardware.

Result: Quantitative tests measured conversational ability and re-identification performance. A user study yielded an average human-likeness score of 4.13 out of 5.

Conclusion: The proposed humanoid head successfully balances expressiveness, real-time AI capability, and cost-effectiveness for practical receptionist deployment.

Abstract: Humanoid robots have become increasingly popular in applications such as social interaction, education, and service roles, which drives the need for more natural and efficient human-robot interactions. However, currently available humanoid heads often face limitations, including high costs, mechanical complexity, and limited adaptability across diverse environments. To address these challenges, we present an articulated humanoid robot head designed for a receptionist role, integrating a mechanical structure with 21 degrees of freedom (DoF), including mechanisms for the mouth, eyes, eyebrows, and neck, and covered with realistic silicone skin to achieve a human-like appearance and expression. The system integrates a model-based architecture that combines SCRFD, ArcFace, and ByTetrack for face recognition and Llama and Whisper for natural language processing, with hardware support enabling real-time operations and human re-identification. The conversational ability and re-identification capabilities of the humanoid robot head were quantitatively measured, while its emotional expressiveness and human likeness were evaluated through a user study, achieving an average human likeness score of 4.13 out of 5.

</details>


### [433] [Back to the museum: Investigation of the acceptance of Android Andrea with and without emotion simulation in a museum](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16428)
*Marcel Heisler,Christian Becker-Asano*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: An android robot named Andrea was deployed at a German museum for six days to chat autonomously with visitors in multiple languages. Three emotion conditions (no emotions, ChatGPT-driven, WASABI architecture) were tested with 73 visitors. Statistical analysis found no positive effects or conscious detection of the two emotion simulation approaches.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To build on previous qualitative results by equipping an android robot with multi-lingual conversational ability in a real museum setting, and to experimentally test whether adding emotion simulation (via ChatGPT or WASABI architecture) improves visitor experience and perceptions.

Method: The android robot Andrea was deployed at a German museum for six days. Three conditions were implemented: no emotions, ChatGPT 4.1-driven emotions, and WASABI emotion simulation. 73 visitors completed an extended TAM2 questionnaire after interacting with the robot.

Result: Statistical analysis showed that the two emotion simulation approaches (ChatGPT and WASABI) had no significant positive effects on visitor subjective evaluations, and visitors could not consciously detect the presence of simulated emotions.

Conclusion: Implementing explicit emotion simulation (via ChatGPT or WASABI) into the android robot's chat architecture did not improve visitor perceptions compared to a no-emotion baseline in this real-world deployment.

Abstract: For a second time, the android robot Andrea was set up at a public museum in Germany for six consecutive days to have conversations with visitors, fully autonomously. Building on previously gathered qualitative results, the robot was now capable of engaging in multi-lingual conversation with the visitors about the museum context. The robot was prepared with context information about the museum in general and its surrounding exhibits this time. The robot featured a slightly artificial sounding voice that was previously evaluated as congruent with its gender-ambiguous but very humanlike design. Three experimental conditions were implemented, in which either (1) the robot simulated no emotions, (2) the robots emotions were determined by ChatGPT 4.1, or (3) the WASABI emotion simulation architecture simulated the robot's emotion dynamics. An extended version of the TAM2 questionnaire was employed to let 73 visitors report on several factors of their opinion about the android robot Andrea after having experienced it. In result, the statistical analysis suggests that these first two approaches to implementing emotions into the chat architecture of our android robot Andrea did not yield any positive effects on the subjective evaluations by the visitors and were not detectable on a conscious level.

</details>


### [434] [Linear Stability Analysis of an INDI Pitch-Rate Controller under Model Mismatch for a Tilt-Rotor VTOL UAV](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16471)
*Lorenzo Schenk,Guillaume Ducard,Christopher Onder*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper analytically characterizes stability regions for INDI-based pitch-rate control in tilt-rotor VTOL UAVs under model mismatch, and proposes robustness-oriented and performance-oriented tuning procedures based on derived feasibility maps.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing INDI controllers for tilt-rotor VTOL UAVs lack an explicit analytical characterization of the admissible model-mismatch range in the fast inner loop, which is essential for safe and reliable flight control.

Method: Isolates the pitch-rate/elevon subchannel of a cascaded INDI controller; derives a closed-form fifth-order transfer function for the full controller-estimator-actuator-plant loop; applies Routh-Hurwitz criterion to assess linear stability; performs three-parameter sweeps to generate stability maps; proposes two tuning procedures (robustness-oriented & performance-oriented) using gain/phase margins and closed-loop bandwidth.

Result: Actuator lag and inertia mismatch are relatively benign at nominal gain; control-effectiveness mismatch (especially sign errors in allocation) is the most dangerous destabilizing factor. Stability regions are interpretable and lead to concrete tuning recommendations.

Conclusion: The work provides practical, analytically grounded tuning guidelines for INDI-based tilt-rotor VTOL controllers, distinguishing between conservative (robustness-oriented) and aggressive (performance-oriented) operating conditions.

Abstract: Incremental Nonlinear Dynamic Inversion (INDI) is attractive for unmanned aerial vehicle (UAV) flight control because it reduces dependence on a full aerodynamic model while retaining strong disturbance-rejection capability. For a tilt-rotor vertical takeoff and landing (VTOL) architecture, however, the admissible model-mismatch range of the fast inner loop is still not characterized analytically in a parameter-explicit way. This paper isolates the pitch-rate/elevon subchannel of an existing cascaded INDI controller and studies its linear stability under model mismatch. A closed-form fifth-order transfer function is derived for the full controller-estimator-actuator-plant interconnection, and stability is characterized through the Routh-Hurwitz criterion over a parameterized linear model. Two representative three-parameter sweeps produce interpretable stability regions. Based on these feasibility maps, two uncertainty-aware tuning procedures are proposed: a robustness-oriented design that maximizes a weighted worst-case combination of gain margin and phase margin, and a performance-oriented design that maximizes worst-case closed-loop bandwidth subject to margin constraints. The results show that actuator lag and inertia mismatch are comparatively benign at nominal gain, whereas control-effectiveness mismatch, particularly a sign error in the allocation, is the most dangerous destabilizing factor, leading to concrete tuning recommendations for conservative and aggressive operating conditions.

</details>


### [435] [Certifiable Safe Model-Based Reinforcement Learning with Control-Affine Dynamics Approximation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16501)
*Hao Zhou,Yanze Zhang,Cameron Reid,Wenhao Luo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a safe RL framework that learns control-affine dynamics using ARFF and quantifies uncertainty via adaptive conformal prediction, enabling certifiable safe policies with control barrier functions for robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing safe model-based RL methods often ignore the structure of residual model uncertainties (e.g., control-affine components), leading to overly conservative behavior or invalid safety guarantees.

Method: 1) Uses Control-Affine Random Fourier Features (ARFF) to model robot dynamics in control-affine form. 2) Applies adaptive conformal prediction (ACP) for model-free uncertainty quantification. 3) Integrates with control barrier functions (CBF) for safety-certified policy synthesis.

Result: Simulation results on cartpole and 3D quadrotor platforms demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

Conclusion: The proposed method reduces conservatism and provides valid safety guarantees by explicitly modeling control-affine residual uncertainties and quantifying them via ACP.

Abstract: Safe model-based reinforcement learning (RL) often bridges control-theoretic analysis and RL for robots to safely explore (partially) unknown system dynamics while deriving control actions for task efficiency. The control performance and safety assurance typically rely on prior knowledge of partially modeled nominal system dynamics and the data-driven models that compensate for residual model uncertainties. However, existing methods often overlook the structure of residual model uncertainties (e.g., components affine in control), which could lead to overly conservative robot behaviors or invalid safety guarantees under the safe learning-based controllers. This paper proposes a safe reinforcement learning framework that learns control-affine dynamics with a certifiable data-driven safe policy using control barrier functions (CBF). Specifically, we first use Control-Affine Random Fourier Features (ARFF) to model robot dynamics in a control-affine form, which offers computational efficiency that scales with dataset size and reduces potential model bias for model-based reinforcement learning. Then, a model-free, efficient uncertainty quantification method using adaptive conformal prediction (ACP) is applied to quantify the uncertainty in the safety constraint arising from the learned control-affine dynamics. This allows for data-driven safety assurance amenable to principled and efficient controller synthesis with CBF. Simulation results on the cartpole and the 3D quadrotor platforms demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed framework.

</details>


### [436] [Foresight Residual RL for Long-Horizon Robot Manipulation with Vision-Language-Action Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16506)
*Yuhan Liu,Xinyu Zhang,Litao Liu,Abdeslam Boularias*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes Foresight Residual RL that augments sparse success rewards with offline-estimated foresight values to improve handoff quality between subtasks in VLA-based assembly, achieving 85.6% success vs 54.5% baseline.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard residual RL over frozen VLA policies fails in long-horizon assembly because it optimizes each subtask in isolation without controlling terminal state quality, leading to brittle downstream transitions.

Method: (i) Train a visual foresight predictor from terminal-state images labeled by downstream rollout statistics; (ii) Train residual policies via backward foresight induction, using foresight score as a reward multiplier on top of sparse success rewards.

Result: 85.6% full-task success on wrench-based nut-tightening in Isaac Gym, vs 54.5% for standard subtask residual RL and lower VLA baselines, while per-subtask success remains unchanged.

Conclusion: Improving long-horizon performance requires shaping *which* successful states are produced at each subtask, not merely ensuring success occurs.

Abstract: Vision-Language-Action (VLA) policies offer strong general-purpose manipulation priors, but often fail on tight-tolerance, contact-rich assembly due to long-horizon credit assignment and subtask coupling: a state that is geometrically successful for the current skill can be brittle for downstream skills. We show this failure mode in residual reinforcement learning (RL) over a frozen VLA base policy: constant sparse success rewards improve each subtask in isolation yet yield little or no gain when skills are chained, because terminal state quality is uncontrolled. We propose Foresight Residual RL, which optimizes handoff quality by augmenting each subtask's sparse success reward with an offline-estimated foresight value -- the probability of future subtask success conditioned on the terminal state of the current subtask. Concretely, we (i) train a visual foresight predictor from images of terminal states of the base policy, labeled using downstream rollout statistics, and (ii) train residual policies via backward foresight induction, using the predictor output as a reward multiplier. On a three-phase wrench-based nut-tightening assembly task in Isaac Gym (grasp, move-insert, rotate), our method achieves 85.6% full-task success, outperforming standard subtask residual RL (54.5%) and VLA baselines, while leaving per-subtask success unchanged. These results highlight that improving long-horizon performance requires shaping which successful states are produced at each sub-task, not only whether success occurs.

</details>


### [437] [Differentiable Reinforcement Learning for Path Tracking by an Agile Fish-Like Robot](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16508)
*Prashanth Chivkula,Kartik Loya,Venkata Ravindhra Reddy Varikuti,Phanindra Tallapragada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper introduces a simulation platform for fish-like robots that enables PID gain learning via backpropagation through time and curriculum training, successfully transferring policies from simulation to a physical robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fish-like robot control is challenging due to poorly modeled fluid-structure interaction and nonlinear underactuated dynamics, and RL progress has been hindered by lack of suitable simulation environments.

Method: Developed a computationally efficient simulation platform approximating fish-like robot motion; used PID control with gains learned via backpropagation through time and curriculum training.

Result: The policy learned in simulation was successfully applied to the physical platform, showing excellent match between simulated and real-world performance.

Conclusion: The proposed simulation-to-real approach effectively addresses control challenges for fish-like robots, enabling practical deployment of learned policies.

Abstract: Fish-like swimming has inspired the design of several dozens if not hundreds of bioinspired robots in the last few decades. But the control and motion planning of such robots has been challenging due to the poorly modeled fluid-structure interaction and the nonlinear underactuated dynamics of such robots. While reinforcement learning has allowed significant advances in the context of ground and aerial robots, the lack of a suitable simulation environment with appropriate computational speed and accuracy have prevented similar progress for fish-like robots. We address this two-fold problem by developing a simulation platform that approximates the motion of our fish-like robot with computational efficiency. Then the motion control and path tracking by the robot is performed using PID control where the (variable) gains are learned using back propagation through time and training on a curriculum. The policy learned in the simulation is then applied on the physical platform, demonstrating an excellent match.

</details>


### [438] [Autonomous VR-Based Risk Detection for Situational Awareness in Dangerous Settings](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16582)
*Mohammad Eskandari,Murali Krishna Varma Indukuri,Stephanie M. Lukin,Cynthia Matuszek*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A VR-based HRI framework using VLM-assisted robots to identify and annotate hazards, showing improved situational awareness and user preference over unannotated baselines in simulated hazardous environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Situational awareness in high-risk environments (e.g., disaster response) requires not only detecting hazards but also clearly communicating them to humans. VLMs are promising for scene understanding, but their role in human-facing robotic systems is underexplored.

Method: A VR-based HRI framework where a robot explores a virtual scene, uses a VLM to identify hazards and annotate points of interest, and presents these annotations to a human operator through an immersive VR interface.

Result: Participants preferred the annotated VR interface over the unannotated baseline; reported high clarity, usefulness, and comfort when interacting with the system.

Conclusion: Combining VLM-based robotic perception with immersive VR visualization is a promising approach for supporting situational awareness in hazardous settings.

Abstract: In high-risk environments such as disaster response, situational awareness depends not only on detecting hazards but also on communicating them clearly to human operators. Vision Language Models (VLMs) have shown strong potential for scene understanding in safety-critical settings, yet their value as part of human-facing robotic systems remains underexplored. We present a VR-based Human Robot Interaction framework for studying how VLM-assisted robots can support situational awareness in simulated hazardous environments. In our system, a robot explores a virtual scene and queries a VLM to identify potential hazards and annotate user-facing points of interest. These annotations are presented to a human operator through an immersive VR interface. This framework enables controlled evaluation of both robotic hazard identification and the communication of safety-critical information to users. Results from our study indicate that the annotated VR interface was preferred over the unannotated baseline and that participants reported high clarity, usefulness, and comfort when interacting with the system. These findings suggest that combining VLM-based robotic perception with immersive visualization is a promising approach for supporting situational awareness in hazardous settings.

</details>


### [439] [An Indoor Navigation System for the Visually Impaired based on UWB Positioning and D* Lite Path Planning Algorithm](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16614)
*Thanh C. Vo,Dong LT. Tran,Huy HM. Le,Duyen N Ha,Tuan Anh Pham,Hai Thanh Dang,Hoang T. Tran*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A system combining UWB positioning and D*Lite algorithm for indoor navigation assistance for visually impaired people, achieving reliable low-latency performance in dynamic indoor environments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To provide indoor navigation assistance for visually impaired individuals where GPS is unavailable, enabling safe and independent mobility in complex indoor spaces.

Method: UWB sensors for precise indoor localization in GPS-denied environments, integrated with the D*Lite path planning algorithm for dynamic obstacle avoidance and rapid route re-planning.

Result: The system operates reliably with low latency, providing safety and flexibility for users in complex indoor spaces.

Conclusion: The proposed UWB-based navigation system with D*Lite algorithm is effective for assisting visually impaired users indoors, offering reliable performance and dynamic adaptability.

Abstract: This paper proposes an indoor navigation system for the visually impaired, leveraging Ultra-Wideband (UWB) positioning technology and the D*Lite path planning algorithm. The system utilizes UWB sensors to provide precision localization in GPS-denied environments. The D* Lite algorithm is integrated to optimize travel trajectories and ensure rapid route re-planning in the presence of dynamic obstacles. Experimental results demonstrate that the system operates reliably with low latency, providing safety and flexibility for users in complex indoor spaces.

</details>


### [440] [SAGE: A Socially-Aware Generative Engine for Heterogeneous Multi-Agent Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16619)
*Lan Hu,Minghui Liwang,Wenbo Zhu,Xinlei Yi,Yiguang Hong,Xianbin Wang,Zhenzhen Jiao,Seyyedali Hosseinalipour*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SAGE is a socially-aware generative framework for heterogeneous multi-agent navigation that uses Heterogeneous Graph Transformer and diffusion-based modeling to jointly predict trajectories and plan robot paths, with a training-free safety-social energy guidance mechanism that improves collision avoidance and social compliance across real and synthetic datasets.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing trajectory prediction and planning methods rely on homogeneous interaction assumptions or geometric collision constraints only, failing to model asymmetric interactions, coupled prediction-planning, and soft social norms in heterogeneous human-robot environments.

Method: SAGE uses a directed heterogeneous graph with Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) to encode type-specific asymmetric interactions, a diffusion-based generative module for joint trajectory prediction and planning, and a training-free safety-social energy guidance mechanism with differentiable collision, kinematic, task-progress, and role-conditioned social-compliance terms.

Result: SAGE improves safety and social compliance while maintaining task performance on ETH/UCY, SDD, and synthetic datasets. The guidance mechanism reduces collision and social-violation rates, scales to 20+ robots, and enables explicit safety-accuracy-task trade-off control without retraining.

Conclusion: SAGE is a scalable and effective framework for socially-aware multi-agent navigation in complex heterogeneous environments.

Abstract: Safe and socially compliant navigation in open human-robot environments requires robots to reason about heterogeneous participants with different dynamics, autonomy levels, and social roles. Existing trajectory prediction and planning methods often rely on homogeneous interaction assumptions or enforce only geometric collision constraints, making it difficult to jointly model asymmetric interactions, coupled prediction-planning, and soft social norms. This paper proposes SAGE, a socially-aware generative engine for heterogeneous multi-agent navigation. SAGE represents robots and surrounding entities as a directed heterogeneous graph and employs a Heterogeneous Graph Transformer (HGT) to encode type-specific asymmetric interactions. Conditioned on the resulting context, a diffusion-based generative module jointly models future entity trajectories and robot trajectory plans. During inference, a training-free safety-social energy guidance mechanism refines sampled robot trajectories using differentiable collision, kinematic, task-progress, and role-conditioned social-compliance terms. Extensive experiments on real-world (ETH/UCY and SDD) and synthetic datasets verify the effectiveness of SAGE in improving safety and social compliance while maintaining task performance. The proposed guidance mechanism consistently reduces collision and social-violation rates, scales to teams of up to 20 robots, and enables explicit control of the safety-accuracy-task trade-off without retraining. These findings demonstrate the potential of SAGE as a scalable framework for socially-aware multi-agent navigation in complex environments.

</details>


### [441] [AI-Augmented Model Predictive Control for Safe and Adaptive Rendezvous and Proximity Operations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16630)
*Luca Sportelli,Tyler Barr,Cagri Kilic,Di Wu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an adaptive MPC framework for autonomous spacecraft rendezvous in adversarial multi-agent environments, combining CW dynamics-based constrained optimization with a data-driven supervisory tuning layer. Evaluated in KSPDG simulations, it outperforms fixed-parameter MPC in robustness and adaptability while ensuring safety and real-time feasibility.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Autonomous RPO in adversarial orbital environments needs guidance that balances pursuit, safety, and real-time adaptability. Learning-based approaches are limited by interpretability, robustness, and constraint awareness concerns in safety-critical orbital robotics.

Method: An adaptive MPC framework combining: (1) constrained receding-horizon MPC with CW dynamics for efficient prediction, (2) actuator limits, keep-out-zone constraints, slack variables, and optional CBF safety filtering, (3) a data-driven supervisory tuning layer that adjusts interpretable MPC parameters (tracking weights, safety penalties, etc.) from offline evaluation and online interaction geometry.

Result: Evaluated in KSPDG Capture-the-Satellite environment via Monte Carlo simulations. Adaptive MPC shows improved closed-loop robustness, adaptive maneuvering, and rendezvous performance over fixed-parameter MPC while maintaining safety and real-time feasibility.

Conclusion: The adaptive MPC framework provides a modular, interpretable foundation for autonomous spacecraft RPO in adversarial settings, balancing performance and safety through parameter adaptation rather than direct thrust command learning.

Abstract: Autonomous rendezvous and proximity operations (RPO) in adversarial orbital environments require guidance architectures balancing target pursuit, safety preservation, and real-time adaptability under dynamically evolving interaction conditions. Although learning-based approaches show promise, their application to safety-critical orbital robotics remains limited by concerns regarding interpretability, robustness, and constraint awareness. This work presents an adaptive Model Predictive Control (MPC) framework for autonomous spacecraft RPO in multi-agent adversarial scenarios. The proposed architecture combines a constrained receding-horizon MPC formulation with a data-driven supervisory tuning layer that adjusts controller parameters from offline closed-loop evaluation and online interaction geometry. Relative motion follows Clohessy-Wiltshire (CW) dynamics, enabling computationally efficient finite-horizon prediction and real-time quadratic optimization. The MPC formulation incorporates actuator limits, predictive keep-out-zone constraints, slack-variable feasibility handling, and optional Control Barrier Function (CBF) safety filtering. Rather than generating thrust commands directly, the adaptive layer modifies interpretable MPC parameters, including tracking weights, safety penalties, minimum-separation objectives, and keep-out-zone objectives. The framework was evaluated in the official Kerbal Space Program Differential Game (KSPDG) Capture-the-Satellite environment through Monte Carlo simulations. Results demonstrate improved closed-loop robustness, adaptive maneuvering behavior, and rendezvous performance compared with fixed-parameter MPC while preserving safety-aware operation and real-time feasibility, providing a modular, interpretable foundation for adaptive spacecraft RPO.

</details>


### [442] [PhyAgentOS: A Self-Evolving Operating System for Embodied Agents with Decoupled Cognitive Planning and Physical Execution](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16636)
*Yang Liu,Weixing Chen,Xinshuai Song,Tao Pu,Siwen Mo,Yongjie Bai,Zihao Chen,Qianran Sun,Liruo Zhong,Ying Shen,Liang Lin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: PhyAgentOS is a runtime system that provides scheduling, verification, memory, benchmarking, and safety as system-level services for robotics AI systems, using a session-centric approach with a file-system-based cognition-physics boundary (State-as-a-File protocol) to enable inspectable, versionable records across different robot embodiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-language-action models, world models, and agentic planners lack a common execution abstraction, shared state, semantic verification, and persistent experience across different robot embodiments, making their composition challenging.

Method: PhyAgentOS introduces a Session-Centered Runtime where sessions (not actions) are the minimum unit of scheduling. It uses a State-as-a-File protocol (Markdown with YAML) as the cognition-physics boundary, a SessionVerifier for evidence-grounded verdicts, epistemic memory for knowledge consolidation, layered safety (preflight, SafetyGuard, heartbeat), and progressive validation across games, simulation, and real robots.

Result: Benchmarked on Optimus-67, StarDojo, and DST-Dojo; validated on 19+ simulated and physical embodiments; gains achieved on LIBERO, Calvin, and RoboCasa365 across multiple VLA models.

Conclusion: PhyAgentOS effectively unifies cognitive planning and physical execution across heterogeneous embodiments through a session-based runtime with file-system state representation, evidence-grounded verification, and layered safety, enabling reusable knowledge without retraining.

Abstract: Vision-language-action models, world models, and agentic planners each advance physical intelligence, yet their composition lacks a common execution abstraction, shared state, semantic verification, and persistent experience across heterogeneous embodiments. We present PhyAgentOS, a runtime foundation delivering scheduling, verification, memory, benchmarking, and safety as system-level services. Its Session-Centered Runtime treats a session, not an action, as the minimum unit of scheduling, compatibility preflight, supervised execution, evidence collection, and acceptance. To decouple cognition from physical execution, the cognition-physics boundary is a file system: the State-as-a-File protocol materializes cross-layer state as Markdown with YAML, yielding inspectable, versionable records without code dependencies between Agent and Runtime layers. These views form a unified cognitive state space aligning intent, capabilities, environment, execution, and experience. The SessionVerifier distinguishes execution termination from semantic task completion via evidence-grounded verdicts of success, failure, or replan. Verified outcomes are consolidated through epistemic memory into reusable knowledge and corrective lessons, closing a trial-and-error loop without retraining. Benchmarking reuses the deployment session and verification path, so results trace to real execution. Layered safety constrains both policy-driven and agent-driven execution: preflight, action bridges, SafetyGuard, heartbeat monitoring, and target-local constraints. Validation is progressive: games test cognitive planning, simulation adds dynamics and control, real robots add hardware noise, with the cognitive layer held constant. PhyAgentOS is benchmarked on Optimus-67, StarDojo, and DST-Dojo, validated on 19+ simulated and physical embodiments, and gains on LIBERO, Calvin, and RoboCasa365 across multiple VLA models.

</details>


### [443] [Token-Wise Latent Streaming from Slow Reasoners to Fast Planners for Dynamic Vision Language Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16806)
*Tianshuai Hu,Yangyi Zhong,Zeying Gong,Lingdong Kong,Xiaodong Mei,Guoyang Zhao,Xiaolu Liu,Song Wang,Rong Li,Junwei Liang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SPARK-VLN is a dual-system framework for dynamic social VLN that streams intermediate hidden states from a slow VLM reasoner to a fast flow-matching expert planner during token generation, reducing observation staleness and improving navigation success and social compliance.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Vision-Language Navigation in dynamic human-centric environments suffers from a fundamental tension: linguistic reasoning is slow and deliberative, but safe social planning requires instant reactivity. This results in observation staleness, where decisions made during inference become unsafe by execution time.

Method: SPARK-VLN introduces three modules: (1) a Token-Wise Hidden Streamer that extracts intermediate hidden states during token generation, (2) a Sequence-to-Slot Latent Bridge that projects these into fixed-size latent slots, and (3) an Evolving Latent Conditioner that infuses them into a fast flow-matching expert planner.

Result: SPARK-VLN improves navigation success and social compliance while maintaining inference efficiency, as evaluated on a new human-centric benchmark that tracks staleness statistics, collisions, and compliance.

Conclusion: The key insight is that intermediate VLM hidden states already encode action-relevant intent before full inference completes, enabling fresh, evolving guidance to a fast planner during inference to mitigate staleness.

Abstract: Vision-Language Navigation in dynamic, human-centric environments exposes a fundamental tension: linguistic reasoning is slow and deliberative, whereas safe, socially compliant planning should be instant and reactive. The resulting observation staleness is safety-critical: a maneuver chosen during inference can already be unsafe by the time it executes. We observe that, long before a VLM finishes its inference, its intermediate hidden states already encode action-relevant intent. We propose SPARK-VLN, a dual-system framework for dynamic social VLN that streams the slow VLM reasoner's knowledge to a fast flow-matching expert planner throughout token generation, providing fresh and evolving guidance during inference. This design is realized by three modules: a Token-Wise Hidden Streamer that extracts intermediate hidden states along the token generation process, a Sequence-to-Slot Latent Bridge that projects them into fixed-size latent slots, and an Evolving Latent Conditioner that infuses them into the expert planner. We also introduce a human-centric benchmark suite for dynamic social vision-language navigation that keeps pedestrians and the robot active throughout inference and reports navigation success, social compliance, human collisions, and explicit staleness statistics. Across these settings, SPARK-VLN mproves navigation success and social compliance while sustaining inference efficiency. Webpage: https://hutslib.github.io/SPARK-VLN/.

</details>


### [444] [GLidE-SLAM: GL-Accelerated Indirect-Direct Embedded SLAM](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16897)
*Carlos A. Pinheiro de Sousa,Heiko Hamann,Oliver Deussen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: GLidE-SLAM is a monocular hybrid indirect-direct SLAM framework that uses GPU-accelerated direct tracking (via OpenGL ES 3.1 compute shaders) for high-frame-rate pose estimation on embedded devices, freeing CPU resources for backend mapping tasks. It achieves up to 9× higher frame rates than CPU-only baselines without sacrificing accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Deploying Visual SLAM on resource-constrained embedded devices (robotics, drones, XR) is challenging because tracking must maintain high frame rates while still leaving compute resources for map extension and maintenance.

Method: A hybrid indirect-direct architecture: GPU-accelerated direct tracking on intermediate frames using OpenGL ES 3.1 compute shaders (pose-only estimation via image-alignment, no depth/map-point creation), while the full indirect pipeline handles map extension and global consistency on the CPU.

Result: Up to 9× higher frame rates than the CPU-only baseline on embedded platforms, while maintaining trajectory accuracy and improving practical deployment on commodity resource-constrained hardware.

Conclusion: The proposed architectural separation and GPU-accelerated direct tracker enable efficient, high-rate Visual SLAM on a broader range of embedded platforms without requiring CUDA, advancing real-time deployment in robotics, drones, and XR systems.

Abstract: With the growing demand for robotics, autonomous drones, and wearable extended reality systems, the deployment of Visual SLAM on embedded devices remains challenging. Tracking must sustain high frame rates while preserving compute resources for map extension and maintenance. This paper presents GLidE-SLAM, a monocular hybrid indirect-direct framework that addresses this by architectural separation: the system performs GPU-accelerated direct tracking on intermediate frames, while reserving the full indirect pipeline for map extension and global consistency. We leverage highly parallel image-alignment operations for pose-only estimation without depth optimization or map point creation, making the workload suitable for GPU offloading and freeing CPU resources for backend tasks. We implement the direct tracker using vendor-agnostic OpenGL ES~3.1 compute shaders, enabling deployment across a broader range of commodity embedded platforms without requiring CUDA support. To our knowledge, this is the first complete direct photometric pose estimator realized via compute shaders for embedded-class devices. Experiments on target platforms demonstrate up to 9$\times$ higher frame rates than the CPU-only baseline while maintaining trajectory accuracy and improving practical deployment across commodity resource-constrained hardware.

</details>


### [445] [Approximate Relative Entropy Constraints for Nonlinear Covariance Steering Under Distribution Ambiguity](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16914)
*Trevor N. Wolf,Jay W. McMahon*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: The paper develops a distributionally robust covariance-steering framework using Kullback-Leibler divergence to handle ambiguity between true nonlinear distributions and Gaussian surrogates, enhancing risk-sensitive performance estimation for nonlinear systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Covariance steering works well for linear systems but extends poorly to nonlinear systems because local linearization creates a Gaussian surrogate that differs significantly from the true nonlinear distribution, leading to inaccurate risk-sensitive metrics like collision probability and mean-squared error.

Method: The authors use a variational representation of exponential integrals to derive upper bounds on risk-sensitive quantities over a KLD ambiguity set. They formulate a bound on the KLD rate of change between the true distribution and a Gaussian surrogate, controlled by decision variables in covariance steering. These constraints are embedded into a sequential convex programming algorithm.

Result: The approach is demonstrated on a challenging nonlinear spacecraft transfer between two near-rectilinear halo orbits.

Conclusion: The proposed distributionally robust framework keeps the true distribution close to its Gaussian surrogate while enforcing risk-sensitive performance bounds, improving safety and accuracy in nonlinear stochastic guidance.

Abstract: Covariance steering provides an efficient framework for designing linear stochastic feedback policies, but its extension to nonlinear systems relies on a Gaussian surrogate obtained through local linearization. Because this surrogate may differ substantially from the true nonlinear state distribution, risk-sensitive quantities such as collision probability and mean-squared error may be inaccurately estimated. This work develops a distributionally robust covariance-steering framework based on the relative entropy, also known as the Kullback-Leibler divergence (KLD), to account for ambiguity in the propagated probability density function. Using a variational representation of exponential integrals, we derive computable upper bounds on risk-sensitive quantities over a KLD ambiguity set. We then formulate an upper bound on the time rate of change of the KLD between the true nonlinear distribution and a Gaussian reference surrogate. Under some assumptions, this bound is controlled by decision variables within a covariance-steering formulation. The resulting constraints are incorporated into a sequential convex programming algorithm to design stochastic guidance policies that keep the true distribution close to its Gaussian surrogate while enforcing bounds on risk-sensitive performance measures. The proposed approach is demonstrated on a challenging nonlinear spacecraft transfer between two near-rectilinear halo orbits.

</details>


### [446] [A BIM-enabled, Agent-based Discrete-event Simulation Platform for Robotic Studies: A Method based on Graph Theory](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16920)
*Ping Xu,Xinghua Gao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a BIM-enabled, agent-based simulation platform that converts indoor environments into graph representations for knowledge-driven robot navigation and operation planning, with simulation results showing efficient and collision-free navigation improved by grid refinement.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing navigation approaches (predefined routes or SLAM) provide limited environmental understanding and cannot supply rich building information (location, geometry, material, operational attributes) needed for complex facility management tasks like pipe leak repair, while BIM data remains underutilized in robotics.

Method: Discretizes indoor environments into grid cells mapped to graph nodes classified as target, obstacle, or regular nodes based on spatial relationships with building elements; assigns traversal costs to edges; uses graph-theoretic algorithms for path planning; applies grid refinement to resolve target-obstacle overlap issues.

Result: Simulation results demonstrate efficient and collision-free navigation using the proposed graph representation; grid refinement mitigates the overlap issue between target-occupied and obstacle-occupied cells, improving spatial accuracy and path feasibility.

Conclusion: The platform supports virtual evaluation of robotic operations prior to deployment and provides a foundation for BIM-informed robotic systems in facility management.

Abstract: Indoor robots are increasingly employed for facility management tasks such as cleaning and inspection. These applications primarily rely on navigation and can be effectively supported by predefined routes or perception-driven Simultaneous Localization and Mapping (SLAM) techniques. However, more complex tasks, such as locating and repairing leaking pipes, require not only navigation but also access to building information, including the location, geometry, material, and operational attributes of components. Existing navigation approaches provide only limited environmental understanding and cannot readily supply such information. In contrast, Building Information Modeling (BIM) contains rich geometric, semantic, and operational information that remains largely underutilized in robotic applications. This study proposes a BIM-enabled, agent-based simulation platform for knowledge-driven indoor robot navigation and operation planning. Within the framework, indoor environments are discretized into grid cells that are mapped to graph nodes and classified as target, obstacle, or regular nodes according to their spatial relationships with building elements. Traversal costs are assigned to edges connecting neighboring nodes, enabling graph-theoretic algorithms to compute efficient and collision-free navigation paths while avoiding obstacles. Simulation results demonstrate that the proposed graph representation enables efficient and collision-free navigation. A key limitation associated with coarse discretization, namely overlap between target-occupied and obstacle-occupied cells, is identified and mitigated through grid refinement, improving spatial accuracy and path feasibility. The proposed platform supports virtual evaluation of robotic operations prior to deployment and provides a foundation for BIM-informed robotic systems in facility management.

</details>


### [447] [PREFAIL: Identifying Precursors to Failures in Robotic Lift-and-Place Tasks to Improve Task Execution Performance](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16921)
*Zeyu Shangguan,Rajas Chitale,Rutvik Patel,Satyandra K. Gupta,Daniel Seita*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes PREFAIL, a method for predicting failure precursors in non-prehensile lift-and-place manipulation by analyzing relative motion of objects, and introduces a dataset with precise latest intervention times to enable actionable failure predictions.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Non-prehensile manipulation is failure-prone under high-speed motions, and existing failure prediction approaches are limited by sensitivity to dynamic actions and dependence on known policy structures, with no precise characterization of the latest intervention time.

Method: PREFAIL predicts failure precursors by analyzing relative motion of target objects with respect to the carrier; a new dataset with latest intervention time labels is introduced for rigorous evaluation.

Result: PREFAIL substantially improves both accuracy and timeliness of responses to failure precursors in both simulation and real-world datasets.

Conclusion: Analyzing relative motion and incorporating precise intervention timing enables more effective and actionable failure prediction for non-prehensile manipulation.

Abstract: Non-prehensile manipulation enables flexible material handling with part carriers, but friction-based support makes high-speed motions failure-prone, while slower operation increases cycle time. Proactive failure prediction is therefore essential for efficient and reliable performance, yet existing approaches remain limited by key constraints, including sensitivity to dynamic actions and high dependence on known policy structures. Furthermore, existing methods and datasets lack a precise characterization of the latest intervention time, leaving it unclear whether a detected failure can still be prevented through timely intervention. In this paper, we investigate lift-and-place tasks for non-prehensile material handling manipulation and propose a more effective approach to predicting precursors to failures (PREFAIL) by analyzing the relative motion of target objects with respect to the carrier. We further introduce a dataset that precisely identifies the latest intervention time for risky manipulations, enabling rigorous evaluation of whether a failure prediction is actionable. We validate our approach on both simulation and real-world datasets. Our experimental results demonstrate that PREFAIL substantially improves both the accuracy and timeliness of responses to failure precursors.

</details>


### [448] [SinD 2.0: A Multi-City UAV Dataset with Semantic Risk Annotations for SOTIF-Oriented Safety Validation at Signalized Intersections](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16943)
*Yunwei Li,Shengjie Fu,Chunrong Chen,Chengxiang Zhao,Yuchen Fan,Mingyu Zhu,Yanchao Xu,Yuxin Zhang,Lan Yang,Chuzhao Li,Jie Ji,Yi He,Abhijit Sarkar,Akash Sonth,Hong Wang,Jun Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: SinD 2.0 is a large-scale drone-based intersection dataset for cross-domain ADS safety validation, covering six signalized intersections across four Chinese cities with 32,682 safety-critical events, hierarchical semantic annotations, and a full-stack testing toolchain.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing naturalistic driving datasets suffer from geographical homogeneity, sparsity of safety-critical events, and lack of semantic risk annotations, limiting ADS safety validation (SOTIF) at signalized intersections.

Method: The paper constructs SinD 2.0 with drone-based data across four Chinese cities, extracts safety-critical events via surrogate safety measures, provides HD maps, SPaT data, and multi-dimensional semantic labels (violations, high-risk interactions, visual shielding, narrow feasible areas), and builds a full-stack testing toolchain supporting scenario extraction, prediction, open-loop/reactive closed-loop testing, and rendering.

Result: Benchmark experiments show significant domain shifts across cities and semantic risk subsets effectively expose performance limitations of ADS algorithms.

Conclusion: SinD 2.0 addresses critical gaps in cross-domain intersection safety validation for ADS, providing a rich dataset and toolchain for SOTIF verification.

Abstract: Safety validation at signalized intersections remains a critical bottleneck for the deployment of autonomous driving systems (ADS), as these scenarios involve dense heterogeneous traffic, contested right of way, and long-tail safety-critical interactions, posing significant challenges to the Safety of the Intended Functionality (SOTIF). Existing naturalistic driving datasets often suffer from geographical homogeneity, sparsity of safety-critical events, and lack of semantic risk annotations, which limit the evaluation of algorithmic generalizability and targeted SOTIF verification. To address these gaps, this paper introduces SinD 2.0, a large-scale drone-based intersection dataset dedicated to cross-domain ADS safety analysis. The main contributions of SinD 2.0 are: (1) Cross-domain diversity: It covers six signalized intersections across four Chinese cities, capturing distinct intersection topologies and regional driving behavior characteristics; (2) High-density risk interactions: A total of 32,682 safety-critical events are extracted via surrogate safety measures, significantly enriching the density of boundary test scenarios; (3) Hierarchical semantic annotations: Besides integration with high-definition (HD) maps and Signal Phase and Timing (SPaT) data, it provides multi-dimensional semantic labels including traffic violations, high-risk interactions, visual shielding, and narrow feasible areas; (4) Full-stack testing toolchain: It supports automated scenario extraction, prediction-only evaluation, open-loop replay, reactive closed-loop testing, and photorealistic rendering. Benchmark experiments demonstrate that SinD 2.0 exhibits significant domain shifts across cities, and the semantic risk subsets can effectively expose the performance limitations of ADS algorithms. The dataset, annotations, and testing toolchain are available at https://github.com/SOTIF-AVLab/SinD/tree/main.

</details>


### [449] [G2-Nav: Grounded and Guarded Vision-Language Costmaps for Robot Social Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16956)
*Yuwen Liao,Yihang Lan,Yizhuo Yang,Ruimeng Liu,Xinhang Xu,Shenghai Yuan,Lihua Xie*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: G2-Nav is a framework for socially compliant robot navigation that uses VLMs to generate interpretable vision-language costmaps rather than making direct planning decisions, with semantic verification and safety checks for robust real-world deployment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing end-to-end VLM-based navigation methods are unpredictable black-boxes, and instruction-following approaches lack full autonomy. A bridge between abstract social reasoning and safe real-world deployment is needed.

Method: G2-Nav translates VLM semantic reasoning into a vision-language costmap (instead of direct planning). It uses open-set perception to evaluate traversable regions and social agents, performs semantic verification on upstream tracking, and introduces a high-frequency safety check before trajectory generation.

Result: Real-world experiments demonstrate safe, efficient, and socially compliant autonomous navigation in unstructured environments.

Conclusion: G2-Nav effectively grounds abstract social reasoning into interpretable costmaps while ensuring safe deployment through semantic verification and safety checks, achieving full autonomy in complex social navigation scenarios.

Abstract: Social navigation requires the robot to reason and respond in complex real-world environments. While recent works attempt to incorporate human-level intelligence into robot planning using large Vision-Language Models (VLMs), end-to-end frameworks often create an unpredictable black-box, and existing instruction-following methods are not designed for full autonomy. To bridge this gap, we present G2-Nav, a novel framework that grounds abstract social reasoning and guards safe real-world deployment. Instead of asking the VLM for direct planning decisions, G2-Nav translates its semantic reasoning into a vision-language costmap with reliability and interpretability. The VLM evaluates traversable regions and social agents from open-set perception, mapping social context into the costmap. To improve real-world robustness, the VLM performs semantic verification on upstream tracking, and we introduce a high-frequency safety check to guard against system latency prior to trajectory generation. We demonstrate through real-world experiments that G2-Nav delivers safe, efficient, and socially compliant autonomous navigation in unstructured environments. Code will be made publicly available.

</details>


### [450] [User-Driven Learning from Demonstration: A Trajectory and Impedance Learning Method](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.16998)
*Zi-Qi Yang,Mehrdad R. Kermani*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a user-driven LfD method that learns from a single demonstration in real-time using 3D FDM + DS, with EKF error compensation and learned impedance variations for compliant, precise robot motion reproduction.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To reduce user effort in robot LfD by eliminating repeated teaching, while addressing safety issues in HRI caused by non-compliant time-indexed trajectory reproduction methods.

Method: Integrates 3D Fast Diffeomorphic Matching (FDM) with Dynamical System (DS)-based motion generation for single-shot learning; uses Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) for error compensation; incorporates an impedance parameterization function to learn and reproduce impedance variations.

Result: Validated through comprehensive experiments on a 7-DOF KUKA LWR IV+ robot, demonstrating precise reproduction, real-time learning from one demonstration, and compliant behavior with perturbation robustness.

Conclusion: The proposed single-shot learning approach effectively reduces user effort while ensuring both compliance and precision, addressing safety issues in HRI.

Abstract: This paper presents a method for user-driven robot Learning from Demonstration (LfD) that reduces user effort while ensuring compliant and precise reproduction. The method eliminates repeated teaching for the same task and enables real-time learning from a single demonstration. Demonstrated motions are reproduced with high precision, while impedance variations are learned in real time to provide both compliance and robustness against perturbations. This mitigates potential safety issues in Human-Robot Interaction (HRI) that arise from conventional time-indexed trajectories lacking compliance. The proposed approach integrates a three-dimensional (3D) Fast Diffeomorphic Matching (FDM) algorithm with a Dynamical System (DS)-based motion generator to achieve real-time single-shot demonstration learning and reproduction. An Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) framework compensates for reproduction errors and recovers from external interactions. Furthermore, an impedance parameterization function is incorporated to learn impedance variations from demonstrations and maintain surface contact for specific applications. The proposed approach is validated through comprehensive experiments on a 7 Degree-of-Freedom (DOF) KUKA LWR IV+ robot.

</details>


### [451] [ADMM-Based Safety-Critical Distributed NMPC for Cooperative Transportation by Quadrupedal Robots](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17007)
*Ruturaj S. Sambhus,Kapi Ketan Mehta,Yicheng Zeng,Kaveh Akbari Hamed*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a distributed nonlinear MPC framework for teams of quadrupedal robots cooperatively transporting a payload. It uses ADMM for decentralized optimization, enforces safety via HOCBFs, and achieves up to 23% faster solve times than centralized NMPC while maintaining comparable performance in simulations and real-world experiments.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable safe, real-time cooperative payload transport by multiple quadrupedal robots in cluttered environments, overcoming the computational bottlenecks of centralized control.

Method: Decomposes a centralized NMPC problem into parallel local subproblems using ADMM, with consensus over payload-state and interaction-wrench trajectories, acceleration-level holonomic constraints, and HOCBF-based safety constraints.

Result: Up to 23% reduction in average NLP solve time vs. centralized NMPC; validated in simulation (2-4 robots) and real experiments (2-3 robots); robust to payload uncertainty, external disturbances, and communication delays.

Conclusion: The proposed DNMPC with explicit payload-state consensus and holonomic constraints improves coordination and tracking over wrench-only methods, offering a scalable and safe solution for multi-robot payload transport.

Abstract: This paper presents a safety-critical distributed nonlinear model predictive control (DNMPC) framework for cooperative payload transportation by teams of quadrupedal robots. The proposed approach models the robotic team and the shared payload as a dynamically coupled networked system with rigid holonomic coupling constraints arising from cooperative transportation. To enable distributed real-time optimization, the centralized finite-horizon optimal control problem is decomposed into parallel local NMPC subproblems coordinated through the alternating direction method of multipliers (ADMM). The resulting distributed framework enforces consensus over both payload-state and interaction-wrench trajectories while explicitly incorporating acceleration-level holonomic coupling constraints within the distributed predictive control formulation. Safety-critical obstacle avoidance constraints for both the robotic agents and payload are enforced using higher-order control barrier functions (HOCBFs). The framework is validated through numerical simulations with teams of two, three, and four quadrupedal robots transporting shared payloads in cluttered environments. Real-time experiments on two- and three-robot teams demonstrate safe and robust transportation under payload uncertainty and external disturbances. Compared with centralized NMPC, the proposed framework achieves up to 23% reduction in average NLP solve time while maintaining comparable closed-loop performance. Ablation studies further demonstrate robustness to communication delays and show that explicit payload-state consensus and holonomic constraints substantially improve payload tracking and distributed coordination over existing wrench-only consensus formulations.

</details>


### [452] [SHAPE: Simultaneous Water Hydraulic Actuation and Position Estimation of a Sensorless Remote Actuator through a Thin and Long Flexible Tube](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17054)
*Yuki Nakamura,Shuto Yoshimura,Tomoyuki Noda,Yoshihiro Nakata*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a novel method using water-filled flexible tubes for simultaneous power transmission and position estimation in water hydraulic robots, enabling stable sensorless position control under varying loads over distances up to 50 meters.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Robot sensors and electronics often fail in harsh environments, and low-impedance joints with limited visibility make accurate positioning challenging under varying loads.

Method: Water-filled flexible tubes are used for dual-purpose power transmission and actuator-side information feedback; volumetric loss is modeled based on pressure fluctuations and minor air entrapment, enabling position estimation without sensors.

Result: Experimental validation confirms stable position control of a sensorless water hydraulic cylinder under varying loads over a tube up to 50 m; a field parameter-identification method accounts for tube and air entrainment variability.

Conclusion: The proposed approach enables reliable remote control of robots in harsh environments by achieving sensorless feedback control previously difficult to implement.

Abstract: Robot sensors and electronic equipment are prone to failure in harsh environments. With water hydraulic drive, thin and long tubes enable remote operation without actuator-side sensors. Furthermore, the elasticity of the tubes reduces the impedance of the joints (actuators), benefiting robot tasks involving unexpected contact with the environment or vibrations. However, owing to the low impedance and limited camera visibility, accurately positioning the joint (or end effector) to the target location under varying load conditions is challenging. This study proposes a novel method that employs water-filled flexible tubes to enable the transmission of driving power and actuator-side information to and from the actuator, respectively, without actuator-side sensors. By modeling volumetric loss during transmission based on pressure fluctuations and incorporating minor air entrapment, simultaneous power transmission and position estimation is achieved through a tube up to 50 m. Thus, it becomes possible to use a feedback control framework that was previously difficult to implement in sensorless systems. Experimental validation confirms stable position control of a sensorless water hydraulic cylinder under varying loads. Furthermore, a field parameter-identification method accounts for tube and air entrainment variability without requiring actuator-side sensors. These contributions promote reliable remote control of robots in harsh environments.

</details>


### [453] [DROID-ANCHOR: Odometry-Anchored Recurrent Metric Depth Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17058)
*Yuxuan Chen,Brook Du*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Metric-DROID is an end-to-end recurrent SLAM system that anchors visual odometry to physical scale by integrating proprioceptive sensors via an LSTM update operator, uncertainty-aware metric backend, and selective fine-tuning, achieving zero-shot metric alignment.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Monocular SLAM systems suffer from scale ambiguity and scale drift, preventing their direct use in autonomous robot navigation which requires precise metric depth estimation. The authors aim to anchor visual SLAM to physical reality.

Method: (1) LSTM Update Operator encoding high-frequency odometry into spatial feature maps for persistent metric bias; (2) Uncertainty-Aware Metric Backend (BA_odom) treating odometry as geometric anchor with learned heteroscedastic covariance; (3) Selective residual fine-tuning preserving pre-trained priors while enabling zero-shot metric alignment.

Result: The system effectively mitigates wheel-slip and sensor noise by intelligently balancing visual re-projection and metric translation residuals through time-varying uncertainty regression.

Conclusion: Metric-DROID provides a principled way to integrate proprioceptive odometry into recurrent visual SLAM, enabling zero-shot metric alignment without losing pre-trained geometric priors.

Abstract: Precise metric depth estimation is fundamental for autonomous robot navigation, yet monocular systems inherently suffer from scale ambiguity and scale drift. While recent recurrent flow-based SLAM systems have demonstrated state-of-the-art robustness, they remain scale-ambiguous.
