Motivation: Robots often face unforeseen dynamic obstacles in real-world scenarios, making pre-determined maps inaccurate for localization. There is a need for a robust, low-cost localization method that works in dynamic environments without requiring full-range sensors like LiDAR.

Method: The framework uses sparse distance sampling to generate a robust prior on the robot's location, accounting for dynamic obstacles based on real-world data insights. This prior is fused over time with odometry to converge to the robot's true pose. The method solves the kidnapped robot problem in real time.

Result: The approach achieves localization performance comparable to SLAM using only 16 distance samples instead of full LiDAR range. It provably converges to ground truth pose in static environments, and maintains guarantees in dynamic environments when change patterns are correctly learned. Effectiveness demonstrated in real-world indoor environments.

Conclusion: Sparse distance sampling combined with odometry can provide robust, provably convergent lifelong localization in dynamic indoor environments, offering advantages in sensor cost, privacy, storage, and bandwidth compared to full-range sensing methods.

Abstract: Localization is a key task in robot navigation, and many techniques exist for it. In many plausible scenarios, a robot might face unforeseen, dynamic obstacles, rendering any pre-determined map inaccurate for localization. In this work, we propose a robust lifelong localization framework in dynamic planar indoor environments, using the robot's odometry and sparse distance sampling. We demonstrate how distance samples can be used to provide a robust prior on the robot's location. This technique can solve the kidnapped robot problem in real time, up to symmetries. Based on insights from real-world recorded data, we also account for dynamic obstacles. We then fuse this prior, over time, with the odometry to converge to the robot's location. A central property of our method is that it provably converges to the robot's ground truth pose even in large indoor environments when the environment is static. We further show that this guarantee also holds in dynamic environments, as long as the nature of those changes has been correctly learned. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach in different real-world indoor environments. In particular, we achieve a localization comparable to SLAM with merely a few (sixteen) distance samples, as opposed to the full LiDAR range. Sufficing with only sparse distance sampling is advantageous in terms of sensor cost, privacy, storage space, and transmission bandwidth.

</details>


### [482] [ConceptTree: Bringing Semantic Transparency to Black-Box Decision Making for Robotic Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17861)
*Yongyan Wen,Feifan Liu,Jinyi Chen,Bo An,Peng Liu,Siyuan Li*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: ConceptTree reframes robotic manipulation skill selection as reasoning over human-interpretable concepts using a normalized concept space and decision tree, achieving transparent, traceable, and intervenable high-level policies that outperform concept-based baselines in long-horizon tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robotic manipulation methods treat skill selection as opaque observation-to-action mappings, offering limited transparency for human oversight and intervention.

Method: ConceptTree learns a normalized concept space grounded in visual inputs, then trains a decision tree over concept-level predicates to predict high-level manipulation skills.

Result: ConceptTree consistently outperforms existing concept-based baselines, especially in complex long-horizon scenarios, and supports fine-grained intervention via modifying individual concepts.

Conclusion: The transparent decision process enables direct inspection and targeted correction of decision errors without retraining, enhancing human oversight.

Abstract: Establishing interpretable decision-making processes in long-horizon robotic manipulation is critical for enabling reliable human oversight and intervention. However, existing approaches to robotic manipulation largely treat skill selection as opaque mappings from observations to actions, offering limited transparency into how decisions are formed. In this work, we propose ConceptTree, a framework that reframes high-level manipulation skill selection as reasoning over human-interpretable concepts, representing high-level policies as a sequence of concept-level predicates over visual observations. Rather than relying on implicit latent representations, our method learns a normalized concept space grounded in visual inputs, over which a decision tree is trained to predict high-level skills. This formulation yields a transparent decision process that is both traceable and intervenable, enabling direct inspection and modification of policy behavior. We evaluate our approach on a set of real-world robotic manipulation tasks with increasing complexity. Experimental results show that ConceptTree consistently outperforms existing concept-based baselines, particularly in complex, long-horizon scenarios. Furthermore, we provide qualitative case studies showing that our model supports fine-grained intervention by modifying individual concepts, enabling targeted correction of decision errors without retraining.

</details>


### [483] [Leveraging Two Robotic Arms for Tight Assembly Performance Gains](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17876)
*Dror Livnat,Yuval Lavi,Michael M. Bilevich,Dan Halperin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A novel end-to-end framework for dual-arm robotic assembly that leverages CAD models to coordinate two arms, achieving >50% reduction in execution time, higher trajectory quality, and faster placement search compared to single-arm systems, with theoretical guarantees and open-source implementation.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To improve efficiency and accuracy in tight assembly operations by utilizing two robotic arms simultaneously, addressing the limitations of single-arm systems in terms of execution time and trajectory quality.

Method: An end-to-end framework that takes digital CAD models and desired assembled part placements as input, plans and executes coordinated dual-arm assembly operations with theoretical guarantees on execution time and trajectory accuracy.

Result: Coordinated dual-arm movement reduces average execution time by >50%, produces higher-quality trajectories, accelerates valid robot placement search, and establishes bounds on robotic cell dimensions.

Conclusion: Dual-arm assembly significantly outperforms single-arm approaches in tight assembly tasks, with validated performance in both simulation and physical robots, supported by open-source software.

Abstract: We provide a novel end-to-end framework for the execution of an assembly operation by two robotic arms, given the digital CAD models of the parts and their desired relative placement in their assembled state. We analyze and demonstrate the advantages of using two robotic arms simultaneously in tight assembly operations, compared to single-arm systems. Our method is implemented in both simulation and using physical robots. It provides theoretical guarantees on execution time and trajectory accuracy, supported by empirical evidence. In particular, we show that coordinated movement of two arms reduces average execution time by more than 50% compared to using a single arm only, produces higher-quality trajectories, and accelerates the search for valid robot placements. Furthermore, we establish bounds on the required dimensions of the robotic cell. Our open source software together with real-life video demonstrations are available in our project page.

</details>


### [484] [Manifold-Guided Motion Planning for Tight Assemblies](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17898)
*Dror Livnat,Michael M. Bilevich,Michal Kleinbort,Dan Halperin*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: CMG-RRT is a sampling-based motion planner for tight rigid-body assembly problems that guides exploration by biasing sampling toward the critical manifold (configurations with contact), achieving 100% success on challenging benchmarks including the first automatic solution of the Elk disentanglement puzzle.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Motion planning for rigid-body assembly is challenging due to tight geometric constraints requiring (near-)zero clearance configurations, which existing planners struggle to handle effectively.

Method: CMG-RRT adaptively biases sampling toward neighborhoods of the critical manifold (configurations with at least one contact point) using a hierarchical subdivision of configuration space, guiding exploration through tight regions.

Result: 100% success rate across challenging rotational assembly benchmarks; first fully automatic solution of the Elk disentanglement puzzle; proved probabilistically complete under standard clearance assumptions.

Conclusion: Sampling near the critical manifold is an effective strategy for solving tight assembly problems, and CMG-RRT provides a theoretically grounded, practical solution that outperforms existing methods.

Abstract: Motion planning for rigid-body assembly poses a fundamental challenge in robotics due to tight geometric constraints. In such scenarios, feasible motions often require passing through (near-)zero clearance configurations in which the parts are tightly constrained by contact. In this work, we introduce Critical-Manifold Guided RRT (CMG-RRT), a sampling-based planner designed specifically for tight assembly problems. Our key observation is that in tight assemblies, valid solution paths lie on or near a critical manifold: the subset of configuration space consisting of poses with at least one contact point between parts. CMG-RRT guides exploration by adaptively biasing sampling toward neighborhoods of the critical manifold using a hierarchical subdivision of the configuration space. We prove that CMG-RRT is probabilistically complete under standard clearance assumptions. Empirical evaluation on challenging rotational assembly benchmarks demonstrates a 100% success rate across all tested instances, including, to the best of our knowledge, the first fully automatic solution of the Elk disentanglement puzzle. Our open source software is available through our project page: https://www.cgl.cs.tau.ac.il/projects/tight-assembly-planning.

</details>


### [485] [UMCP: A Unified Multi-Task Collaborative Perception Network for Luggage Trolley Pose Estimation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17950)
*Zhirui Sun,Zhihao Jiang,Yao Wang,Jianwei Peng,Jiankun Wang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents UMCP, a unified multi-task network based on YOLOv12 that simultaneously performs luggage trolley detection, keypoint detection, and orientation estimation, reducing latency and deployment costs while maintaining accuracy.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing visual perception approaches for luggage trolley collection rely on cascaded multi-model inference, leading to high inference latency and deployment costs. The goal is to achieve both high accuracy and real-time performance in cluttered, dynamic environments.

Method: A unified multi-task collaborative perception network (UMCP) based on YOLOv12, integrating keypoint feature fusion with orientation features via an Orientation Feature Enhancement Module (OFEM), and using circular probability distribution modeling with KL divergence loss for better orientation estimation.

Result: The proposed method achieves competitive overall accuracy while substantially reducing model complexity and computational cost compared to existing cascaded multi-model approaches.

Conclusion: UMCP effectively balances accuracy and efficiency for autonomous luggage trolley collection in dynamic environments, offering a practical single-model solution.

Abstract: In robotic autonomous luggage trolley collection, robots must continuously localize scattered luggage trolleys in cluttered and dynamic environments. This requires the vision system to achieve both high accuracy and real-time performance. However, existing visual perception approaches for luggage trolleys often rely on cascaded multi-model inference, leading to increased inference latency and high deployment costs. To address these limitations, this article presents a unified multi-task collaborative perception network (UMCP) that simultaneously performs luggage trolley detection, keypoint detection and orientation estimation. Based on the YOLOv12 architecture, keypoint features are fused with orientation features and then fed into an orientation feature enhancement module (OFEM), thereby improving orientation estimation accuracy. In addition, circular probability distribution modeling with a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence loss is adopted to enhance orientation estimation accuracy further. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive overall accuracy while substantially reducing model complexity and computational cost compared with existing methods. A website about this work is available at https://sites.google.com/view/robot-umcp.

</details>


### [486] [Does Robust VIO Need More Learning? Geometry-Verified Visual Measurements under Distribution Shift](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17956)
*Yangyang Ning,Shu Liang,Quanbo Ge,Tianchen Deng,Yuhua Qi,Shenghai Yuan*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper proposes a minimal-learning stereo VIO framework where learned correspondence (SEA-RAFT) is used only for visual measurement generation with uncertainty prediction, while all temporal tracking, geometric verification, and state estimation remain explicit. The method shows robust performance under distribution shifts, outperforming deeper learned pipelines.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To investigate whether robust VIO under distribution shift truly requires deeper learned estimation throughout the pipeline, or whether learning can be confined to visual measurement generation while keeping other components explicit and geometric.

Method: Uses SEA-RAFT only for dense stereo correspondences and uncertainty prediction; sparse sampling of dense flow at feature locations; filtering via predicted uncertainty and epipolar consistency; sliding-window stereo-inertial estimator with uncertainty-weighted reprojection factors; uncertainty propagation through triangulation for 3D Gaussian mapping.

Result: Demonstrates accurate and stable estimation on EuRoC, VIODE, and 4Seasons under motion blur, dynamic scenes, illumination changes, and large indoor-to-outdoor distribution shifts. Ablations confirm learned flow alone is insufficient; gains come from combining learned proposals with geometric verification and uncertainty-aware weighting.

Conclusion: For OOD-robust VIO, carefully integrated learned visual measurements with geometric constraints are more effective than learning a larger fraction of the estimation pipeline.

Abstract: Learning is increasingly introduced into visual-inertial odometry (VIO), ranging from learned feature front-ends to learning-dominant motion and geometry estimation. However, learning more of the pipeline does not necessarily improve robustness when deployment conditions differ from the training distribution. This work asks whether robust VIO under distribution shift truly requires deeper learned estimation, or whether learning can be confined to visual measurement generation. We propose a minimal-learning stereo VIO framework in which SEA-RAFT is used only to propose dense stereo correspondences and predict their uncertainty, while temporal tracking, geometric verification, and state estimation remain explicit. Dense flow is sampled at sparse feature locations, filtered using predicted uncertainty and stereo epipolar consistency, and incorporated into a sliding-window stereo-inertial estimator through uncertainty-weighted reprojection factors. The same uncertainty is further propagated through stereo triangulation for downstream anisotropic 3D Gaussian mapping. Experiments on EuRoC, VIODE, and 4Seasons demonstrate accurate and stable estimation under motion blur, dynamic scenes, illumination changes, and large indoor-to-outdoor distribution shifts. Ablations show that learned flow alone is insufficient: the gains arise from combining learned correspondence proposals with geometric verification and uncertainty-aware weighting. These results suggest that, for OOD-robust VIO, carefully integrated learned visual measurements can be more effective than learning a larger fraction of the estimation pipeline. Code and configs for the benchmark will be open-source upon acceptance. A supplementary video is available at https://drive.google.com/file/d/1EVRhOkhanmNXHbQS1Vr80FoEIAYOYOV2/view

</details>


### [487] [MEVION: Low-Cost Open-Source Data Collection System for Powerful and High-Speed Dual-Arm Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17970)
*Kento Kawaharazuka,Yoshiki Obinata,Hirokazu Ishida,Jihoon Oh,Temma Suzuki,Shintaro Inoue,Keita Yoneda,Ayumu Iwata,Kei Okada*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: MEVION is a low-cost, open-source dual-arm robot data collection system that overcomes the force and speed limitations of ALOHA, capable of generating higher torque (60 Nm) at lower cost (~$14,000) using sheet metal welding and closed-link mechanisms inspired by quadruped robots.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing low-cost dual-arm systems like ALOHA cannot generate sufficient force and speed for handling heavy objects or performing fast manipulations, limiting data collection for robotic foundation models.

Method: The system uses four 6-DoF arms with parallel grippers, sheet metal welding for low-cost large body construction, and a closed-link mechanism in the elbow joint (inspired by quadruped robots) to reduce distal mass and enhance force/speed output.

Result: Each arm weighs 7.0 kg, achieves maximum torque of 60 Nm, and the entire system costs approximately USD 14,000. It enables data collection for previously impossible object manipulation tasks and supports imitation learning.

Conclusion: MEVION successfully demonstrates that a low-cost, open-source design can achieve high force and speed capabilities, expanding the range of manipulable tasks for dual-arm robot data collection systems.

Abstract: The global competition for developing robotic foundation models is intensifying. Among the data collection systems used for dual-arm robots, ALOHA is representative of being low-cost and open-source, and is widely adopted by researchers as a de facto standard. However, due to its limited ability to generate high forces and speeds, it is difficult to handle heavy objects or perform fast manipulations. To address this, we developed MEVION, a low-cost and open-source dual-arm robot data collection system capable of generating greater force and speed. All parts of this robot can be sourced through e-commerce, and by extensively utilizing sheet metal welding, its large body structure is constructed with a small number of components at low cost, while also simplifying assembly. MEVION is equipped with four 6-DoF arms with parallel grippers. Each arm weighs 7.0 kg and has a maximum torque of 60 Nm, and the entire system can be constructed for about USD 14,000. The elbow joint adopts a closed-link mechanism similar to those used in quadruped robots, which reduces the distal mass and enables higher force and speed output at the end-effector. We demonstrate that MEVION enables data collection for object manipulation tasks not previously possible and supports imitation learning-based motion generation. All hardware and software of this work are included in the Supplementary Materials or https://github.com/haraduka/mevion.

</details>


### [488] [RynnBrain 1.1: Towards More Capable and Generalizable Embodied Foundation Model](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17977)
*Kehan Li,Bohan Hou,Minghao Zhu,Tianyi Zhang,Zesen Cheng,Zhikai Wang,Sicong Leng,Xin Li,Xiao Lin,Biying Yao,Minghua Zeng,Jiangpin Liu,Ronghao Dang,Jiayan Guo,Siteng Huang,Haoyu Zhao,Heng Ping,Yaxi Zhao,Kexiang Wang,Tong Lu,Shengke Xue,Jiahao Tang,Yulei Wang,Zejing Wang,Jianwei Gao,Shijian Lu,Chengju Liu,Jianfei Yang,Mingxiu Chen,Deli Zhao*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RynnBrain 1.1 is a family of embodied foundation models (2B-122B-A10B) with unified spatio-temporal training, contact-point prediction, and 3D grounding, achieving SOTA on embodied benchmarks and outperforming Qwen-based VLAs in real-robot tasks.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To advance embodied AI by developing foundation models that tightly integrate perception, reasoning, localization, and planning with physical grounding, improving upon RynnBrain 1.0 by adding contact-point prediction and native 3D grounding for better robot manipulation alignment.

Method: Unified spatio-temporal and physically grounded training framework; introduces contact-point prediction across all scales; native 3D grounding for 2B and 9B models; RynnBrain-VLA with unified cross-embodiment action space and embodiment-specific masking; deployed on Unitree G1, Astribot-S1, and Tianji-Wuji robots.

Result: 122B-A10B model outperforms all evaluated proprietary and open-source models on VSI-Bench, MMSI, and RefSpatial-Bench. Real-robot experiments show RynnBrain-initialized policies outperform Qwen-based and generalist VLAs; joint multi-task and multi-embodiment training improves success rates over per-task training.

Conclusion: RynnBrain 1.1 establishes strong embodied cognition and grounding capabilities, with real-robot validation demonstrating the effectiveness of physical grounding and multi-embodiment training for practical robot manipulation.

Abstract: We present RynnBrain 1.1, a family of embodied foundation models spanning 2B, 9B, and 122B-A10B scales. Trained with a unified spatio-temporal and physically grounded framework, RynnBrain 1.1 supports embodied perception, spatial reasoning, localization, and planning. Compared with RynnBrain 1.0, it further introduces contact-point prediction across the model family and native 3D grounding for the 2B and 9B models, yielding representations and outputs that are more directly aligned with robot manipulation. We also develop RynnBrain-VLA with a unified cross-embodiment action space and embodiment-specific masking, and deploy it on Unitree G1, Astribot-S1, and Tianji-Wuji. RynnBrain 1.1 achieves strong results on embodied cognition, localization, and 3D grounding, with the 122B-A10B model outperforming all evaluated proprietary and open-source models on VSI-Bench, MMSI, and RefSpatial-Bench. Real-robot experiments show that RynnBrain-initialized policies outperform Qwen-based and representative generalist VLAs, while joint multi-task and multi-embodiment training improves process scores and success rates over per-task training.

</details>


### [489] [Distilling Global Traversability Priors for Image-based Affordance Prediction in Off-road Environments](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.17984)
*Matthew Sivaprakasam,Samuel Triest,Micah Nye,Deegan Atha,Shehryar Khattak,David Fan,Wenshan Wang,Sebastian Scherer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents a method for long-range off-road navigation that extracts traversability-aware frontiers from first-person-view images using satellite imagery supervision, improving performance over existing methods by 10%+.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Standard navigation methods are myopic in long-horizon scenarios because metric maps built from LiDAR/cameras have limited depth sensing range, causing robots to discard distant data and make suboptimal short-sighted decisions.

Method: The approach extracts long-range traversability-aware frontiers from FPV images, leveraging satellite imagery to compute feasible navigation paths for image/pose pairs as supervision signals, reducing reliance on human demonstration data.

Result: The method outperforms existing approaches by more than 10% in offline benchmarks and reduces the number of human interventions in real-world experiments.

Conclusion: Learning traversability-aware frontiers from satellite-supervised FPV images effectively mitigates myopic behaviors in long-range off-road navigation without requiring extensive human demonstrations.

Abstract: Standard methods for autonomous navigation in unstructured terrain are prone to myopic behaviors in long-horizon scenarios. The use of metric maps built from LiDAR or cameras provides necessary local geometry and semantic information but is strictly limited by depth sensing range. By discarding data beyond the mapping horizon robots suffer from suboptimal, short-sighted decisions. To recover this lost information, we focus on extracting long-range traversability-aware frontiers directly from first-person-view (FPV) images. By leveraging satellite imagery, we compute the set of feasible navigation paths for a dataset of image/pose pairs and use them to supervise our network, reducing the need for extensive human demonstration data. We demonstrate that this approach improves performance in long-range off-road navigation over existing methods by more than 10% in various offline benchmarks and reduces the number of human interventions incurred in a set of real-world experiments. More details can be found at https://theairlab.org/ss_frontiers_iros .

</details>


### [490] [Closing the Loop in Humanoid VLA: Persistent 3D Object Tokens for Verifiable Loco-Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18016)
*Peng Ren,Haoyang Ge,Jiang Zhao,Cong Huang,Yukun Shi,Pei Chi,Kai Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: POT-VLA uses Persistent Object Tokenization (POT) to maintain 3D object records that condition both action generation and geometric verification, improving humanoid loco-manipulation success rates from 39/80 to 71/80 on a Unitree G1 robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation requires treating task objects as persistent physical entities across movement, contact, occlusion, and recovery, but object-state divergence causes conditioning failures when the object state used for action generation differs from that used for verification.

Method: POT maintains role-indexed 3D object records from RGB-D observations and converts them into object tokens for a whole-body action expert. The same object records condition action generation and support geometric predicate checks, creating a closed-loop system where object state is both actionable and verifiable.

Result: POT-VLA improved from 39/80 to 71/80 successes (vs. GR00T-N1.7 baseline) over eight real-world task families, and achieved 44/50 vs. 37/50 on Being-0-aligned service tasks.

Conclusion: Persistent object-centered state is a useful abstraction for verifiable humanoid VLA execution, especially for tasks requiring maintained 3D relations.

Abstract: Vision-language-action policies are a promising foundation for general robot control, but long-horizon humanoid loco-manipulation requires the robot to treat task objects as persistent physical entities across movement, contact, occlusion, and recovery. We study this problem as object-state divergence: the object state used to condition a whole-body action can differ from the state used to decide whether the action achieved the intended physical relation. We propose \emph{Persistent Object Tokenization} (POT), which maintains role-indexed 3D object records from RGB-D observations and converts them into object tokens for a whole-body action expert. Instantiated as \emph{POT-VLA}, the same object records condition action generation and support geometric predicate checks, yielding a closed-loop execution system in which object state is both actionable and verifiable. On a Unitree G1, POT-VLA improves a matched direct GR00T-N1.7 baseline from 39/80 to 71/80 successes over eight real-world task families. In an external Being-0-aligned reference, POT-VLA achieves 44/50 successes on aligned service tasks, compared with the 37/50 success reported by the Being-0 paper. The largest gains occur on tasks requiring maintained 3D relations, suggesting that persistent object-centered state is a useful abstraction for verifiable humanoid VLA execution.

</details>


### [491] [RoboHarness: Memory-Driven Orchestration of Heterogeneous Robot Policies for Long-Horizon Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18060)
*Jinbang Huang,Yuanzhao Hu,Zhiyuan Li,Ran Qi,Yixin Xiao,Zhanguang Zhang,Mark Coates,Tongtong Cao,Yingxue Zhang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: RoboHarness is a unified framework that encapsulates heterogeneous robot policies (VLAs, RL, TAMP) as agentic skills, using multi-modal memory and online evidence for capability-aware routing and a Memory Bridge for stable policy handoffs, achieving strong zero-shot long-horizon planning results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing planning methods overlook uncertain capability boundaries and cross-policy distribution mismatch when orchestrating heterogeneous policies, limiting their effectiveness for long-horizon robotic tasks.

Method: RoboHarness encapsulates diverse robot control systems as reusable agentic skills, uses multi-modal execution memory and online evidence to characterize policy capability boundaries for capability-aware decomposition and routing, and employs Memory Bridge to retrieve execution trajectories and guide robots toward in-distribution state regions for stable handoffs.

Result: Experiments on three public benchmarks, 500 customized tasks, and 135 real-robot experiments show substantial improvements in zero-shot long-horizon planning and out-of-distribution robustness.

Conclusion: RoboHarness effectively enables capability-aware routing and stable policy orchestration across heterogeneous policies, outperforming existing methods in zero-shot long-horizon planning and robustness.

Abstract: Long-horizon robotic tasks require diverse capabilities that no single policy can reliably provide. Heterogeneous policies offer complementary strengths, but orchestrating them requires reasoning over uncertain capability boundaries and cross-policy distribution mismatch, which are largely overlooked by existing planning methods built on homogeneous, predefined skills with fixed applicability. We propose RoboHarness, a unified framework that encapsulates independently developed robot control systems as reusable agentic skills. Although instantiated in this work with VLAs, RL policies, and task-and-motion planning (TAMP) systems, RoboHarness is designed as a general framework compatible with a broader range of robot policies, such as navigation policies, model predictive controllers, and world-action models. RoboHarness uses multi-modal execution memory and online evidence to characterize policy capability boundaries for capability-aware decomposition and routing. To stabilize policy handoffs, its Memory Bridge retrieves execution trajectories associated with the next policy, estimates its in-distribution state region, and guides the robot toward that region without joint policy retraining. Extensive experiments on three public benchmarks, 500 customized tasks, and 135 real-robot experiments demonstrate effective capability-aware routing and stable policy orchestration, yielding substantial improvements in zero-shot long-horizon planning and out-of-distribution robustness.

</details>


### [492] [UniETP: Unifying Environments for Generalizable Embodied Task Planning](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18062)
*Peiran Xu,Jiaqi Zheng,Ziyou Wang,Yadong Mu*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper introduces UniETP, a unified interface that integrates four embodied simulators (AI2-THOR, VirtualHome, Habitat, BEHAVIOR) into a standardized observation/action space, along with an automatically-constructed multi-difficulty dataset, to address fragmentation in Embodied Task Planning research.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing embodied task planning simulators and datasets are fragmented, each using different observation formats, action types, and task domains, which hinders comprehensive model evaluation and scalable training data collection.

Method: UniETP unifies four simulators (AI2-THOR, VirtualHome, Habitat, BEHAVIOR) into a consistent observation and action space, builds a standardized evaluation system for complicated task goals, and automatically constructs a diverse dataset with varying difficulty levels across task logic, instance grounding, and instruction understanding.

Result: Extensive experiments on the proposed benchmark evaluate embodied planning capabilities of recent models and identify performance bottlenecks.

Conclusion: UniETP provides a unified, standardized, yet diverse benchmark to facilitate generalizable embodied planning research, addressing the fragmentation problem. Code and data will be released.

Abstract: This paper focuses on the problem of Embodied Task Planning, where an agent is required to execute a sequence of atomic actions within an interactive environment to complete a user-specified task. Though a variety of simulators and datasets have previously been built for this task, these efforts are largely isolated, with each using its own observation format, action type, and task domain. This fragmentation complicates comprehensive model evaluation and hinders the scalability of training data. As an effort towards generalizable embodied planning, we propose UniETP, a unified interface integrating four commonly-used simulators (AI2-THOR, VirtualHome, Habitat, BEHAVIOR). UniETP is characterized by both standardization and diversity. On one hand, it formalizes all the simulators into a consistent observation and action space, and builds an evaluation system to support complicated task goal. On the other hand, it enhances task diversity and complexity across dimensions like task logic, instance grounding, and instruction understanding, constructing a new dataset with varied levels of difficulty in an automatic manner. Extensive experiments on the proposed benchmark are conducted to evaluate the embodied planning capabilities of recent models and analyze the performance bottlenecks. Codes and data will be available at https://github.com/woyut/UniETP .

</details>


### [493] [Technical Design Review of Duke Robotics Club's Oogway & Crush: AUVs for RoboSub 2026](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18075)
*Patrick Zheng,Saagar Arya,Hung Le,Mathew Chu,Nathanael Ren,Niko Weaver,Isabella Chen,Jill Wang,Raine Cheng,Siddharth Kini,Avrick Altmann,Srinath Iyer,Ivan Chen,Ian Suh,Parker Jones,Pierson Jones,Sebastian deSouza,Suhaani Sriram,Suvas Aggarwal*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Duke Robotics Club presents two AUVs (Oogway and Crush) for RoboSub 2026, expanding to target all four design goals—movement, vision, manipulation, and acoustic tracking—through sustained reliability improvements across mechanical, electrical, and software subsystems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To expand beyond previously narrowed scope and target all four RoboSub design goals (movement, vision, manipulation, acoustic tracking) for the first time while maintaining competition-required reliability.

Method: Reliability investments across three subsystems: (1) Mechanical: two additional thrusters, CFD-optimized case for pitch stability; (2) Electrical: repaired failure points, upgraded thruster control, redesigned acoustics PCB with higher-order filters; (3) Software: improved state estimation, sonar-based detection, vision-driven task planning, IVC, and testing infrastructure.

Result: Ability to attempt a broader set of tasks while maintaining reliability required for competition strategy, with improvements in pinger detection reliability, coordinated autonomous runs, and pitch stability.

Conclusion: Sustained reliability investments across all subsystems enable successful expansion to full-scope RoboSub competition participation while preserving the reliability needed for competitive performance.

Abstract: The Duke Robotics Club presents Oogway and Crush, our AUVs for RoboSub 2026. This year's strategy expands on our previously narrowed scope, targeting all four of RoboSub's design goals for the first time: movement, vision, manipulation, and acoustic tracking. This expansion is based on sustained reliability investment across all three subsystems. Mechanically, Crush gained two additional thrusters and a CFD-optimized case, providing pitch stability. Electrically, we addressed accumulated failure points by repairing unreliable connections and upgraded thruster control hardware. We also redesigned our acoustics system, adding a new custom PCB with higher-order filters, significantly improving pinger detection reliability. On the software side, improvements to state estimation, sonar-based object detection, vision-driven task planning, and IVC enable more capable and coordinated autonomous runs. Paired with investments in our testing infrastructure to maximize our limited pool time, we can now attempt a broader set of tasks while maintaining the reliability our competition strategy demands.

</details>


### [494] [Importance Sampling and PCA for Finding Failures in Commercial Autonomous Vehicles](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18106)
*Hailey Warner,Duncan Eddy,Shreya Parjan,Caroline Cahilly,Harrison Delecki,Matthias Kleinstauber,Chaitanya Shinde,Jerry Lopez,Mykel J. Kochenderfer*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Two rare-event discovery algorithms (AST and DiFS) are applied to a commercial autonomous trucking stack, finding simulated collisions that Monte Carlo methods miss. PCA-based analysis classifies failure modes and recovers generalized noise trajectories for systematic diagnosis.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Prior rare-failure discovery methods were only tested in simulations with simple, academic driving stacks; it was unknown whether they generalize to the more robust planners used in real commercial autonomous systems.

Method: Applied Adaptive Stress Testing (AST, RL-based) and Diffusion-based Failure Sampling (DiFS) to a commercial trucking stack; used PCA to classify failure modes, cluster principal components, and invert PCA to recover generalized noise trajectories.

Result: Both algorithms found simulated collisions during merge and cut-in maneuvers where traditional Monte Carlo simulation failed. PCA-based analysis produced generalized noise trajectories that reproduce failures in identical and similar scenarios.

Conclusion: Rare-event discovery methods generalize to commercial-grade systems, and the PCA pipeline provides a practical path from failure discovery to systematic diagnosis of perception-level flaws.

Abstract: Methods for discovering rare failures in autonomous systems have so far been demonstrated almost exclusively in simulations with simple, academic driving stacks, leaving open whether they generalize to the more robust planners used in commercial systems. We address this gap by applying two rare-event discovery algorithms to a commercial autonomous trucking stack. Adaptive stress testing (AST) uses reinforcement learning to search for the most likely noise trajectories leading to a simulated collision, while diffusion-based failure sampling (DiFS) trains a denoising diffusion model to sample a diverse set of failures. We show that both algorithms find simulated collisions during merge and cut-in maneuvers where traditional Monte Carlo simulation does not. To make these failures actionable, we introduce a statistical analysis based on principal component analysis (PCA) that classifies failures into common modes and identifies the timesteps that most influence the outcome. We cluster the principal components and invert the PCA transform to recover generalized noise trajectories, and show that these trajectories reproduce failures in identical and similar scenarios. This provides a path from failure discovery to systematic diagnosis of perception-level flaws.

</details>


### [495] [Isaac Sim-to-Real: Reinforcement Learning based Locomotion for Quadrupeds](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18135)
*Jordan Dowdy,Jean Chagas Vaz*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper presents an RL-based whole-body locomotion framework using Nvidia's Isaac Sim and Isaac Lab for training, achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on the Unitree Go1 robot, with competitive velocity tracking and superior disturbance recovery.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To address the sim-to-real gap in legged locomotion RL policies, enabling robust whole-body control that transfers directly from simulation to physical hardware without fine-tuning.

Method: A robust RL locomotion framework trained using Nvidia's Isaac Sim and Isaac Lab simulation tools, achieving zero-shot sim-to-real transfer on the Unitree Go1 quadruped robot.

Result: The policy achieves similar velocity tracking to the robot's integrated controller, better recovery from large disturbances, linear velocities of 2.0 m/s, and angular velocities of 1.8 rad/s on physical hardware.

Conclusion: The proposed RL framework successfully enables zero-shot sim-to-real transfer for whole-body quadruped locomotion, outperforming the existing controller in disturbance recovery.

Abstract: Learning-based approaches to locomotion have risen in popularity in recent years, showing the capability for complex legged locomotion and whole-body control. Reinforcement learning (RL), the primary learning-based approach for locomotion, often utilizes a high-performance simulation tool, providing a controlled and efficient training and development environment. However, policies that perform well in simulation frequently encounter unexpected challenges when deployed on a physical system, known as the sim-to-real gap. This work presents a robust RL locomotion framework capable of whole-body control. The proposed RL framework utilizes Nvidia's new set of simulation tools, Isaac Sim, and its companion RL framework, Isaac Lab, for training, achieving a zero-shot sim-to-real policy. The performance of our policy is validated on physical hardware using the Unitree Go1, with experimental results showing similar velocity tracking performance to the quadruped's integrated controller, with a greater ability to recover from large disturbances, and achieve linear velocities of 2.0 m/s and angular velocities of 1.8 rad/s.

</details>


### [496] [World Translation: Minimizing Sim-to-Real Gap with Backward Dynamics Extraction and Unpaired Domain Translation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18154)
*Xinchen Yao,Leixin Chang,Hua Chen*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: World Translation bridges sim-to-real gaps by extracting unobservable dynamics information backward from observed transitions and translating it across domains as an unpaired style-transfer problem, outperforming baselines when observation history is uninformative.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Real-to-sim learned dynamics models suffer from partial observability—the same observation can lead to different transitions due to unobservable factors, and existing methods that recover these from observation history fail when history is uninformative (e.g., sudden contact events).

Method: Proposes World Translation: instead of forward prediction from history, extract unobservable dynamics information backward from an observed transition, then treat the sim-to-real dynamics transfer as an unpaired domain-translation problem that preserves dynamics content while transferring domain style.

Result: Outperforms baselines in dynamics modeling accuracy across humanoid, quadruped, and manipulator platforms, with largest gains when unobservable factors cannot be recovered from history. Validated via real-robot deployment on Go2 quadruped showing improved policy transfer.

Conclusion: Leveraging backward extraction of unobservable dynamics and unpaired domain translation effectively addresses partial observability in learned dynamics, enabling more accurate sim-to-real policy transfer.

Abstract: The gap between simulation and reality remains a fundamental challenge in deploying simulation-trained robotic policies in the real world. Real-to-sim methods narrow this gap from the real side, learning transition dynamics from real data to build a more realistic digital world. Learned dynamics models are their dominant instance. Such methods, however, face a partial observability problem: the same observation may branch to different transitions due to unobservable factors. Existing methods assume these factors can be recovered from observation history. However, this may fail whenever observation history is uninformative, such as a sudden contact event with no prior warning. To address this limitation, we propose \textit{World Translation}, which exploits a complementary strength of simulators and learned dynamics. Simulators are deterministic but physically imperfect, while learned models are accurate but underdetermined under partial observability. Rather than predicting transitions forward from history, we extract the unobservable dynamics information backward from an observed transition, then translate this feature across simulation and reality as an unpaired domain-translation problem that preserves dynamics content while transferring domain style. Experiments across humanoid, quadruped, and manipulator platforms show that our method achieves more accurate dynamics modeling than baselines, with the largest gains when unobservable factors cannot be recovered from observation history. Real-robot deployment on Go2 quadruped confirms improved policy transfer.

</details>


### [497] [Imitation of Arm Gestures by the Semi-Humanoid Robot NICO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18197)
*Anastasiya Ihnatovich,Igor Farkaš*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: A system for human arm gesture imitation by the semi-humanoid robot NICO using MediaPipe pose estimation and analytical geometry, tested with six participants showing promising but limited results.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: To enable seamless human-robot interaction by allowing humanoid robots to imitate human arm gestures, leveraging anthropomorphic features for more natural communication.

Method: Uses MediaPipe to extract 3D coordinates of body landmarks from RGB frames, computes joint angles via geometric relations, and maps them to NICO's motor configuration for motion execution.

Result: Produced meaningful imitative motions from monocular RGB input in preliminary experiments with six participants, but showed limitations in complex poses and wrist-related movements.

Conclusion: The approach is viable for imitation learning from RGB input but requires improvements for complex gestures and wrist articulation.

Abstract: Seamless human-robot interaction (HRI) requires a number of perceptual and motor abilities from the robot, one of them being the imitation of human gestures. Humanoid robots have an advantage in HRI thanks to their anthropomorphic features. In this work, we develop a system for imitation of human arm gestures by the semi-humanoid robot NICO based on analytical geometry and a pretrained MediaPipe pose-estimation model. For each input RGB frame, 3D coordinates of relevant human body landmarks, including arm joints and hand keypoints, are obtained using the MediaPipe framework. Joint angles are then computed from these coordinates using derived geometric relations. Finally, the computed angles are properly mapped to NICO's motor configuration and executed in a predefined motion sequence. Preliminary experiments on several representative arm gestures with six participants of different height indicate that the proposed method can produce meaningful imitative motions from monocular RGB input only, while also highlighting limitations in more complex poses and wrist-related movements.

</details>


### [498] [Learning Adaptive Safety Margins for Visual Navigation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18200)
*Junyi Hu,Shuaihang Yuan,Geeta Chandra Raju Bethala,Anthony Tzes,Yi Fang*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Proposes a context-conditioned safety critic with three loss terms to rank diffusion-based trajectory proposals for robot navigation, achieving state-of-the-art SR and SPL in simulation and zero-shot transfer to a humanoid robot.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Fixed safety margins cause failures (detours or risky shortcuts) in cluttered spaces; diffusion planners generate diverse trajectories but lack reliable selection mechanisms.

Method: Three-term safety critic: (1) safety term (clearance-budget penalty + CBF residual), (2) efficiency term (smoothness + safety-gated detour-ratio penalty), (3) distance-constraint matching term. Trained with privileged ESDF, distilled to perception-only via two-stage teacher-student.

Result: Highest SR and SPL on PointGoal navigation in HM3D/MP3D (including cross-dataset); zero-shot sim-to-real transfer to Unitree G1 humanoid.

Conclusion: Adaptive, learned safety margins outperform fixed margins; the approach generalizes across scenes, datasets, and robot platforms without task-specific tuning.

Abstract: Robots in cluttered indoor spaces often fail not because they cannot generate collision-free paths, but because a fixed safety margin is mis-calibrated: conservative margins cause detours and timeouts, while permissive margins lead to near-boundary shortcuts under perception bias. Diffusion-based planners propose diverse trajectory candidates from egocentric RGB-D, yet reliable selection remains the bottleneck. We propose a context-conditioned safety critic that learns an adaptive clearance preference for ranking diffusion proposals, decomposed into three complementary terms: (i) a safety term with a clearance-budget penalty and a control-barrier-function residual for waypoint- and transition-wise safety, (ii) an efficiency term combining a smoothness penalty with a safety-gated detour-ratio penalty that avoids detours without incentivizing risky shortcuts, and (iii) a distance-constraint matching term that anchors the learned budget to realized ESDF clearances to prevent margin collapse. We train the critic with privileged ESDF geometry in simulation and distill it into a perception-only selector via a two-stage teacher-student procedure. On PointGoal navigation in HM3D and MP3D, including cross-dataset transfer, our method achieves the highest success rate (SR) and success weighted by path length (SPL) among strong diffusion, optimization, and RL baselines. Trained purely in simulation, it transfers to a Unitree G1 humanoid and navigates cluttered indoor scenes without task-specific tuning.

</details>


### [499] [Optimization of sim-to-real transfer in the humanoid robot NICO](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18210)
*Juraj Gavura,Igor Farkaš*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: This paper extends a low-cost haptic calibration method from 2D reaching to 3D tabletop grasping on the humanoid robot NICO, using YOLO detection, stereo vision from built-in fisheye cameras, and visual feedback to improve grasping success without external tracking systems.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: The sim-to-real gap causes small positioning errors that prevent successful grasping when simulated movements are executed on physical robots. The authors aim to address this with a low-cost calibration approach that doesn't rely on expensive RGB-D cameras or motion capture systems.

Method: The method extends a prior haptic calibration approach from reaching to grasping by adding: (1) YOLO-based object and hand detection, (2) stereo vision-based localization using the robot's built-in low-resolution fisheye cameras, (3) task-specific corrections for grasp execution, and (4) a visual feedback model to align the hand with the detected object before grasping.

Result: The fully nonlinear calibration model achieved the best performance inside the calibrated area, while the visual feedback model achieved the highest overall grasping success across the full tabletop workspace.

Conclusion: A calibration-based grasping pipeline without RGB-D cameras or external tracking is feasible. The visual feedback model provides better generalization across the workspace, whereas nonlinear calibration excels in the calibrated region.

Abstract: Robotic grasping requires accurate coordination between visual perception, object localization, inverse kinematics, and hand control. However, when movements planned in simulation are executed on a physical robot, the sim-to-real gap can cause small positioning errors that prevent successful grasping. In our previous work, we introduced a low-cost haptic calibration method that improved 2D reaching accuracy of the humanoid robot NICO. In this paper, we extend this approach from reaching to tabletop object grasping by adding YOLO-based object and hand detection, stereo vision-based localization using the robot's built-in low-resolution fisheye cameras, and task-specific corrections for grasp execution. Together, these components form a novel calibration-based grasping pipeline that does not require RGB-D cameras, motion capture, or external tracking systems. We also implemented a visual feedback model that aligns the robot hand with the detected object before grasping. Our results show that the fully nonlinear calibration model achieved the best performance inside the calibrated area, while the visual feedback model achieved the highest overall grasping success across the full tabletop workspace.

</details>


### [500] [FM-VLA: Force-based Memory for Vision-Language-Action Models in Contact-Rich Manipulation](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18231)
*Ruicheng Li,Qixiu Li,Ruichun Ma,Yu Deng,Lin Luo,Zhiying Du,Jianfeng Xiang,Huizhi Liang,Ruicheng Wang,Jiaolong Yang,Baining Guo*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: FM-VLA is a vision-language-action model augmented with force-based memory tokens (encoded via VAE) to enable temporal reasoning in contact-rich, non-Markovian robotic manipulation tasks, achieving over 80% success rate with minimal overhead.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing memory-augmented VLAs rely on vision-based memory (e.g., sampled past image frames), which is computationally expensive and fails when temporal events are visually ambiguous, such as pushing a button multiple times with small movements.

Method: FM-VLA encodes force histories into compact force memory tokens using a VAE pretrained on force time series reconstruction. These force latent representations, combined with short state history, are projected as additional conditioning tokens to the action expert module.

Result: Over 80% success rate on three memory-dependent tasks (finding hidden block, pressing button, wiping dish for specific number of times) with minimal inference overhead, significantly outperforming baselines.

Conclusion: Force-based memory is a lightweight and effective alternative to vision-based memory for non-Markovian, contact-rich manipulation tasks, enabling VLAs to reason over accumulated contact event history.

Abstract: Vision-language-action (VLA) models have achieved impressive generalization in robotic manipulation, and recent memory-augmented VLAs have relaxed the Markovian assumption by conditioning on past images or language summaries. Vision-based memory approaches address this by conditioning on sampled past image frames, but they are computationally expensive and fundamentally limited when temporal events are visually ambiguous, e.g., pushing a button multiple times with small movements. We propose FM-VLA, a VLA model with force-based memory, enabling temporal context reasoning for non-Markovian, contact-rich manipulation. We encode force histories into compact force memory tokens with a variational autoencoder (VAE) pretrained with force time series reconstruction. By projecting force latent representations and short state history as additional conditioning tokens to the action expert module, we enable VLAs to leverage accumulated contact event history to guide manipulation. We evaluate FM-VLA on three memory-dependent tasks, including finding a hidden block, pressing a button, and wiping a dish for a specific number of times. Our lightweight force memory achieves over 80% success rate with minimal inference overhead, significantly outperforming baseline approaches. Project page: https://qft-333.github.io/FM-VLA-Page/

</details>


### [501] [Patch Policy: Efficient Embodied Control via Dense Visual Representations](https://arxiv.org/abs/2607.18236)
*Gaoyue Zhou,Zichen Jeff Cui,Ada Langford,Bowen Tan,Yann LeCun,Lerrel Pinto*

Main category: cs.RO

TL;DR: Patch Policy is a lightweight extension for transformer-based robot policies that directly uses dense pre-trained patch tokens from Vision Transformers (ViTs), achieving significant performance improvements (40% relative over global-pooled methods, 18% over OpenVLA-OFT with 0.7% parameters) while maintaining fast inference speed.


<details>
  <summary>Details</summary>
Motivation: Existing robot policies either compress visual features into single global tokens (losing spatial detail) or train from scratch (missing benefits of large-scale pre-training). Heavy VLAs that use dense features are too slow for reactive control. The goal is to efficiently leverage dense pre-trained ViT features without the computational overhead.

Method: A minimal architectural extension with a block-causal attention mask that allows transformer-based policies to attend over many patch tokens per observation while preserving temporal causality and incorporating other state information, all without a full VLM backbone.

Result: 40% relative improvement over global-pooled representations across 4 simulated and 3 real-world suites. Surpasses fine-tuned OpenVLA-OFT by 18% while using only ~0.7% of the parameters. Maintains fast inference suitable for high-frequency reactive control.

Conclusion: Patch Policy offers a simple, effective pipeline for robotics to leverage dense visual pre-training without sacrificing efficiency, enabling the community to easily benefit from progress in visual representation learning.

Abstract: Pretrained dense visual features from Vision Transformers (ViTs) are powerful yet have been underutilized in robot learning. Modern robot policies either compress each observation into a single global token, or rely on visual backbones trained from scratch, sacrificing both fine-grained spatial detail and the benefits of large-scale visual pre-training. While there exist policies that do operate on dense patch features like large vision-language-action models (VLAs), they tend to be heavy and slow, inheriting the full cost of a billion-parameter vision-language model (VLM) backbone. We close this gap with Patch Policy, a minimal architectural extension that enables transformer-based policies to consume dense pre-trained patch tokens directly without the computational overhead of a full VLM. At its core is a block-causal attention mask that preserves the temporal causality of standard policies while letting the model attend over many patch tokens per observation, alongside other state information. Patch Policy is lightweight, fast, and highly effective. Across four simulated and three real-world environment suites, our method achieves a 40% relative improvement over policies using state-of-the-art global-pooled representations. Furthermore, it surpasses fine-tuned OpenVLA-OFT by 18% while using roughly 0.7% of the parameters. We believe Patch Policy provides a pipeline for the robotics community to readily leverage continuing progress in visual representation learning, without sacrificing the training efficiency or inference speed required for high-frequency, reactive control. Videos can be viewed at https://patch-policy.github.io

</details>
